clues: A 78-year-old female presents with sudden distortion of central vision in her left eye over the past 2 weeks.
Fundoscopy reveals a grayish-green subretinal lesion with surrounding subretinal fluid and hemorrhage in the macula.

OCT shows subretinal hyperreflective material with intraretinal and subretinal fluid. Fluorescein angiography demonstrates a classic choroidal neovascular membrane with early hyperfluorescence and late leakage.
The patient has a history of dry AMD with large soft drusen in both eyes, and her sister also has AMD.
Anti-VEGF injection (ranibizumab, aflibercept, or bevacizumab) is the first-line treatment for this condition.
teachingPoints: Wet AMD accounts for ~10% of AMD cases but causes ~90% of severe vision loss from AMD.
Amsler grid testing can help patients self-monitor for metamorphopsia, an early sign of conversion from dry to wet AMD.
Anti-VEGF therapy has revolutionized treatment; monthly or treat-and-extend protocols are standard of care.
Risk factors include age >55, smoking, family history, Caucasian race, and the CFH and ARMS2 gene variants.

clues: A 62-year-old African American male presents for a routine eye exam; he is asymptomatic.
Intraocular pressure is 26 mmHg in both eyes. Gonioscopy shows open angles bilaterally.
Humphrey visual field testing reveals an arcuate scotoma in the superior field of the left eye. OCT RNFL shows thinning of the inferior temporal sector corresponding to the field defect.
Cup-to-disc ratio is 0.8 in both eyes with an inferior notch in the left optic nerve. The patient's mother had glaucoma.
First-line medical therapy is a prostaglandin analog such as latanoprost, which lowers IOP by increasing uveoscleral outflow.
teachingPoints: POAG is the leading cause of irreversible blindness worldwide and is more prevalent and aggressive in African Americans.
Glaucoma damage is irreversible; early detection through screening IOP, optic nerve evaluation, and visual fields is crucial.
Target IOP is typically a 20-30% reduction from baseline; treatment is individualized based on disease severity and risk factors.
SLT (selective laser trabeculoplasty) is an effective alternative or adjunct to drops and can be used as initial therapy.

clues: A 70-year-old male with a history of atrial fibrillation presents with sudden, painless loss of vision in his right eye 2 hours ago.
Visual acuity is counting fingers. There is a relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) in the right eye.
Fundoscopy reveals diffuse retinal whitening with a cherry-red spot at the macula. Fluorescein angiography shows delayed arterial filling and a boxcar pattern of blood flow.
The left carotid artery has a bruit on auscultation. Echocardiography is ordered to evaluate for an embolic source.
This is an ophthalmologic emergency analogous to a stroke; workup should include carotid imaging and cardiac evaluation for embolic sources.
teachingPoints: CRAO causes acute retinal ischemia; the retina can tolerate only ~90 minutes of complete ischemia before irreversible damage occurs.
The cherry-red spot appearance is due to the fovea's thinness allowing visualization of the underlying choroidal circulation against the surrounding edematous opaque retina.
Acute management may include ocular massage, anterior chamber paracentesis, or intra-arterial thrombolysis, though evidence is limited.
Giant cell arteritis must be ruled out in patients over 50 with CRAO; check ESR and CRP urgently.

clues: A 55-year-old Asian female presents to the ER with severe right eye pain, headache, nausea, and blurred vision that began suddenly this evening.
The right eye is injected with a mid-dilated, fixed pupil. Corneal edema is present. IOP is 58 mmHg.
Gonioscopy of the fellow eye shows narrow angles (Shaffer grade 1). The anterior chamber is shallow on slit-lamp exam.
The patient was recently started on topiramate for migraines. She is hyperopic with a short axial length.
Definitive treatment is laser peripheral iridotomy (LPI); the fellow eye should also receive prophylactic LPI.
teachingPoints: Acute angle closure is a true ophthalmic emergency; permanent vision loss can occur within hours if IOP is not lowered.
Initial medical treatment includes timolol, apraclonidine, pilocarpine, oral/IV acetazolamide, and IV mannitol if needed.
Risk factors include hyperopia, shallow anterior chamber, female sex, Asian descent, and age over 40.
Several medications can precipitate angle closure, including topiramate, sulfonamides, anticholinergics, and sympathomimetics.

clues: A 58-year-old male with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with gradual blurring of central vision in both eyes over several months.
Visual acuity is 20/60 bilaterally. Fundoscopy shows hard exudates in a circinate pattern around the fovea with microaneurysms and dot-blot hemorrhages.
OCT macula reveals central subfield thickness of 450 microns with intraretinal cystic spaces and subretinal fluid. HbA1c is 9.2%.
The patient has background diabetic retinopathy without neovascularization. He also has nephropathy and hypertension.
First-line treatment is intravitreal anti-VEGF injection; focal/grid laser and intravitreal steroids are adjuncts.
teachingPoints: DME is the most common cause of vision loss in diabetic patients and can occur at any stage of diabetic retinopathy.
The DRCR.net Protocol T trial showed aflibercept was superior to bevacizumab for eyes with baseline VA of 20/50 or worse.
Systemic control of blood glucose, blood pressure, and lipids is critical for reducing progression of DME.
OCT is essential for diagnosis and monitoring treatment response; central subfield thickness correlates with disease activity.

clues: A 62-year-old myopic male reports seeing flashing lights and a shower of new floaters in his right eye, followed by a curtain-like shadow in the inferior visual field.
Dilated fundus exam reveals a superotemporal horseshoe retinal tear with subretinal fluid extending to the macula.
B-scan ultrasonography confirms the retinal detachment with a mobile, folded retina and posterior vitreous detachment.
The patient is highly myopic (-8.00 D) and had cataract surgery in the same eye 6 months ago.
Surgical repair options include pneumatic retinopexy, scleral buckle, or pars plana vitrectomy depending on the characteristics of the detachment.
teachingPoints: Rhegmatogenous RD is the most common type, caused by a retinal break allowing vitreous fluid to enter the subretinal space.
Risk factors include high myopia, prior cataract surgery, trauma, lattice degeneration, and family history.
Macula-on detachments are more urgent as they have better visual prognosis if repaired before the macula detaches.
Patients should be educated about symptoms of PVD (flashes and floaters) and to seek immediate evaluation if a shadow or curtain appears.

clues: A 32-year-old male presents with a red, painful left eye with photophobia and tearing that developed over 2 days.
Slit-lamp exam shows fine keratic precipitates, 2+ anterior chamber cells and flare, and posterior synechiae at the 6 o'clock position.
IOP is 8 mmHg (lower than the fellow eye at 14 mmHg). There is no vitritis or retinal pathology on dilated exam.
The patient has a history of recurrent low back pain and morning stiffness. Previous episodes of similar eye symptoms have occurred in the other eye.
HLA-B27 testing is positive. Workup for ankylosing spondylitis is initiated. Treatment includes topical steroids and cycloplegics.
teachingPoints: HLA-B27-associated uveitis is the most common cause of acute anterior uveitis in young adults and is typically unilateral and alternating.
Aggressive topical corticosteroid therapy (e.g., prednisolone acetate 1% hourly) with a slow taper is essential to prevent complications.
Cycloplegics (cyclopentolate or atropine) help with pain by preventing ciliary spasm and reduce the risk of posterior synechiae formation.
Systemic associations include ankylosing spondylitis, reactive arthritis, inflammatory bowel disease, and psoriatic arthritis.

clues: A 22-year-old male presents with progressively worsening vision in both eyes that is not fully correctable with glasses.
Retinoscopy reveals a scissoring reflex. Slit-lamp exam shows Vogt striae and a Fleischer ring at the base of the cone.
Corneal topography demonstrates inferior corneal steepening with an asymmetric bowtie pattern. Pachymetry shows central thinning to 460 microns.
The patient has a history of chronic eye rubbing due to allergic conjunctivitis and a family history of keratoconus in his brother.
Corneal collagen cross-linking (CXL) with riboflavin and UV-A light is indicated to halt progression; a rigid gas-permeable contact lens improves vision.
teachingPoints: Keratoconus is a progressive, non-inflammatory ectasia of the cornea that typically presents in the second decade of life.
Munson sign (bulging of the lower lid on downgaze) and Rizzuti sign (focused light beam on nasal limbus) are classic clinical signs in advanced cases.
Corneal cross-linking is FDA-approved for progressive keratoconus and has been shown to halt progression in the vast majority of patients.
Eye rubbing is a major modifiable risk factor; counseling patients to avoid rubbing is an important part of management.

clues: A 65-year-old female with a history of hypertension presents with painless, gradual blurring of vision in her left eye over 1 week.
Visual acuity is 20/200 OS. Fundoscopy reveals diffuse flame-shaped hemorrhages in all four quadrants, dilated tortuous veins, cotton-wool spots, and disc edema—a 'blood and thunder' appearance.
OCT shows significant macular edema with central subfield thickness of 580 microns. Fluorescein angiography shows delayed venous filling and widespread capillary non-perfusion.
Blood pressure is 170/100 mmHg. Labs reveal elevated fasting glucose and lipid panel abnormalities.
Intravitreal anti-VEGF is first-line for macular edema from CRVO; ischemic CRVO carries risk of neovascular glaucoma requiring close follow-up.
teachingPoints: CRVO is strongly associated with systemic vascular disease: hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and hypercoagulable states.
The distinction between ischemic and non-ischemic CRVO is critical; ischemic CRVO has >10 disc areas of capillary non-perfusion on FA and worse prognosis.
Neovascular glaucoma can develop in up to 60% of ischemic CRVO cases within 3 months (the '90-day glaucoma').
The CRUISE trial demonstrated that ranibizumab significantly improves visual acuity in CRVO-associated macular edema.

clues: A 40-year-old female presents with a painful, watery, red left eye with foreign body sensation and photophobia for 3 days.
Slit-lamp exam with fluorescein staining reveals a branching dendritic ulcer with terminal bulbs on the central cornea. Corneal sensation is decreased.
The ulcer stains well with fluorescein (epithelial edges) and rose bengal (surrounding devitalized cells). There is mild anterior chamber reaction.
The patient had a similar episode 2 years ago in the same eye that resolved with antiviral drops. She reports recent stress and sun exposure.
Treatment is topical ganciclovir gel or oral valacyclovir; topical corticosteroids are contraindicated in epithelial disease as they worsen viral replication.
teachingPoints: HSV keratitis is the most common infectious cause of corneal blindness in developed countries.
The classic dendritic ulcer with terminal bulbs is pathognomonic; it must be distinguished from pseudodendrites seen in herpes zoster, which lack terminal bulbs.
The HEDS trial showed that oral acyclovir reduces recurrence of HSV keratitis by approximately 50%.
Topical steroids should never be used for epithelial HSV keratitis but are an important part of treatment for stromal keratitis (always with antiviral cover).

clues: A 28-year-old obese female presents with bilateral headaches worse in the morning, transient visual obscurations, and pulsatile tinnitus.
Visual acuity is 20/20 bilaterally. Fundoscopy shows bilateral optic disc swelling with peripapillary hemorrhages, obscured disc margins, and an enlarged blind spot on confrontation.

Humphrey visual field shows enlarged blind spots bilaterally. MRI brain is normal, and MRV shows no venous sinus thrombosis. Lumbar puncture reveals opening pressure of 32 cm H2O with normal CSF composition.
The patient has a BMI of 38 and recently started oral contraceptives. She also takes tetracycline for acne.

The diagnosis is idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH/pseudotumor cerebri). Treatment includes weight loss, acetazolamide, and possibly optic nerve sheath fenestration or CSF shunting if vision is threatened.
teachingPoints: Papilledema is bilateral disc swelling due to elevated intracranial pressure and must be distinguished from other causes of disc edema (e.g., optic neuritis, NAION).
IIH classically affects young obese women of childbearing age; medications such as tetracyclines, vitamin A, and growth hormone can be contributing factors.
Acetazolamide reduces CSF production and is the first-line medical treatment; the IIHTT trial confirmed its efficacy in combination with weight loss.
Visual field testing is the most important metric for monitoring disease progression; loss of visual acuity is a late finding.

clues: A 52-year-old male who works outdoors presents with a painless, fleshy growth on the nasal side of his right eye that has been slowly growing over years.
A triangular, fibrovascular tissue extends from the nasal conjunctiva onto the cornea, reaching approximately 2 mm from the visual axis. There is associated conjunctival injection.
Corneal topography shows irregular astigmatism induced by the lesion. Visual acuity is 20/30 with best correction.
The patient lives in a tropical climate and has significant UV exposure. He does not wear sunglasses regularly.
Surgical excision with conjunctival autograft is the preferred technique, as it has the lowest recurrence rate compared to bare sclera or amniotic membrane transplant.
teachingPoints: Pterygia are caused by UV exposure and are most common in the 'pterygium belt' between 37°N and 37°S latitude.
Indications for surgery include encroachment on the visual axis, significant induced astigmatism, motility restriction, or cosmetic concern.
Recurrence rates are highest with bare sclera technique (~50%) and lowest with conjunctival autograft (~5-10%).
Pterygia must be distinguished from pingueculae (which do not extend onto the cornea) and from conjunctival neoplasias such as squamous cell carcinoma.

clues: A 30-year-old female presents with subacute painful vision loss in her right eye over 5 days, with pain worsening on eye movement.
Visual acuity is 20/100 OD. There is a relative afferent pupillary defect. Color vision is markedly reduced (1/14 Ishihara plates).

The optic disc appears normal on fundoscopy (retrobulbar neuritis). MRI orbits with contrast shows enhancement of the right optic nerve. MRI brain reveals several periventricular white matter lesions.
Visual field testing shows a central scotoma. The patient reports she experienced tingling in her legs 6 months ago that resolved spontaneously.
IV methylprednisolone speeds recovery but does not improve final visual outcome. MRI findings and clinical history suggest high risk for multiple sclerosis.
teachingPoints: The Optic Neuritis Treatment Trial (ONTT) showed that IV steroids speed recovery but oral prednisone alone actually increased recurrence risk.
Optic neuritis is the presenting symptom of MS in approximately 20% of patients; the 15-year risk of MS is ~50% overall, higher with brain lesions on MRI.
Most patients recover vision to 20/40 or better within weeks to months, even without treatment.
Uhthoff phenomenon—worsening of vision with heat or exercise—is characteristic of demyelinating optic neuritis.

clues: A 72-year-old male presents 3 days after uneventful cataract surgery with rapidly worsening vision, pain, and redness in the operated eye.
Visual acuity is hand motions. Slit-lamp exam reveals lid edema, conjunctival injection, corneal edema, a hypopyon, and dense fibrin in the anterior chamber.
B-scan ultrasonography shows dense vitreous opacities and membranes. There is no retinal detachment.
The intraocular lens is in the bag. Vitreous tap shows gram-positive cocci. The surgery was performed at an ambulatory surgery center.
The Endophthalmitis Vitrectomy Study (EVS) guides management: intravitreal antibiotics (vancomycin + ceftazidime) for all; vitrectomy recommended if presenting vision is light perception or worse.
teachingPoints: Post-cataract endophthalmitis occurs in approximately 0.05-0.1% of cases. The most common organisms are coagulase-negative staphylococci.
The EVS showed no benefit of IV antibiotics; intravitreal injection is the cornerstone of treatment.
Intracameral cefuroxime at the end of cataract surgery significantly reduces endophthalmitis risk (ESCRS study).
Late-onset (>6 weeks) endophthalmitis is often caused by Cutibacterium acnes and presents more indolently with granulomatous inflammation.

clues: A 2-year-old child is brought in by parents who noticed a white pupillary reflex (leukocoria) in photos taken with flash.
Examination under anesthesia reveals a large white-yellow endophytic retinal mass with calcifications. The affected eye has no red reflex.
B-scan ultrasound shows a hyperechoic intraocular mass with calcification. MRI orbits and brain shows no optic nerve or extraocular extension.
The tumor involves more than 50% of the retina with vitreous seeding. The other eye appears normal. There is no family history.
Enucleation is indicated for advanced unilateral disease with no chance of useful vision; intra-arterial chemotherapy and focal therapy are used for salvageable eyes.
teachingPoints: Retinoblastoma is the most common intraocular malignancy of childhood; the average age at diagnosis is 18 months.
Leukocoria is the most common presenting sign; the differential includes Coats disease, persistent fetal vasculature, toxocariasis, and retinopathy of prematurity.
The RB1 tumor suppressor gene mutation is responsible; bilateral cases are heritable and carry risk of secondary malignancies (e.g., osteosarcoma).
Survival rates exceed 95% in developed countries with prompt treatment, but delayed diagnosis can be life-threatening.

clues: A 38-year-old female presents with bilateral eye protrusion, lid retraction, and a gritty foreign body sensation that has worsened over 3 months.
Hertel exophthalmometry measures 24 mm bilaterally (normal <21 mm). Upper lid retraction, lid lag on downgaze, and inferior scleral show are noted. Extraocular motility shows restriction in upgaze.
CT orbits reveals bilateral enlargement of the inferior and medial rectus muscles with tendon sparing. Thyroid function tests show suppressed TSH with elevated free T4.
The patient has been recently diagnosed with Graves disease and is on methimazole. She is a current smoker.
Teprotumumab (an IGF-1R inhibitor) is FDA-approved for TED and has shown significant reduction in proptosis and diplopia. Smoking cessation is critical.
teachingPoints: Thyroid eye disease is the most common cause of both unilateral and bilateral proptosis in adults.
The Clinical Activity Score (CAS) helps determine if the disease is in the active inflammatory phase (responds to immunosuppression) or the chronic fibrotic phase.
Compressive optic neuropathy is the most feared complication; urgently treated with IV steroids and/or orbital decompression surgery.
Smoking is the single most important modifiable risk factor; it increases severity and reduces treatment response.

clues: A 25-year-old female contact lens wearer presents with a painful red left eye with mucopurulent discharge and reduced vision since yesterday.
A large central corneal infiltrate (3 mm) with overlying epithelial defect, stromal thinning, and a ring-shaped infiltrate is seen. There is an associated hypopyon.
Corneal scraping for gram stain shows gram-negative rods. Culture is plated on blood agar, chocolate agar, and Sabouraud agar.
The patient admits to sleeping in her monthly contact lenses and rinsing them with tap water. She swims regularly with contacts in.
Aggressive fortified topical antibiotics (tobramycin + cefazolin) every 30-60 minutes are started empirically. A fluoroquinolone monotherapy is an acceptable alternative for smaller ulcers.
teachingPoints: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the most common organism in contact lens-related bacterial keratitis and can progress rapidly to corneal perforation.
Contact lens risk factors include overnight wear, poor hygiene, swimming with lenses, and using tap water instead of sterile solution.
Corneal cultures should be obtained before starting antibiotics in large, central, or sight-threatening ulcers.
Never patch a corneal ulcer; patching creates a warm, moist environment that promotes bacterial growth.

clues: A 58-year-old male with diabetes and hypertension notices painless visual field loss in his left eye upon waking this morning.
Visual acuity is 20/40 OS. There is a relative afferent pupillary defect. The left optic disc is swollen with peripapillary hemorrhages. The right disc has a small cup-to-disc ratio (disc at risk).
Visual field testing reveals an inferior altitudinal defect. ESR is 12 mm/hr and CRP is normal, ruling out GCA.
The patient takes sildenafil for erectile dysfunction and has a history of obstructive sleep apnea. Blood pressure is 150/95 mmHg.
There is no proven treatment for NAION; managing vascular risk factors and discontinuing vasoconstrictive medications are recommended. The fellow eye is at 15% risk over 5 years.
teachingPoints: NAION is the most common acute optic neuropathy in patients over age 50 and is caused by hypoperfusion of the short posterior ciliary arteries.
The 'disc at risk' (small cup-to-disc ratio with crowded nerve fibers) in the fellow eye is a structural risk factor.
PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil) and nocturnal hypotension are associated risk factors; obstructive sleep apnea is increasingly recognized as a contributing factor.
It is critical to rule out GCA (arteritic AION) in patients over 50 with ESR, CRP, and CBC, as arteritic AION requires immediate high-dose steroids.

clues: A 5-year-old girl is found to have reduced vision in her right eye (20/80) during a school vision screening; the left eye is 20/20.
Cover-uncover test reveals a small right esotropia. Cycloplegic refraction shows +5.00 D in the right eye and +1.00 D in the left eye.
The anterior and posterior segments are normal in both eyes. There is no organic cause for the decreased vision.
There is a significant anisometropia (difference in refractive error between the two eyes). The child has no prior eye examinations.
Treatment involves full-time spectacle correction followed by occlusion therapy (patching the good eye) or atropine penalization of the better eye.
teachingPoints: Amblyopia is the most common cause of monocular vision loss in children, affecting approximately 2-3% of the population.
Treatment is most effective before age 7-8 but the PEDIG studies have shown benefit in older children up to age 17.
Common causes include strabismus, anisometropia, and visual deprivation (e.g., ptosis, cataract).
The ATS studies showed that 2 hours of patching per day is as effective as 6 hours for moderate amblyopia.

clues: A 60-year-old Caucasian male is found to have an incidental pigmented fundus lesion during a routine dilated exam. He is asymptomatic.
Fundoscopy reveals an elevated, dome-shaped, dark-brown choroidal mass measuring approximately 12 mm in base and 4 mm in thickness, with overlying orange pigment (lipofuscin) and subretinal fluid.
B-scan ultrasound shows a mushroom-shaped lesion with low-to-medium internal reflectivity, choroidal excavation, and acoustic hollowing. The lesion has documented growth over 6 months.
PET-CT and liver function tests are ordered for metastatic workup. Liver is the most common site of metastasis.
Treatment options include plaque brachytherapy for medium-sized tumors or enucleation for large tumors. The COMS trial guides management by tumor size.
teachingPoints: Choroidal melanoma is the most common primary intraocular malignancy in adults; risk factors include light skin, light iris color, and ocular melanocytosis.
Features distinguishing melanoma from a choroidal nevus include thickness >2 mm, subretinal fluid, symptoms, orange pigment, and proximity to the disc (mnemonic: 'To Find Small Ocular Melanoma').
GEP (gene expression profiling) and chromosome 3 monosomy are the most important prognostic factors for metastatic risk.
Despite successful local treatment, approximately 50% of patients with high-risk molecular features develop metastatic disease, primarily to the liver.

clues: A premature infant born at 26 weeks gestational age and weighing 800 grams is undergoing routine screening eye examinations in the NICU.
Dilated fundus exam at 32 weeks postmenstrual age reveals a demarcation line between vascular and avascular retina in Zone II of both eyes.
One week later, the ridge has developed an extraretinal fibrovascular proliferation (Stage 3) in Zone II over 8 contiguous clock hours with plus disease (dilated, tortuous posterior pole vessels).
The infant received supplemental oxygen for 6 weeks. Birth weight and gestational age are the strongest risk factors.
This meets criteria for Type 1 ROP (treatment-warranted). Laser photocoagulation of the avascular retina or intravitreal anti-VEGF (bevacizumab) is indicated.
teachingPoints: ROP screening is recommended for infants born at ≤30 weeks GA or ≤1500g birth weight, beginning at 31 weeks PMA or 4 weeks chronological age.
The ETROP study established Type 1 and Type 2 ROP classifications to guide treatment decisions.
Anti-VEGF therapy (BEAT-ROP trial) is effective especially for Zone I disease, but requires longer follow-up as late recurrences can occur.
Stage 4 (partial retinal detachment) and Stage 5 (total retinal detachment) carry very poor visual prognosis.

clues: A 7-year-old boy presents with a swollen, red, painful right eye with fever (39°C) and malaise for 2 days following an upper respiratory infection.
There is proptosis, chemosis, restricted and painful extraocular movements, and decreased visual acuity (20/60) in the right eye. Eyelids are erythematous and swollen.
CT orbits with contrast shows a subperiosteal abscess along the medial orbital wall with opacification of the adjacent ethmoid sinus.
The patient had a recent sinus infection. Nasal congestion and purulent rhinorrhea are present. WBC is elevated at 18,000.

IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (ampicillin-sulbactam or ceftriaxone + metronidazole) are initiated. Surgical drainage of the subperiosteal abscess is required if there is no improvement in 24-48 hours.
teachingPoints: Orbital cellulitis is most commonly caused by contiguous spread from ethmoid sinusitis in children.
Distinguishing preseptal from orbital cellulitis is critical: proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, decreased vision, and pain with eye movement indicate orbital involvement.
Complications include cavernous sinus thrombosis, intracranial abscess, meningitis, and permanent vision loss from optic nerve compression.
CT with contrast is the imaging modality of choice; it helps identify subperiosteal or orbital abscesses that may require surgical drainage.

clues: A 65-year-old female complains of blurred vision that is worse in the morning and gradually improves throughout the day, progressing over several years.
Slit-lamp exam reveals bilateral corneal guttae (golden-brown excrescences on the endothelium) in a beaten-metal appearance. Mild stromal and epithelial edema is present.
Specular microscopy shows decreased endothelial cell density (800 cells/mm²) with polymegethism and pleomorphism. Pachymetry shows increased corneal thickness (620 microns).
The patient's mother had a similar condition requiring corneal transplant. Symptoms worsen with humid weather.
DMEK or DSAEK (endothelial keratoplasty) is the treatment of choice for advanced disease, with faster visual recovery than penetrating keratoplasty.
teachingPoints: Fuchs dystrophy is the most common corneal dystrophy requiring surgical intervention and is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern.
Morning blurriness improving throughout the day is characteristic—overnight lid closure prevents tear evaporation, worsening corneal edema, which clears as the cornea dehydrates during the day.
Hypertonic saline drops (5% NaCl) and a hair dryer at arm's length can help dehydrate the cornea and improve morning symptoms in early disease.
Cataract surgery in Fuchs patients requires special consideration as it can accelerate corneal decompensation; combined cataract/DMEK procedures are common.

clues: A 45-year-old female presents with fluctuating binocular diplopia and droopy eyelids that worsen throughout the day and improve after rest.

Variable ptosis is present, worse on the left. Orbicularis oculi weakness is noted. Extraocular movements show a pattern that does not conform to a single cranial nerve palsy. Pupillary function is normal.
The ice test is positive—ptosis improves after applying an ice pack to the closed lid for 2 minutes. Acetylcholine receptor antibodies are positive.
The patient reports difficulty swallowing at the end of the day. CT chest is ordered to evaluate for thymoma.
Treatment includes pyridostigmine (acetylcholinesterase inhibitor) for symptomatic relief; systemic immunosuppression and thymectomy may be needed if generalized symptoms develop.
teachingPoints: Ocular involvement is the initial presentation in approximately 50-75% of myasthenia gravis patients; about half progress to generalized disease within 2 years.
Myasthenia can mimic any ocular motility disorder; it should be considered whenever diplopia and ptosis fluctuate and do not follow a cranial nerve pattern.
The ice test is a simple bedside test with high specificity; cooling improves neuromuscular transmission by inhibiting acetylcholinesterase at the neuromuscular junction.
Patients should be warned about medications that can exacerbate myasthenia, including aminoglycosides, beta-blockers, and fluoroquinolones.

clues: A 28-year-old male presents with blurred vision and floaters in his right eye for 1 week.
Fundoscopy reveals a fluffy white retinal lesion adjacent to a pigmented chorioretinal scar ('satellite lesion'). There is moderate vitritis with a 'headlight in the fog' appearance.
Anterior chamber shows 1+ cells. Toxoplasma IgG antibodies are positive; IgM is negative, suggesting reactivation of latent infection.
The patient is immunocompetent and was born in Brazil, where Toxoplasma gondii is endemic. He has had a previous episode in the same eye.
Treatment is triple therapy: pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + folinic acid (leucovorin) + systemic corticosteroids. Alternative: TMP-SMX + prednisone.
teachingPoints: Toxoplasmosis is the most common cause of infectious posterior uveitis worldwide.
The classic presentation is a focal necrotizing retinitis adjacent to an old chorioretinal scar, representing reactivation of congenital or acquired infection.
Corticosteroids should always be given WITH anti-parasitic cover, never alone, as steroids alone can lead to fulminant infection.
Lesions threatening the macula or optic nerve are treated more aggressively; peripheral lesions in immunocompetent patients may be observed.

clues: A 50-year-old male with poorly controlled type 1 diabetes for 30 years presents with sudden onset of floaters and decreased vision in his right eye.
Fundoscopy reveals neovascularization at the disc (NVD) and elsewhere (NVE), with a preretinal hemorrhage. There is vitreous hemorrhage partially obscuring the view.
Fluorescein angiography of the left eye (better view) shows widespread capillary non-perfusion, neovascularization with leakage, and microaneurysms. OCT shows tractional changes.
HbA1c is 10.5%. The patient has nephropathy requiring dialysis and peripheral neuropathy.

Panretinal photocoagulation (PRP) is the standard treatment for PDR. Anti-VEGF injections are used as adjuncts. Vitrectomy is needed for non-clearing vitreous hemorrhage or tractional retinal detachment.
teachingPoints: PDR is characterized by retinal neovascularization driven by severe retinal ischemia and VEGF upregulation.
The DRS and ETDRS trials established PRP as the standard treatment, reducing the risk of severe vision loss by >50%.
Protocol S (DRCR.net) showed that anti-VEGF (ranibizumab) was non-inferior to PRP for PDR over 2 years, with less visual field loss.
Vitreous hemorrhage from PDR is a common cause of acute vision loss in diabetic patients and may require vitrectomy if it does not clear in 1-3 months.

clues: A 6-month-old infant is brought in by parents who noticed her eyes crossing inward since about 3 months of age.
A large-angle constant esotropia of approximately 40 prism diopters is present. Cross-fixation is observed (uses right eye in left gaze and vice versa).
Cycloplegic refraction shows +2.00 D bilaterally, which is age-appropriate. Inferior oblique overaction is not yet present. No latent nystagmus is observed.
The child was born full-term with no birth trauma. Family history is positive for strabismus. There is no improvement with spectacle trial.
Bilateral medial rectus recessions are the standard surgical treatment, ideally performed between 6 and 12 months of age for optimal binocular development.
teachingPoints: Infantile esotropia presents before 6 months of age with a large, constant deviation; it is not caused by refractive error and requires surgery.
Early surgery (before age 2) improves the chance of developing some degree of binocular vision (peripheral fusion).
Amblyopia may coexist and should be treated before or concurrent with surgery.
Dissociated vertical deviation (DVD) and inferior oblique overaction commonly develop later and may require additional surgery.

clues: A 35-year-old female presents with a painless, firm lump on her upper right eyelid present for 6 weeks.

A non-tender, well-defined, rubbery nodule is palpable within the tarsal plate. The overlying skin is normal. Eversion of the lid reveals a focal granulomatous lesion against the palpebral conjunctiva.
There is associated meibomian gland inspissation and posterior blepharitis. The lesion does not transilluminate.
The patient has a history of acne rosacea and recurrent styes. She reports poor lid hygiene.
Conservative management (warm compresses, lid massage, lid scrubs) is first-line. If persistent after 4-6 weeks, incision and curettage from the conjunctival surface is curative.
teachingPoints: A chalazion is a chronic sterile lipogranulomatous inflammation of a meibomian gland, distinct from a hordeolum (stye) which is an acute infection.
Recurrent chalazia in the same location in an older patient should raise suspicion for sebaceous gland carcinoma; biopsy is indicated.
Intralesional triamcinolone injection is an alternative to surgery for patients who prefer non-surgical management.
Underlying blepharitis and meibomian gland dysfunction should be treated to reduce recurrence.

clues: A 40-year-old male presents with bilateral blurred vision and photophobia 3 weeks after a penetrating injury to his left eye that was surgically repaired.
Both eyes show granulomatous anterior uveitis with mutton-fat keratic precipitates and iris nodules. Fundoscopy reveals multifocal yellowish-white choroidal lesions (Dalen-Fuchs nodules).
FA shows pinpoint hyperfluorescent leaking lesions at the RPE level. OCT demonstrates subretinal fluid and choroidal thickening bilaterally.
The previously injured (exciting) eye had an open globe injury with uveal tissue prolapse. The fellow (sympathizing) eye was completely normal until now.
High-dose systemic corticosteroids with a slow taper are initiated; steroid-sparing immunosuppressive agents (mycophenolate, azathioprine) are typically required long-term.
teachingPoints: Sympathetic ophthalmia is a rare bilateral granulomatous panuveitis that occurs after penetrating trauma or surgery to one eye, mediated by autoimmunity against uveal/retinal antigens.
The latent period is typically 2 weeks to several months after the inciting injury; it can occur even years later.
Enucleation of the exciting eye within 2 weeks of injury was historically performed to prevent SO, but is controversial if inflammation is already present.
Prognosis is generally good with prompt and aggressive immunosuppression; delayed treatment can lead to chronic inflammation and vision loss in both eyes.

clues: A 22-year-old female contact lens wearer presents with severe pain disproportionate to clinical findings in her left eye, with redness and photophobia worsening over 2 weeks despite antibiotic drops.
Slit-lamp exam shows a paracentral epithelial irregularity with a perineural infiltrate (radial keratoneuritis). Early ring infiltrate is forming.
Confocal microscopy reveals double-walled cysts in the corneal stroma. Culture on non-nutrient agar with E. coli overlay eventually grows trophozoites.
The patient made homemade saline to store her contact lenses and frequently swam in a lake wearing her lenses.
Treatment requires prolonged dual therapy with polyhexamethylene biguanide (PHMB) + propamidine (Brolene), applied hourly initially. Treatment duration is typically 3-6 months or longer.
teachingPoints: Acanthamoeba keratitis is notoriously difficult to treat due to the organism's cyst form, which is resistant to most antimicrobials.
Radial keratoneuritis (perineural infiltrates) is a nearly pathognomonic early finding, caused by the organism's tropism for corneal nerves.
Early diagnosis is key; confocal microscopy provides rapid in-vivo diagnosis, while culture on non-nutrient agar is the gold standard.
Risk factors include contact lens use with tap water exposure, swimming or hot tub use with lenses, and use of non-sterile solutions.

clues: A 55-year-old male smoker presents with a new droopy right eyelid noticed by his wife over the past month.
There is mild right-sided ptosis (2 mm) with a smaller right pupil (anisocoria greater in dim light). The lower lid is slightly elevated (upside-down ptosis).
Cocaine drops fail to dilate the right pupil, confirming Horner syndrome. Hydroxyamphetamine dilates the right pupil, localizing the lesion to the preganglionic (second-order) neuron.
The patient has a 40-pack-year smoking history and reports right arm and shoulder pain. Chest X-ray reveals an apical lung mass (Pancoast tumor).
Urgent CT chest and biopsy of the apical mass are performed. The Horner syndrome itself requires no treatment; the underlying cause must be identified and treated.
teachingPoints: The classic triad of Horner syndrome is miosis, ptosis, and anhidrosis (facial), resulting from disruption of the oculosympathetic pathway.
Pharmacologic testing helps localize the lesion: cocaine test confirms Horner; hydroxyamphetamine differentiates preganglionic (dilates) from postganglionic (does not dilate).
Causes vary by location: first-order (stroke, demyelination), second-order (Pancoast tumor, trauma), third-order (carotid dissection, cluster headache).
New-onset Horner syndrome requires urgent workup including CT/MRI/MRA of the head, neck, and chest to rule out life-threatening causes like dissection or malignancy.

clues: An 8-year-old boy presents with severe bilateral eye itching, thick ropy mucous discharge, and photophobia that worsens every spring and summer.
Eversion of the upper lids reveals giant cobblestone papillae (>1 mm). Limbal Horner-Trantas dots (gelatinous limbal nodules) are present.
A superior corneal shield ulcer with mucous plaque is seen in the right eye. Conjunctival scraping shows abundant eosinophils.
The patient has a history of asthma and eczema. He has been using over-the-counter antihistamine drops without improvement.
Treatment includes topical mast cell stabilizers (cromolyn, lodoxamide), short pulse of topical steroids for flares, and topical cyclosporine 0.05% for steroid-sparing maintenance.
teachingPoints: VKC is a severe IgE and T-cell mediated allergic condition primarily affecting boys in warm, dry climates; it typically resolves after puberty.
Shield ulcers occur from mechanical trauma by giant papillae and toxic effects of eosinophil-derived proteins on the epithelium.
Topical cyclosporine or tacrolimus are effective steroid-sparing agents for chronic management and reducing dependence on corticosteroids.
Chronic steroid use must be avoided due to the risk of steroid-induced glaucoma and cataracts, especially in children.

clues: A 60-year-old male with hypertension presents with sudden blurred vision in the lower half of his right visual field noticed 3 days ago.
Fundoscopy shows flame-shaped hemorrhages, cotton-wool spots, and retinal edema confined to the superotemporal quadrant. Venous dilation and tortuosity are present in the affected sector.
OCT shows macular edema with cystic changes. Fluorescein angiography demonstrates delayed filling and leakage in the superotemporal distribution with capillary non-perfusion.
The occlusion is at an arteriovenous crossing point where a thickened artery compresses the underlying vein. Blood pressure is 165/100.
Intravitreal anti-VEGF is first-line treatment for macular edema from BRVO (BRAVO trial). Grid laser is an alternative.
teachingPoints: BRVO typically occurs at AV crossing points where the artery and vein share a common adventitial sheath; the artery compresses the vein.
Hypertension, diabetes, and hyperlipidemia are the main systemic risk factors; blood pressure control is essential.
Neovascularization can develop in ischemic BRVO; sectoral PRP is indicated if neovascularization occurs.
Prognosis is generally better than CRVO; approximately 50-60% of patients have spontaneous improvement in vision over 6-12 months.

clues: A 48-year-old female presents with chronic bilateral burning, irritation, and crusting of the eyelids for several years, worse in the morning.

Slit-lamp exam reveals collarettes around the base of the eyelashes (anterior blepharitis) and thickened, turbid meibomian gland secretions with capped orifices (posterior blepharitis/MGD). Lid margin telangiectasia is present.
Tear break-up time is reduced (4 seconds). Meibography shows gland dropout in both upper and lower lids.
The patient has a history of rosacea with facial flushing and rhinophyma. She notes her symptoms worsen with prolonged screen use.
Lid hygiene is the cornerstone: warm compresses, lid massage, and lid scrubs. Oral doxycycline (anti-inflammatory dose) is effective for meibomian gland dysfunction associated with rosacea.
teachingPoints: Blepharitis is one of the most common ocular conditions and a leading cause of evaporative dry eye disease.
Demodex mites (D. folliculorum and D. brevis) are increasingly recognized as a cause of anterior blepharitis; cylindrical dandruff at lash bases is characteristic.
Tea tree oil or ivermectin-based lid wipes can be effective for Demodex blepharitis.
LipiFlow (thermal pulsation) and intense pulsed light (IPL) therapy are in-office treatments for meibomian gland dysfunction.

clues: A 70-year-old female presents with chronic bilateral red eyes, recurrent conjunctivitis, and progressive scarring that has not responded to multiple antibiotic courses.
Slit-lamp shows bilateral inferior fornix foreshortening, symblepharon (adhesion of palpebral and bulbar conjunctiva), and trichiasis. The cornea has superficial vascularization.
Conjunctival biopsy with direct immunofluorescence shows linear deposition of IgG, IgA, and C3 at the basement membrane zone.
The patient has blistering lesions in her mouth and nasal mucosa. She was initially diagnosed with chronic allergic conjunctivitis.
Systemic immunosuppression (dapsone, mycophenolate, or cyclophosphamide) is required to halt disease progression. Topical therapy alone is insufficient.
teachingPoints: OCP is a chronic autoimmune cicatrizing disease that targets basement membranes of mucous membranes, including the conjunctiva, causing progressive scarring and blindness.
Conjunctival biopsy with DIF is essential for diagnosis; it should be taken from uninflamed conjunctiva for best yield.
Foster staging: Stage I (subconjunctival fibrosis), Stage II (fornix foreshortening), Stage III (symblepharon), Stage IV (ankyloblepharon with frozen globe).
Trichiasis from OCP causes corneal damage; management of lashes (epilation, cryotherapy) is important while systemic therapy controls the underlying disease.

clues: A 3-month-old infant is brought in with chronic tearing and mucopurulent discharge from the left eye since birth.
The left eye shows epiphora and crusting of the lashes. Pressure over the lacrimal sac produces reflux of mucopurulent material. No erythema or swelling over the sac.
The eye is otherwise white and quiet. Fluorescein dye disappearance test shows retained dye in the left eye after 5 minutes. No corneal abnormality.
The condition has been present since birth and is intermittent, worsening with upper respiratory infections.
Conservative management (crigler massage, warm compresses, topical antibiotics for discharge) resolves ~90% of cases by age 12 months. Probing is performed if symptoms persist beyond 12 months.
teachingPoints: CNLDO occurs in up to 6-20% of newborns due to failure of canalization of the valve of Hasner at the distal end of the nasolacrimal duct.
Crigler (lacrimal sac) massage involves firm downward strokes over the lacrimal sac to create hydrostatic pressure that can rupture the membranous obstruction.
Office probing has a success rate of >90% when performed between ages 6-12 months.
Dacryocystitis (infection of the lacrimal sac) presenting as an erythematous, tender swelling below the medial canthal tendon is a complication that requires systemic antibiotics.

clues: A 75-year-old female presents with sudden, painless visual loss in her right eye. She reports a 2-week history of headache, scalp tenderness, and jaw claudication.
Visual acuity is no light perception OD. There is a right RAPD. The right optic disc is pale and swollen (chalky white edema). The left disc is normal.
ESR is 95 mm/hr and CRP is 8.5 mg/dL (both markedly elevated). CBC shows thrombocytosis (platelets 520,000). Temporal artery biopsy reveals granulomatous vasculitis with giant cells.
The patient reports 10 pounds of unintentional weight loss, malaise, and pain when chewing steak (jaw claudication) over the past month.
Immediate high-dose IV methylprednisolone (1g/day for 3 days) followed by oral prednisone taper is critical to protect the fellow eye.
teachingPoints: GCA is the most common systemic vasculitis in patients over 50; untreated, it carries a high risk of bilateral sequential blindness.
Arteritic AION from GCA causes more severe vision loss than non-arteritic AION and is a medical emergency to protect the fellow eye.
Temporal artery biopsy is the gold standard; skip lesions occur, so a long specimen (≥2 cm) is recommended. Treatment should never be delayed while awaiting biopsy.
Tocilizumab (IL-6 receptor inhibitor) is an effective steroid-sparing agent approved for GCA relapse.

clues: A 45-year-old farmer presents with a progressively worsening red, painful right eye for 10 days. He was hit in the eye by a tree branch 2 weeks ago.
Slit-lamp exam shows a grayish-white corneal infiltrate with feathery borders, satellite lesions, and an immune ring (Wessely ring). There is a small hypopyon.
Corneal scraping with KOH prep reveals septate, branching hyphae. Culture on Sabouraud agar at 25°C grows Fusarium species after 5 days.
The patient is a farmer in a rural tropical area. The initial injury was from vegetative matter. He was treated with topical steroids by a local provider, which worsened the condition.
Topical natamycin 5% is the first-line treatment for filamentous fungal keratitis. Topical voriconazole is an adjunct. Treatment duration is weeks to months.
teachingPoints: Fungal keratitis should be suspected after vegetable/organic matter trauma, in tropical climates, or when a corneal ulcer worsens on antibacterial treatment.
The MUTT trial (Mycotic Ulcer Treatment Trial) showed natamycin was superior to voriconazole for Fusarium keratitis.
Satellite lesions, feathery borders, and an immune ring are classic features distinguishing fungal from bacterial keratitis.
Topical corticosteroids are contraindicated in fungal keratitis and are a common reason for worsening; they should be stopped immediately if fungal infection is suspected.

clues: A 25-year-old male presents with difficulty seeing at night and bumping into objects in his peripheral vision, gradually worsening since his teenage years.
Fundoscopy reveals bilateral bone-spicule pigmentation in the mid-peripheral retina, attenuated retinal arterioles, and waxy pallor of the optic discs.

ERG (electroretinogram) shows severely reduced and delayed rod responses with moderately reduced cone responses. OCT shows loss of the outer retinal layers (ellipsoid zone disruption) except at the fovea.
The patient's mother is an asymptomatic carrier; his maternal uncle is affected. Genetic testing reveals a mutation in the RPGR gene (X-linked inheritance).
There is no cure. Low vision aids, genetic counseling, and monitoring for complications (CME, cataracts) are key. Gene therapy trials and retinal implants are areas of active research.
teachingPoints: RP is the most common inherited retinal dystrophy, affecting approximately 1 in 4,000 people worldwide.
Cystoid macular edema is a treatable complication of RP that can be managed with topical or oral carbonic anhydrase inhibitors.
Inheritance can be autosomal dominant (mildest), autosomal recessive, or X-linked (most severe). Over 80 genes have been identified.
Luxturna (voretigene neparvovec) is an FDA-approved gene therapy for RPE65-associated retinal dystrophy, a form of RP/LCA.

clues: A 72-year-old male reports gradually worsening distance vision and glare at night over the past 2 years; reading vision has paradoxically improved.
Slit-lamp exam shows a yellow-brown nuclear sclerotic lens change bilaterally, more advanced in the right eye. There is a 'second sight' phenomenon (myopic shift improving near vision).
Best-corrected visual acuity is 20/60 OD, 20/40 OS. Refraction shows a myopic shift of -2.00 D from previous records. Glare testing shows significant disability.
The patient is a retired pilot who is having difficulty driving at night. His daily activities are significantly impacted.
Phacoemulsification with posterior chamber intraocular lens implantation is the standard of care when visual function impairs daily activities.
teachingPoints: Nuclear sclerotic cataracts cause a myopic shift ('second sight') by increasing the refractive index of the lens nucleus; patients may temporarily read without glasses.
Cataracts are the leading cause of reversible blindness worldwide.
Modern IOL options include monofocal, multifocal, EDOF (extended depth of focus), and toric (for astigmatism correction), allowing customized visual rehabilitation.
The most important indication for surgery is functional impairment of the patient's quality of life, not a specific visual acuity threshold.

clues: A 60-year-old male on warfarin presents alarmed by a bright red patch on the white of his right eye noticed upon waking. He has no pain or vision changes.
A well-circumscribed, flat, bright-red area of blood beneath the conjunctiva covers the inferotemporal quadrant. The eye is otherwise quiet with normal vision.
INR is 3.8 (supratherapeutic). Blood pressure is 155/95 mmHg.
The patient reports vigorous coughing from a recent cold. He takes warfarin for atrial fibrillation.
Reassurance is the primary treatment; it typically self-resolves in 2-3 weeks. Artificial tears for comfort. The underlying anticoagulation and blood pressure should be addressed.
teachingPoints: Subconjunctival hemorrhage is extremely common and benign; it is caused by rupture of small conjunctival or episcleral blood vessels.
Risk factors include anticoagulation, hypertension, Valsalva maneuvers (coughing, sneezing, straining), trauma, and blood dyscrasias.
Recurrent bilateral SCH should prompt evaluation for bleeding disorders.
Unlike a hyphema (blood in the anterior chamber), an SCH requires no specific ophthalmic treatment and has no visual significance.

clues: A 68-year-old male presents with a painful, vesicular rash on his right forehead and upper eyelid for 4 days, along with a red, painful right eye.

Dermatomal vesicular eruption respecting the midline in V1 distribution. Hutchinson sign is positive (vesicles on the tip of the nose). Slit-lamp shows pseudodendritic keratitis, anterior uveitis with mild cells and flare.
IOP is 30 mmHg in the right eye (trabeculitis). Corneal sensation is reduced. There are no retinal findings.
The patient is immunocompetent but has not received the shingles vaccine. He reports severe neuropathic pain.
Oral valacyclovir 1g TID for 7-10 days (started within 72 hours of rash onset), topical steroids for uveitis, IOP-lowering drops for trabeculitis, and lubrication.
teachingPoints: HZO occurs when latent VZV reactivates in the trigeminal (V1) ganglion; ocular involvement occurs in ~50% of V1 zoster cases.
Hutchinson sign (nasal tip vesicles) indicates involvement of the nasociliary nerve and predicts ocular complications.
Postherpetic neuralgia is the most common long-term complication and can be debilitating; gabapentin or pregabalin can help.
Recombinant zoster vaccine (Shingrix) is >90% effective in preventing HZO and is recommended for adults over age 50.

clues: A 25-year-old male presents after being struck in the face with a baseball. He has double vision on upgaze, swelling, and numbness of the right cheek.
Enophthalmos, infraorbital hypoesthesia (V2 distribution), and restricted upgaze with diplopia are noted. Forced duction test is positive for restriction in upgaze.
CT orbits (coronal and axial) shows a fracture of the right orbital floor with herniation of orbital fat and inferior rectus muscle into the maxillary sinus (teardrop sign). No globe rupture.
The patient is a college athlete who was not wearing protective equipment. There is subcutaneous emphysema.

Surgical repair (orbital floor implant) is indicated for persistent diplopia in primary or downgaze, significant enophthalmos (>2 mm), or large fracture (>50% of floor). Surgery ideally within 2 weeks.
teachingPoints: Orbital floor fractures result from increased intraorbital hydraulic pressure transmitted to the thinnest bone (floor or medial wall).
Patients should be instructed not to blow their nose, as this can force air through the fracture into the orbit (orbital emphysema) and risk compartment syndrome.
Infraorbital hypoesthesia occurs from injury to the infraorbital nerve (V2) running in the orbital floor.
Pediatric 'trapdoor' fractures are a surgical emergency—the bone snaps back, entrapping the muscle, causing a white-eyed blowout fracture with vagal symptoms (nausea, bradycardia).

clues: A 10-year-old girl from an underserved community presents with a recurrent red, irritated right eye and photophobia.
A white-yellow nodule (phlyctenule) is seen at the limbus with surrounding injection. A fascicular blood vessel leads toward the cornea from the nodule.
The corneal lesion stains minimally with fluorescein. No anterior chamber reaction. Evaluation reveals underlying chronic staphylococcal blepharitis.
The child is malnourished and has a history of tuberculosis contact. TB skin test is positive.
Topical corticosteroid-antibiotic combination for the acute episode; lid hygiene for underlying blepharitis. Tuberculosis screening and systemic treatment if TB is confirmed.
teachingPoints: Phlyctenulosis is a cell-mediated hypersensitivity reaction to microbial antigens, most commonly staphylococcal or mycobacterial proteins.
It is most common in children, especially in areas with high TB prevalence and poor nutrition.
Corneal involvement can cause fascicular ulceration and scarring if untreated, leading to permanent vision loss.
Recurrent phlyctenulosis should prompt TB testing and evaluation for staphylococcal blepharitis.

clues: An 80-year-old female presents with sudden painless vision loss in her left eye upon waking. She reports new-onset headache, jaw pain when chewing, and shoulder/hip stiffness.
Visual acuity is counting fingers OS with a dense RAPD. The left optic disc shows chalky-white pallor with edema. The right disc shows mild pallor.
ESR is 110 mm/hr, CRP is 12 mg/dL, and platelets are 580,000—all markedly elevated. Temporal artery is tender and non-pulsatile on palpation.
She has lost 15 pounds over 2 months. Temporal artery biopsy is scheduled but treatment must not be delayed.
Start IV methylprednisolone 1g daily immediately (do not wait for biopsy) for 3 days, then taper to oral prednisone with gastric protection.
teachingPoints: Arteritic AION from GCA is a medical emergency; without treatment, the fellow eye can be lost within days to weeks.
Jaw claudication is the most specific symptom for GCA; scalp tenderness, weight loss, polymyalgia rheumatica symptoms, and elevated inflammatory markers support the diagnosis.
Biopsy remains the gold standard but skip lesions can cause false negatives; a negative biopsy does not exclude GCA if clinical suspicion is high.
Long-term steroid therapy is typically required for 1-2 years; tocilizumab is used as a steroid-sparing agent (GiACTA trial).

clues: A 30-year-old teacher presents with acute bilateral watery red eyes with mild discomfort and a gritty sensation. The left eye was affected first, and the right eye followed 3 days later.
Bilateral conjunctival injection with watery discharge and follicular response on the inferior palpebral conjunctiva. A tender preauricular lymph node is palpable on the left.
Pseudomembranes are forming on the inferior palpebral conjunctiva of the left eye. No corneal staining with fluorescein currently. Adenovirus rapid antigen test is positive.
Several students in the patient's class have similar symptoms. The patient reports a recent cold.
Treatment is supportive: cold compresses, artificial tears, and strict hygiene. Pseudomembranes should be peeled to prevent symblepharon. It is highly contagious; hand hygiene is critical.
teachingPoints: EKC (typically adenovirus serotypes 8, 19, 37) is the most common cause of viral conjunctivitis and is highly contagious via direct contact and fomites.
Subepithelial corneal infiltrates (SEIs) may develop 2-3 weeks later, causing photophobia and reduced vision; these can persist for months.
There is no specific antiviral treatment for adenoviral conjunctivitis; povidone-iodine and topical ganciclovir are being studied.
Patients should be advised to avoid work/school for 10-14 days and to avoid sharing towels, eye drops, or touching their eyes to prevent spread.

clues: A 68-year-old female notices a central blind spot and distorted vision in her right eye that has progressed over weeks.
Visual acuity is 20/200 OD. Slit-lamp biomicroscopy with a 78D lens reveals a round, red-based lesion at the fovea with surrounding cuff of subretinal fluid. Watzke-Allen test is positive.
OCT reveals a full-thickness macular hole with a minimum diameter of 450 microns. There is mild vitreomacular traction. Epiretinal membrane is present.
The fellow eye shows a partial posterior vitreous detachment with vitreomacular adhesion, placing it at risk.
Pars plana vitrectomy with ILM peeling and gas tamponade (SF6 or C3F8) is the standard treatment. Postoperative face-down positioning is typically recommended.
teachingPoints: Idiopathic full-thickness macular holes are caused by tangential and anteroposterior vitreous traction on the fovea, most common in women over 60.
Closure rates with modern vitrectomy techniques exceed 90%, with significant visual improvement expected in most patients.
The MIVI-TRUST trial showed that ocriplasmin (enzymatic vitreolysis) can close smaller holes non-surgically in select cases.
The risk of macular hole formation in the fellow eye is approximately 10-15%; a complete PVD in the fellow eye is protective.

clues: A 55-year-old female presents with a painful, red swelling below the medial canthus of her left eye for 3 days, with tearing and discharge.
A tender, erythematous, fluctuant swelling is present over the left lacrimal sac, inferior and medial to the medial canthal tendon. Purulent material expressed from the punctum on pressure.
CT shows dilation of the left lacrimal sac with surrounding soft tissue inflammation. No orbital involvement.
The patient has a history of chronic tearing and a prior episode of dacryocystitis on the same side treated with antibiotics.
Warm compresses, oral antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate), and incision and drainage if pointing. Definitive treatment is dacryocystorhinostomy (DCR) after the acute infection resolves.
teachingPoints: Acute dacryocystitis is caused by bacterial infection of the lacrimal sac secondary to nasolacrimal duct obstruction; Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus are the most common organisms.
The swelling is characteristically below the medial canthal tendon; swelling above suggests a lacrimal gland mass (dacryoadenitis), which is a different entity.
DCR surgery has a success rate of >90% for chronic nasolacrimal duct obstruction and prevents recurrent dacryocystitis.
Congenital dacryocystitis (dacryocystocele) presenting at birth as a bluish cystic swelling requires urgent treatment to prevent secondary infection.

clues: A 5-year-old boy is brought in by his parents who noticed a white pupillary reflex (leukocoria) in the left eye.
Dilated exam reveals telangiectatic and aneurysmal retinal vessels in the temporal periphery with massive subretinal exudation extending to the macula, producing a yellowish retinal detachment.
Fluorescein angiography shows 'light bulb' aneurysms with vascular telangiectasia and profuse leakage. B-scan ultrasound shows no calcification (distinguishing it from retinoblastoma).
The condition is unilateral. The child is male. There is no family history of eye disease.
Treatment involves ablation of abnormal vessels with laser photocoagulation or cryotherapy to reduce exudation. Anti-VEGF injections may be adjunctive.
teachingPoints: Coats disease is an idiopathic, typically unilateral condition of retinal vascular telangiectasia and exudation, primarily affecting young boys.
The most important differential diagnosis is retinoblastoma; absence of calcification on ultrasound/CT and the characteristic telangiectatic vessels help distinguish Coats from retinoblastoma.
Advanced cases (Stage 4-5) with total retinal detachment carry a very poor visual prognosis.
Coats disease is classified by staging (Shields classification) which guides management from mild exudation (Stage 2) to painful blind eye requiring enucleation (Stage 5).

clues: A 70-year-old female presents with gradual distortion and mild blurring of vision in her left eye over the past year.
Visual acuity is 20/40 OS. Slit-lamp biomicroscopy reveals a glistening, cellophane-like membrane on the macular surface. Retinal vessels appear tortuous and dragged.
OCT shows a hyperreflective membrane on the inner retinal surface with loss of the foveal contour, retinal thickening, and intraretinal cystic changes.
The patient had a posterior vitreous detachment in the same eye 2 years ago. Amsler grid shows metamorphopsia.
Observation is appropriate for mild cases. Pars plana vitrectomy with membrane peeling is indicated when symptoms significantly affect vision or daily function.
teachingPoints: ERMs are composed of glial cells, RPE cells, and myofibroblasts that proliferate on the inner retinal surface, often idiopathic following PVD.
Most ERMs are asymptomatic and discovered incidentally; surgery is reserved for cases with significant metamorphopsia or vision loss.
Visual recovery after ERM peeling is generally good, with improvement in acuity and reduced metamorphopsia, though full recovery may take months.
Secondary causes of ERM include prior retinal detachment surgery, retinal vascular disease, uveitis, and trauma.

clues: A 3-year-old child presents with swelling, redness, and warmth of the right upper eyelid following an insect bite 2 days ago.
The right upper lid is erythematous, edematous, and tender. Vision is normal. Extraocular movements are full and painless. No proptosis or RAPD.
Temperature is 38.2°C. White blood cell count is mildly elevated. CT orbits is normal with no postseptal involvement.
The child had a bug bite near the eyebrow that was scratched open. There is no sinusitis.
Oral antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate) cover skin flora including Staph and Strep. Close follow-up in 24-48 hours to ensure no progression to orbital cellulitis.
teachingPoints: Preseptal cellulitis is an infection anterior to the orbital septum; it does NOT involve the orbit itself and has a much better prognosis than orbital cellulitis.
Key differentiating features from orbital cellulitis: normal vision, full painless extraocular movements, no proptosis, and no RAPD.
In children, preseptal cellulitis most commonly follows skin trauma, insect bites, or upper respiratory infections.
If there is any concern for orbital involvement (proptosis, pain with eye movement, vision changes), CT with contrast is mandatory.

clues: A 35-year-old Hispanic female presents with bilateral blurred vision, headache, tinnitus, and neck stiffness developing over 1 week.
Bilateral serous retinal detachments with 'sunset glow' fundus (depigmented, orange-red choroid). Anterior segment shows bilateral granulomatous anterior uveitis with mutton-fat KPs.

FA shows multiple pinpoint hyperfluorescent leaking dots at the RPE level with pooling of dye in subretinal fluid. OCT shows multilobulated serous retinal detachments with choroidal thickening. Lumbar puncture reveals CSF pleocytosis.
The patient reports hearing loss, tinnitus, and is developing poliosis (whitening of eyelashes). She has no history of ocular surgery or trauma (ruling out sympathetic ophthalmia).
Systemic corticosteroids (high-dose oral or IV pulse) with early introduction of steroid-sparing immunosuppression (mycophenolate or azathioprine) for at least 6 months.
teachingPoints: VKH is a bilateral granulomatous panuveitis with exudative retinal detachments, targeting melanocyte-containing tissues (uvea, meninges, inner ear, skin, hair).
The disease primarily affects darkly pigmented individuals (Hispanic, Asian, Native American, Middle Eastern).
Chronic recurrent VKH leads to 'sunset glow' fundus, peripapillary atrophy, subretinal fibrosis, and nummular depigmented scars (Dalen-Fuchs nodules).
Inadequate treatment leads to chronic recurrent inflammation with complications including cataracts, glaucoma, and subretinal neovascularization.

clues: A 3-year-old girl's parents notice her eyes crossing inward intermittently, especially when looking at close objects. It was first noticed around age 2.
An esotropia of 25 prism diopters is measured at near. Cycloplegic refraction reveals +5.50 D in each eye (high hyperopia).
When wearing full hyperopic spectacle correction, the eyes are straight at both distance and near. No amblyopia is present.
The child's father wore bifocals as a child for crossed eyes. The deviation started around age 2 and is intermittent.
Full hyperopic correction with glasses is the treatment. The esotropia resolves fully with glasses. No surgery is needed if the deviation is fully accommodative.
teachingPoints: Accommodative esotropia is caused by excessive accommodative convergence in uncorrected hyperopia; correcting the hyperopia relaxes accommodation and eliminates the convergence drive.
Typical onset is between ages 1-4 years, coinciding with development of accommodative-convergence mechanisms.
If a residual esotropia remains despite full hyperopic correction, this may require bifocals (for high AC/A ratio) or surgery (partially accommodative ET).
Children may 'outgrow' the need for full hyperopic correction as they grow, but should be monitored for years.

clues: A 38-year-old male presents with blurred central vision and a 'dark spot' in his right eye for 2 weeks. He reports images appearing smaller in the affected eye (micropsia).
Visual acuity is 20/40 OD. Fundoscopy reveals a round, well-demarcated serous elevation of the neurosensory retina at the macula.

OCT shows a neurosensory retinal detachment with subretinal fluid at the fovea. Fluorescein angiography demonstrates an expanding dot of hyperfluorescence (smokestack or inkblot pattern) indicating an RPE leak.
The patient is a high-stress type-A personality, recently started on oral prednisone for another condition. He has sleep apnea.
Most cases resolve spontaneously within 3-4 months. If persistent, thermal laser or photodynamic therapy with verteporfin to the leakage point may be considered. Discontinue corticosteroids.
teachingPoints: CSC is caused by focal RPE dysfunction leading to serous neurosensory detachment; it predominantly affects young to middle-aged men.
Corticosteroid use (systemic, topical, inhaled, or even nasal) is a well-known risk factor and should be discontinued when possible.
Chronic or recurrent CSC (lasting >4 months) carries risk of permanent RPE atrophy and photoreceptor damage.
Half-dose photodynamic therapy (PDT) with verteporfin has become the preferred treatment for chronic CSC, particularly for extrafoveal leaks.

clues: A 32-year-old myopic male presents with halos around lights and intermittent blurring after vigorous exercise.
Slit-lamp shows a deep anterior chamber with Krukenberg spindle (vertical spindle of pigment on the corneal endothelium). There is dense trabecular meshwork pigmentation on gonioscopy.
IOP is 32 mmHg bilaterally. Visual fields show early nasal step. OCT RNFL shows borderline thinning. Transillumination defects are seen in the mid-peripheral iris in a spoke-like pattern.
The patient is a young myopic male who runs marathons. IOP spikes have been documented after exercise.
Topical IOP-lowering therapy (prostaglandin analogs or beta-blockers). Laser iridotomy has been proposed to flatten the iris and reduce pigment liberation, though evidence is mixed.
teachingPoints: Pigment dispersion syndrome results from friction between the posterior iris surface and the zonular fibers, liberating melanin granules that clog the trabecular meshwork.
The classic patient is a young, myopic male; the condition can spontaneously improve with age as the lens thickens and pushes the iris forward.
Exercise-induced IOP spikes and pigment showers can cause intermittent blurred vision and halos.
Approximately 35-50% of PDS patients develop pigmentary glaucoma over 15 years.

diagnosis: Cranial Nerve III Palsy (Posterior Communicating Artery Aneurysm)
clues: A 50-year-old female presents with acute onset of double vision, droopy right eyelid, and a severe headache.
Complete right ptosis. The right eye is deviated 'down and out' (hypotropia and exotropia). The right pupil is fixed and dilated (8 mm), unreactive to light.
CT angiography of the head reveals a posterior communicating artery (PComm) aneurysm on the right side.
The patient describes the headache as the 'worst headache of her life.' She has a history of hypertension and smoking.
Emergent neurosurgical or neurointerventional consultation for aneurysm clipping or coiling is required; a pupil-involving CN3 palsy is an aneurysm until proven otherwise.
teachingPoints: A pupil-involving third nerve palsy is a neurosurgical emergency; the pupillary fibers run on the periphery of CN3 and are compressed first by an expanding aneurysm.
The 'rule of the pupil': pupil involvement in CN3 palsy = aneurysm until proven otherwise (compressive). Pupil sparing = often microvascular (diabetes, hypertension).
PComm aneurysm is the most common cause of compressive CN3 palsy; subarachnoid hemorrhage from rupture can be fatal.
All patients with a new CN3 palsy should have urgent neuroimaging (CTA or MRA); even partially pupil-involving palsies require aneurysm workup.

clues: A 6-year-old boy presents with decreased vision and strabismus in his left eye noticed by his school nurse.
Visual acuity is 20/200 OS. Dilated exam reveals a peripheral granuloma in the inferotemporal retina with fibrous bands extending to the disc and macula, causing macular heterotopia (dragged disc).
B-scan ultrasound shows a peripheral hyperechoic mass without calcification. ELISA for Toxocara antibodies is positive (serum). Aqueous humor ELISA confirms intraocular antibody production.
The child has a pet dog and a habit of eating dirt (pica). He frequently plays in a sandbox.
Treatment may include oral corticosteroids or periocular steroids to control inflammation. Antihelminthics (albendazole) are sometimes used. Vitrectomy may be needed for tractional complications.
teachingPoints: Toxocariasis is caused by Toxocara canis (dog roundworm) larvae that migrate to the eye; it is an important cause of unilateral leukocoria and must be differentiated from retinoblastoma.
Three presentations: posterior pole granuloma, peripheral granuloma with vitreous bands, and chronic endophthalmitis.
Unlike retinoblastoma, there is no calcification on imaging; ELISA testing for Toxocara antibodies helps confirm the diagnosis.
Prevention includes deworming pets, hand hygiene, covering sandboxes, and discouraging pica in children.

clues: A 30-year-old myopic female is found to have bilateral peripheral retinal findings during a routine dilated exam. She is asymptomatic.

Dilated fundus exam reveals circumferentially oriented, well-defined areas of retinal thinning with criss-crossing white lines (sclerosed vessels) in the peripheral retina bilaterally, predominantly superotemporal.
There are atrophic round holes within the lattice lesion in the right eye. No subretinal fluid. No flap tears.
The patient is moderately myopic (-6.00 D). She is interested in LASIK and is curious about her retinal findings.

Observation is appropriate for asymptomatic lattice with or without atrophic holes. Prophylactic laser is considered for symptomatic tears, lattice with subretinal fluid, or in the fellow eye of a patient with prior RD.
teachingPoints: Lattice degeneration is the most clinically significant peripheral retinal degeneration, found in 6-8% of the general population and more common in myopes.
It is a risk factor for rhegmatogenous retinal detachment, as retinal tears can develop at the edges of lattice lesions.
Prophylactic laser of asymptomatic lattice lesions in the general population is NOT recommended; the risk-benefit ratio does not favor treatment.
Patients with lattice should be counseled on symptoms of retinal tears/detachment (flashes, floaters, curtain) and to seek prompt evaluation.

clues: A 65-year-old male presents with a slowly growing, painless, gelatinous mass on the nasal conjunctiva of his right eye present for several months.
A papillomatous, leukoplakic, gelatinous mass is present at the nasal limbus with feeder vessels. It extends onto the adjacent cornea.
Anterior segment OCT shows a thickened, hyperreflective epithelium with an abrupt transition from normal to abnormal tissue. Impression cytology confirms dysplastic cells.
The patient has significant outdoor sun exposure, is HIV-positive, and has a history of HPV-related skin lesions.
Excisional biopsy with 'no-touch technique,' wide margins, and cryotherapy to the conjunctival edges is the standard surgical approach. Topical interferon alpha-2b or 5-FU may be used adjunctively.
teachingPoints: OSSN encompasses the spectrum from dysplasia (CIN) to carcinoma in situ to invasive squamous cell carcinoma of the conjunctiva.
Risk factors include UV exposure, HIV infection, HPV (types 16 and 18), older age, and fair skin.
Topical interferon alpha-2b is effective as primary or adjunctive therapy with minimal side effects.
Recurrence rate is approximately 5-15% with proper surgical technique; long-term follow-up is essential.

clues: A 25-year-old female is noted to have unequal pupil sizes during a routine eye exam. She is asymptomatic.
The right pupil is 4.5 mm and the left is 3.5 mm in dim light. In bright light, the right is 3 mm and the left is 2 mm. The anisocoria (1 mm difference) is the same in all lighting conditions.
Both pupils react briskly and equally to light and accommodation. There is no ptosis, no motility deficit, and no dilation lag.
Old photographs confirm the anisocoria has been present since childhood.
Physiologic anisocoria requires no treatment or workup. Reassurance is provided.
teachingPoints: Physiologic anisocoria (up to 1 mm difference) is present in approximately 20% of the normal population.
The key distinguishing feature is that the degree of anisocoria remains the same in light and dark conditions, unlike pathologic causes.
If anisocoria is greater in the dark, the smaller pupil is the abnormal one (Horner syndrome). If greater in the light, the larger pupil is abnormal (CN3 palsy, pharmacologic dilation, Adie pupil).
Old photographs are invaluable for documenting longstanding anisocoria and avoiding unnecessary workup.

clues: A 14-year-old boy presents after being hit in the right eye with a paintball during a game. He has pain, blurred vision, and photophobia.
Visual acuity is 20/100 OD. Slit-lamp shows a layered blood collection filling the inferior one-third of the anterior chamber (Grade II hyphema). IOP is 28 mmHg.
Gonioscopy is deferred in the acute setting. No iris sphincter tears or lens subluxation. Dilated exam shows no retinal pathology visible through the haze.
The patient takes ibuprofen for headaches. He is African American (sickle cell screening indicated).
Treatment: shield protection, head elevation, bed rest, cycloplegics (atropine), topical steroids, and IOP management. Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs. Screen for sickle cell trait/disease.
teachingPoints: Rebleeding typically occurs 3-5 days after initial injury and is the most significant risk; aminocaproic acid or tranexamic acid can reduce rebleed risk.
Sickle cell trait/disease greatly increases the risk of elevated IOP from hyphema because sickled RBCs clog the trabecular meshwork; carbonic anhydrase inhibitors should be avoided.
Grading: I (<1/3 AC), II (1/3-1/2 AC), III (>1/2 AC), IV (total/8-ball hyphema). Higher grades have worse prognosis.
Surgical washout is considered for total hyphema, persistent IOP elevation unresponsive to medication, or corneal blood staining.

clues: A 40-year-old African American female presents with chronic bilateral red eyes, blurred vision, and floaters that have been intermittent over 6 months.

Slit-lamp exam reveals bilateral granulomatous anterior uveitis with mutton-fat keratic precipitates, iris nodules (Koeppe at pupillary margin, Busacca on iris surface), and tent-shaped peripheral anterior synechiae.
Chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Serum ACE and lysozyme are elevated. CT chest confirms hilar and mediastinal lymphadenopathy.
The patient has skin lesions consistent with erythema nodosum and reports shortness of breath. She has been treated for 'pink eye' multiple times without resolution.
Topical corticosteroids for anterior uveitis; systemic corticosteroids or methotrexate for posterior segment involvement or systemic disease.
teachingPoints: Sarcoidosis is a multisystem granulomatous disease; ocular involvement occurs in 25-50% of cases and can be the presenting manifestation.
