Any segment of the eye can be affected: anterior uveitis (most common), intermediate uveitis (snowball vitreous opacities), posterior uveitis (candle wax drippings along veins), and lacrimal gland enlargement.
The International Workshop on Ocular Sarcoidosis (IWOS) criteria help establish the diagnosis of ocular sarcoidosis in the absence of tissue biopsy.
Chronic sarcoid uveitis often requires long-term steroid-sparing immunosuppression to prevent vision-threatening complications.

clues: A 6-year-old girl's parents report that one eye drifts outward when she is tired, daydreaming, or in bright sunlight. She sometimes squints or closes one eye in sunlight.
At distance, a 20 prism diopter intermittent exotropia is measured. At near, the eyes are orthophoric. She demonstrates good control with accurate re-fixation after cover testing.
Stereoacuity is near-normal (40 seconds of arc). Cycloplegic refraction shows minimal refractive error. No amblyopia.
The condition has been present since age 3 and seems to be increasing in frequency. No family history of strabismus.

Observation if well-controlled. Minus lens therapy or part-time occlusion can improve control. Surgery (lateral rectus recession bilaterally) is indicated for poor control, increasing deviation, or loss of binocularity.
teachingPoints: Intermittent exotropia is the most common type of exotropia in children; control is the key factor in management decisions, not the size of deviation.
The Newcastle Control Score and the Office Control Score help quantify the frequency and quality of control.
Bright sunlight triggers the deviation (photodiplopsia); patients commonly squint one eye in bright light.
Surgery is successful in approximately 60-80% of cases, though recurrence is common and may require re-operation.

clues: A 70-year-old male presents with recurrent episodes of blurred vision, red eye, and elevated IOP in his right eye, which had cataract surgery 5 years ago.
Slit-lamp shows anterior chamber cells, a microhyphema, and an anterior chamber IOL that is malpositioned with one haptic eroding the iris.
IOP is 35 mmHg OD. Gonioscopy shows blood in Schlemm canal. Anterior segment OCT reveals the IOL haptic abutting and eroding into the iris root.
The original cataract surgery was complicated by posterior capsule rupture, requiring an anterior chamber IOL placement.
Definitive treatment is IOL exchange or repositioning; medical management of IOP and inflammation provides temporary relief.
teachingPoints: UGH syndrome is caused by mechanical irritation of uveal tissue by a malpositioned or poorly designed IOL, causing recurrent uveitis, glaucoma, and hyphema.
It was more common with older-style rigid anterior chamber IOLs but can occur with any malpositioned lens.
Medical treatment alone is insufficient; the mechanical cause must be addressed to prevent recurrence.
IOL exchange with placement of a scleral-fixated or iris-sutured posterior chamber IOL is the preferred surgical approach.

clues: A 30-year-old female notices that her left pupil is larger than her right. She reports mild blurred vision at near in the left eye.
The left pupil is dilated (6 mm) and reacts poorly to direct light but shows slow, tonic near constriction (light-near dissociation). The pupil re-dilates slowly after near effort.
Slit-lamp shows vermiform (worm-like) segmental iris sphincter movements on the left. Dilute pilocarpine (0.125%) constricts the left pupil but not the right (denervation supersensitivity).
Deep tendon reflexes are diminished in the lower extremities (Holmes-Adie syndrome). The patient is otherwise healthy.
Reassurance is the primary management. Dilute pilocarpine for cosmetic or symptomatic near blur. Reading glasses may help.
teachingPoints: Adie tonic pupil results from damage to the ciliary ganglion or short posterior ciliary nerves, most often idiopathic or post-viral in young women.
Denervation supersensitivity to dilute cholinergic agents (0.125% pilocarpine) is the diagnostic hallmark.
When associated with absent deep tendon reflexes, it is called Holmes-Adie syndrome.
Over time, the affected pupil may become miotic due to progressive aberrant reinnervation of the iris sphincter.

clues: A 72-year-old female with hypertension presents with sudden visual loss in her right eye.

Fundoscopy reveals a large, round, fusiform dilation of a retinal arteriole at an arteriovenous crossing in the superotemporal arcade, surrounded by a ring of hemorrhage (subretinal, intraretinal, and preretinal).
FA shows the fusiform arterial dilation with surrounding blocked fluorescence from hemorrhage and late staining/leakage. OCT shows macular edema with subretinal and intraretinal fluid.
Blood pressure is 180/100 mmHg. The hemorrhage involves multiple retinal layers ('hourglass' hemorrhage pattern).
Most RAMs involute spontaneously. Blood pressure control is important. Laser photocoagulation or anti-VEGF may be considered for persistent macular edema or chronic hemorrhage.
teachingPoints: Retinal artery macroaneurysms are focal dilations of retinal arterioles, most common in hypertensive elderly women.
The 'hourglass' or multilevel hemorrhage pattern (preretinal, intraretinal, and subretinal blood simultaneously) is characteristic.
Most RAMs thrombose and resolve spontaneously; visual prognosis depends on the extent of macular involvement.
The differential includes diabetic microaneurysms, capillary hemangioma, and branch retinal vein occlusion.

clues: A 50-year-old obese male presents with chronic unilateral (right eye) mucous discharge, irritation, and redness that is worse upon waking.
The right upper eyelid is easily everted with minimal upward traction (lax, floppy lid). The palpebral conjunctiva shows a papillary reaction with keratinization.
The patient sleeps predominantly on his right side. Corneal evaluation shows superior punctate epithelial erosions.
BMI is 42. He has severe obstructive sleep apnea diagnosed by polysomnography. He is not compliant with CPAP.
Eyelid taping or shield at night; treat underlying OSA with CPAP. For refractory cases, horizontal lid-tightening surgery (full-thickness pentagonal wedge resection) is effective.
teachingPoints: Floppy eyelid syndrome is strongly associated with obstructive sleep apnea; the eyelid spontaneously everts during sleep, exposing the conjunctiva.
The side of sleep preference correlates with the more affected eye.
All patients diagnosed with FES should be screened for obstructive sleep apnea.
Keratoconus is also associated with FES and OSA, possibly due to mechanical rubbing during sleep.

clues: A 63-year-old female presents with sudden onset of flashing lights in her right eye and a large new floater described as a 'cobweb' or 'ring,' noticed yesterday.

Visual acuity is 20/20 OD. Dilated exam reveals a Weiss ring (detached peripapillary vitreous) floating in the vitreous cavity. No retinal tears, holes, or hemorrhage on 360-degree scleral depressed exam.
OCT shows a clear separation of the posterior hyaloid face from the retinal surface.
The patient is mildly myopic. She had cataract surgery in the left eye 1 year ago (PVD already complete in that eye).
Reassurance and patient education. Return precautions for new symptoms (increase in floaters, new flashes, curtain/shadow) that could indicate a retinal tear. Follow-up dilated exam in 4-6 weeks.
teachingPoints: Acute PVD occurs when the vitreous separates from the retinal surface due to liquefaction and syneresis; it is a normal age-related event.
PVD causes retinal tears in approximately 10-15% of cases; a careful peripheral retinal examination is mandatory.
The Weiss ring is a circular vitreous opacity from the detached peripapillary glial tissue; it is pathognomonic for PVD.
Risk factors for PVD include age >50, myopia, prior cataract surgery, prior YAG capsulotomy, and trauma.

clues: A 55-year-old female on hydroxychloroquine for systemic lupus for 12 years presents for her annual screening eye exam. She reports no visual symptoms.
Visual acuity is 20/25 bilaterally. Fundoscopy appears grossly normal, though subtle RPE mottling is noted around the fovea.

SD-OCT shows thinning of the parafoveal outer retina with disruption of the ellipsoid zone in a ring pattern. Multifocal ERG shows parafoveal depression of retinal function. Humphrey 10-2 visual field shows a partial ring scotoma.
The patient has been taking HCQ 400 mg/day (6.5 mg/kg) for 12 years. She also has chronic kidney disease.
Hydroxychloroquine should be discontinued in consultation with her rheumatologist. The retinal damage is irreversible and may progress even after stopping the drug.
teachingPoints: The AAO recommends baseline eye exam within the first year of starting HCQ, then annual screening starting at 5 years of use (or sooner with risk factors).
SD-OCT and Humphrey 10-2 visual field are the primary screening tools; multifocal ERG provides additional sensitivity.
Risk factors for toxicity include cumulative dose (>5 years use), real body weight dosing >5 mg/kg/day, renal impairment, and concomitant tamoxifen use.
Toxicity progresses even after drug cessation because HCQ has a long tissue half-life; early detection before symptoms develop is critical.

clues: A 55-year-old female with known Graves disease and thyroid eye disease presents with decreased vision and color desaturation in her left eye over 2 weeks.
Visual acuity is 20/60 OS (was 20/20). RAPD is present on the left. Color vision is 4/14 Ishihara plates. Hertel shows bilateral proptosis. Optic disc appears mildly edematous.
CT orbits shows markedly enlarged extraocular muscles (especially medial and inferior rectus) compressing the optic nerve at the orbital apex. Humphrey visual fields show a central scotoma OS.
The patient's TED has been active for 6 months. She is euthyroid on methimazole but still has significant proptosis and restrictive myopathy.
Urgent IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy followed by orbital decompression surgery if no rapid improvement.
teachingPoints: Compressive optic neuropathy occurs in about 5% of TED patients and is the most serious sight-threatening complication.
It is caused by enlarged extraocular muscles compressing the optic nerve at the crowded orbital apex.
Optic neuropathy can occur without severe proptosis because the tight posterior orbit creates a compartment effect.
Orbital decompression (removal of medial and/or inferior orbital walls) effectively relieves optic nerve compression.

clues: A 28-year-old Turkish male presents with acute blurred vision, floaters, and photophobia in both eyes. He has had 3 similar episodes in the past year.
There is bilateral non-granulomatous anterior uveitis with shifting hypopyon. Fundoscopy reveals diffuse retinal vasculitis with flame hemorrhages and disc edema.
FA shows diffuse retinal vascular leakage with branch retinal vein occlusion pattern. OCT shows macular edema bilaterally.
The patient has a history of recurrent painful oral and genital ulcers and pathergy (skin hyperreactivity to minor trauma). HLA-B51 is positive.
Systemic immunosuppression is mandatory: azathioprine, cyclosporine, or anti-TNF agents (infliximab, adalimumab). Corticosteroids alone are insufficient for long-term control.
teachingPoints: Behçet disease is a systemic vasculitis common along the ancient Silk Road (Turkey, Middle East, East Asia); ocular involvement occurs in 70% of cases.
A shifting hypopyon (mobile layered inflammatory cells) is characteristic and almost pathognomonic of Behçet uveitis.
Retinal vasculitis with occlusive vasculopathy can cause devastating vision loss; early aggressive immunosuppression improves prognosis.
The VISUAL trials demonstrated the efficacy of adalimumab for non-infectious uveitis, including Behçet-related uveitis.

clues: A 3-month-old infant presents with excessive tearing, photophobia, and blepharospasm noticed by the parents since the first weeks of life.
The corneal diameters are enlarged (13 mm bilaterally; normal <11.5 mm). Corneal edema is present. Under anesthesia, IOP is 30 mmHg in both eyes.

Gonioscopy under anesthesia reveals an immature-appearing angle with a high iris insertion and absence of the angle recess (trabeculodysgenesis). Haab striae (breaks in Descemet membrane from corneal stretching) are present.
The axial length is increased for age. The optic disc cups are enlarged. There is no family history.
Goniotomy or trabeculotomy is the first-line surgical treatment; medical therapy (timolol, dorzolamide) is a temporizing bridge to surgery.
teachingPoints: Primary congenital glaucoma results from developmental anomaly of the trabecular meshwork (isolated trabeculodysgenesis); it is bilateral in 70% of cases.
The classic triad of tearing, photophobia, and blepharospasm in an infant should raise immediate suspicion.
Haab striae are horizontal or curvilinear breaks in Descemet membrane due to corneal stretching from elevated IOP; they are pathognomonic.
Early surgical intervention is essential; medical therapy alone is rarely sufficient. Success rates for goniotomy/trabeculotomy exceed 80%.

clues: A 50-year-old male is referred for evaluation of a pigmented iris lesion noticed during a routine eye exam.

Slit-lamp shows a well-defined, darkly pigmented, dome-shaped mass on the inferior iris stroma measuring 4 mm in base. It has intrinsic vascularity. There is localized corectopia (pupil distortion) toward the mass.

Gonioscopy shows the lesion extends into the angle but does not involve the ciliary body. UBM (ultrasound biomicroscopy) confirms the lesion is confined to the iris with no ciliary body extension. Anterior segment OCT shows shadowing from the mass.
The lesion has documented growth on serial photography over 12 months. The patient has light-colored irides.
Options include observation for small stable lesions, sector iridectomy for localized tumors, or plaque brachytherapy for larger tumors not amenable to excision.
teachingPoints: Iris melanomas account for about 5% of all uveal melanomas and have the best prognosis among uveal melanoma types due to their earlier detection.
Features suspicious for malignancy (vs. nevus): documented growth, size >3 mm, intrinsic vascularity, ectropion uveae, sectoral cataract, and elevated IOP.
Metastatic risk is lower (~3-5%) than posterior uveal melanomas, partly because iris melanomas tend to be smaller at diagnosis.
Fine needle aspiration biopsy and GEP (gene expression profiling) can help determine metastatic risk in borderline cases.

clues: A 52-year-old postmenopausal female presents with chronic bilateral eye irritation, burning, and fluctuating blurry vision that worsens with reading and computer use.

Slit-lamp shows reduced tear meniscus height. Fluorescein staining reveals inferior punctate epithelial erosions. Tear break-up time is 4 seconds. Schirmer test (without anesthesia) measures 3 mm in 5 minutes bilaterally.
Meibomian glands appear normal. The patient has dry mouth and joint pain. Autoimmune workup shows positive anti-SSA (Ro) and anti-SSB (La) antibodies.
The patient has Sjögren syndrome. She takes antihistamines and works at a computer 8+ hours daily.
Stepped approach: artificial tears, punctal plugs, topical cyclosporine 0.05% or lifitegrast, and management of Sjögren syndrome. Avoid antihistamines if possible.
teachingPoints: Dry eye disease has two main categories: aqueous-deficient (reduced tear production) and evaporative (meibomian gland dysfunction); many patients have a mixed mechanism.
Sjögren syndrome is the most common autoimmune cause of aqueous-deficient dry eye; systemic workup is warranted when severe dry eye presents with dry mouth.
DEWS II (Dry Eye Workshop) classifies and guides management using a severity-based treatment algorithm.
Topical cyclosporine and lifitegrast are anti-inflammatory therapies that address the underlying inflammatory component of dry eye disease.

clues: A 35-year-old male presents with rapidly progressive bilateral proptosis, chemosis, ophthalmoplegia, and severe headache following a recent sinus infection and facial cellulitis.
Bilateral proptosis, complete ophthalmoplegia, dilated and fixed pupils, decreased corneal sensation (V1), and chemosis. Visual acuity is reduced bilaterally. He is febrile (40°C) and appears toxic.
MRI with MRV of the brain reveals filling defects in the cavernous sinuses bilaterally. CT shows opacification of the sphenoid sinus. Blood cultures grow Staphylococcus aureus.
The patient had a nasal boil (furuncle) on the upper lip that he squeezed 1 week ago. The infection spread despite oral antibiotics.

IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (vancomycin + meropenem or ceftriaxone + metronidazole) and anticoagulation with heparin (controversial but often used). Neurosurgical consultation for possible sinus drainage.
teachingPoints: Cavernous sinus thrombosis is a life-threatening emergency most commonly caused by contiguous spread of infection from the sinuses, face, or orbit.
Bilateral involvement (via intercavernous sinuses) distinguishes CST from orbital cellulitis, which is typically unilateral.
CN III, IV, V1, V2, and VI all pass through or adjacent to the cavernous sinus, explaining the multiple cranial neuropathies.
Mortality has decreased from ~100% to ~30% with antibiotics, but morbidity remains high with cranial nerve deficits persisting in many survivors.

clues: A 70-year-old Scandinavian male presents with gradually decreasing vision and elevated IOP discovered during a routine exam.

Slit-lamp shows white, dandruff-like fibrillar material on the anterior lens capsule in a target pattern (central disc, clear zone, and peripheral rim). Iris transillumination defects are present at the pupillary margin.
IOP is 28 mmHg OD, 24 mmHg OS. Gonioscopy shows heavy trabecular meshwork pigmentation. OCT RNFL shows bilateral thinning. Visual fields show inferior arcuate scotoma OD.
The patient has noticed difficulty with pupillary dilation at past eye exams. His irides appear moth-eaten.
Topical IOP-lowering therapy is first-line. SLT may be effective. PXF patients often require earlier surgical intervention as the glaucoma tends to be more aggressive.
teachingPoints: Pseudoexfoliation is the most common identifiable cause of open-angle glaucoma worldwide; it is highly prevalent in Scandinavian, Mediterranean, and certain African populations.
The exfoliative material deposits in the trabecular meshwork, reducing aqueous outflow and causing IOP elevation.
PXF eyes are at higher risk during cataract surgery due to poor pupil dilation, zonular weakness, and risk of IOL decentration.
PXF is also associated with zonular dialysis, lens subluxation, and phacodonesis; a capsular tension ring may be needed during cataract surgery.

diagnosis: Optic Disc Pit with Serous Macular Detachment
clues: A 30-year-old female presents with decreased central vision in her left eye noticed over the past month.

Visual acuity is 20/80 OS. Fundoscopy reveals a small, oval, grayish depression at the inferotemporal aspect of the left optic disc with an associated serous retinal detachment extending to the macula.
OCT shows schisis-like separation of the inner retinal layers with a serous neurosensory detachment at the fovea. There is a communication between the optic pit and the subretinal/intraretinal fluid.
The right optic disc is normal. There is no inflammation. The pit has likely been present since birth but the maculopathy is a new development.
Vitrectomy with gas tamponade and possibly laser barricade around the disc pit is the most commonly performed treatment for symptomatic maculopathy.
teachingPoints: Optic disc pits are congenital anomalies occurring in approximately 1 in 11,000 people; they are typically unilateral and located in the inferotemporal disc.
Serous macular detachment develops in 25-75% of eyes with optic disc pits, usually in the third to fourth decade of life.
The source of subretinal fluid is debated—it may be vitreous fluid, CSF, or fluid from leaky vessels within the pit.
Without treatment, the chronic serous detachment leads to permanent outer retinal damage and poor visual outcome.

clues: A 28-year-old male presents to the ER with acute onset of severe eye pain, tearing, and foreign body sensation after being poked in the right eye by a tree branch while hiking.

Visual acuity is 20/40 OD due to tearing. Slit-lamp with fluorescein reveals a linear epithelial defect on the cornea with uptake of fluorescein. The surrounding stroma is clear. No infiltrate or AC reaction.
Seidel test is negative (no aqueous leak). There is no foreign body under the upper lid on lid eversion. IOP is normal.
The injury was from vegetable matter (tree branch). There is no contact lens use.
Topical antibiotic (erythromycin ointment or fluoroquinolone drops) and cycloplegic for pain. Oral analgesics as needed. No patching. Follow-up in 24-48 hours to ensure healing.
teachingPoints: Corneal abrasions are one of the most common eye injuries seen in emergency departments; most heal within 24-72 hours.
Vegetable or organic matter injuries carry a higher risk of fungal keratitis; patients should be counseled to return if symptoms worsen.
Do not patch corneal abrasions—studies show no benefit and patching may delay healing.
Recurrent corneal erosion syndrome is a potential complication, especially after fingernail or organic matter injuries.

clues: A 35-year-old HIV-positive male (CD4 count 80) presents with blurred vision in his right eye and headache for 1 week.
Fundoscopy reveals bilateral large areas of necrotizing retinitis WITHOUT the classic old scar. The lesions are multifocal, bilateral, and more extensive than typical immunocompetent toxoplasma.
MRI brain shows ring-enhancing lesions. Toxoplasma IgG is positive. PCR of aqueous humor is positive for Toxoplasma gondii DNA.
The patient is not on antiretroviral therapy and has no history of Toxoplasma prophylaxis. He has both ocular and CNS involvement.
Systemic anti-toxoplasma therapy (pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + folinic acid) plus initiation of ART. Intravitreal clindamycin may be added for severe ocular disease.
teachingPoints: In immunocompromised patients, toxoplasma retinochoroiditis can present atypically: bilateral, multifocal, without pre-existing scars, and with CNS co-involvement.
The classic 'satellite lesion adjacent to an old scar' may be absent in AIDS patients; lesions are often larger and more necrotizing.
CNS toxoplasmosis should always be considered when ocular toxoplasmosis presents in an HIV-positive patient with low CD4.
Secondary prophylaxis must be maintained until immune reconstitution (CD4 >200 for at least 6 months on ART).

clues: An 8-year-old girl is evaluated for an abnormal head turn. Her parents have noted the head turn since infancy but were reassured it would resolve.

The left eye has limited abduction (cannot look left) with relatively normal adduction. On attempted adduction, the left globe retracts and the palpebral fissure narrows. No strabismus in primary position due to compensatory head turn.
Forced duction test shows no mechanical restriction. MRI of the brainstem and orbits shows absence of the left CN6 nucleus.
The patient has a face turn to the left (toward the affected side) to maintain binocular vision. She has no amblyopia.

No treatment needed if there is no strabismus in primary position and the head turn is acceptable. Surgery (medial rectus recession) is considered for significant head turn or misalignment in primary gaze.
teachingPoints: Duane syndrome is a congenital cranial dysinnervation disorder due to absence or hypoplasia of the abducens (CN6) nucleus with aberrant innervation of the lateral rectus by CN3.
Type I (most common, 78%): limited abduction. Type II: limited adduction. Type III: both limited.
Globe retraction and palpebral fissure narrowing on adduction are pathognomonic, caused by co-contraction of the medial and lateral rectus muscles.
Duane syndrome is unilateral in 80% of cases, more common on the left side and in females.

clues: A 60-year-old Japanese female is found to have optic disc cupping and visual field loss on a routine exam. She has no symptoms.
IOP is consistently 15 mmHg in both eyes (within the normal range). Cup-to-disc ratio is 0.7 OD and 0.8 OS with a disc hemorrhage at the inferotemporal rim OS. There is RNFL thinning.
Humphrey visual fields show paracentral scotomas more prominent in the inferior hemifield. 24-hour IOP monitoring shows a peak of 18 mmHg (nocturnal dip pattern).
The patient has Raynaud phenomenon, migraine headaches, and a family history of glaucoma. Blood pressure is 100/60 mmHg.
IOP-lowering therapy to achieve a 30% reduction from baseline is the only proven treatment (CNTGS trial). Address vascular risk factors; avoid excessive nocturnal blood pressure dipping.
teachingPoints: Normal tension glaucoma is a form of open-angle glaucoma in which optic nerve damage and visual field loss occur despite IOP within the statistically normal range (<21 mmHg).
Disc hemorrhages (Drance hemorrhages) are more common in NTG and are a sign of disease progression.
Vascular dysregulation (low blood pressure, vasospasm, migraine) is thought to play a significant role in the pathogenesis.
NTG is particularly common in Japanese populations, where it accounts for the majority of glaucoma cases.

clues: A 12-year-old boy presents with bilateral blurred vision and is found to have high myopia and a shallow anterior chamber despite his young age.

Slit-lamp shows bilateral small, spherical lenses with an increased anteroposterior diameter. The equator of the lens is visible through a dilated pupil (360° zonular laxity). Anterior chamber is shallow.
IOP is 30 mmHg bilaterally. Gonioscopy shows narrow angles due to anterior displacement of the lens-iris diaphragm. The child is short-statured with brachydactyly.
The patient has a diagnosis of Weill-Marchesani syndrome. Genetic testing confirms an ADAMTS10 mutation.

Lens extraction is often required to manage both the refractive error and the angle closure. Miotics (pilocarpine) may paradoxically worsen angle closure by increasing pupillary block; cycloplegics may open the angle.
teachingPoints: Microspherophakia is a rare condition in which the lens is small and spherical, causing high lenticular myopia and risk of pupillary block glaucoma.
Associations include Weill-Marchesani syndrome, Marfan syndrome, and homocystinuria.
Unlike typical angle closure, miotics worsen the condition by pulling the lens-iris diaphragm forward; cycloplegics and laser iridotomy are initial management strategies.
Lens subluxation is common and may require specialized surgical techniques such as intracapsular extraction.

clues: A 10-year-old boy presents with headaches, growth retardation, and difficulty seeing objects to the sides noticed by his teachers.
Visual acuity is 20/25 bilaterally. Confrontation visual fields suggest bitemporal deficits. Formal perimetry confirms a complete bitemporal hemianopia respecting the vertical meridian.
MRI brain shows a large suprasellar cystic and solid mass with calcification, compressing the optic chiasm. The lesion enhances peripherally.
The child has delayed puberty and is below the 5th percentile for height. Endocrine workup reveals panhypopituitarism.
Neurosurgical resection (transsphenoidal or transcranial) with hormone replacement therapy. Radiation may be needed for residual or recurrent tumor.
teachingPoints: Craniopharyngioma is the most common suprasellar tumor in children and frequently presents with visual field defects from chiasmal compression.
Bitemporal hemianopia is the hallmark of chiasmal compression, as the crossing nasal retinal fibers (representing temporal visual fields) are affected first.
Calcification within a suprasellar lesion in a child is highly suggestive of craniopharyngioma.
Despite being benign (WHO Grade I), craniopharyngiomas have high recurrence rates (~20-50%) and cause significant morbidity from hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction.

clues: A newborn is noted to have a white pupillary reflex in the left eye during the newborn nursery examination.
A dense white lens opacity occupies the central visual axis of the left eye. The red reflex is absent on the left. The right eye has a normal red reflex.
B-scan ultrasound shows a normal posterior segment with no retinal mass (rules out retinoblastoma). TORCH screen and metabolic workup (including galactosemia) are sent.
The mother had a rubella-like illness during the first trimester. Family history is unremarkable.
Urgent cataract extraction within the first 6-8 weeks of life is critical to prevent dense deprivation amblyopia. Contact lens or IOL correction and aggressive amblyopia management follow.
teachingPoints: Unilateral dense congenital cataracts are a surgical emergency because the sensitive period for visual development requires early intervention to prevent irreversible amblyopia.
The differential for leukocoria includes retinoblastoma (most critical to rule out), Coats disease, PFV, toxocariasis, and ROP.
TORCH infections (toxoplasmosis, rubella, CMV, herpes) are important congenital causes; metabolic causes include galactosemia (oil-droplet cataract) and Lowe syndrome.
Aphakic glaucoma is a long-term complication occurring in up to 50% of children who undergo cataract surgery in infancy.

clues: A 25-year-old African American male with sickle cell SC disease presents for a routine dilated eye exam. He is asymptomatic.
Dilated fundus exam reveals bilateral peripheral sea-fan neovascularization. There are also salmon-colored patches (salmon patch hemorrhages), iridescent spots, and black sunbursts in the periphery.
FA confirms the neovascularization with leakage at the border of perfused and non-perfused retina. There are large areas of peripheral capillary non-perfusion.
The patient has SC hemoglobin type, which has a higher incidence of proliferative retinopathy than SS type.
Laser photocoagulation to the ischemic retina feeding the sea-fan neovascularization. Anti-VEGF may be used adjunctively. Vitrectomy for non-clearing vitreous hemorrhage or tractional detachment.
teachingPoints: Paradoxically, sickle cell SC disease (not SS) has the highest rate of proliferative retinopathy; the higher hemoglobin allows better retinal perfusion and more ischemia-reperfusion injury.

The Goldberg classification stages sickle retinopathy: Stage I (peripheral arteriolar occlusion), II (AV anastomoses), III (neovascularization—sea fans), IV (vitreous hemorrhage), V (retinal detachment).
Sea-fan neovascularization is the hallmark; it can auto-infarct spontaneously in some patients.
All patients with sickle cell disease should have annual dilated fundus exams starting at age 10.

clues: A 16-year-old tall, thin male presents with blurred vision and monocular diplopia (double vision with one eye) that has been worsening.
Slit-lamp exam reveals bilateral superotemporal subluxation of the lens with visible equatorial lens edge through a dilated pupil. Iridodonesis (tremulous iris) is present.
Refraction shows high astigmatism with a significant myopic shift from the previous year. Echocardiography reveals mitral valve prolapse and aortic root dilation (4.5 cm).
The patient is 6'3" with an arm span exceeding his height (marfanoid habitus). He has long fingers (arachnodactyly), pectus excavatum, and joint hypermobility.

Ophthalmologic management includes spectacle correction or aphakic contact lens if the lens is significantly displaced. Lens extraction with IOL is needed for severe subluxation. Cardiology referral is critical.
teachingPoints: Marfan syndrome is caused by FBN1 gene mutations affecting fibrillin-1; ectopia lentis occurs in approximately 60-80% of patients, classically in the superotemporal direction.
The differential for ectopia lentis includes Marfan (superotemporal), homocystinuria (inferonasal), Weill-Marchesani, and trauma.
Homocystinuria must be distinguished from Marfan as it carries different anesthetic and surgical risks; a urine homocysteine test is important.
The most life-threatening feature of Marfan syndrome is aortic root dilation and dissection; regular cardiac follow-up is essential.

clues: A 68-year-old female reports gradual return of blurry vision and glare in her right eye, 2 years after successful cataract surgery.
Visual acuity is 20/60 OD (was 20/20 after surgery). Slit-lamp shows opacification and wrinkling of the posterior lens capsule behind the IOL. The IOL itself is clear and well-positioned.
Red reflex shows haziness through the center. Retro-illumination reveals a distinct Elschnig pearl pattern on the posterior capsule.
The patient had an uneventful phacoemulsification with a posterior chamber IOL 2 years ago.
Nd:YAG laser posterior capsulotomy is a quick, definitive outpatient procedure that immediately restores visual clarity.
teachingPoints: PCO is the most common long-term complication of cataract surgery, occurring in approximately 20-40% of patients within 2-5 years.
It is caused by proliferation and migration of residual lens epithelial cells on the posterior capsule.
YAG capsulotomy carries small risks including IOL pitting, IOP spike, cystoid macular edema, and retinal detachment.
Modern IOL designs (square-edged optics) have reduced PCO rates by creating a barrier to lens epithelial cell migration.

clues: A newborn is noted to have a small eye (microphthalmia) and absent red reflex on the left during the newborn exam.
Examination under anesthesia reveals a retrolental fibrovascular membrane attached to the posterior lens capsule, with elongated ciliary processes drawn centrally. The eye is microphthalmic.
B-scan shows no calcification (distinguishing from retinoblastoma). There is a dense stalk of tissue extending from the optic disc to the posterior lens. CT shows no intraocular mass.
The condition is unilateral. No family history. The infant is otherwise healthy.
Lensectomy with anterior vitrectomy may be performed to clear the visual axis; visual prognosis depends on the severity and extent of posterior segment involvement.
teachingPoints: PFV results from failure of regression of the embryonic hyaloid vascular system; it is the most common congenital vitreous anomaly.
PFV is typically unilateral and associated with microphthalmos, in contrast to retinoblastoma which is typically found in a normal-sized or larger eye.
Anterior PFV (retrolental membrane, shallow anterior chamber) has a better prognosis than posterior PFV (stalk, retinal folds, retinal detachment).
Complications include progressive cataract, glaucoma, vitreous hemorrhage, and tractional retinal detachment.

clues: A 14-year-old asymptomatic girl is referred for evaluation of bilateral 'swollen optic discs' found during a school screening exam.

The optic discs appear elevated with irregular, scalloped margins and absent venous pulsations. There is no obscuration of major vessels and no peripapillary hemorrhages. Spontaneous venous pulsations are absent bilaterally.

B-scan ultrasound shows bilateral highly reflective calcified optic disc drusen. Autofluorescence shows autofluorescent deposits at the disc. OCT shows a lumpy, irregular disc contour with RNFL bundle compression.
Visual acuity is 20/20 bilaterally. Visual fields show enlarged blind spots. The child is healthy with no headaches or neurologic symptoms.

No treatment is needed for asymptomatic ONHD. Monitor visual fields annually, as progressive RNFL loss can occur. Counsel the patient to carry documentation of disc drusen to avoid unnecessary workup in the future.
teachingPoints: ONHD is the most common cause of pseudopapilledema; it must be distinguished from true papilledema which requires urgent workup for elevated ICP.
Drusen are calcified deposits that accumulate within the optic nerve head, causing the appearance of disc elevation.
B-scan ultrasound is the gold standard for diagnosis, showing highly reflective deposits that persist on reduced gain.
ONHD can cause progressive visual field loss (usually asymptomatic) and rarely anterior ischemic optic neuropathy.

clues: A 40-year-old HIV-positive male (CD4 count 35) presents with floaters and a scotoma in his left eye progressing over 2 weeks.

Dilated exam reveals a large area of full-thickness retinal necrosis with hemorrhage ('pizza pie' appearance) along the superotemporal arcade. The leading edge is white and granular, with scattered hemorrhage.
The retinitis follows the vascular arcades with a brush-fire pattern. There is minimal vitritis (unlike toxoplasmosis). OCT shows full-thickness retinal involvement. CMV PCR of aqueous is positive.
The patient was not on antiretroviral therapy and did not receive CMV prophylaxis. He recently had CMV viremia.
Intravitreal ganciclovir injection for immediate treatment + systemic valganciclovir. Initiate ART with careful timing to avoid immune recovery uveitis.
teachingPoints: CMV retinitis is the most common opportunistic ocular infection in AIDS, occurring almost exclusively when CD4 <50 cells/μL.
The characteristic 'pizza pie' fundus appearance (hemorrhagic necrosis with white granular borders) is virtually diagnostic.
Immune recovery uveitis (IRU) can occur after ART initiation as the immune system recovers and mounts an inflammatory response against CMV antigens; it may cause cystoid macular edema.
Maintenance therapy can be discontinued once CD4 exceeds 100 cells/μL for at least 3-6 months on ART, with no active retinitis.

clues: A 45-year-old male presents with vertical diplopia that is worse when looking down and to the left, and when tilting his head to the right.
Right hypertropia that increases in left gaze and right head tilt (positive 3-step test for right CN4 palsy). He has a compensatory left head tilt.
Maddox rod testing confirms an excyclotorsion. Double Maddox rod shows 8 degrees of torsion. MRI brain is normal.
The patient is a cyclist who had a minor head injury 3 months ago when he hit a pothole. Old photos show no prior head tilt.
If decompensated (symptomatic), treatment includes prism glasses for small deviations or inferior oblique weakening surgery (myectomy or recession) for larger deviations.
teachingPoints: CN4 is the most commonly injured cranial nerve due to its long intracranial course and thin caliber; trauma is the most common acquired cause.
The Parks-Bielschowsky 3-step test localizes the paretic muscle: hypertropia, worse in contralateral gaze, and worse with ipsilateral head tilt.
Congenital CN4 palsy can decompensate in adulthood; old photos showing a longstanding head tilt are an important clue.
Bilateral CN4 palsies (from trauma) present with a 'V-pattern' esotropia, excyclotorsion >10 degrees, and a chin-down head position.

clues: A 28-year-old female presents with acute onset of blurred vision, photopsias (flashing lights), and a scotoma in her left eye following a viral upper respiratory infection.
Fundoscopy shows multiple small (100-200 μm) white-yellow dots at the level of the outer retina/RPE scattered throughout the posterior pole and mid-periphery. Foveal granularity is present.
FA shows early wreath-like hyperfluorescence of the dots. OCT shows disruption of the ellipsoid zone. The ERG shows reduced a-wave amplitude. Enlarged blind spot is present on visual fields.
The patient is a young myopic woman who had a flu-like illness 1 week ago. The condition is unilateral.
Observation only—MEWDS is self-limiting and resolves spontaneously within 6-10 weeks with excellent visual recovery in the vast majority of cases.
teachingPoints: MEWDS is a benign, self-limiting inflammatory condition primarily affecting young myopic women, often preceded by a viral prodrome.
It is classified among the 'white dot syndromes' which include MEWDS, APMPPE, birdshot chorioretinopathy, multifocal choroiditis, and serpiginous choroidopathy.
The pathognomonic foveal granularity and enlarged blind spot help distinguish MEWDS from other white dot syndromes.
Recurrence rate is low (~10%); prognosis is excellent with nearly complete visual recovery.

clues: A 65-year-old diabetic male with a history of central retinal vein occlusion 2 months ago presents with a severely painful, red right eye and decreased vision.

IOP is 52 mmHg OD. Slit-lamp shows rubeosis iridis (neovascularization of the iris) at the pupillary margin and angle. Gonioscopy reveals neovascularization of the angle with peripheral anterior synechiae.
Fluorescein angiography shows extensive retinal ischemia with >10 disc areas of capillary non-perfusion (ischemic CRVO). OCT shows macular edema.
The patient's CRVO was ischemic at presentation. He missed his follow-up appointments. His diabetes is poorly controlled.

Panretinal photocoagulation and intravitreal anti-VEGF (bevacizumab) urgently to regress neovascularization. IOP management with aqueous suppressants (avoid miotics and prostaglandins). Glaucoma drainage device if IOP uncontrolled.
teachingPoints: NVG results from retinal ischemia driving VEGF production, which stimulates neovascularization of the iris and angle, leading to synechial angle closure.
The three most common causes are diabetic retinopathy, ischemic CRVO, and ocular ischemic syndrome (carotid occlusive disease).
PRP treats the underlying cause by ablating ischemic retina and reducing VEGF drive.
Prognosis for NVG is poor without early intervention; many eyes progress to a painful blind eye requiring cyclodestruction or enucleation.

clues: A 35-year-old construction worker presents immediately after splashing liquid cement (alkite) in both eyes. He is in severe pain with markedly reduced vision.

After copious irrigation, pH is 9.5 (still alkaline). The conjunctiva is ischemic white (blanched) with no injection. The cornea is diffusely opaque. Large epithelial defects are present bilaterally. Limbal ischemia involves >6 clock hours.
IOP is 35 mmHg bilaterally (due to shrinkage of the collagen in the sclera and trabecular meshwork). There is no view of the posterior segment.
This is an alkali burn classified as Roper-Hall Grade IV (>50% limbal ischemia, opaque cornea).

Immediate copious irrigation until pH normalizes (7.0-7.4) is the single most important initial step. Then: topical antibiotics, cycloplegics, aggressive lubrication, citrate and ascorbate drops, IOP control, and amniotic membrane transplant.
teachingPoints: Alkali burns are more severe than acid burns because alkalis penetrate deeper through saponification of cell membranes (liquefactive necrosis), reaching the anterior chamber.
Limbal stem cell damage determines long-term prognosis; severe limbal ischemia (>6 clock hours) indicates a poor outcome.
Never wait for an ophthalmologist before irrigating—immediate irrigation at the scene is the most critical intervention.
Long-term complications include limbal stem cell deficiency, corneal scarring, glaucoma, and symblepharon; limbal stem cell transplant or keratoprosthesis may eventually be needed.

clues: A 55-year-old female is referred for evaluation of a dark lesion on the optic disc discovered incidentally during a diabetic eye screening exam.
A jet-black, well-circumscribed, dome-shaped lesion is seen on the inferior portion of the right optic disc, slightly overlapping the adjacent retina. Visual acuity is 20/20.

B-scan shows a solid lesion with high internal reflectivity at the disc. OCT shows the mass is superficial and does not involve deeper optic nerve tissue. Visual fields show a mild enlarged blind spot.
The lesion has been present for at least 2 years on review of old photos with no documented growth.
Observation with serial photography and visual field testing. Melanocytomas are benign and rarely require treatment unless they cause progressive visual field loss.
teachingPoints: Melanocytoma is a benign, deeply pigmented (magnocellular) nevus of the optic disc, most common in dark-skinned individuals.
Despite their alarming jet-black appearance, they are benign; malignant transformation occurs in <1-2% of cases.
They can cause an afferent pupillary defect and visual field defects from compression of optic nerve fibers.
Rare complications include spontaneous necrosis (tumor necrosis causing disc edema and vision loss) and rarely malignant transformation.

clues: An 80-year-old female presents with gradual, progressive central vision loss in both eyes over several years. She has difficulty reading and recognizing faces.

Fundoscopy reveals bilateral large soft drusen (>125 μm) with areas of RPE mottling and pigment clumping. A well-demarcated area of RPE and photoreceptor atrophy (geographic atrophy) surrounds but partially spares the foveal center.

OCT shows loss of the outer retinal layers and RPE in areas of geographic atrophy. Fundus autofluorescence shows central hypo-autofluorescence with a hyperautofluorescent border (indicating active atrophy expansion).
She has a 30-year smoking history (quit 10 years ago). Her brother also had macular degeneration.
AREDS2 supplements (lutein, zeaxanthin, vitamin C, E, zinc, copper) to slow progression. Low vision rehabilitation. Pegcetacoplan or avacincaptad pegol for geographic atrophy.
teachingPoints: Dry AMD accounts for approximately 90% of all AMD cases. Geographic atrophy is the advanced form of dry AMD.
AREDS2 formula reduces the risk of progression to advanced AMD by approximately 25% in intermediate AMD cases.
Pegcetacoplan (Syfovre) and avacincaptad pegol (Izervay) are FDA-approved complement inhibitors that slow the growth of geographic atrophy.
Low vision aids (magnifiers, CCTV, eccentric viewing training) can significantly improve functional ability in patients with advanced AMD.

clues: A 65-year-old male presents with progressive painless swelling of the right upper eyelid and mild proptosis over several months.
A smooth, rubbery, salmon-pink subconjunctival mass is present in the superior fornix. There is non-tender proptosis and mild restriction of upgaze. No afferent pupillary defect.
CT orbits shows a well-circumscribed, molding (conforming to the shape of the globe) extraconal mass in the superolateral orbit. Biopsy reveals a MALT (mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue) lymphoma.
The patient reports no systemic symptoms (no B-symptoms). Systemic workup including PET-CT shows no extraorbital disease (Stage IE).
Low-grade localized orbital MALT lymphoma is treated with external beam radiation therapy (~24-30 Gy) with excellent local control. Observation or rituximab are alternatives.
teachingPoints: Lymphoma is the most common malignant orbital tumor in adults; the majority are low-grade B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphomas, most commonly MALT type.
A 'salmon patch' lesion of the conjunctiva is a classic presentation that should always be biopsied.
Orbital lymphoma molds around structures rather than eroding bone, which distinguishes it from more aggressive tumors.
Complete systemic workup is essential; approximately 30-40% of patients with orbital lymphoma have or will develop systemic lymphoma.

clues: A 55-year-old male with a history of IV drug use and recent hospitalization for endocarditis presents with acute painful vision loss in his right eye.
Visual acuity is hand motions OD. There is a hypopyon, dense vitritis obscuring the fundus view, and conjunctival injection. There is no prior history of eye surgery or trauma.
B-scan shows dense vitreous opacities. Blood cultures are positive for Candida albicans. Vitreous tap culture also grows Candida. Echocardiography shows aortic valve vegetation.
The patient is currently being treated for Candida endocarditis with IV antifungals. He has a central venous catheter.
Intravitreal injection of amphotericin B + voriconazole. Pars plana vitrectomy for severe cases. Continue systemic antifungal therapy for the underlying candidemia.
teachingPoints: Endogenous endophthalmitis is caused by hematogenous spread of organisms to the eye; Candida is the most common fungal cause, often in immunocompromised or IV drug-using patients.
Risk factors include IV drug use, indwelling catheters, immunosuppression, endocarditis, and prolonged hospitalization.
Fungal endophthalmitis classically presents more indolently than bacterial, with string-of-pearls vitreous opacities and fluffy white chorioretinal infiltrates.
Screening dilated eye exams are recommended for all patients with candidemia, as subclinical ocular involvement is common.

clues: A 40-year-old female presents with unilateral decreased vision, an irregular pupil, and a diagnosis of refractory glaucoma in her left eye.

Slit-lamp shows unilateral corneal edema with a beaten-bronze corneal endothelium, corectopia (displaced pupil), iris atrophy with hole formation, and peripheral anterior synechiae extending beyond the trabecular meshwork.
Specular microscopy reveals abnormal 'ICE cells' (dark areas with a light boundary) replacing normal endothelial cells. IOP is 35 mmHg OS. Gonioscopy shows broad PAS.
The condition is unilateral in a young-to-middle-aged woman. There is no family history. The right eye is completely normal.
Medical IOP management initially; glaucoma drainage surgery (tube shunt) when medical therapy fails. DSEK/DMEK may be needed for corneal decompensation.
teachingPoints: ICE syndrome is a spectrum of diseases (Chandler syndrome, essential/progressive iris atrophy, Cogan-Reese syndrome) caused by abnormal corneal endothelial cell proliferation.
The abnormal endothelial cells migrate across the angle and iris, forming a membrane that causes PAS, glaucoma, corectopia, and iris atrophy.
It is almost always unilateral and predominantly affects young to middle-aged women; it may be associated with HSV.
Glaucoma in ICE syndrome is often refractory to medical therapy and trabeculectomy; tube shunt surgery has the best long-term success.

clues: A 50-year-old female presents with acute bilateral ptosis, diplopia, dysphagia, and progressive weakness over 48 hours. She has no prior neurological history.
Bilateral complete ptosis with near-total external ophthalmoplegia. Pupils are dilated and poorly reactive (mydriasis). Facial and bulbar weakness present. Deep tendon reflexes are diminished.
Edrophonium (Tensilon) test is negative. Acetylcholine receptor antibodies are negative. EMG shows decremental response. CT chest shows no thymoma. Stool toxin assay is sent.
The patient reports eating home-canned vegetables 24 hours before symptom onset. The descending paralysis pattern (cranial nerves first, then limbs) is noted.
Botulism antitoxin should be administered emergently. Supportive care with monitoring for respiratory failure and potential intubation. Public health notification is required.
teachingPoints: Botulism causes a descending flaccid paralysis from presynaptic blockade of acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction, unlike myasthenia which is postsynaptic.
Pupil involvement (mydriasis) distinguishes botulism from myasthenia gravis, where pupils are always spared.
The triad of descending paralysis, dilated pupils, and absence of fever should raise immediate suspicion for botulism.
Wound botulism (from IV drug use) and infant botulism (from honey) are other important clinical scenarios to recognize.

clues: A 45-year-old immunocompetent male presents with painful red eye, blurred vision, and photophobia in his left eye for 5 days.

Slit-lamp shows anterior chamber cells and flare. Dilated exam reveals full-thickness white-yellow areas of peripheral retinal necrosis with an abrupt border between necrotic and normal retina. Moderate to severe vitritis is present.
PCR of aqueous humor is positive for varicella-zoster virus (VZV). Fluorescein angiography shows arteriolar non-perfusion in areas of necrosis with late staining.
The necrosis is rapidly progressing circumferentially. The patient had chickenpox as a child but no recent shingles. He is HIV-negative.
IV acyclovir (10 mg/kg TID) for 5-10 days followed by oral valacyclovir for 6-12 weeks. Prophylactic laser barricade posterior to the necrosis to prevent retinal detachment.
teachingPoints: ARN is defined by the American Uveitis Society criteria: peripheral necrotizing retinitis, rapid circumferential spread, evidence of herpesvirus, and prominent vitritis.
VZV is the most common cause in older patients; HSV is more common in younger patients.
Retinal detachment occurs in 50-75% of ARN cases due to retinal breaks in areas of necrosis; prophylactic barricade laser may reduce this risk.
Bilateral involvement (BARN) occurs in approximately one-third of cases, often affecting the second eye within weeks.

clues: A 12-year-old boy with a tall, thin build and joint hypermobility presents with a sudden shower of floaters and flashing lights in his right eye.

Dilated exam reveals an optically empty vitreous cavity with a membranous vitreous veil. There is a giant retinal tear superiorly with a retinal detachment. High myopia (-10 D) is present bilaterally.
The fellow eye shows the same characteristic 'empty vitreous' with a membranous vitreous anomaly and lattice-like changes peripherally. Hearing test reveals mild sensorineural hearing loss.
The patient's mother is highly myopic and had retinal detachment surgery at age 20. The patient has midface hypoplasia, a flat nasal bridge, and Pierre Robin sequence was present at birth.
Urgent retinal detachment repair. Prophylactic treatment (cryotherapy or laser retinopexy) of the fellow eye is strongly recommended given the very high detachment risk.
teachingPoints: Stickler syndrome (Type 1, COL2A1 mutation) is the most common inherited cause of rhegmatogenous retinal detachment in children and young adults.
The characteristic vitreous anomaly is a congenital membranous vitreous with an optically empty vitreous cavity behind it.
Systemic features include midface hypoplasia, cleft palate/Pierre Robin sequence, sensorineural hearing loss, and premature arthropathy.
Prophylactic 360-degree cryotherapy or laser of the fellow eye significantly reduces retinal detachment risk in Stickler patients (Cambridge prophylaxis protocol).

clues: A 40-year-old female presents with an acute IOP spike to 50 mmHg and angle closure symptoms despite having had a patent laser peripheral iridotomy.

Slit-lamp shows a patent LPI. The anterior chamber is of normal depth centrally but the iris root is flat and inserts anteriorly. Gonioscopy shows appositional closure in the angle despite the iridotomy.
Ultrasound biomicroscopy (UBM) reveals anteriorly positioned large ciliary processes that push the peripheral iris forward against the trabecular meshwork (non-pupillary block mechanism).
The patient previously had an LPI for narrow angles 1 year ago. She had been told her angles would stay open after the procedure.
Argon laser peripheral iridoplasty (ALPI) to contract the peripheral iris root is the definitive treatment. Long-term pilocarpine or IOP-lowering drops may also be needed.
teachingPoints: Plateau iris syndrome is angle closure that persists after elimination of pupillary block by iridotomy, due to anteriorly rotated ciliary processes.
It should be suspected in any patient who has angle closure recurrence after a patent LPI, especially if they are younger than typical angle closure patients.
UBM is the imaging modality of choice to visualize the ciliary body position and confirm the diagnosis.
ALPI works by thermally contracting the peripheral iris stroma, pulling it away from the angle—distinct from LPI which relieves pupillary block.

clues: A 28-year-old male presents with acute painful red eye, photophobia, and tearing in his right eye. He reports similar episodes alternating between eyes over the past 3 years.
Slit-lamp shows non-granulomatous anterior uveitis with fine keratic precipitates and 3+ cells. Posterior synechiae are forming. Hypopyon is present.
IOP is low at 6 mmHg OD. No posterior segment inflammation. HLA-B27 is positive. Sacroiliac joint X-rays show bilateral sacroiliitis.
The patient has had chronic lower back pain and morning stiffness for 5 years, improving with exercise. He sees a rheumatologist for ankylosing spondylitis.
Intensive topical prednisolone acetate with cycloplegic. Systemic treatment with a TNF-alpha inhibitor (adalimumab) is considered for frequent recurrences.
teachingPoints: Anterior uveitis occurs in approximately 25-40% of patients with ankylosing spondylitis and is the most common extra-articular manifestation.
The uveitis is characteristically acute, unilateral, alternating, recurrent, and non-granulomatous.
Adalimumab is the only FDA-approved biologic for non-infectious intermediate, posterior, and panuveitis, and reduces uveitis flares in AS patients.
Fibrin formation and hypopyon can occur in severe HLA-B27 uveitis; aggressive treatment prevents complications such as cataract, glaucoma, and band keratopathy.

clues: A 72-year-old female presents with a recurrent 'chalazion' on her right upper eyelid that has been treated with incision and curettage 3 times over the past year.
A firm, yellowish nodule is present in the upper tarsus with loss of meibomian gland orifices. There is diffuse thickening of the upper lid (pagetoid spread) with madarosis (loss of lashes).
Biopsy of the lid lesion shows lobules of cells with sebaceous differentiation and pagetoid spread into the conjunctival epithelium. Oil red O stain is positive on frozen section.
The patient has a history of previous radiation therapy to the face for acne as a teenager. She has no known genetic syndromes.
Wide surgical excision with map biopsies (Mohs or modified Mohs technique) for margin control. Sentinel lymph node biopsy for staging. Exenteration may be needed for extensive orbital invasion.
teachingPoints: Sebaceous gland carcinoma is the second most common malignant eyelid tumor (after basal cell carcinoma in some series) and is a great masquerader of benign conditions.
The classic clue is a 'recurrent chalazion' that keeps coming back in the same location, especially in elderly patients—this must be biopsied.
Pagetoid spread (intraepithelial spread to the conjunctiva and skin) is characteristic and requires map biopsies to determine extent.
Muir-Torre syndrome (MSH2/MLH1 mutations) should be considered in younger patients with sebaceous neoplasms; it is associated with visceral malignancies.

clues: A 25-year-old male presents with bilateral eye redness, photophobia, tearing, and progressive blurriness over several weeks.
Slit-lamp shows deep stromal corneal haze with ghost vessels (empty blood vessels) in the deep stroma bilaterally. There is no epithelial defect. Mild anterior uveitis is present.
FTA-ABS is positive. RPR titer is 1:64. The patient tests negative for HIV. Corneal sensation is normal.
The patient was adopted and has no knowledge of his birth history. He has Hutchinson teeth (notched incisors) and hearing difficulties.
IV penicillin G for neurosyphilis (treat as late/tertiary syphilis). Topical steroids for inflammation. The corneal scarring may require penetrating keratoplasty once inactive.
teachingPoints: Interstitial keratitis is the most common ocular manifestation of congenital syphilis, part of the Hutchinson triad (IK, Hutchinson teeth, sensorineural deafness).
Ghost vessels are pathognomonic—they represent previously vascularized deep corneal stroma that has become avascular after resolution of active inflammation.
Other causes of IK include tuberculosis, Cogan syndrome (with vestibulo-auditory dysfunction), Lyme disease, and herpes simplex.
The cornea may remain relatively clear between episodes but becomes progressively more scarred with each recurrence.

clues: A 65-year-old female presents with progressive proptosis, redness, and a whooshing sound in her right ear (pulsatile tinnitus) over several weeks.

There is pulsatile proptosis, corkscrew (arterialized) episcleral vessels, chemosis, and elevated IOP (28 mmHg). A bruit is audible with the stethoscope over the globe. Mild ophthalmoplegia is present.
CT angiography reveals a dural arteriovenous fistula between branches of the external carotid artery and the right cavernous sinus with enlarged superior ophthalmic vein.
The presentation is insidious (indirect/dural type fistula). There is no history of trauma. Orbital color Doppler shows reversal of flow in the superior ophthalmic vein.
Endovascular embolization via interventional neuroradiology is the treatment of choice. Low-flow dural fistulas may be observed initially or treated with manual carotid compression.
teachingPoints: Direct (high-flow) CCFs result from trauma causing a tear between the ICA and cavernous sinus. Indirect (low-flow/dural) CCFs are spontaneous and more common in older women.
Arterialized (corkscrew) episcleral vessels are the hallmark clinical sign, distinguishing CCF from other causes of red eye and proptosis.
Elevated IOP results from elevated episcleral venous pressure impeding aqueous outflow through Schlemm canal.
Cranial neuropathies (CN3, 4, 5, 6) occur from compression within the cavernous sinus; CN6 is most commonly affected due to its location within the sinus.

clues: A 60-year-old female with chronic uveitis presents with gradually worsening vision and a white opacity across the central cornea.

Slit-lamp shows a horizontal band of white-gray, granular, chalky deposits at the level of Bowman layer in the interpalpebral zone with clear areas (Swiss cheese holes) where corneal nerves penetrate Bowman layer.
The deposits do not extend to the limbus (a clear zone separates the band from the peripheral cornea). The underlying uveitis is quiescent but has left posterior synechiae and a secondary cataract.
The patient has a history of chronic granulomatous anterior uveitis and has been on long-term topical steroids. Serum calcium is elevated (hyperparathyroidism).
Chelation with EDTA (ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid) applied after epithelial removal dissolves the calcium deposits and can be performed at the slit lamp. Excimer laser PTK is an alternative.
teachingPoints: Band keratopathy is the deposition of calcium hydroxyapatite in Bowman layer and the superficial stroma, occurring in the interpalpebral zone where CO2 evaporation raises local pH.
Common causes include chronic uveitis (especially JIA-associated), hypercalcemia, chronic eye disease (phthisis bulbi), silicone oil in the eye, and chronic topical medication use.
The Swiss cheese pattern of lucent holes within the band corresponds to corneal nerve penetrations through Bowman layer.
EDTA chelation is a simple office-based procedure that effectively removes the calcium and improves vision and comfort.

clues: A 70-year-old male with a history of cardiovascular disease presents with dull aching pain in his right eye and gradually declining vision over weeks.

Visual acuity is 20/200 OD. Dilated exam shows dilated but non-tortuous retinal veins, dot-and-blot hemorrhages predominantly in the mid-peripheral retina, and mild disc edema. Neovascularization of the disc is present. IOP is low (8 mmHg) compared to the fellow eye (16 mmHg).

Fluorescein angiography shows prolonged arteriovenous transit time (>11 seconds), retinal arterial staining, and choroidal perfusion delay. Carotid Doppler reveals >90% stenosis of the right internal carotid artery.
The patient has intermittent episodes of transient monocular vision loss (amaurosis fugax). He has diabetes, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. He is a smoker.
Carotid endarterectomy or stenting for the carotid stenosis (vascular surgery referral). PRP for ocular neovascularization. Anti-VEGF for macular edema.
teachingPoints: OIS results from chronic ocular hypoperfusion due to severe ipsilateral carotid artery stenosis (>90%); it is bilateral in 20% of cases.
Unlike CRVO, veins in OIS are dilated but not tortuous, hemorrhages are mid-peripheral (not all four quadrants centrally), and IOP is often asymmetrically low due to reduced aqueous production.
NVG develops in approximately 20% of OIS patients; paradoxically, PRP may not be as effective because the ischemia is at the level of choroidal perfusion.
OIS is a marker for severe systemic atherosclerosis; the 5-year mortality rate is approximately 40%, mainly from cardiac and cerebrovascular events.

clues: An 80-year-old female presents with acute pain, redness, and decreased vision in her left eye. She has had progressive bilateral cataracts for years but declined surgery.
IOP is 55 mmHg OS. The left eye has an intumescent (swollen) white cataract with a very shallow anterior chamber. Gonioscopy shows 360-degree appositional closure.
The right eye has a moderate nuclear cataract with a deep anterior chamber and open angles. A-scan shows a thickened lens in the left eye compared to the right.
The patient has not seen an ophthalmologist in 10 years. She has no history of glaucoma. The attack started after she was given dilating drops for an eye exam at her primary care office.
Emergency IOP lowering with medical therapy, then cataract extraction (the definitive treatment). Removal of the intumescent lens resolves the angle closure mechanism.
teachingPoints: Phacomorphic glaucoma is secondary angle-closure glaucoma caused by an intumescent (swollen) lens pushing the iris forward and closing the angle by a pupillary block and non-pupillary block mechanism.

It must be distinguished from phacolytic glaucoma (lens protein leakage through an intact capsule causing open-angle inflammation) and phacoantigenic uveitis (lens protein exposure after capsular disruption).
Cataract extraction is both diagnostic and curative; the angle typically reopens once the swollen lens is removed.
Laser peripheral iridotomy may provide temporary relief of the pupillary block component but definitive treatment is lens removal.

aliases: Nonsyphilitic interstitial keratitis with hearing loss
clues: A 30-year-old female presents with bilateral eye redness, photophobia, and tearing along with sudden-onset vertigo and hearing loss over 2 weeks.
Slit-lamp shows bilateral patchy deep stromal corneal infiltrates without vascularization in the early stage. Mild anterior chamber reaction is present.
Audiometry confirms bilateral sensorineural hearing loss with vestibular dysfunction. FTA-ABS is negative (ruling out syphilis). ESR and CRP are elevated.
The patient reports dizziness, nausea, tinnitus, and ataxia (Ménière-like attacks). She has no history of STIs. Cardiac evaluation reveals aortic valve murmur.

Systemic corticosteroids are first-line. Hearing aids or cochlear implant for hearing loss. Immunosuppressives (methotrexate, cyclophosphamide) for refractory or severe cases with systemic vasculitis.
teachingPoints: Cogan syndrome is a rare autoimmune disease characterized by non-syphilitic interstitial keratitis and vestibulo-auditory dysfunction resembling Ménière disease.
It can be associated with systemic vasculitis, including aortitis and coronary vasculitis, which can be life-threatening.
Early treatment with systemic steroids can prevent permanent hearing loss; delay leads to irreversible cochlear damage.
Distinguishing Cogan from syphilitic IK is essential: negative syphilis serology, vestibular symptoms, and absence of ghost vessels support Cogan syndrome.

clues: A newborn is noted to have a firm, bluish, non-tender swelling below the medial canthus of the right eye present since birth.
A tense, cystic, bluish-gray mass is visible below the medial canthal tendon. The overlying skin is intact without erythema. There is mild epiphora but no discharge.
The mass transilluminates. Dacryocystography is not needed but CT would show a dilated lacrimal sac. Nasal endoscopy reveals an intranasal cyst (nasal component of the dacryocystocele).
The mass was present at birth and has not changed in size. It is not inflamed or infected. The left side is normal.
Endoscopic marsupialization of the intranasal cyst (most important step) combined with probing of the nasolacrimal duct. If infected (dacryocystitis), systemic antibiotics are started first.
teachingPoints: A congenital dacryocystocele results from obstruction at both the valve of Hasner (distal) and the valve of Rosenmuller (proximal), trapping mucus in the lacrimal sac.
Unlike simple CNLDO, a dacryocystocele presents as a visible mass at birth and has both an external and an intranasal component.
The intranasal cyst can cause respiratory distress in neonates (obligate nasal breathers), especially if bilateral.
Secondary infection (dacryocystitis) can develop rapidly and requires urgent systemic antibiotics; the mass becomes erythematous and tender.

clues: A 65-year-old Asian male presents with acute onset of blurred vision and metamorphopsia in his right eye.

Fundoscopy reveals a reddish-orange subretinal lesion in the peripapillary area with a large serous and hemorrhagic pigment epithelial detachment (PED). There is subretinal hemorrhage near the macula.

Indocyanine green (ICG) angiography reveals a branching vascular network terminating in polypoidal dilations (polyps) that appear as hyperfluorescent nodules. OCT shows a peaked (thumb-like) PED with a double-layer sign.
The lesion does not respond well to anti-VEGF monotherapy alone. There are no typical drusen in either eye.
Combination therapy with photodynamic therapy (PDT) plus anti-VEGF intravitreal injection is the most effective treatment for polyp regression (EVEREST II trial).
teachingPoints: PCV is a distinct entity from typical wet AMD, characterized by polypoidal choroidal vascular dilations, predominantly affecting Asian and Black/African-descent populations.
ICG angiography is the gold standard for diagnosis; standard FA alone may miss the polyps.
PCV accounts for approximately 50% of wet AMD-like presentations in Asian populations but only ~8-13% in Caucasian populations.
Massive submacular hemorrhage is a characteristic complication of PCV; pneumatic displacement with intravitreal gas and anti-VEGF can be used in severe cases.

clues: A 55-year-old diabetic male presents with acute horizontal binocular diplopia that is worse when looking to the right.
There is an esotropia in primary position that worsens on right gaze. The right eye cannot abduct past midline. Adduction, vertical movements, and pupils are normal.
Blood glucose is 280 mg/dL. HbA1c is 9.1%. MRI brain is unremarkable. MRA shows no aneurysm.
The patient has poorly controlled type 2 diabetes and hypertension. He denies headache, trauma, or other neurologic symptoms.
Observation for microvascular CN6 palsy with prism glasses for diplopia. Expect recovery within 3-6 months. If no improvement, further workup is needed.
teachingPoints: CN6 palsy is the most common ocular motor nerve palsy; microvascular disease (diabetes, hypertension) is the most common cause in adults over 50.
In children, CN6 palsy can indicate raised intracranial pressure (false localizing sign) or pontine glioma and requires urgent neuroimaging.
Microvascular palsies are self-limiting and resolve within 3-6 months; failure to improve should prompt re-evaluation with neuroimaging.
The abducens nerve has the longest intracranial course, making it vulnerable to injury at multiple sites from the pons to the cavernous sinus to the orbit.

clues: A 35-year-old female presents with recurrent episodes of eye pain and foreign body sensation along with gradually decreasing vision in both eyes since her 20s.
Slit-lamp shows discrete, well-demarcated, white, crumb-like or breadcrumb-shaped opacities in the central anterior stroma bilaterally. The intervening stroma between deposits is clear.
The deposits stain bright red with Masson trichrome stain (hyaline/amyloid-negative). Genetic testing shows a TGFBI gene mutation (R555W on chromosome 5q31).
The patient's father had a similar condition and required a corneal transplant. The peripheral cornea is spared.
PTK (phototherapeutic keratectomy) for superficial deposits causing recurrent erosions. Penetrating or deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty (DALK) for visually significant central opacity.
teachingPoints: Granular corneal dystrophy type 1 is an autosomal dominant condition caused by TGFBI mutations, with hyaline deposits in the corneal stroma.
The deposits are discrete and well-separated with clear intervening stroma, unlike lattice dystrophy (branching lines) or macular dystrophy (diffuse haze).
LASIK is absolutely contraindicated in TGFBI dystrophies as it can cause severe recurrence of deposits in the flap interface.
Recurrence in the graft can occur after keratoplasty, typically starting at the host-graft interface.

clues: A 22-year-old male presents with painless, sequential bilateral central vision loss; the left eye was affected 2 months ago and the right eye started losing vision last week.

Visual acuity is 20/400 OD and counting fingers OS. Color vision is severely impaired bilaterally. Pupils show a bilateral sluggish response with no RAPD (both equally affected). Fundoscopy shows peripapillary telangiectatic microangiopathy and nerve fiber layer swelling without true disc leakage on FA.
OCT shows temporal RNFL thickening (pseudo-edema) followed by thinning. MRI is normal (no optic nerve enhancement, unlike optic neuritis). Visual fields show dense central scotomas.
The patient's maternal uncle lost vision at a similar age. The patient is a social smoker. Mitochondrial DNA testing reveals the m.11778G>A mutation (MT-ND4 gene).

Idebenone (a coenzyme Q10 analog) may improve visual outcomes if started early. Gene therapy (lenadogene nolparvovec) targeting the MT-ND4 mutation is emerging. Avoid tobacco, alcohol, and mitochondrial toxins.
teachingPoints: LHON is a maternally inherited mitochondrial optic neuropathy; the three primary mutations (m.11778, m.3460, m.14484) account for >95% of cases.
The classic fundus finding is peripapillary microangiopathy with pseudo-edema (the disc appears swollen but does NOT leak on fluorescein angiography).
Males are affected 4-5 times more often than females due to incomplete penetrance influenced by hormonal, genetic (X-linked modifier), and environmental factors.
Spontaneous recovery is rare with the 11778 mutation (~4%) but more common with the 14484 mutation (~50%).

clues: An 85-year-old male presents with a painful red right eye and severely decreased vision. He has a longstanding mature white cataract.

IOP is 48 mmHg OD. A dense white mature (morgagnian) cataract is present. The anterior chamber shows a deep chamber with heavy flare and large, refractile particles but no hypopyon. Gonioscopy shows open angles.
Anterior chamber paracentesis reveals macrophage-laden fluid (swollen macrophages containing lens material). No lens capsule rupture is visible.
The patient has refused cataract surgery for years. Vision has been poor for a long time, but the pain is new.
Cataract extraction is the definitive treatment. Medical IOP reduction and anti-inflammatory therapy are bridges to surgery.
teachingPoints: Phacolytic glaucoma is an open-angle glaucoma caused by leakage of high-molecular-weight lens proteins through an intact but permeable hypermature lens capsule, clogging the trabecular meshwork.
It must be distinguished from phacomorphic (closed-angle from swollen lens), phacoantigenic/lens particle (ruptured capsule with inflammatory response), and UGH syndrome.
The hallmark is heavy flare without cells (or with large macrophages) in the setting of an open angle and a mature cataract.
The condition resolves completely after lens extraction; recurrence does not occur once the lens is removed.

clues: A 55-year-old male presents with gradually progressive against-the-rule astigmatism and intermittent eye irritation over several years.

Slit-lamp shows a peripheral corneal thinning groove in the superior cornea with an intact epithelium, fine superficial vascularization crossing the lesion, and a lipid line at the leading central edge. The central cornea is clear.
Corneal topography confirms high against-the-rule astigmatism. Pachymetry of the thinned area measures 200 microns (vs. 550 centrally). No infiltrate or ulceration is present.
The condition is bilateral but asymmetric. The thinning has progressed slowly over years. There is no history of autoimmune disease.
Spectacle or contact lens correction for astigmatism. Surgical options for progressive thinning include crescentic lamellar keratoplasty or peripheral corneal patch grafting.
teachingPoints: Terrien marginal degeneration is a rare, slowly progressive, non-inflammatory peripheral corneal thinning disorder, most common in males in the 4th-5th decades.
The intact epithelium overlying the thinned area and lipid deposits distinguish it from Mooren ulcer (which has an ulcerative epithelial defect and is inflammatory).
Spontaneous perforation is rare but can occur after minor trauma to the thinned cornea.
Against-the-rule astigmatism develops because the superior corneal thinning causes flattening superiorly, steepening the horizontal meridian.

clues: A 4-year-old girl with a known history of oligoarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis is found to have a white, quiet eye with anterior chamber cells on routine screening exam.
Slit-lamp shows 2+ anterior chamber cells and flare bilaterally in white, non-injected eyes. Band keratopathy is developing in the right eye. Posterior synechiae are present bilaterally.
Visual acuity is 20/40 OD and 20/25 OS. The patient is asymptomatic regarding her eyes. ANA is positive. RF is negative.
The child was diagnosed with JIA at age 2 (oligoarticular, ANA-positive). She has not been screened by ophthalmology until now.
Topical corticosteroids (prednisolone acetate) with slow taper. Methotrexate or adalimumab for steroid-sparing immunosuppression. EDTA chelation for band keratopathy.
teachingPoints: JIA-associated uveitis is the most common cause of anterior uveitis in children; it is characteristically chronic, bilateral, insidious (white eye), and non-granulomatous.
Risk factors for uveitis in JIA: oligoarticular subtype, female sex, ANA positivity, young age at onset, and disease duration <4 years.
AAP/AAO guidelines recommend screening every 3 months for high-risk JIA patients (young, ANA+, oligoarticular, recent onset).
Complications if untreated include band keratopathy, posterior synechiae, cataract, glaucoma, CME, and amblyopia—making screening critical.

clues: A 3-month-old infant is brought in with a rapidly growing, bright red mass on the left upper eyelid that appeared at 2 weeks of age.

A soft, compressible, bright-red, lobulated mass is present on the left upper eyelid, partially obstructing the visual axis. The mass increases in size with crying (Valsalva). Induced ptosis and astigmatism are present.
The lesion is superficial (strawberry appearance) with a deeper component visible on orbital MRI, which shows a well-circumscribed, enhancing mass without bony erosion. Doppler shows high flow.
The infant is in the proliferative phase (rapid growth). The visual axis is partially occluded, and cycloplegic refraction shows 3 diopters of astigmatism OD vs. 0.5 OS.
Oral propranolol (first-line systemic treatment) to accelerate involution and reduce amblyogenic risk. Timolol gel 0.5% topically for smaller superficial lesions.
teachingPoints: Infantile capillary hemangiomas are the most common benign orbital tumors of childhood; they follow a pattern of rapid proliferation (first year) then slow involution (ages 1-10).
Amblyopia from visual axis occlusion or induced astigmatism is the primary ophthalmologic concern and the main indication for treatment.
Oral propranolol has revolutionized management, replacing systemic steroids as first-line therapy (Léauté-Labrèze landmark study).
