PHACE syndrome (posterior fossa malformations, hemangioma, arterial anomalies, cardiac defects, eye anomalies) should be considered with large facial hemangiomas.

clues: A 60-year-old male on treatment for pulmonary tuberculosis presents with bilateral progressive blurred vision and difficulty distinguishing red and green colors over the past month.
Visual acuity is 20/80 bilaterally. Color vision is severely impaired (Ishihara 2/14 plates). Pupils are sluggish bilaterally. Optic discs show mild temporal pallor.
Visual field testing reveals bilateral central or cecocentral scotomas. OCT shows thinning of the papillomacular bundle. There is no enhancement on MRI of the optic nerves.
The patient has been on ethambutol (25 mg/kg/day) for 4 months as part of RIPE therapy. He also has renal insufficiency.
Ethambutol must be stopped immediately. Vision may partially recover over weeks to months after cessation, but delayed discontinuation leads to irreversible damage.
teachingPoints: Ethambutol causes a dose-dependent optic neuropathy by chelating zinc and copper, disrupting mitochondrial function in retinal ganglion cells.
Risk factors include dose >15 mg/kg/day, renal insufficiency (reduced clearance), prolonged use, and pre-existing optic nerve disease.
Baseline and monthly visual acuity and color vision testing are recommended for patients on ethambutol.
Red-green dyschromatopsia and central scotomas are the earliest signs; if detected promptly, the damage is often reversible upon drug discontinuation.

aliases: Pilocytic astrocytoma of the optic nerve
clues: A 4-year-old girl with neurofibromatosis type 1 presents with mild proptosis of the left eye and decreased visual acuity discovered during a routine NF1 screening exam.

Visual acuity is 20/80 OS (limited by age-appropriate testing). There is a left RAPD and mild proptosis. Optic disc pallor is present on the left. Multiple café-au-lait spots and axillary freckling are seen on skin exam.
MRI orbits and brain shows fusiform enlargement and kinking of the left optic nerve with a pilocytic astrocytoma pattern (T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, enhancing). The chiasm is not involved.
The child has 8 café-au-lait macules (>5 mm), axillary freckling, and Lisch nodules on slit-lamp exam, confirming NF1.
Observation with serial MRI and visual assessments if vision is stable. Chemotherapy (carboplatin + vincristine) if there is progressive vision loss, chiasmal involvement, or rapid growth.
teachingPoints: Optic pathway gliomas occur in approximately 15-20% of NF1 patients, most commonly in children under age 6.
NF1-associated OPGs have a more indolent course than sporadic OPGs and many remain stable or even regress spontaneously.
Lisch nodules (iris hamartomas) are a hallmark of NF1 found in >90% of adults with the condition; they are benign and do not affect vision.
Radiation therapy is avoided in NF1 patients due to the risk of secondary malignancies and moyamoya syndrome.

clues: A 60-year-old female with long-standing rheumatoid arthritis presents with a painful red right eye and progressive peripheral corneal thinning over 2 weeks.

A crescent-shaped stromal ulcer with an epithelial defect is present at the peripheral cornea (3-5 o'clock). There is adjacent conjunctival injection, a stromal infiltrate, and progressive thinning threatening perforation. Scleritis is also present.
The corneal thinning is progressive. Cultures are negative for infection. Rheumatoid factor is strongly positive. Anti-CCP antibodies are elevated. ESR is 85.
The patient has severe rheumatoid arthritis with subcutaneous nodules and has been on methotrexate. Her disease has been poorly controlled recently.

Systemic immunosuppression escalation is essential (often cyclophosphamide or rituximab for RA-associated PUK). Tissue adhesive or corneal patch grafting for impending perforation. Topical measures alone are insufficient.
teachingPoints: PUK associated with rheumatoid arthritis is a marker of severe systemic vasculitis and carries a significant mortality risk if untreated.
RA patients with PUK have a mortality rate of approximately 25-40% within 10 years if systemic vasculitis is not treated—this is a 'red flag' for life-threatening disease.
PUK must be distinguished from Mooren ulcer (autoimmune but limited to the cornea with no associated systemic vasculitis) and Terrien marginal degeneration (non-ulcerative, non-inflammatory).
Corneal perforation can occur rapidly; tissue adhesive (cyanoacrylate glue) is used as an emergency temporizing measure.

clues: A 45-year-old male presents with metamorphopsia and blurred vision in his right eye. He has no systemic complaints.
Fundoscopy reveals an orange-red, dome-shaped subretinal mass in the posterior pole with overlying subretinal fluid extending to the fovea.

B-scan ultrasound shows a dome-shaped lesion with high internal reflectivity (unlike the low reflectivity of melanoma). ICG angiography shows early hyperfluorescence that persists. OCT shows subretinal fluid over the tumor.
The lesion has no pigmentation, no orange lipofuscin, and no calcification. The patient has no phakomatosis (no Sturge-Weber syndrome).
Photodynamic therapy (PDT) with verteporfin is the preferred treatment for symptomatic circumscribed choroidal hemangiomas. Low-dose external beam radiation and anti-VEGF are alternatives.
teachingPoints: Circumscribed choroidal hemangiomas are benign vascular hamartomas that are typically unilateral and sporadic (no systemic association), unlike diffuse choroidal hemangiomas which occur in Sturge-Weber syndrome.
High internal reflectivity on B-scan is the key distinguishing feature from choroidal melanoma (which has low-medium reflectivity).
PDT effectively induces tumor regression and resolution of subretinal fluid with minimal collateral damage to overlying retina.
Diffuse choroidal hemangiomas in Sturge-Weber syndrome cause a 'tomato-ketchup' fundus and are associated with secondary glaucoma.

clues: A 16-year-old student presents with eyestrain, headaches, and blurred vision when reading for prolonged periods. Symptoms resolve when she stops reading.
Distance visual acuity is 20/20. Near point of convergence is receded to 15 cm (normal <8 cm). Exophoria of 12 prism diopters at near with ortho at distance. Accommodative function is normal.
Negative fusional vergence at near is normal but positive fusional vergence (convergence amplitude) is reduced. Stereoacuity is mildly reduced at near.
Symptoms worsened when school switched to extensive tablet-based learning. She avoids reading and her grades have dropped.
Office-based vergence and accommodative therapy (CITT protocol) is the most effective treatment, superior to home-based pencil push-ups or prism glasses.
teachingPoints: Convergence insufficiency is one of the most common binocular vision disorders, affecting approximately 5% of school-age children.
The CITT (Convergence Insufficiency Treatment Trial) showed that office-based vision therapy was significantly more effective than home exercises, placebo therapy, or pencil push-ups.
Base-in prism glasses can provide symptomatic relief but do not address the underlying vergence deficit.
CI is often misdiagnosed as ADHD or a learning disability; all children with reading difficulties should have a comprehensive binocular vision evaluation.

clues: A 10-year-old boy is brought in with a painless, fleshy growth on the inner corner of his right eye that has been slowly growing for several months.

A pedunculated, lobulated, pink, fleshy mass is present on the caruncle/medial conjunctiva with a papillomatous surface showing finger-like projections. The surface has intrinsic vascularity giving a 'cluster of grapes' appearance.
The lesion has a fibrovascular core with each frond containing a central vascular channel on clinical exam. It does not extend onto the cornea.
The child has had common skin warts on his hands. There is no family history of malignancy.
Observation for possible spontaneous regression in children. Excision with cryotherapy if symptomatic or growing. Topical interferon alpha-2b or mitomycin C for recurrent cases.
teachingPoints: Conjunctival papillomas are benign epithelial tumors most commonly caused by HPV types 6 and 11.
In children, they are often pedunculated and may spontaneously regress. In adults, they can be sessile and have a slightly higher risk of dysplasia.
Recurrence after excision is common (~20%); adjunctive cryotherapy or topical interferon reduces recurrence rates.
Unlike OSSN, conjunctival papillomas are typically in younger patients, pedunculated, and lack leukoplakia or keratin.

clues: A newborn infant is noted to have bilateral central corneal opacities at birth. The parents are non-consanguineous.
Both corneas have central corneal opacities (leukomas) with iris strands adhering to the posterior corneal surface (irido-corneal adhesions). The peripheral cornea is relatively clear.

Anterior segment OCT confirms absence of the central corneal endothelium and Descemet membrane with iridocorneal adhesions. UBM shows the lens may be adherent to the posterior cornea (Type II). IOP is elevated bilaterally.
The infant has micrognathia and cardiac defects on exam. Genetic testing reveals a PAX6 mutation.
Penetrating keratoplasty may be needed to clear the visual axis, but outcomes are guarded. Aggressive amblyopia management is critical. IOP must be monitored for secondary glaucoma.
teachingPoints: Peters anomaly is a form of anterior segment dysgenesis caused by failure of neural crest cell migration, resulting in absence of the central corneal endothelium and Descemet membrane.
Type I: corneal opacity with iridocorneal adhesions. Type II: additionally has keratolenticular adhesions (lens touches the cornea), carrying a worse prognosis.
Peters anomaly may be associated with systemic abnormalities (Peters Plus syndrome): short stature, developmental delay, cleft lip/palate, and cardiac defects.
Corneal transplant success rates in infants are much lower than in adults due to graft failure, amblyopia, and glaucoma; long-term visual prognosis remains guarded.

clues: A 65-year-old female presents with mildly blurred vision and distortion in her right eye that has been gradually worsening for months.
Visual acuity is 20/50 OD. Biomicroscopy shows a taut posterior hyaloid face attached to the fovea pulling it anteriorly.
OCT clearly demonstrates vitreomacular adhesion with tractional distortion of the foveal contour (foveal 'tenting'). Central subfield thickness is increased. There is no full-thickness macular hole.
The attachment footprint is less than 1500 microns (focal VMT). Mild intraretinal cystic changes are developing.
Options include observation (may release spontaneously), intravitreal ocriplasmin (enzymatic vitreolysis) for focal VMT ≤1500 μm, or pars plana vitrectomy for symptomatic progressive cases.
teachingPoints: VMT occurs when an incomplete PVD leaves the vitreous persistently attached to the fovea with anteroposterior traction distorting the macular architecture.
OCT is essential for diagnosis and classification (focal vs. broad; with or without macular hole).
The MIVI-TRUST trials established ocriplasmin as a non-surgical option for focal VMT, with a 26% pharmacologic resolution rate with a single injection.
VMT can progress to full-thickness macular hole formation if left untreated in progressive cases.

clues: A 45-year-old female with type 1 diabetes presents with mildly blurred vision in her right eye. She has background diabetic retinopathy.
Visual acuity is 20/40 OD. The right optic disc is swollen with telangiectatic surface vessels and mild peripapillary hemorrhages. There is no RAPD. The left disc is normal.

Fluorescein angiography shows disc hyperfluorescence and leakage but no evidence of NVD (no early hyperfluorescence in a lacy pattern). OCT shows disc edema with peripapillary NFL thickening. Visual fields show an enlarged blind spot.
ESR, CRP, and blood pressure are normal (ruling out GCA and malignant hypertension). MRI brain is normal. The disc swelling is not consistent with NAION (no altitudinal defect).
Observation is the typical management. Most cases resolve spontaneously over 3-6 months with good visual outcome. Optimizing diabetic control is important.
teachingPoints: Diabetic papillopathy is a benign, self-limiting disc swelling occurring in diabetic patients (type 1 or 2) that must be distinguished from papilledema, NAION, NVD, and GCA.
Visual acuity is typically only mildly reduced, RAPD is absent or minimal, and visual fields show enlarged blind spots (not altitudinal defects).
The key distinction from NVD is the absence of early leaking neovascular vessels on FA; diabetic papillopathy shows only disc leakage.
It is important to differentiate from NAION (which presents with sudden vision loss, RAPD, and altitudinal field defect) since the management and prognosis differ significantly.

aliases: Benign mixed tumor of lacrimal gland
clues: A 40-year-old male presents with slowly progressive painless proptosis and inferionasal globe displacement in his right eye over 1 year.
There is right proptosis with the globe displaced inferomedially (down and in). A firm, non-tender mass is palpable in the superolateral orbit. No limitation of extraocular movements.

CT orbits shows a well-defined, round, encapsulated mass in the lacrimal gland fossa with smooth bony remodeling (fossa enlargement) but no bone destruction. The mass shows heterogeneous contrast enhancement.
The mass has been growing slowly for >12 months. There is no pain (pain would suggest malignancy—adenoid cystic carcinoma). No inflammatory signs.
Complete excision with intact capsule (no biopsy first) via lateral orbitotomy is mandatory. Incisional biopsy risks seeding and recurrence with potential malignant transformation.
teachingPoints: Pleomorphic adenoma is the most common epithelial tumor of the lacrimal gland; it is benign but has a risk of malignant transformation (pleomorphic adenocarcinoma) if incompletely excised or left untreated.
The golden rule: NEVER perform an incisional biopsy of a suspected pleomorphic adenoma; capsule violation leads to tumor seeding, recurrence, and potential malignant transformation.
CT features of smooth bony remodeling (not destruction) with a well-encapsulated mass are characteristic of benign tumors; bone destruction suggests malignancy.
Adenoid cystic carcinoma is the most common malignant lacrimal gland tumor and presents with pain, rapid growth, and bony destruction—a very different clinical picture.

clues: A 55-year-old female on long-term tamoxifen for breast cancer prevention presents for routine ophthalmic screening. She reports no visual symptoms.
Visual acuity is 20/25 bilaterally. Fundoscopy reveals subtle refractile yellow-white crystalline deposits in the inner retinal layers of the parafoveal region bilaterally.
OCT shows hyperreflective deposits in the inner retinal layers and cystoid macular changes. Central subfield thickness is mildly increased.
The patient has been on tamoxifen 20 mg daily for 7 years. Cumulative dose is high (>50 grams).
Discontinuation of tamoxifen in consultation with oncologist. The crystalline deposits may persist, but macular edema can improve after cessation. Anti-VEGF or topical NSAIDs for persistent CME.
teachingPoints: Tamoxifen retinopathy presents with intraretinal crystalline deposits, cystoid macular edema, and punctate retinal pigment epithelial changes, typically at cumulative doses >100g.
Annual ophthalmologic screening is recommended for patients on tamoxifen, including OCT and visual acuity testing.
The crystalline deposits are thought to be products of axonal degeneration in the inner retinal layers (nerve fiber and ganglion cell layers).
Tamoxifen retinopathy is much less common and less severe than hydroxychloroquine toxicity but should still be monitored.

clues: A 20-year-old male presents with floaters and an inferior visual field curtain 2 weeks after being punched in his left eye during a fight.
Dilated exam reveals a retinal dialysis (circumferential retinal tear at the ora serrata) inferotemporally with a shallow retinal detachment extending from the dialysis toward the macula.
B-scan confirms the retinal detachment with a dialysis configuration. The macula is still attached (macula-on RD).
There is a resolving subconjunctival hemorrhage and angle recession on gonioscopy from the blunt trauma. The fellow eye is normal.
Urgent surgical repair: scleral buckle is the preferred technique for retinal dialysis with high success rates (>95% single-operation reattachment). Cryotherapy is applied to the dialysis.
teachingPoints: Retinal dialysis is a circumferential tear at or near the ora serrata; it is the most common form of retinal break caused by blunt ocular trauma.
The inferotemporal quadrant is most commonly affected in traumatic dialysis; superonasal dialysis is more often idiopathic (non-traumatic).
Scleral buckle has an excellent success rate for dialysis-associated RD because the tear configuration is well-supported by the buckle.
These patients are typically young and may have associated traumatic findings: hyphema, angle recession, iridodialysis, lens subluxation, and commotio retinae.

clues: A 22-year-old female presents with decreased central vision in her right eye after being struck by a tennis ball yesterday.

Visual acuity is 20/60 OD. Fundoscopy reveals a grayish-white discoloration of the retina at the posterior pole (commotio retinae), centered at the fovea. A cherry-red spot-like appearance may be seen at the fovea.

OCT shows disruption and hyperreflectivity of the outer retinal layers (photoreceptor inner segment/outer segment junction) with preservation of the inner retinal layers. There is no subretinal fluid or hemorrhage.
The injury was from blunt trauma. No globe rupture. IOP is normal. There is no hyphema or retinal break.
Observation only—commotio retinae typically resolves spontaneously over days to weeks. Visual prognosis depends on the degree of photoreceptor damage.
teachingPoints: Commotio retinae (Berlin edema) is a contusion injury of the outer retinal layers caused by blunt trauma, resulting in photoreceptor disruption.
Despite its name, it is not true edema—the whitening represents disruption and fragmentation of photoreceptor outer segments.
When limited to the macula, it is called 'commotio maculae' and carries a guarded visual prognosis if the foveal photoreceptors are severely disrupted.
Peripheral commotio (away from the fovea) has an excellent prognosis, while foveal involvement may result in permanent mild vision loss.

clues: A 35-year-old male presents with recurrent episodes of mild unilateral blurred vision, colored halos, and dull eye ache in his left eye, each lasting a few days.
IOP is 45 mmHg OS during the episode (15 mmHg OD). Slit-lamp shows 1+ fine keratic precipitates (often a single large KP), mild anterior chamber cells (trace to 1+), and open angles bilaterally.
The cornea is mildly edematous from the IOP spike. There is no posterior involvement. Between episodes, the eye is completely normal with normal IOP.
The patient has had 5-6 similar self-limited episodes over 3 years, always in the same eye. CMV testing of aqueous humor may be considered.
Topical IOP-lowering agents (timolol, apraclonidine) and short course of topical steroids during episodes. Prophylactic topical NSAIDs or oral valacyclovir may reduce recurrence frequency.
teachingPoints: Posner-Schlossman syndrome is characterized by recurrent episodes of unilateral mild anterior uveitis with markedly elevated IOP out of proportion to the degree of inflammation.
The IOP elevation is thought to result from trabeculitis (inflammation of the trabecular meshwork) and possibly associated with CMV.
Recent studies have found CMV DNA in aqueous humor in many PSS patients; topical ganciclovir or oral valganciclovir may reduce recurrences in CMV-positive cases.
Between attacks, patients have normal IOP and no inflammation; however, cumulative damage to the trabecular meshwork can cause chronic IOP elevation over years.

clues: A 40-year-old female presents with acute onset of unilateral right eye pain, swelling, redness, and diplopia over 3 days.
There is right proptosis, periorbital swelling, chemosis, restricted extraocular movements (particularly in elevation), and mild disc edema. The pain is severe.

CT orbits shows diffuse enhancement and enlargement of the right lateral rectus muscle including the tendon (tendon involvement distinguishes this from thyroid eye disease). There is lacrimal gland enlargement and fat stranding.
Thyroid function tests are normal. ESR and CRP are elevated. WBC is normal. The condition is unilateral.
Dramatic response to systemic corticosteroids within 24-48 hours is characteristic and supports the diagnosis. Biopsy if atypical or steroid-resistant to rule out lymphoma.
teachingPoints: IOID (orbital pseudotumor) is a diagnosis of exclusion—a non-specific inflammatory condition of the orbit with no identifiable local or systemic cause.
Involvement of the tendon insertion of the extraocular muscles is a key distinguishing feature from thyroid eye disease (which shows tendon sparing).
The dramatic response to corticosteroids within 24-48 hours is so characteristic that failure to improve should prompt biopsy to rule out lymphoma or other pathology.
IgG4-related disease should be considered when orbital inflammation is bilateral, involves the lacrimal glands, or is recurrent; serum IgG4 and tissue biopsy can confirm.

clues: An 8-year-old boy with intellectual disability presents with bilateral poor vision. He is tall and thin with a marfanoid body habitus.
Slit-lamp exam reveals bilateral inferonasally dislocated crystalline lenses. Iridodonesis is present. There is high myopia in both eyes.
The lens dislocation is inferonasal (down and in), as opposed to the superotemporal subluxation seen in Marfan syndrome. Urine amino acid analysis shows elevated homocysteine and methionine levels.
The child has a malar flush, developmental delay, and a history of a thromboembolic event (DVT at age 6). Genetic testing confirms cystathionine beta-synthase deficiency.
Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) supplementation for pyridoxine-responsive forms. Lens surgery if needed with extreme caution (high thromboembolic risk—avoid general anesthesia if possible).
teachingPoints: Homocystinuria (cystathionine beta-synthase deficiency) is an autosomal recessive disorder causing ectopia lentis in ~90% of patients, with a characteristically inferonasal direction.
The thromboembolic risk is the most dangerous feature; general anesthesia significantly increases this risk. Nitrous oxide is specifically contraindicated.
Marfan syndrome has superotemporal lens subluxation; homocystinuria has inferonasal dislocation—the direction of subluxation is a critical distinguishing feature.
Approximately 50% of patients respond to pyridoxine (vitamin B6); non-responders require dietary methionine restriction and betaine supplementation.

clues: An 18-year-old male presents with bilateral central scotomas and blurred vision 24 hours after watching a solar eclipse without proper eye protection.
Visual acuity is 20/80 bilaterally. Fundoscopy reveals small, well-defined, yellow-white foveal lesions bilaterally.
OCT shows a focal disruption of the outer retinal layers (ellipsoid zone and interdigitation zone) at the foveal center with a small sub-foveal hyporeflective cavitation.
The patient looked directly at the eclipse for approximately 30 seconds without eclipse glasses. Friends who wore proper filters are unaffected.
No proven treatment exists. Observation is the standard approach. Most patients recover to 20/20-20/40 over weeks to months, but a small permanent scotoma may persist.
teachingPoints: Solar retinopathy is a photochemical injury (not thermal) to the foveal photoreceptors and RPE from concentrated solar radiation.
Even brief unprotected viewing of a solar eclipse can cause permanent foveal damage; normal pupillary constriction is insufficient protection.
OCT findings (outer retinal disruption, subfoveal cavitation) may persist long-term even when visual acuity recovers.
This condition also occurs from staring at the sun during religious rituals, drug-induced sun gazing, and military laser exposure.

clues: A 5-year-old girl is noticed to have difficulty looking up and to the left with her right eye. Her parents report this has been present since infancy.
There is limited elevation in adduction of the right eye. In primary position, there is mild hypotropia of the right eye. Forced duction test shows restriction in elevation in adduction.
The right eye can elevate normally in abduction but not in adduction (inability to elevate the adducted eye). There is a V-pattern. No superior oblique overaction.
The condition has been present since birth and is congenital. She occasionally tilts her chin up to compensate.

Observation if mild with no significant strabismus in primary position or abnormal head posture. Surgery (SO tendon lengthening, spacer, or tenotomy) for significant hypotropia in primary gaze or a compensatory chin-up position.
teachingPoints: Brown syndrome is the inability to elevate the eye in adduction due to restriction of the superior oblique tendon-trochlea complex (not a muscle overaction).
The key clinical test is positive forced duction in elevation in adduction, confirming a mechanical restriction rather than a paresis.
Acquired Brown syndrome can occur from trochlear inflammation (RA, sinusitis, trauma) and may be intermittent with a palpable click at the trochlea.
Congenital Brown syndrome is often bilateral (10%), and mild cases may improve spontaneously during childhood.

clues: A 45-year-old male with a history of a systemic connective tissue disease presents with acute metamorphopsia and decreased vision in his right eye.

Fundoscopy reveals bilateral irregular, dark reddish-brown lines radiating from the optic disc in a spoke-like pattern (angioid streaks). A small gray-green subretinal lesion (CNV) is present adjacent to a streak near the fovea.
FA shows early hyperfluorescence of the CNV with late leakage. OCT confirms subretinal fluid and a hyperreflective lesion (Type 2 CNV). The streaks show window defects on FA.
The patient has yellowish papular skin lesions on the neck (plucked-chicken skin appearance). Skin biopsy shows fragmented and calcified elastic fibers—consistent with pseudoxanthoma elasticum.
Anti-VEGF intravitreal injections for the CNV complication. Protective eyewear to prevent ocular trauma (risk of choroidal rupture due to brittle Bruch membrane).
teachingPoints: Angioid streaks are crack-like breaks in a calcified and brittle Bruch membrane; the mnemonic PEPSI lists associated conditions: Pseudoxanthoma elasticum, Ehlers-Danlos, Paget disease, Sickle cell, Idiopathic.
PXE is the most common systemic association, caused by ABCC6 gene mutations affecting elastic tissue throughout the body.
CNV is the most sight-threatening complication, developing in up to 70-86% of eyes with angioid streaks over time.
Patients should avoid contact sports and activities with risk of blunt ocular trauma, as the brittle Bruch membrane is susceptible to traumatic choroidal rupture.

clues: A 50-year-old Caucasian female presents with bilateral floaters, blurred vision, nyctalopia, and color vision changes gradually worsening over several months.

Fundoscopy reveals bilateral multiple small, ovoid, cream-colored choroidal lesions scattered in a birdshot pattern radiating from the optic disc. There is mild vitritis bilaterally and retinal vasculitis.

FA shows diffuse retinal vascular leakage and disc hyperfluorescence. ICG angiography reveals many more hypofluorescent dark spots than are clinically visible (choroidal involvement). ERG shows severely reduced rod and cone function. HLA-A29 is positive.
The patient is a middle-aged Caucasian woman. There is no granulomatous anterior uveitis. The lesions do not correspond to typical laser scars or drusen.
Systemic immunosuppression with mycophenolate mofetil or cyclosporine. Corticosteroids for acute flares but inadequate as monotherapy. Long-term steroid-sparing therapy is required.
teachingPoints: Birdshot chorioretinopathy has the strongest HLA association of any uveitic disease (HLA-A29 positive in >95% of cases).
It almost exclusively affects middle-aged Caucasians and presents with a distinctive pattern of cream-colored choroidal lesions.
ERG is the most important functional test for monitoring disease activity and progression, as it detects retinal dysfunction before visual acuity loss.

Unlike most other posterior uveitic conditions, birdshot rarely causes CME as its primary complication; instead, progressive retinal dysfunction (nyctalopia, visual field loss, color vision changes) predominates.

clues: A 30-year-old male with known advanced keratoconus presents with sudden onset of severely blurred vision, pain, and tearing in his right eye.
The right cornea is markedly edematous and opaque with a visible break in Descemet membrane. Stromal and epithelial edema are severe. Visual acuity is hand motions.
OCT of the anterior segment shows a gap in Descemet membrane with massive stromal swelling and fluid-filled intrastromal clefts. The cone is very steep.
The patient has a history of vigorous eye rubbing. His keratoconus was previously managed with rigid gas-permeable contact lenses.

Hypertonic saline drops, cycloplegics for comfort. Intracameral air or SF6 injection can hasten resolution by tamponading the Descemet break. Corneal transplant once the edema resolves if scarring precludes contact lens use.
teachingPoints: Acute hydrops occurs when Descemet membrane ruptures in advanced keratoconus, allowing aqueous humor to flood into the corneal stroma.
Although painful and dramatic, acute hydrops often results in a flatter cornea after resolution (due to scarring), which can paradoxically improve contact lens fitting.
Resolution typically takes 6-10 weeks. Intracameral air/gas injection is increasingly used to speed recovery.
Hydrops is most common in advanced keratoconus and keratoglobus; Down syndrome patients with keratoconus have a particularly high risk.

clues: A 35-year-old female presents with horizontal diplopia and an odd sensation when looking to the sides. She reports a history of a transient neurological event 2 years ago.
On right gaze, the left eye fails to adduct (does not cross midline) and the right eye shows abduction nystagmus. Convergence is preserved (the left eye can adduct on convergence).
MRI brain reveals a demyelinating plaque in the left medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF) in the dorsal pons. Multiple periventricular white matter lesions are present.
The patient had an episode of optic neuritis 2 years ago. CSF shows oligoclonal bands. She meets McDonald criteria for multiple sclerosis.
Treatment of underlying MS with disease-modifying therapy (e.g., natalizumab, ocrelizumab). Acute INO from an MS flare may respond to IV methylprednisolone.
teachingPoints: INO results from a lesion in the medial longitudinal fasciculus, which connects CN6 nucleus to the contralateral CN3 nucleus for coordinated horizontal eye movements.
In young adults, MS is the most common cause (often bilateral INO, 'WEBINO'—wall-eyed bilateral INO). In elderly, stroke is the most common cause (typically unilateral).
Preserved convergence distinguishes INO from CN3 palsy, as convergence uses a separate supranuclear pathway that bypasses the MLF.
Bilateral INO in a young patient is highly suggestive of multiple sclerosis and should prompt MRI and MS workup.

clues: A 40-year-old male with a known history of IV drug use presents with fever, malaise, and mildly decreased vision bilaterally.
Dilated fundus exam reveals multiple flame-shaped and dot retinal hemorrhages with white or pale centers (Roth spots) scattered throughout both fundi.
Blood cultures grow Streptococcus viridans. Echocardiography shows a vegetation on the mitral valve consistent with infective endocarditis. CBC shows anemia.
The patient has splinter hemorrhages under his fingernails, Janeway lesions on his palms, and Osler nodes on his fingertips.
Treat the underlying endocarditis with IV antibiotics. The Roth spots resolve as the systemic condition is treated. No specific ocular treatment is needed.
teachingPoints: Roth spots (white-centered retinal hemorrhages) are classically associated with bacterial endocarditis but are non-specific; they can occur in leukemia, anemia, diabetes, and other conditions.
The white center represents fibrin-platelet aggregates at the site of a septic embolus or localized ischemia within the hemorrhage.
Finding Roth spots on a dilated fundus exam can be an important clue to the diagnosis of subacute bacterial endocarditis.
Any patient with Roth spots and unexplained fever should have blood cultures and echocardiography to evaluate for endocarditis.

clues: A 25-year-old Asian female presents for evaluation of a lifelong bluish-gray discoloration of her left periorbital skin and sclera.

There is a flat, patchy, blue-gray hyperpigmentation of the periorbital skin in the V1 and V2 distribution of the left side. The left sclera/episclera shows slate-gray pigmentation. The ipsilateral iris is darker than the right (iris heterochromia).
Gonioscopy shows heavy pigmentation of the angle. Fundoscopy reveals a diffusely dark choroid. IOP is normal bilaterally.
The pigmentation has been present since birth and involves the skin, episclera, uveal tract, and orbit unilaterally.
Regular monitoring for glaucoma (pigment-related), uveal melanoma (choroidal and iris), and orbital melanoma. Cosmetic skin treatment with Q-switched laser if desired.
teachingPoints: Nevus of Ota is congenital melanocytic hyperpigmentation of tissues innervated by the first and second divisions of the trigeminal nerve, most common in Asian populations.
The lifetime risk of uveal melanoma is increased (estimated 1 in 400 in Caucasians with oculodermal melanocytosis); annual dilated fundus exams are recommended.
Glaucoma risk is increased due to melanocyte infiltration of the trabecular meshwork.
Unlike a conjunctival nevus (which moves with the conjunctiva), episcleral melanocytosis does NOT move with the conjunctiva, as the pigment is in the deeper episcleral/scleral tissue.

clues: A 55-year-old male who had a penetrating keratoplasty 6 months ago presents with progressive redness, photophobia, and blurred vision in the grafted eye over the past 5 days.
Slit-lamp shows ciliary injection, stromal edema of the graft, fine keratic precipitates arranged in a linear pattern (Khodadoust line) on the graft endothelium, and anterior chamber cells.

The Khodadoust rejection line is progressing from the graft-host junction toward the center of the graft. Specular microscopy shows endothelial cell loss in the affected zone. Corneal thickness is increased.
The patient had recently stopped his topical steroid (prednisolone acetate) taper prematurely on his own. He missed his last follow-up appointment.
Urgent intensive topical corticosteroids (prednisolone acetate 1% every hour). Periocular or systemic steroids for severe cases. Do not delay treatment.
teachingPoints: Endothelial graft rejection is the leading cause of corneal graft failure after penetrating keratoplasty; it can occur months to years after transplant.
The Khodadoust line is a linear array of inflammatory cells on the graft endothelium that represents the advancing front of immunologic rejection.
Early recognition and aggressive treatment can reverse rejection; delayed treatment leads to irreversible endothelial cell loss and graft failure.
DMEK and DSAEK have lower rejection rates than full-thickness PK because less donor tissue (no stroma or epithelium) is transplanted.

clues: A 28-year-old female presents with sudden hearing loss, visual disturbances (scotomas), and confusion with personality changes over the past 2 weeks.

Fundoscopy reveals bilateral branch retinal artery occlusions in multiple arteriolar branches, giving a segmental arteriolar wall hyperfluorescence on FA ('box-carring' pattern). No hemorrhages or exudates.

MRI brain shows characteristic 'snowball' lesions in the corpus callosum (central callosal involvement), which is highly specific. Audiometry confirms low- and mid-frequency sensorineural hearing loss.
The patient is a young woman with the classic clinical triad. She was initially suspected of having multiple sclerosis.
Aggressive immunosuppression early (IV methylprednisolone, IVIG, plasma exchange). Long-term immunosuppressive therapy (mycophenolate, azathioprine) to prevent relapses.
teachingPoints: Susac syndrome is an autoimmune endotheliopathy affecting the microvasculature of the retina, cochlea, and brain, causing the classic triad of branch retinal artery occlusions, hearing loss, and encephalopathy.
Central corpus callosum lesions on MRI ('snowball lesions') are highly characteristic and distinguish Susac from MS (which causes periventricular and peripheral callosal lesions).
The complete triad is not always present simultaneously, making early diagnosis challenging; each component may appear weeks to months apart.
Branch retinal artery occlusions in a young patient without cardiovascular risk factors should prompt consideration of Susac syndrome, especially if neurologic or audiologic symptoms are present.

clues: A 30-year-old asymptomatic female is found to have bilateral posterior corneal abnormalities during a routine eye exam.

Slit-lamp shows bilateral vesicular lesions (small blisters), geographic (band-like) opacities, and railway-track-like lines on the posterior corneal surface at the level of Descemet membrane. The cornea is clear overall.
Specular microscopy shows abnormal endothelial cells that appear epithelial-like. Corneal thickness is normal. Gonioscopy reveals broad iridocorneal adhesions in the right eye.
The patient's mother has similar corneal findings. Most patients are asymptomatic. Family screening reveals autosomal dominant inheritance.
Observation for most patients. Monitor for glaucoma (iridocorneal adhesions can cause secondary angle closure). Endothelial keratoplasty only if corneal edema develops, which is rare.
teachingPoints: PPCD is an autosomal dominant corneal dystrophy in which Descemet membrane and endothelial cells are abnormal, with endothelial cells taking on epithelial-like characteristics.
The 'epithelialization' of the endothelium means these cells can proliferate (normal endothelium cannot), which can form membranes over the angle.
Most patients remain asymptomatic throughout life; corneal decompensation requiring surgery is uncommon.
PPCD must be distinguished from ICE syndrome: PPCD is bilateral, hereditary, and usually asymptomatic, while ICE is unilateral, acquired, and progressive.

clues: A 30-year-old male presents with blurred vision in his left eye 1 week after sustaining blunt ocular trauma from a basketball.
Fundoscopy reveals a crescent-shaped, white-yellow subretinal streak concentric to and temporal to the optic disc. There is overlying subretinal hemorrhage clearing around the edges.

OCT shows a full-thickness break in the Bruch membrane-RPE-choriocapillaris complex with overlying retinal disruption. FA shows early hypofluorescence (blocked by hemorrhage) with late hyperfluorescence (window defect from RPE loss).
The injury was from blunt force to the globe. There is no globe rupture. Commotio retinae is also present peripherally.
Observation initially—subretinal hemorrhage usually clears in weeks. Long-term risk of choroidal neovascularization at the rupture site requires monitoring; anti-VEGF if CNV develops.
teachingPoints: Traumatic choroidal rupture is a full-thickness break in Bruch membrane, the choriocapillaris, and the RPE caused by blunt trauma; indirect ruptures (concentric to disc) are more common than direct ruptures.
CNV develops at the rupture site in approximately 15-30% of cases, sometimes years after the original injury.
The crescent-shaped white streak becomes more visible as overlying hemorrhage clears, sometimes discovered incidentally years after trauma.
Subfoveal choroidal rupture carries the worst visual prognosis; peripheral ruptures have minimal visual impact unless CNV develops.

clues: A 70-year-old male presents with a complaint of progressive 'heavy' upper eyelids that obstruct his superior visual field, especially when driving.
Bilateral redundant, overhanging upper eyelid skin that rests on the eyelashes. The skin is thin and non-erythematous. Levator function is normal (>15 mm). The brows are in normal position.

Visual field testing (Humphrey or Goldmann) with the lids taped up shows significant improvement compared to the untaped visual field, demonstrating functional impairment. A superior field cut is present untaped.
The patient has noticed this gradually over years. He frequently raises his eyebrows to see better (frontalis overaction). He has no systemic disease.

Upper eyelid blepharoplasty (removal of excess skin, and possibly orbicularis and orbital fat) is the treatment. Visual field documentation of functional impairment is typically required for insurance coverage.
teachingPoints: Dermatochalasis is an age-related laxity and redundancy of the upper eyelid skin, the most common indication for upper lid blepharoplasty.
It must be distinguished from ptosis (drooping of the eyelid margin itself due to levator dysfunction); both can coexist and both should be addressed.
Functional blepharoplasty requires visual field testing demonstrating superior field obstruction that improves with lid taping.
Blepharoplasty is one of the most common oculoplastic procedures and has high patient satisfaction rates.

clues: A 30-year-old myopic female presents with acute onset of photopsias (shimmering lights) and a scotoma in her left eye over a few days.

Visual acuity is 20/25 OS. Fundoscopy appears largely normal or shows very subtle RPE mottling in the affected zone. A temporal scotoma is present on visual fields that does not correspond to the disc or typical neurologic patterns.

OCT reveals a well-demarcated area of outer retinal (ellipsoid zone) loss temporally. Multifocal ERG shows reduced amplitudes in the affected zone. Fundus autofluorescence shows a hyperautofluorescent line at the border of the lesion (trizonal pattern).
The patient is a young myopic woman who had a flu-like illness 1 week ago. The fundus looks deceptively normal.
No proven treatment. Observation; the disease may stabilize or slowly expand. Immunosuppression has been tried for progressive cases but evidence is limited.
teachingPoints: AZOOR is characterized by acute loss of outer retinal (photoreceptor) function in one or more retinal zones, often with photopsias and visual field defects, but a near-normal fundus appearance.
The trizonal pattern on FAF (inner zone of hypo-AF, middle zone of hyper-AF line, and outer normal zone) is characteristic.
AZOOR is related to the white dot syndrome spectrum and predominantly affects young myopic women.
The diagnosis is often delayed because the fundus can look deceptively normal; OCT and multifocal ERG are essential for detecting and mapping the outer retinal dysfunction.

clues: A premature infant born at 24 weeks gestation weighing 650 grams develops rapidly progressive retinal vascular changes during routine ROP screening at 33 weeks PMA.

Dilated exam shows flat neovascularization at the junction of vascular and avascular retina in Zone I. Plus disease is prominent with severely dilated and tortuous posterior pole vessels. There is no typical ridge.
Retcam photography documents the flat neovascularization and severe plus disease. The disease is progressing rapidly between weekly examinations.
This is aggressive posterior ROP (AP-ROP), a severe variant that does not follow the typical staged progression. The infant required prolonged mechanical ventilation.
Urgent treatment with intravitreal anti-VEGF (bevacizumab) is often preferred for AP-ROP in Zone I due to faster action than laser. Close long-term follow-up is essential for late recurrence.
teachingPoints: AP-ROP is a rapidly progressive form of ROP in Zone I or posterior Zone II that can progress to Stage 5 within days if untreated.
Unlike typical ROP, AP-ROP may not form a classic ridge—the neovascularization is flat and easily missed.
Anti-VEGF has become the treatment of choice for Zone I AP-ROP, but late recurrences (at 50-70 weeks PMA) can occur and require prolonged follow-up.
The RAINBOW trial compared ranibizumab to laser for ROP, supporting anti-VEGF as a primary treatment option for posterior disease.

clues: A 12-year-old girl presents with a unilateral red eye, swollen eyelid, and a large tender preauricular lymph node on the same side for 2 weeks.
The right conjunctiva shows granulomatous follicles and papillae with a small conjunctival granuloma. The ipsilateral preauricular and submandibular lymph nodes are significantly enlarged and tender.
Bartonella henselae IgG titer is markedly elevated (1:512). Fundoscopy shows a swollen optic disc with a macular star of hard exudates (neuroretinitis).
The patient has a new kitten at home that scratched her face near the right eye 3 weeks ago.
Oral azithromycin or doxycycline for 2-4 weeks. The neuroretinitis and adenopathy typically resolve with antibiotics. Most cases are self-limited in immunocompetent patients.
teachingPoints: Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome is the combination of granulomatous conjunctivitis with ipsilateral regional lymphadenopathy; cat-scratch disease (Bartonella henselae) is the most common cause.
Neuroretinitis (disc edema + macular star) is a classic ocular complication of cat-scratch disease and should prompt serologic testing.
Other causes of Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome include tularemia, sporotrichosis, tuberculosis, and syphilis.
The macular star forms over 1-2 weeks as lipid exudates migrate to the outer plexiform layer of the macula in a radial pattern.

clues: A 35-year-old male presents with blurred vision in his right eye. He has been using topical prednisolone acetate drops for 6 months for anterior uveitis.
IOP is 38 mmHg OD (was 14 mmHg before starting steroids). Cup-to-disc ratio has increased from 0.3 to 0.5 OD. Visual acuity is 20/30 OD.
OCT RNFL shows new thinning of the inferior temporal sector OD. Humphrey visual field shows an early superior nasal step. Gonioscopy shows open angles.
The patient has been on high-dose topical steroids without IOP monitoring. He has a family history of glaucoma.

Taper and discontinue the topical steroid (switch to a lower-potency steroid or non-steroidal anti-inflammatory). Add IOP-lowering drops. If IOP does not normalize, steroid-induced damage may be permanent.
teachingPoints: Approximately 30-40% of the general population are 'steroid responders' who develop clinically significant IOP elevation from corticosteroid use.
All routes of steroid administration can cause IOP elevation: topical (most common and potent), periocular, intravitreal, inhaled, intranasal, and even topical skin application near the eyes.
Risk factors for steroid response include POAG or family history of glaucoma, high myopia, type 1 diabetes, and young age.
IOP monitoring should be performed at 2-4 weeks after starting any ocular or periocular corticosteroid therapy, and regularly thereafter.

diagnosis: Optic Disc Melanocytoma with Malignant Transformation
clues: A 60-year-old male with a previously stable optic disc melanocytoma returns with decreased vision and a documented increase in lesion size over 6 months.

The jet-black disc lesion has enlarged significantly, now extending 3 mm into the vitreous. There is prominent disc edema, peripapillary hemorrhages, and an RAPD. Visual acuity has dropped from 20/25 to 20/200.

B-scan shows interval growth of the mass with lower internal reflectivity than before (suggesting melanoma). MRI shows enhancement. Fluorescein angiography shows blockage centrally with surrounding hyperfluorescence.
Serial photographs confirm documented growth. The lesion was previously stable for 10 years. There is no prior history of malignancy.
Enucleation or plaque brachytherapy depending on size. Systemic metastatic workup including liver imaging.
teachingPoints: Malignant transformation of optic disc melanocytoma to melanoma occurs in <1-2% of cases but must be suspected with documented growth, vision loss, or tumor necrosis.
Any documented growth of a melanocytoma should raise concern for malignant transformation; serial photography is essential for monitoring.
Spontaneous necrosis of melanocytoma can mimic tumor growth with acute disc edema and hemorrhage but usually stabilizes.
Fine needle aspiration biopsy can help confirm malignant transformation by demonstrating melanoma cells, though the location on the optic disc makes this challenging.

clues: A 25-year-old male presents with mildly decreased vision in his left eye. His father died from a renal cell carcinoma at age 40.
Dilated exam reveals a small, orange-red, globular retinal lesion in the superotemporal periphery with a dilated, tortuous feeder artery and draining vein. There is surrounding subretinal exudation.

FA shows rapid filling and intense hyperfluorescence of the tumor in the early arterial phase with late leakage. OCT shows subretinal fluid extending toward the macula. Genetic testing reveals a VHL gene mutation.
MRI brain shows a cerebellar hemangioblastoma. Abdominal CT shows bilateral renal cysts. His father had the same gene mutation.

Laser photocoagulation or cryotherapy for small peripheral tumors. Anti-VEGF or PDT for larger or juxtapapillary tumors. Lifelong systemic surveillance for CNS hemangioblastomas, renal cell carcinoma, and pheochromocytoma.
teachingPoints: Von Hippel-Lindau disease is an autosomal dominant tumor predisposition syndrome caused by VHL gene mutations on chromosome 3p25; retinal hemangioblastomas are the most common and often earliest manifestation.
Retinal hemangioblastomas can cause vision loss from exudative retinal detachment, macular edema, and vitreous hemorrhage if untreated.
Systemic tumors include cerebellar and spinal hemangioblastomas, renal cell carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, and pancreatic cysts/tumors.
Annual dilated fundus exam, MRI brain/spine, and abdominal imaging are recommended for VHL patients starting in childhood.

clues: An 85-year-old woman from a rural area presents with years of blindness in her right eye. She finally seeks care due to pain and redness.

The right eye has a white, liquefied cortex with a brown nuclear fragment sinking to the inferior portion of the lens capsule. IOP is 45 mmHg. Shallow anterior chamber and phacolytic inflammation with heavy flare.
B-scan shows no posterior segment abnormality. The lens shows a dense inferior opacity (sunken nucleus) with liquefied superior cortex.
The patient has had no eye care for over 20 years. The left eye has a dense nuclear sclerotic cataract with VA of counting fingers.
Emergency IOP management followed by cataract extraction. Special surgical considerations include capsular fragility and the need to manage the free-floating nucleus.
teachingPoints: A Morgagnian cataract is a hypermature cataract in which the cortex has completely liquefied and the dense nucleus sinks inferiorly within the capsular bag.
It can cause phacolytic glaucoma (lens protein leakage through the intact capsule) or phacomorphic glaucoma (lens swelling).
Surgery is technically more challenging due to weak capsule, poor visualization, and the risk of capsular rupture with nuclear drop.
This presentation is common in developing countries where access to cataract surgery is limited.

clues: A 7-year-old child with known tuberous sclerosis complex presents for routine ophthalmologic screening.

Dilated exam reveals a white, mulberry-like, calcified, elevated retinal lesion at the optic disc margin in the right eye. A flat, translucent, non-calcified lesion is also present in the peripheral retina of the left eye.

B-scan shows calcification within the disc-adjacent lesion. OCT demonstrates a hyperreflective, elevated retinal mass arising from the nerve fiber layer. FA shows autofluorescence of the calcified hamartoma.
The child has facial angiofibromas, hypomelanotic macules (ash-leaf spots), and a subependymal giant cell astrocytoma on brain MRI. TSC2 gene mutation is confirmed.
Observation; retinal astrocytic hamartomas in TSC are typically stable and rarely require treatment unless causing exudation or vitreous hemorrhage (rare 'aggressive' form).
teachingPoints: Retinal astrocytic hamartomas occur in approximately 50% of patients with tuberous sclerosis complex and are usually bilateral and multifocal.
Three morphologic types: flat/translucent (most common), elevated/calcified (mulberry), and transitional (features of both).
Rarely, aggressive retinal astrocytic hamartomas can enlarge rapidly with exudation and hemorrhage; these require treatment with anti-VEGF, PDT, or cryotherapy.
TSC is an autosomal dominant condition caused by TSC1 (hamartin) or TSC2 (tuberin) mutations affecting the mTOR signaling pathway.

clues: A 20-year-old college student presents with acute onset bilateral red eyes with subconjunctival hemorrhages and lid swelling during an outbreak on campus.
Bilateral conjunctival injection with prominent subconjunctival hemorrhages, follicular response, lid edema, and serous discharge. Preauricular lymphadenopathy is present.
The hemorrhages are bilateral and extensive, covering much of the bulbar conjunctiva. Cornea is clear. No anterior chamber reaction.
Multiple students in the dormitory have developed similar symptoms within 24-48 hours of each other. The incubation period is very short (8-48 hours).
Supportive care: cold compresses, artificial tears. Strict infection control measures. The condition is self-limited, resolving in 1-2 weeks.
teachingPoints: Epidemic hemorrhagic conjunctivitis is caused by enterovirus 70 or coxsackievirus A24 variant, characterized by an extremely short incubation period and explosive outbreaks.
The hallmark feature distinguishing it from adenoviral conjunctivitis is the prominent subconjunctival hemorrhage.
It is highly contagious with attack rates of 50-80% in close contacts; hand hygiene is the most important preventive measure.
Rarely, enterovirus 70 can cause a polio-like acute hemorrhagic conjunctivitis-associated radiculomyelitis.

clues: A 35-year-old highly myopic female (-12.00 D) presents with acute central distortion and decreased vision in her left eye.

Fundoscopy reveals a tilted, myopic disc with peripapillary atrophy, posterior staphyloma, and lacquer cracks (breaks in Bruch membrane). A small gray-green subretinal lesion is present at the fovea with surrounding hemorrhage.
OCT shows a Type 2 CNV (above the RPE) with subretinal fluid and hemorrhage. The retina is thin. FA shows a well-defined small CNV with leakage. Axial length is 29 mm.
The patient has progressive high myopia since childhood. She has a family history of pathologic myopia.
Anti-VEGF intravitreal injection (aflibercept or ranibizumab) as needed. The RADIANCE and MYRROR trials demonstrated efficacy of anti-VEGF for myopic CNV. Fewer injections needed than in AMD.
teachingPoints: Myopic CNV is the most common cause of CNV in patients under age 50 and a leading cause of visual impairment in highly myopic eyes.
Lacquer cracks (mechanical breaks in the Bruch membrane-RPE complex) are the sites where CNV most commonly develops in pathologic myopia.
Unlike AMD-related CNV, myopic CNV responds well to anti-VEGF with fewer injections required (PRN dosing is typically effective).
Myopic macular degeneration also includes macular atrophy, Fuchs spot (pigmented scar from regressed CNV), and posterior staphyloma formation.

clues: A 28-year-old male presents with a misshapen pupil, glare, and monocular diplopia after sustaining blunt trauma to his left eye 1 month ago.

The left pupil has a D-shaped (polycoria-like) configuration. There is a visible detachment of the iris root from the ciliary body in the inferotemporal quadrant (iridodialysis). The underlying ciliary body and lens equator are visible through the defect.
Gonioscopy shows angle recession in the area of the dialysis with cyclodialysis. IOP is 10 mmHg OS (lower than fellow eye due to increased uveoscleral outflow through the cyclodialysis).
The trauma was from a fist strike. There was an associated hyphema that has since resolved. The patient has glare and photophobia from the iris defect.
Observation if asymptomatic. A painted contact lens or prosthetic iris implant for glare. Surgical iridodialysis repair (suture to the scleral spur) for large defects causing significant symptoms.
teachingPoints: Iridodialysis is a traumatic dehiscence of the iris root from the ciliary body, allowing light to enter through the peripheral defect causing glare and monocular diplopia.
It is commonly associated with other blunt trauma findings: angle recession, hyphema, cyclodialysis, lens subluxation, and retinal dialysis.
Long-term monitoring for glaucoma is essential, as angle recession glaucoma can develop years after trauma in up to 10% of patients.
Cyclodialysis (separation of the ciliary body from the scleral spur) can cause chronic hypotony if significant.

clues: A 15-year-old girl presents with mild bilateral eye irritation and photophobia that has been present since early childhood.
Slit-lamp shows innumerable tiny, discrete, round, clear-to-translucent microcysts within the corneal epithelium bilaterally, extending from limbus to limbus. They are best seen on retroillumination.
The microcysts stain faintly with fluorescein when they rupture on the surface. Visual acuity is 20/25 bilaterally. Corneal thickness is normal.
The patient's mother has similar corneal findings. Genetic testing shows a KRT3 or KRT12 mutation (keratin gene).
Lubrication with artificial tears. PTK (phototherapeutic keratectomy) for recurrent erosions or significant visual impairment. Recurrence after PTK is common.
teachingPoints: Meesmann corneal dystrophy is an autosomal dominant epithelial dystrophy caused by mutations in corneal-specific keratin genes (KRT3 or KRT12).
The microcysts contain a peculiar substance ('peculiar substance' on electron microscopy) that represents abnormal cytoplasmic material from dysfunctional epithelial cells.
Despite the dramatic appearance of innumerable cysts, most patients maintain good vision and the condition is generally mild.
Recurrent corneal erosions can occur and are the primary source of symptoms.

clues: A 35-year-old HIV-positive male presents with a bright red, vascular subconjunctival mass in his right eye that has been growing over weeks.
A bright-red to violaceous, highly vascular, flat-to-slightly-elevated subconjunctival lesion is present in the inferior fornix. It resembles a subconjunctival hemorrhage but does not resolve.
Biopsy shows spindle-shaped cells forming vascular channels with extravasated red blood cells. HHV-8 immunostaining is positive. CD4 count is 120.
The patient has cutaneous Kaposi sarcoma lesions on his legs and hard palate. He is on suboptimal antiretroviral therapy.
Optimization of ART is the first priority. For localized conjunctival KS: excision, cryotherapy, or local radiation. Intralesional or systemic chemotherapy for extensive disease.
teachingPoints: Conjunctival Kaposi sarcoma is associated with HHV-8 and advanced HIV/AIDS; it is the most common conjunctival malignancy in HIV-positive patients in endemic areas.
It can masquerade as a subconjunctival hemorrhage, chalazion, or pyogenic granuloma—biopsy is essential for any persistent or atypical vascular conjunctival lesion in HIV patients.
Immune reconstitution with effective ART can cause regression of KS lesions.
Ocular KS can also involve the eyelids and orbit; eyelid lesions appear as violaceous nodules.

clues: A 35-year-old myopic female presents with bilateral photopsias, floaters, and paracentral scotomas gradually worsening over months.

Fundoscopy reveals bilateral multiple small (50-200 μm), round, yellow-white, punched-out chorioretinal lesions scattered in the posterior pole and mid-periphery. There is mild vitritis and anterior chamber cells bilaterally.
FA shows early hypofluorescence and late staining/hyperfluorescence of the lesions. OCT shows sub-RPE deposits and focal disruptions of the outer retina. Some lesions show peripapillary scarring.
The patient is a young, myopic Caucasian woman. She has developed a CNV at the fovea adjacent to one of the scars. Sarcoidosis and TB workup are negative.
Anti-VEGF for the CNV complication. Systemic immunosuppression (mycophenolate, corticosteroids) for active inflammation causing vision-threatening lesions.
teachingPoints: Multifocal choroiditis with panuveitis is a chronic inflammatory condition predominantly affecting young myopic women, with a high risk of CNV development.
CNV is the most common cause of severe vision loss in MCP, occurring in up to 30% of patients, often arising at the edge of chorioretinal scars.
MCP is part of the 'white dot syndrome' spectrum but is distinguished by its chronic, bilateral, recurrent nature with vitritis.
Differentiating MCP from ocular histoplasmosis (POHS) can be challenging; the presence of vitritis favors MCP (POHS has no vitritis by definition).

clues: A 40-year-old male from the Ohio River Valley presents with new metamorphopsia and a small central scotoma in his right eye.

Fundoscopy reveals bilateral small, discrete, atrophic 'histo spots' (punched-out chorioretinal scars) scattered in the mid-periphery. There is peripapillary atrophy bilaterally. A gray-green subretinal membrane is present at the right fovea.
FA shows a classic subfoveal CNV with early hyperfluorescence and late leakage. OCT confirms a Type 2 CNV with subretinal fluid. There is NO vitritis (quiet vitreous is a hallmark).
The patient grew up in the Ohio-Mississippi River Valley (Histoplasma capsulatum endemic region). He has never had symptomatic histoplasmosis.
Anti-VEGF intravitreal injection for the CNV. No need for antifungal treatment as the organism is not actively replicating in the eye.
teachingPoints: POHS is characterized by the classic triad: histo spots (peripheral chorioretinal scars), peripapillary atrophy, and maculopathy (CNV), with absence of vitreous cells.
The absence of vitritis is the defining feature that distinguishes POHS from multifocal choroiditis and other inflammatory conditions.
CNV develops in a small percentage of eyes with histo spots; the risk is highest for lesions close to the fovea.
The MPS (Macular Photocoagulation Study) initially established laser for POHS-related CNV; anti-VEGF has now largely replaced laser as first-line treatment.

clues: A 30-year-old female presents with a mildly uncomfortable, red right eye for 3 days. She notes that it looks worse than it feels.

Sectoral injection of the superficial episcleral vascular plexus in the temporal quadrant. The redness blanches completely with topical phenylephrine 2.5%. There is no tenderness to palpation, no scleral thinning, and no anterior chamber reaction.
Vision is 20/20. IOP is normal. There is no discharge or corneal involvement.
This is her first episode. She has no systemic complaints and no history of autoimmune disease.
Reassurance and artificial tears for mild cases. Topical NSAIDs (ketorolac) for symptomatic relief. Oral NSAIDs for recurrent or nodular episcleritis. Self-limited in 1-2 weeks.
teachingPoints: Episcleritis is a benign, self-limited inflammation of the episclera that is almost always less severe than it appears.
The phenylephrine blanching test distinguishes episcleritis (superficial vessels blanch) from scleritis (deep scleral vessels do NOT blanch).
Most cases are idiopathic; systemic workup is reserved for recurrent or bilateral cases, where associations include rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, and inflammatory bowel disease.
Unlike scleritis, episcleritis does NOT cause destructive complications and does NOT threaten vision.

clues: A 60-year-old female with severe rheumatoid arthritis presents with excruciating, boring eye pain in her right eye that wakes her from sleep. Pain radiates to the temple and jaw.

There is a sector of blue-gray scleral thinning with visible underlying dark uveal tissue. The surrounding sclera is injected with a deep violaceous hue that does NOT blanch with phenylephrine. There is adjacent corneal stromal keratitis.
B-scan shows scleral thinning and edema. The pain does not respond to topical steroids. ESR and CRP are markedly elevated. Rheumatoid factor is strongly positive.
The patient has severe seropositive RA with rheumatoid nodules and vasculitis. She has PUK developing adjacent to the scleral thinning.
Urgent systemic immunosuppression with cyclophosphamide or rituximab. Systemic corticosteroids as a bridge. Scleral patch grafting if perforation threatens. NSAIDs are insufficient.
teachingPoints: Necrotizing scleritis is the most severe form of scleritis and is strongly associated with systemic vasculitis, particularly rheumatoid arthritis.
RA patients with necrotizing scleritis have a significantly increased mortality rate from systemic vasculitis if not treated with systemic immunosuppression.
Scleromalacia perforans is necrotizing scleritis WITHOUT inflammation (painless), also associated with severe RA, and can lead to globe perforation.
The pain of scleritis is characteristically severe, deep, boring, worse at night, radiating to the face, and unresponsive to topical treatment.

clues: A 65-year-old female develops a shallow anterior chamber, elevated IOP, and worsening vision 2 days after undergoing trabeculectomy for POAG.

IOP is 40 mmHg. The anterior chamber is uniformly shallow (axially flat, not just peripherally). The lens-iris diaphragm is pushed forward. There is no pupillary block (patent PI). The bleb appears flat.
UBM shows anterior rotation of the ciliary body with the lens pushed forward. There is no choroidal effusion or suprachoroidal hemorrhage on B-scan. The posterior chamber is compressed.
The patient just had filtering surgery. The anterior chamber was deep at the end of surgery but shallowed on postoperative day 1.

Medical: intensive cycloplegics (atropine) to relax the ciliary body + aqueous suppressants + hypertonic agents. If medical therapy fails: Nd:YAG laser hyaloidotomy through the peripheral iridotomy or pars plana vitrectomy with lens extraction.
teachingPoints: Malignant glaucoma (aqueous misdirection) occurs when aqueous humor is misdirected posteriorly into or behind the vitreous rather than flowing anteriorly, pushing the lens-iris diaphragm forward.
It classically occurs after incisional glaucoma surgery in hyperopic eyes with shallow anterior chambers, but can occur after cataract surgery or even spontaneously.
Unlike pupillary block, the anterior chamber is uniformly shallow (central and peripheral), and LPI does not resolve it.
Vitrectomy with lensectomy and anterior hyaloidectomy is the definitive treatment for refractory cases.

clues: A 50-year-old female is found unconscious and is diagnosed with subarachnoid hemorrhage from a ruptured intracranial aneurysm. An ophthalmology consult is requested.

Dilated exam reveals bilateral dense vitreous hemorrhage with subhyaloid (sub-internal limiting membrane) hemorrhages in the posterior pole. A dome-shaped preretinal hemorrhage overlies the macula bilaterally.
B-scan confirms bilateral vitreous hemorrhage without retinal detachment. CT brain shows diffuse subarachnoid blood with hydrocephalus.
The patient required emergent neurosurgical clipping. She is in the ICU with raised intracranial pressure.

Observation for most cases—vitreous hemorrhage often clears spontaneously over weeks to months. Vitrectomy for non-clearing hemorrhage (typically wait 2-3 months) or if dense hemorrhage prevents fundus monitoring.
teachingPoints: Terson syndrome is intraocular hemorrhage (vitreous, subhyaloid, or subretinal) associated with acute subarachnoid hemorrhage, occurring in approximately 10-40% of SAH patients.
The hemorrhage is thought to result from acute elevation of intracranial pressure transmitted through the optic nerve sheath to the retinal and preretinal venous system.
The presence of Terson syndrome is associated with higher mortality and worse neurological outcomes in SAH patients.
If vitreous hemorrhage is found incidentally without known neurological history, SAH should be considered and urgent neuroimaging obtained.

clues: A male infant is found to have bilateral leukocoria and no visual tracking at 2 months of age.
Examination under anesthesia reveals bilateral retrolental fibrovascular masses filling the vitreous cavity with total retinal detachments (retinal folds). Microphthalmos is present.
B-scan shows bilateral complex retinal detachments with vitreous membranes. No calcification (distinguishing from retinoblastoma). Genetic testing reveals an NDP gene mutation on the X chromosome.
The infant is male. The maternal grandfather was blind from birth. The infant also failed the newborn hearing screen.
There is no effective treatment to restore vision once retinal detachments are established. Genetic counseling is essential. Monitor for hearing loss and intellectual disability.
teachingPoints: Norrie disease is a rare X-linked recessive condition caused by NDP gene mutations, resulting in congenital bilateral blindness from retrolental fibrovascular proliferation and retinal detachment.
The NDP gene encodes norrin, which is important in retinal vascular development—the same pathway involved in familial exudative vitreoretinopathy (FEVR).
Progressive sensorineural hearing loss occurs in about 30% of patients, and intellectual disability in about 50%.
The differential includes retinoblastoma, ROP, Coats disease, PFV, and FEVR; genetic testing is essential for definitive diagnosis.

clues: A 55-year-old male presents with a chronic foreign body sensation in both eyes, worse with blinking.
Eversion of the upper eyelids reveals multiple small, hard, yellowish-white deposits embedded in the palpebral conjunctiva. Some are protruding through the conjunctival surface.
The protruding concretions are causing linear corneal epithelial staining (abrasion from the rough surface rubbing against the cornea with each blink).
The patient has chronic tarsitis and a history of chronic blepharitis. He wears no contact lenses.
Removal of protruding/symptomatic concretions with a needle tip under topical anesthesia at the slit lamp. Buried, non-symptomatic concretions can be observed.
teachingPoints: Conjunctival concretions are small calcific or proteinaceous deposits in the subepithelial conjunctival stroma, extremely common in older adults.
They are usually asymptomatic unless they erode through the conjunctival surface and mechanically irritate the cornea.
Associated with chronic conjunctival inflammation: trachoma, chronic blepharitis, dry eye, and aging.
Removal is a quick in-office procedure; recurrence is common since the underlying chronic inflammation persists.

clues: A 25-year-old male presents with bilateral acute painless central vision loss and scotomas developing over a few days following a flu-like illness.

Fundoscopy reveals bilateral large, flat, creamy-white, placoid lesions at the level of the RPE scattered throughout the posterior pole. Some lesions are fading with RPE window defects (healing phase).

FA shows characteristic early hypofluorescence (blocked choroidal fluorescence) and late hyperfluorescence (staining) of the active lesions. OCT shows outer retinal and RPE disruption. Mild vitritis is present.
The patient had a viral upper respiratory infection 1 week ago. He is young, healthy, and has no systemic disease.
Observation for most cases—APMPPE is typically self-limited with good visual recovery over weeks. Systemic steroids considered for foveal involvement or CNS vasculitis.
teachingPoints: APMPPE is an inflammatory condition affecting the choriocapillaris and RPE, typically presenting after a viral prodrome in young healthy adults.
Most patients recover 20/20-20/40 vision within 2-6 weeks, though mild visual complaints (scotomas, color vision changes) may persist.
Rare but serious CNS vasculitis (cerebral vasculitis) can occur and must be watched for; headache or neurologic symptoms warrant urgent MRI/MRA.
The classic FA pattern of early hypo/late hyper differentiates APMPPE from other white dot syndromes.

clues: A 5-year-old boy is referred after failing a school vision screening. His uncle was blind from a childhood retinal condition.

Dilated exam reveals an avascular peripheral retina temporally with a ridge of fibrovascular tissue, straightening of the temporal retinal arcade vessels, and dragging of the optic disc temporally. There is a small exudative retinal detachment inferiorly.
FA shows abrupt cessation of the retinal vasculature temporally with peripheral non-perfusion and leakage at the ridge. The nasal retina is fully vascularized. There is no history of prematurity.
The child was born full-term at 40 weeks and 3500 grams. There is a strong family history. Genetic testing reveals an FZD4 mutation (autosomal dominant FEVR).
Laser photocoagulation of the avascular retina to prevent progression. Surgery (scleral buckle or vitrectomy) for retinal detachment. Screen family members.
teachingPoints: FEVR is a hereditary condition of incomplete peripheral retinal vascularization that mimics ROP but occurs in full-term infants without oxygen exposure.
The key distinction from ROP is the absence of prematurity and supplemental oxygen history; FEVR is genetic.
Inheritance is variable: autosomal dominant (FZD4, LRP5), autosomal recessive (LRP5), and X-linked (NDP)—the NDP gene is shared with Norrie disease.
Disease severity is highly variable even within families; asymptomatic carriers with only peripheral avascularization on FA should still be monitored.

clues: A 72-year-old male with a history of bilateral parieto-occipital strokes has difficulty directing his gaze to objects of interest. He can't 'look at' things on command.

Voluntary horizontal saccades are absent or severely impaired. He cannot generate saccades to visual targets. Reflexive saccades (vestibulo-ocular reflex) are intact. He has simultanagnosia and optic ataxia in addition.
MRI brain shows bilateral parieto-occipital infarcts. Neuropsychological testing confirms the full Balint syndrome triad.
The patient has bilateral watershed infarcts from a hypotensive episode during cardiac surgery. Smooth pursuit is relatively preserved.
No specific treatment for the eye movement disorder. Rehabilitation with occupational therapy focusing on compensatory strategies. Treatment of underlying cerebrovascular disease.
teachingPoints: Acquired oculomotor apraxia (an inability to generate voluntary saccades) is part of Balint syndrome, caused by bilateral parieto-occipital lesions.
Balint syndrome consists of three elements: oculomotor apraxia, optic ataxia (inability to reach for objects accurately), and simultanagnosia (inability to perceive multiple objects at once).
Unlike congenital oculomotor apraxia (which presents in childhood with characteristic head thrusting), acquired forms are due to structural brain lesions.
Common causes include bilateral strokes, Alzheimer disease, Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease, and posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES).

clues: A 25-year-old female presents with gradually worsening metamorphopsia and a visual field defect in her left eye.
Fundoscopy reveals a well-defined, slightly elevated, orange-yellow plaque-like lesion in the juxtapapillary and macular choroid of the left eye with overlying RPE changes.

B-scan ultrasound shows a highly reflective lesion with dense acoustic shadowing posteriorly (characteristic of bone). CT orbits confirms intraocular calcification in the posterior pole. OCT shows the lesion beneath the RPE with overlying decalcification and subretinal fluid.
The patient is a young woman (typical demographic). The lesion has grown slightly over the past 2 years on serial imaging.
Observation for stable, asymptomatic tumors. Anti-VEGF for CNV complication. PDT for the decalcifying areas with subretinal fluid. No treatment exists to halt the bony growth.
teachingPoints: Choroidal osteoma is a rare benign tumor composed of mature bone that replaces the choroid, predominantly affecting young women.
The dense posterior acoustic shadowing on B-scan is pathognomonic and reflects the bony nature of the tumor.
Decalcification over time can lead to overlying RPE atrophy and CNV, which are the main causes of vision loss.
Unlike calcification in retinoblastoma (which is within the tumor mass), choroidal osteoma involves mature lamellar bone replacing choroidal tissue.

clues: A 40-year-old male presents for routine follow-up. He had blunt ocular trauma with hyphema to his right eye 10 years ago.

IOP is 30 mmHg OD, 16 mmHg OS. Gonioscopy reveals widening (deepening) of the ciliary body band with irregular, torn iris processes in 270 degrees of the angle in the right eye. The left eye angle is normal.
OCT RNFL shows inferior thinning OD. Visual fields show a superior arcuate defect OD. The anterior chamber shows old pigment on the lens capsule (Vossius ring).
The patient was in a fistfight 10 years ago and had a Grade II hyphema that resolved. He was told the eye was fine and was discharged from follow-up.
Topical IOP-lowering therapy. SLT may have reduced efficacy due to damaged trabecular meshwork. Filtering surgery if medical therapy fails.
teachingPoints: Angle recession is a tear between the longitudinal and circular fibers of the ciliary muscle, caused by blunt trauma; glaucoma can develop years to decades later.
The risk of glaucoma increases with the extent of angle recession: >180 degrees of recession carries the highest risk.
Glaucoma develops from progressive scarring and dysfunction of the trabecular meshwork in the area of recession.
Annual IOP monitoring is recommended for life after traumatic hyphema, as angle recession glaucoma can present decades after the initial injury.

clues: A 3-year-old girl presents with recurrent thick, woody, white pseudomembranes forming on the palpebral conjunctiva of both eyes that keep recurring after removal.
Eversion of the upper lids reveals thick, white-yellow, firm, woody (ligneous) pseudomembranes adherent to the tarsal conjunctiva. They are difficult to peel and bleed when removed.
Biopsy shows amorphous eosinophilic material (fibrin) with surrounding inflammatory cells. Plasma plasminogen activity is severely reduced (<2% of normal). Genetic testing shows a PLG gene mutation.
The membranes have recurred multiple times after surgical excision. The child has had similar membranes on her gingiva and vaginal mucosa.

Topical plasminogen (lys-plasminogen) drops are the targeted treatment. Topical cyclosporine and heparin can also help. Systemic fresh frozen plasma infusion provides temporary plasminogen replacement.
teachingPoints: Ligneous conjunctivitis is a rare condition caused by homozygous type I plasminogen deficiency, leading to impaired fibrinolysis and accumulation of fibrin-rich pseudomembranes on mucosal surfaces.
The condition is named for the wood-like (ligneous) consistency of the pseudomembranes.
