Mucosal membranes can form anywhere: conjunctiva (most common), gingiva, respiratory tract, genitourinary tract, and middle ear.
Topical plasminogen replacement is the most specific therapy, directly addressing the underlying deficiency at the mucosal surface.

clues: A 25-year-old male presents with sudden onset of constant binocular horizontal diplopia that began 1 week ago. He reports no trauma, headache, or other neurological symptoms.

A comitant (same measurement in all directions of gaze) esotropia of 20 prism diopters is present at both distance and near. Extraocular movements are full without restriction or palsy. Pupils are normal.
MRI brain with contrast including attention to the posterior fossa is normal (no Chiari malformation, no mass, no demyelination). Neurological exam is completely normal.
The patient has been spending 10+ hours daily on his smartphone for the past year. Cycloplegic refraction reveals minimal uncorrected hyperopia.
Prisms can provide temporary diplopia relief. Botulinum toxin injection to the medial rectus can help. If persistent (>6 months), bilateral medial rectus recession surgery is effective.
teachingPoints: Acute acquired comitant esotropia in adults requires neuroimaging to rule out intracranial pathology, particularly Chiari malformation, CNS tumors, and demyelinating disease.
There has been an increasing incidence linked to excessive smartphone/screen use at close range (excessive accommodative convergence).
Unlike CN6 palsy (incomitant, worse in one gaze direction), AACE is comitant (equal in all gaze positions).
Once intracranial causes are excluded, the prognosis for surgical correction is excellent with high success rates.

clues: A 35-year-old female is referred by her optometrist for evaluation of a dark lesion noted behind the iris during routine dilation.
Gonioscopy reveals a smooth, round, dark-brown cystic lesion arising from the posterior pigment epithelium of the iris near the pupillary margin. It transilluminates on retroillumination.
UBM shows a thin-walled, fluid-filled cyst arising from the iris pigment epithelium. Anterior segment OCT confirms the cystic (not solid) nature of the lesion.
The lesion was discovered incidentally. There is no change in IOP, pupil shape, or vision. No prior trauma or surgery.
Observation only—primary iris pigment epithelial cysts are benign and rarely require treatment unless they cause pupillary block, angle closure, or visual axis obstruction.
teachingPoints: Primary iris pigment epithelial cysts are common, benign, fluid-filled cysts that arise from the neuroepithelial layers of the iris.
The key diagnostic feature is transillumination—cysts transmit light (appear translucent) whereas solid tumors (melanoma) do not.
UBM and anterior segment OCT are essential for differentiating cysts from solid iris tumors and for confirming the thin-walled cystic structure.
Secondary iris cysts can form after trauma, surgery, or prolonged miotic use, and are more likely to cause complications than primary cysts.

clues: A 15-year-old boy from a rural area in the southeastern US presents with progressive unilateral vision loss and vitritis in his right eye over several months.

Visual acuity is 20/200 OD. Fundoscopy shows a hazy vitreous with multifocal, gray-white, outer retinal lesions that have been migrating to different locations on serial exams. There is optic disc edema. The fellow eye is normal.
Careful wide-angle fundus examination reveals a small (400-1000 μm) motile, white, glistening subretinal worm in the peripheral retina. ERG shows severely reduced responses in the affected eye.
The patient plays in wooded areas and has exposure to raccoons (Baylisascaris procyonis is the suspected roundworm). He has no systemic symptoms.
Direct laser photocoagulation of the subretinal worm to kill it in situ. This halts the progressive damage. Antihelminthics (albendazole) may be used adjunctively.
teachingPoints: DUSN is caused by a subretinal nematode (Baylisascaris, Toxocara, Ancylostoma, or other roundworms) that migrates through the subretinal space, causing progressive inflammation and retinal destruction.
Finding the worm is the key to diagnosis; serial fundus examinations and widefield imaging help track the migrating worm.
If untreated, DUSN progresses to severe optic atrophy, retinal vessel narrowing, and RPE degeneration (mimicking retinitis pigmentosa).
DUSN is one of the few treatable causes of unilateral visual loss in children in endemic areas, making identification of the worm critical.

clues: A 50-year-old male presents with a small, non-seeing, chronically uncomfortable right eye. He had a severe open globe injury 20 years ago.

The right eye is small (shrunken), soft (hypotonus), and has band keratopathy, a thickened sclera, and disorganized intraocular contents. Vision is no light perception. The eye has chronic low-grade pain.
B-scan shows a disorganized globe with calcification, thick choroidal detachment, and reduced axial length. The eye is cosmetically disfigured.
The patient has had multiple surgeries in the past including vitrectomy and silicone oil. The eye has been blind for 15 years.

Options include a cosmetic scleral shell over the phthisical eye, evisceration (removal of intraocular contents with preservation of the scleral shell), or enucleation (removal of the entire globe) with prosthetic fitting.
teachingPoints: Phthisis bulbi is the end-stage of severe ocular disease or trauma, characterized by a shrunken, non-functional, hypotonus globe with disorganized internal structures.
Causes include severe trauma, end-stage glaucoma, chronic retinal detachment, endophthalmitis, and chronic uveitis.
Sympathetic ophthalmia risk remains a concern in phthisical eyes from penetrating trauma, even decades after injury.
Enucleation with an orbital implant and custom prosthetic eye provides the best cosmetic outcome and eliminates the risk of sympathetic ophthalmia.

clues: A 15-year-old male presents with bilateral blurred vision and floaters that have been gradually worsening over several months.

Visual acuity is 20/40 bilaterally. There is minimal anterior chamber reaction but moderate vitritis (2+ vitreous cells) bilaterally. Dilated exam reveals bilateral snowbanking (white exudative material over the pars plana inferiorly) and snowball vitreous opacities.
OCT shows cystoid macular edema bilaterally. FA demonstrates CME and peripheral vascular leakage. Systemic workup for sarcoidosis, MS, and infections is negative.
The patient is a young male with no systemic disease. The condition is bilateral with a characteristic pattern.

Periocular (sub-Tenon) triamcinolone or intravitreal dexamethasone implant for CME. Systemic immunosuppression (methotrexate, mycophenolate) for chronic disease. Cryotherapy to the snowbank in refractory cases.
teachingPoints: Pars planitis is a subtype of intermediate uveitis defined by snowbanking and/or snowball opacities in the absence of an associated systemic condition.
CME is the most common cause of vision loss in intermediate uveitis/pars planitis.
It most commonly affects children and young adults, is bilateral in 80%, and has a chronic course with remissions and exacerbations.
MS and sarcoidosis must be excluded before labeling intermediate uveitis as pars planitis (an idiopathic diagnosis).

clues: A 30-year-old asymptomatic female is found to have an unusual retinal lesion on routine eye exam.
Dilated exam shows a cluster of dark, grape-like, saccular vascular lesions (berry-like) along a retinal vein in the mid-periphery. The aneurysmal outpouchings are filled with dark blood.

FA shows slow filling of the saccular lesions with a characteristic plasma-erythrocyte layering (fluorescence capping at the top of each saccule). There are NO feeder vessels (distinguishing from capillary hemangioblastoma).
The patient has skin hemangiomas. She is asymptomatic with 20/20 vision. MRI brain may be considered to rule out CNS cavernomas.
Observation for most cases—retinal cavernous hemangiomas are typically stable and do not require treatment unless vitreous hemorrhage occurs.
teachingPoints: Retinal cavernous hemangiomas are rare, benign vascular hamartomas characterized by grape-like clusters of thin-walled saccular aneurysms.
The characteristic FA finding is 'fluorescein capping' or 'plasma-erythrocyte separation' within the saccules, visible as a horizontal fluid level.
Unlike capillary hemangioblastomas (VHL), cavernous hemangiomas have no feeder artery, do not leak on FA, and rarely cause exudation.
They may be associated with CNS and skin cavernous hemangiomas as part of a neurocutaneous syndrome.

clues: A 65-year-old male with a history of herpes zoster ophthalmicus 5 years ago presents with a non-healing corneal epithelial defect in his right eye that has been present for 8 weeks.

The right cornea has an oval, smooth-edged, persistent epithelial defect with rolled edges. The surrounding epithelium is loose and hazy. There is NO injection or pain despite the ulcer. Corneal sensation is absent (measured with a cotton wisp esthesiometer—Cochet-Bonnet test is <1 cm).

The ulcer has not responded to conventional antibiotic therapy. Fluorescein shows a clean-based defect with no infiltrate (non-infectious). Schirmer testing shows reduced tear production on the affected side.
The patient had severe HZO 5 years ago with involvement of the nasociliary nerve. Corneal sensation has been permanently lost.

Cenegermin (recombinant human nerve growth factor—Oxervate) topical drops are FDA-approved specifically for neurotrophic keratopathy. Alternatives: bandage contact lens, tarsorrhaphy, amniotic membrane transplant.
teachingPoints: Neurotrophic keratopathy results from damage to the trigeminal sensory innervation of the cornea, leading to impaired epithelial healing, reduced tear production, and corneal ulceration.
Mackie classification: Stage 1 (punctate epitheliopathy), Stage 2 (persistent epithelial defect with smooth rolled edges), Stage 3 (corneal melting/perforation).
Common causes include herpes simplex/zoster keratitis, post-surgical (acoustic neuroma removal), diabetes, and topical anesthetic abuse.
Cenegermin (NGF) was the first drug specifically approved for neurotrophic keratitis and promotes corneal nerve and epithelial regeneration.

clues: Parents of a 6-month-old infant are concerned about web-like strands across the pupil of the baby's left eye.

Slit-lamp shows fine, pigmented strands bridging across the pupil from the iris collarette to the collarette on the opposite side. The strands do NOT attach to the lens capsule. The red reflex is slightly reduced through the strands.
The pupil dilates partially around the strands. Vision (assessed by fixation and following behavior) appears mildly reduced in the left eye. No cataract is present.
The strands have been present since birth. They are remnants of the tunica vasculosa lentis that normally regresses by the 8th month of gestation.
Observation if mild (most cases resolve or thin out). Pupil dilation may improve visual axis. YAG laser or surgical membranectomy if dense enough to cause amblyopia.
teachingPoints: Persistent pupillary membranes are remnants of the fetal tunica vasculosa lentis; they are very common in premature infants and usually regress spontaneously.
They originate from the iris collarette (minor arterial circle), distinguishing them from posterior synechiae (which attach the pupillary margin to the lens capsule).
Most PPMs are visually insignificant and require no treatment.
Rarely, dense PPMs can obstruct the visual axis and cause deprivation amblyopia requiring intervention.

clues: A 50-year-old male presents with progressive bilateral central vision loss and metamorphopsia over several months to years.

Fundoscopy reveals bilateral geographic, grayish-white lesions originating from the peripapillary area and extending centrifugally in a serpiginous (snake-like) pattern. Active edges are grayish-white while older areas show chorioretinal atrophy with pigment clumping.

FA shows early hypofluorescence of active lesions (blocked fluorescence) and late staining of active edges. ICG shows hypofluorescent patches extending well beyond what is clinically visible. OCT shows outer retinal/RPE disruption with choroidal infiltration at the active edge.
The patient has bilateral disease extending from the disc. TB QuantiFERON is negative. No vitritis is present (unlike tubercular serpiginous-like choroiditis).
Systemic immunosuppression (azathioprine, mycophenolate, cyclosporine) is required for long-term control. Corticosteroids for acute flares. Triple-agent therapy may be needed for refractory disease.
teachingPoints: Serpiginous choroidopathy is a chronic, progressive, bilateral inflammatory disease of the choriocapillaris and RPE that starts peripapillary and extends centrifugally.
The geographic, snake-like pattern of spread originating from the disc is pathognomonic.
Tubercular serpiginous-like choroiditis (an important mimic) should be excluded with TB testing; it is more common in TB-endemic regions and presents with more vitritis.
Recurrences are common at the edges of old scars; each recurrence extends the area of damage toward the fovea, making early aggressive treatment important.

clues: A 2-year-old child presents with bilateral severe ptosis and limited eye movements in all directions since birth. She uses a chin-up head position to see.
Both eyes are fixed in a hypotropic (infraducted) position. Bilateral complete ptosis is present. Attempted upgaze, adduction, and abduction are severely limited. Bell phenomenon is absent.

Forced duction testing under anesthesia reveals tight, fibrotic extraocular muscles. MRI orbits shows hypoplastic superior division of CN3 and small superior rectus/levator muscles. Genetic testing reveals a KIF21A mutation.
The condition has been present since birth. Other family members are affected (autosomal dominant inheritance in CFEOM1).
Surgery for ptosis (frontalis sling) and strabismus correction (large recessions of the tight fibrotic muscles) to improve the chin-up head position. Results are limited by fibrosis.
teachingPoints: CFEOM is a group of congenital cranial dysinnervation disorders caused by mutations affecting oculomotor nerve development, leading to fibrosis and dysfunction of extraocular muscles.
CFEOM1 (KIF21A mutation) is the most common type: bilateral ptosis with the eyes fixed in infraduction.
Unlike congenital ptosis from levator dystrophy (which has isolated ptosis with normal motility), CFEOM has global restriction of eye movements.
Surgery is palliative rather than curative; the fibrotic muscles limit the degree of correction achievable.

clues: A 45-year-old male with small, hyperopic eyes presents with bilateral progressive visual field loss and shallow serous retinal detachments.

Fundoscopy reveals bilateral non-rhegmatogenous serous retinal detachments with shifting subretinal fluid. There is exudative choroidal detachment. The axial length is 18 mm (nanophthalmic range). There is no vitritis.
FA shows multiple leopard-spot areas of RPE window defects. B-scan confirms choroidal and retinal detachments without mass. Scleral thickness is increased (>2 mm vs. normal <1 mm).
There is no inflammation, no tumor, and no vascular cause identified. The sclera is abnormally thick on UBM.
Partial-thickness sclerectomy (vortex vein decompression windows) to allow increased transscleral protein and fluid outflow. This is the specific treatment for nanophthalmic uveal effusion.
teachingPoints: Uveal effusion syndrome results from impaired transscleral outflow of protein and fluid from the suprachoroidal space, usually due to abnormally thick sclera in nanophthalmic eyes.
The abnormally thick sclera compresses the vortex veins and impedes protein diffusion, leading to oncotic accumulation of suprachoroidal fluid.
The leopard-spot pattern on FA is characteristic and helps differentiate it from VKH, central serous, and other causes of serous RD.
Surgery on nanophthalmic eyes carries high risk of intraoperative uveal effusion and malignant glaucoma.

clues: A 28-year-old female presents with recurrent corneal erosions and progressively blurred vision since her late teens.

Slit-lamp shows fine, refractile, branching lattice lines in the anterior and mid-stroma of both corneas. The lines are best seen with retroillumination and are more prominent centrally. There is a ground-glass central haze.
The lattice lines stain with Congo red (amyloid deposits showing apple-green birefringence under polarized light). Genetic testing confirms a TGFBI gene mutation (R124C).
Her father had a corneal transplant at age 45 for a similar condition. The deposits have been slowly progressing.
PTK for recurrent erosions. DALK or PK when vision is significantly affected. Recurrence in the graft occurs in most patients over time.
teachingPoints: Lattice corneal dystrophy type I is an autosomal dominant TGFBI dystrophy characterized by amyloid deposition in the corneal stroma appearing as refractile branching lattice lines.
Recurrent corneal erosions are often the presenting symptom before the lattice lines become visually significant.
Unlike granular dystrophy (discrete crumb-like hyaline deposits), lattice dystrophy has branching filamentous amyloid deposits.
LASIK is contraindicated in TGFBI dystrophies as it can trigger severe recurrence of deposits in the lamellar interface.

clues: A 50-year-old female presents with decreased vision in her right eye and is found to have a yellow-orange retinal mass inferiorly.

Dilated exam shows a yellow-pink, dome-shaped, exudative retinal mass in the inferior periphery with surrounding subretinal exudation, hard exudates reaching the macula, and an associated exudative retinal detachment. The mass has surface telangiectasia.

FA shows prominent intrinsic tumor vascularity with late leakage. B-scan shows a dome-shaped lesion with medium internal reflectivity. There is NO dilated feeder artery (distinguishing from VHL hemangioblastoma).
The patient has no history of prior retinal disease or uveitis (primary/idiopathic type). She has no family history or systemic findings.
Cryotherapy is the first-line treatment. Plaque brachytherapy for larger tumors. Anti-VEGF and intravitreal steroids for associated exudation. Laser is often insufficient alone.
teachingPoints: Vasoproliferative tumors are benign, reactive glial-vascular proliferations of the peripheral retina, most commonly located inferotemporally.
They are classified as primary (idiopathic, 75%) or secondary (associated with uveitis, retinitis pigmentosa, prior retinal detachment, or Coats disease).
The absence of a dilated feeder artery distinguishes VPT from retinal capillary hemangioblastoma of VHL disease.
Exudative complications (macular hard exudates, subretinal fluid, exudative RD) are the main cause of vision loss and the primary indication for treatment.

clues: An 18-year-old male with new-onset tremor and psychiatric symptoms is referred for an ophthalmology exam as part of his workup.

Slit-lamp examination reveals a golden-brown ring of pigment at the level of Descemet membrane at the corneal periphery bilaterally (Kayser-Fleischer ring). The ring is most prominent superiorly and inferiorly. A sunflower cataract (greenish anterior subcapsular opacity) is also present.
Serum ceruloplasmin is low. 24-hour urine copper is elevated. Liver biopsy shows elevated hepatic copper content.
The patient has liver enzyme abnormalities and neuropsychiatric symptoms (tremor, dysarthria, personality changes) consistent with Wilson disease.
Treatment of Wilson disease with copper-chelating agents (penicillamine or trientine) and dietary copper restriction. The KF ring fades with successful treatment.
teachingPoints: Kayser-Fleischer rings are copper deposits in Descemet membrane of the cornea and are present in >95% of Wilson disease patients with neurologic involvement.
They are best seen with gonioscopy or slit-lamp; they may not be visible to the naked eye in early stages, especially in brown-eyed patients.
The sunflower cataract does not typically affect vision and also resolves with copper-chelation therapy.
KF rings are not 100% specific for Wilson disease—they can rarely be seen in other cholestatic liver diseases—but in a young patient with neuropsychiatric symptoms, they are virtually diagnostic.

clues: A 10-year-old boy presents with lifelong difficulty seeing in the dark and at night. He is moderately myopic.
Visual acuity is 20/40 bilaterally with moderate myopia (-5.00 D). The fundus appears normal. There is nystagmus.

Full-field ERG shows the pathognomonic 'negative ERG' pattern: normal a-wave amplitude but markedly reduced b-wave amplitude under scotopic conditions (electronegative ERG). Photopic ERG is relatively preserved.
His mother's brother has the same condition. Genetic testing reveals a NYX gene mutation (X-linked complete CSNB).
No treatment is available to restore night vision. Corrective lenses for myopia. Low-vision aids for night conditions. Genetic counseling.
teachingPoints: CSNB is a group of inherited retinal disorders with non-progressive rod dysfunction causing lifelong nyctalopia with a normal-appearing fundus.
The 'negative ERG' (b:a ratio <1 under scotopic conditions) is the electrophysiologic hallmark, reflecting impaired signal transmission from photoreceptors to bipolar cells.
Complete CSNB (e.g., NYX, GRM6 mutations) affects ON-bipolar cell signaling, while incomplete CSNB (CACNA1F, CABP4) affects both ON and OFF pathways.
CSNB must be distinguished from retinitis pigmentosa (which is progressive and has an abnormal fundus) and vitamin A deficiency (which is acquired and reversible).

clues: A 55-year-old male presents 6 weeks after a failed retinal detachment repair with recurrent detachment and progressively worsening vision.

The retina is detached with fixed, rigid folds. Star folds are present on the posterior retinal surface, and subretinal bands are visible. The vitreous shows membranes and traction. The retina is immobile and cannot be flattened.
B-scan confirms a detached, thickened, immobile retina with traction membranes. The redetachment is due to contraction of epiretinal and subretinal fibrotic membranes.
The original detachment was a macula-off RRD repaired with vitrectomy. The eye has had chronic inflammation postoperatively. PVR grade C is classified.
Repeat vitrectomy with membrane peeling, relaxing retinectomy, perfluorocarbon liquid, endolaser, and silicone oil tamponade.
teachingPoints: PVR is the leading cause of failure of retinal detachment surgery, occurring in approximately 5-10% of RRD repairs.
It is caused by proliferation and contraction of RPE cells, glial cells, and fibroblasts that form membranes on and beneath the retinal surface.
Risk factors include large/chronic detachments, vitreous hemorrhage, multiple surgeries, inflammation, and giant retinal tears.
Even with aggressive surgical management, visual outcomes are often limited due to retinal damage from chronic detachment and repeated surgery.

clues: A 7-year-old boy is found to have a yellow lesion at the macula during a school vision screening. Visual acuity is 20/30.
Fundoscopy reveals a bilateral, well-circumscribed, round, egg-yolk-like (vitelliform) yellow deposit at the fovea, approximately 1 disc diameter in size.

EOG (electro-oculogram) shows a markedly abnormal Arden ratio (<1.5; normal >1.8) bilaterally. Full-field ERG is normal. OCT shows hyperreflective subretinal material at the fovea. Genetic testing reveals a BEST1 gene mutation.
His father has a similar macular appearance but with a 'scrambled egg' pattern (more advanced stage). Family history reveals autosomal dominant inheritance.
No treatment in the vitelliform stage. Monitor for CNV development (which can occur in later stages) and treat with anti-VEGF. Low vision rehabilitation for advanced cases.
teachingPoints: Best disease is an autosomal dominant macular dystrophy caused by BEST1 (bestrophin-1) mutations affecting RPE chloride channel function.
The stages progress from previtelliform → vitelliform (egg yolk) → pseudohypopyon (layering) → scrambled egg → atrophic.
EOG is the key diagnostic test: abnormal in affected patients AND carriers, whereas ERG is normal.
Visual acuity is often surprisingly good even with a large vitelliform lesion, but declines in the atrophic and CNV stages.

clues: A 30-year-old male is noted to have bilateral white rings around both corneas during a routine exam. He has no ocular complaints.
A bilateral, symmetrical, white-gray lipid deposit is present at the corneal periphery, separated from the limbus by a clear zone (lucid interval of Vogt). The deposits are circumferential.
Fasting lipid panel reveals total cholesterol of 350 mg/dL and LDL of 260 mg/dL. The patient was not aware of any cholesterol problems.
The patient is 30 years old (arcus in patients under 40 is termed arcus juvenilis). Family history reveals premature coronary artery disease (father had MI at age 45).
Refer to primary care/cardiology for evaluation of familial hyperlipidemia. The corneal arcus itself requires no ophthalmic treatment.
teachingPoints: Arcus senilis is extremely common in patients over 60 and is generally benign. However, arcus juvenilis (under age 40) warrants systemic lipid evaluation.
The deposit consists of cholesterol, phospholipids, and triglycerides in the peripheral corneal stroma at the level of Bowman and Descemet layers.
The lucid interval (clear zone between the arcus and the limbus) distinguishes arcus from band keratopathy and other peripheral corneal deposits.
In young patients, arcus may be the presenting sign of familial hypercholesterolemia, particularly type II hyperlipoproteinemia.

clues: A 5-year-old boy presents with rapidly progressive proptosis of the right eye developing over just 2-3 weeks, with associated lid swelling and displacement of the globe.
There is marked right proptosis with the globe displaced inferolaterally. The mass is firm and non-tender in the superonasal orbit. Motility is restricted in upgaze.

CT/MRI shows a well-circumscribed, homogeneously enhancing mass in the superonasal orbit with bony destruction of the orbital roof. Biopsy shows small round blue cells with desmin and myogenin positivity (embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma).
The child was well 3 weeks ago. The tumor has grown explosively. There is no family history of malignancy.
Systemic chemotherapy (vincristine, actinomycin D, cyclophosphamide—VAC protocol) plus radiation therapy. Surgery is reserved for biopsy; excision is not the primary treatment.
teachingPoints: Rhabdomyosarcoma is the most common primary malignant orbital tumor of childhood; the embryonal subtype (superonasal orbit) is most common.
Rapid proptosis in a child (over days to weeks) is the hallmark—this speed of onset distinguishes RMS from most other orbital tumors.
With modern multimodal therapy (chemo + radiation), survival rates exceed 90% for orbital RMS.
Orbital RMS has a better prognosis than RMS at other sites due to earlier detection from visible proptosis and the orbit's amenability to radiation.

clues: A 22-year-old male presents to the ER with his eyes involuntarily locked in an upward and lateral position for the past hour. He is distressed and unable to bring his eyes down.

Both eyes are tonically deviated upward and to the right. The patient cannot voluntarily bring his eyes to primary position. There is associated torticollis and jaw dystonia. He is anxious but neurologically intact otherwise.
He started metoclopramide (Reglan) for nausea yesterday. Vital signs and glucose are normal. No history of seizures or neurologic disease.
The patient recently started a dopamine receptor antagonist. He has no prior neurologic history.
IV diphenhydramine (Benadryl) 50 mg or benztropine (Cogentin) 1-2 mg IV provides rapid relief. Discontinue the offending medication.
teachingPoints: Oculogyric crisis is an acute dystonic reaction causing involuntary sustained upward (or lateral) conjugate eye deviation, most commonly triggered by dopamine-blocking medications.
Common offending drugs include metoclopramide, haloperidol, prochlorperazine, and other antipsychotics/antiemetics.
Anticholinergic agents (diphenhydramine, benztropine) provide rapid relief, usually within minutes of IV administration.
It can be frightening for patients and is sometimes mistaken for a seizure or psychiatric event; recognition of the medication history is key.

clues: A 75-year-old male presents with chronic tearing, irritation, and redness of his right eye. The symptoms have been worsening gradually over years.

The right lower eyelid is lax and everted (turned outward), exposing the palpebral conjunctiva which appears keratinized and thickened. There is punctal malposition (punctum everted away from the globe). The cornea shows inferior exposure keratopathy with punctate epithelial erosions.

Snap-back test shows the lower lid fails to return to the globe without a blink (poor lid tone). Medial canthal tendon laxity is present. The horizontal lid laxity exceeds 8 mm on the distraction test.
The patient has age-related tissue laxity. He has no history of facial nerve palsy, skin disease, or previous surgery.

Surgical correction with a lateral tarsal strip procedure (horizontal lid-tightening) is the standard approach. Medial conjunctival spindle procedure may be added for medial ectropion with punctal malposition.
teachingPoints: Involutional ectropion is the most common type, caused by age-related laxity of the lower lid retractors and horizontal lid laxity.
Other types include cicatricial (skin scarring), paralytic (CN7 palsy), and mechanical (tumor weight pulling the lid down).
Chronic exposure of the palpebral conjunctiva leads to keratinization; chronic tearing results from punctal eversion and poor tear drainage.
The lateral tarsal strip is a reliable, outpatient procedure with high success rates for involutional ectropion.

clues: A 72-year-old female presents with foreign body sensation, tearing, and redness of her left eye. She reports that her eyelashes are rubbing against her eye.

The left lower eyelid is turned inward with the eyelash margin contacting the corneal surface (trichiasis from entropion). Corneal epithelial staining is present from mechanical abrasion. Horizontal lid laxity is present.
The entropion can be temporarily reduced by pulling the lid downward. It recurs with forced eyelid closure (demonstrating orbicularis override component).
The patient has age-related changes with lower lid retractor dehiscence, horizontal laxity, and orbicularis overriding.

Temporary: taping the lower lid or botulinum toxin to the pretarsal orbicularis. Definitive: surgical correction combining lower lid retractor reinsertion, horizontal lid-tightening (lateral tarsal strip), and/or everting sutures.
teachingPoints: Involutional entropion results from a combination of lower lid retractor dehiscence, horizontal lid laxity, and overriding of the preseptal orbicularis muscle.
Corneal ulceration and scarring can result from chronic lash contact with the corneal surface.
Everting sutures (Quickert sutures) are a quick office-based temporizing procedure with ~70% success rate.
The surgical repair addresses all three pathologic components for the lowest recurrence rate.

clues: A 30-year-old male presents with vision loss in his right eye after a motor vehicle accident where he struck his forehead on the steering wheel.
Visual acuity is light perception OD. There is a right RAPD. The optic disc appears normal acutely (retrobulbar optic nerve injury). No open globe injury. Color vision is absent OD.
CT orbits/head shows a fracture of the orbital apex and optic canal. No intracranial hemorrhage. MRI shows edema of the intracanalicular optic nerve without hematoma.
The patient has forehead and periorbital ecchymosis. The vision loss was noted immediately after the trauma.

High-dose IV methylprednisolone was historically used (CRASH trial raised concerns). Optic canal decompression surgery is considered for progressive vision loss with documented canal fracture. No intervention has proven superior to observation.
teachingPoints: Traumatic optic neuropathy occurs from indirect injury (transmitted force from periorbital trauma) to the optic nerve, most commonly in the intracanalicular segment where the nerve is most tightly confined.
The CRASH trial raised concerns about high-dose steroids in head injury; the optimal treatment for TON remains controversial with no level 1 evidence for any intervention.
The disc appears normal initially because the injury is behind the eye (retrobulbar); optic atrophy develops 4-6 weeks later.
Prognosis depends on initial visual acuity; patients with light perception or better at presentation have a better chance of recovery than those with no light perception.

clues: A 55-year-old female presents with gradual bilateral blurred central vision and difficulty reading over several years.

Visual acuity is 20/50 bilaterally. Fundoscopy shows subtle graying of the parafoveal retina (temporal more than nasal), with right-angle venules and crystalline deposits in the temporal parafoveal area.

OCT shows loss of the ellipsoid zone temporally, intraretinal hyporeflective cavities (not true cysts—they don't leak on FA), and inner retinal draping over the outer retinal defect. FA shows temporal parafoveal telangiectasia with late staining but minimal leakage. Macular pigment density is reduced.
The patient has no history of diabetes. The condition is bilateral and symmetric. Her brother has similar findings.
No proven treatment for the degenerative component. Anti-VEGF for subretinal neovascularization (occurs in ~15% of cases). CNTF (ciliary neurotrophic factor) implant is under investigation.
teachingPoints: MacTel2 is a bilateral, acquired condition of unknown etiology affecting Müller cell and photoreceptor function in the parafoveal region, predominantly in patients aged 40-60.
The parafoveal cavities on OCT are not true cystoid macular edema—they represent neurodegenerative changes and do NOT respond to anti-VEGF or steroids.
Loss of macular pigment (lutein and zeaxanthin) in the temporal parafovea is an early sign detectable by fundus autofluorescence and macular pigment optical density testing.
Subretinal neovascularization is the main treatable complication and is the leading cause of severe vision loss in MacTel2.

clues: A 16-year-old female presents with progressive bilateral central vision loss over the past year. She has difficulty reading the whiteboard at school.
Visual acuity is 20/100 bilaterally. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral beaten-bronze macular atrophy with yellow-white pisciform (fish-shaped) flecks at the level of the RPE extending beyond the arcades.

Fundus autofluorescence shows a characteristic dark choroid (absent background fluorescence) with hyperautofluorescent flecks. OCT shows loss of the ellipsoid zone at the fovea. ERG may be normal or show cone-rod pattern loss.
Her parents are unaffected carriers. Genetic testing confirms biallelic ABCA4 gene mutations (autosomal recessive).
No proven treatment currently. Avoid excessive vitamin A supplementation (worsens lipofuscin accumulation). Low vision rehabilitation. Gene therapy and complement inhibitor trials are underway.
teachingPoints: Stargardt disease is the most common inherited macular dystrophy, caused by ABCA4 mutations leading to accumulation of toxic lipofuscin (A2E) in the RPE.
The 'dark choroid' on FA is caused by masking of choroidal fluorescence by excessive lipofuscin in the RPE—this is nearly pathognomonic.
Excessive vitamin A supplementation is contraindicated because the ABCA4 transporter normally removes toxic retinoid byproducts, and extra vitamin A increases their accumulation.
Visual prognosis is variable but most patients decline to 20/200 or worse; peripheral vision is usually preserved.

clues: A 5-year-old boy presents with nystagmus, photophobia, and reduced visual acuity. His skin and hair color are normal.

Visual acuity is 20/100 bilaterally with pendular nystagmus. Slit-lamp shows iris transillumination defects. Fundoscopy reveals foveal hypoplasia (absent foveal reflex) and blonde fundus with visible choroidal vessels due to reduced RPE melanin.
OCT confirms foveal hypoplasia (absence of the normal foveal pit). VEP shows crossed asymmetry (excess crossing of temporal retinal fibers at the chiasm). Genetic testing shows a GPR143 mutation.
The patient's maternal grandfather had similar visual problems. The mother is an asymptomatic carrier with a mud-splattered (mosaic) fundus pattern on exam.
Tinted glasses or contact lenses for photophobia. Correction of refractive error (high astigmatism is common). Low vision aids. Strabismus surgery if significant.
teachingPoints: X-linked ocular albinism results from GPR143 gene mutations affecting melanin biosynthesis in the RPE and iris, while skin and hair pigmentation are relatively normal.
Foveal hypoplasia is the primary cause of reduced vision; it occurs because melanin is required for normal foveal development in utero.
VEP crossed asymmetry (misrouting of optic nerve fibers at the chiasm) is characteristic of all forms of albinism and is a useful diagnostic test.
Female carriers often show a characteristic mosaic or mud-splattered pattern of RPE pigmentation on dilated exam.

clues: A 50-year-old female with a history of Graves disease treated with radioactive iodine presents with persistent vertical diplopia despite being euthyroid.
There is a left hypotropia worse in upgaze. The left eye cannot elevate fully. Forced duction testing is positive—the inferior rectus is tight and fibrotic, restricting upgaze.
CT orbits shows enlarged, fibrotic inferior and medial rectus muscles bilaterally, left worse than right. The tendon insertions are thickened (fibrotic phase). Hertel shows mild residual proptosis.
The patient's TED has been clinically inactive (CAS <3) for over 12 months. She has stable measurements but persistent diplopia affecting daily activities.
Strabismus surgery with inferior rectus recession on adjustable sutures. Surgery is performed only after the disease has been stable and inactive for at least 6 months.
teachingPoints: Thyroid-related restrictive strabismus is the most common cause of acquired strabismus in adults and results from fibrosis of the extraocular muscles in the chronic (inactive) phase of TED.
The inferior rectus is the most commonly affected muscle, causing restriction of upgaze and hypotropia; the medial rectus is second most common.
Strabismus surgery should be delayed until the disease is inactive and measurements are stable for at least 6 months to prevent the need for reoperation.
Adjustable sutures are preferred due to the unpredictable dose-response relationship of operating on fibrotic muscles.

clues: A 65-year-old Caucasian male presents with a growing pigmented lesion on the bulbar conjunctiva of his right eye noticed over the past 6 months.

A raised, nodular, darkly pigmented lesion with irregular borders and feeder vessels is present on the temporal bulbar conjunctiva. There are areas of surrounding flat pigmentation (PAM with atypia) extending onto the cornea.
Incisional biopsy confirms invasive melanoma arising from primary acquired melanosis with atypia. Sentinel lymph node mapping of the preauricular and submandibular nodes is performed.
The patient has a history of extensive UV exposure. The pigmented patch has been present for years but the nodular component is new.
Wide local excision with 'no-touch' technique, cryotherapy to the margins, and adjunctive topical mitomycin C or interferon for residual PAM. Sentinel lymph node biopsy for staging. Systemic workup.
teachingPoints: Conjunctival melanoma can arise de novo (25%), from primary acquired melanosis with atypia (75%), or rarely from a pre-existing nevus.
PAM with atypia is the most important precursor lesion; PAM without atypia has minimal malignant potential.
Conjunctival melanoma has a 10-year mortality rate of approximately 25-30%, with metastasis occurring via lymphatic (preauricular, submandibular nodes) and hematogenous routes.
Local recurrence is common (~50%); long-term surveillance with serial photography and exfoliative cytology is essential.

clues: A 35-year-old male immigrant from South Asia presents with chronic bilateral blurred vision, floaters, and recurrent anterior uveitis over the past year that responds temporarily to steroids.

Bilateral granulomatous anterior uveitis with mutton-fat KPs and broad-based posterior synechiae. Fundoscopy shows multifocal choroidal granulomas (yellowish deep lesions) and a serpiginous-like choroiditis pattern with vitritis.
QuantiFERON-TB Gold is strongly positive. Chest X-ray shows hilar lymphadenopathy and an old calcified granuloma. OCT shows choroidal granulomas as sub-RPE elevations.
The patient emigrated from a TB-endemic region 5 years ago. He has no pulmonary symptoms. Sputum is AFB-negative.
Anti-tubercular therapy (ATT: isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) for at least 6-9 months plus systemic corticosteroids (started 1-2 weeks after initiating ATT).
teachingPoints: Presumed ocular TB is diagnosed based on clinical features consistent with TB, positive immunologic evidence (QuantiFERON or tuberculin skin test), and response to ATT, even without culture confirmation.
Tubercular serpiginous-like choroiditis is a distinct entity more common in TB-endemic regions; unlike true serpiginous choroidopathy, it is associated with vitritis and multifocal choroiditis.
Ethambutol ocular toxicity must be monitored during ATT; baseline and monthly visual acuity and color vision testing are recommended.
Corticosteroids alone (without ATT) can worsen ocular TB; they should always be given with anti-tubercular cover.

clues: A 45-year-old male presents to the ER after being struck in the eye by a metal fragment while using a hammer and chisel without safety glasses.

Visual acuity is hand motions. The eye is soft with hypotony (IOP <5 mmHg by palpation—do NOT measure with tonometer). There is a full-thickness corneal laceration with iris prolapse, a shallow anterior chamber, and a teardrop-shaped (peaked) pupil pointing toward the wound.

CT orbits (NO MRI due to possible metallic IOFB) shows a metallic intraocular foreign body in the vitreous cavity and a disrupted globe contour. Seidel test is positive (fluorescein streaming from the wound).
The injury occurred from a high-velocity metal-on-metal strike. The patient is up-to-date on tetanus vaccination.
Rigid eye shield placement (no pressure), IV antibiotics (vancomycin + ceftazidime), tetanus prophylaxis, NPO status, and urgent surgical repair with wound closure and IOFB removal.
teachingPoints: Open globe injury is defined as a full-thickness wound of the cornea and/or sclera; it includes rupture (blunt), laceration, penetrating injury, perforating injury (entrance and exit), and IOFB.
A peaked (teardrop) pupil pointing toward the wound is a classic sign of globe rupture with iris incarceration.
CT is the imaging modality of choice; MRI is absolutely contraindicated if a metallic foreign body is suspected.
Endophthalmitis risk is highest with IOFB (7-13%); intravitreal antibiotics at the time of primary repair reduce this risk.

clues: A 35-year-old male with a history of IV drug abuse presents with gradually worsening vision in both eyes.

Fundoscopy reveals innumerable tiny, glistening, refractile yellow-white crystalline deposits in the small retinal arterioles and capillaries of the macular region bilaterally. There are areas of capillary non-perfusion peripherally.

FA shows arteriolar occlusion and ischemia in the macular and peripheral retina. OCT shows the refractile deposits in the inner retinal layers. Peripheral neovascularization is present in the right eye.
The patient has been injecting crushed oral tablets (methylphenidate) intravenously for years. The talc filler in the tablets accumulates in the pulmonary and then retinal vasculature.
PRP for peripheral neovascularization. Anti-VEGF for macular edema. Cessation of IV drug use is critical. No method exists to remove the deposited talc particles.
teachingPoints: Talc retinopathy occurs when insoluble filler particles (talc, cornstarch) from crushed oral medications injected IV lodge in the retinal microvasculature, causing ischemia.
The particles first deposit in the pulmonary vasculature; as pulmonary arteriovenous shunts develop from chronic embolization, particles bypass the lungs and reach the retinal circulation.
Macular ischemia from capillary occlusion is the primary cause of visual loss; peripheral ischemia can lead to neovascularization and vitreous hemorrhage.
The glistening refractile deposits in the perifoveal arterioles are characteristic and should raise suspicion for IV drug abuse.

clues: A 45-year-old female presents with a dull, deep, boring pain in her left eye that radiates to the brow and temple, with associated blurred vision and mild proptosis.

There is mild proptosis, restricted motility, and choroidal folds on fundoscopy. A dome-shaped choroidal mass is seen inferiorly, mimicking a choroidal tumor. Disc edema and exudative retinal detachment are present.
B-scan ultrasound shows the pathognomonic 'T-sign': fluid in the sub-Tenon space outlining the optic nerve, creating a T-shaped echolucent space. CT shows scleral thickening.
The patient has rheumatoid arthritis. She was initially referred for evaluation of a possible choroidal melanoma. The 'tumor' is actually scleral/choroidal thickening from inflammation.
Systemic NSAIDs for mild cases. Oral corticosteroids or immunosuppressive agents (mycophenolate) for moderate to severe cases. Must treat the underlying autoimmune condition.
teachingPoints: Posterior scleritis is often misdiagnosed because it can mimic choroidal tumors, orbital disease, and other intraocular pathology.
The T-sign on B-scan (fluid around the optic nerve in sub-Tenon space) is pathognomonic and the single most useful diagnostic finding.
Associated systemic conditions include rheumatoid arthritis, granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Wegener), relapsing polychondritis, and SLE.
Unlike anterior scleritis where the inflammation is visible, posterior scleritis is hidden behind the equator and requires imaging for diagnosis.

clues: A 30-year-old male is found to have a small white ring on the cornea during a routine contact lens check. He is asymptomatic.
Slit-lamp reveals a small (1 mm), well-defined, white ring at the level of Bowman layer in the inferior paracentral cornea. There is no epithelial defect, infiltrate, or inflammation.
The ring is a residual iron deposit. There is no active foreign body. No staining with fluorescein. Visual acuity is 20/20.
The patient recalls getting 'something in his eye' about a year ago that fell out on its own. He did not seek medical attention at the time.
No treatment is necessary. The ring is a benign remnant and is visually insignificant.
teachingPoints: Coat's white ring is a small, well-defined ring of iron (ferritin) deposit in Bowman layer, representing the remnant of a previous metallic corneal foreign body that rusted in situ.
It is benign, visually insignificant, and requires no treatment or removal.
It should not be confused with a Fleischer ring (keratoconus), Kayser-Fleischer ring (Wilson disease), or Wessely immune ring (infectious keratitis).
The ring persists indefinitely as a harmless corneal scar and is a common incidental finding on slit-lamp exam.

clues: An 8-year-old girl is referred after failing a school vision screening. Her father also has poor vision that was never fully explained.

Visual acuity is 20/60 bilaterally. Both optic discs show temporal pallor (wedge-shaped pallor of the temporal disc). Color vision testing reveals a tritanopia (blue-yellow color defect). Pupils are sluggishly reactive without RAPD (symmetric involvement).
OCT RNFL shows bilateral temporal thinning of the nerve fiber layer. Visual fields reveal bilateral central or cecocentral scotomas. MRI brain and orbits are normal.
The father has similar disc pallor with 20/80 vision. Genetic testing reveals an OPA1 gene mutation (autosomal dominant). The family history spans three generations.
No proven treatment. Low vision rehabilitation. Idebenone is sometimes tried (limited evidence). Genetic counseling. Avoid mitochondrial toxins (tobacco, alcohol).
teachingPoints: Autosomal dominant optic atrophy (Kjer disease) is the most common inherited optic neuropathy, caused by OPA1 gene mutations affecting mitochondrial dynamics in retinal ganglion cells.
The classic findings are insidious bilateral temporal disc pallor, blue-yellow dyschromatopsia (tritanopia), and cecocentral scotomas, typically presenting in the first decade.
Tritanopia distinguishes DOA from LHON (which causes red-green dyschromatopsia) and from other optic neuropathies.
Approximately 20% of DOA patients develop 'DOA plus' syndrome with sensorineural hearing loss, myopathy, and progressive external ophthalmoplegia.

clues: A 28-year-old female presents with bilateral foreign body sensation, tearing, photophobia, and mildly blurred vision that has waxed and waned for months.

Slit-lamp shows bilateral scattered coarse, stellate, granular, raised epithelial opacities in the central cornea. The conjunctiva is white and quiet (no injection). There is no staining with fluorescein around the lesions.
The opacities are limited to the epithelium and have a slightly negative fluorescein staining pattern (raised lesions deflect the dye). No anterior chamber reaction. No corneal neovascularization.
The condition has recurred 4 times over 2 years with symptom-free intervals. It does not respond to artificial tears or antibiotics alone.

Low-dose topical corticosteroid (fluorometholone or loteprednol) provides dramatic relief but recurrence is common upon cessation. Therapeutic soft contact lens or topical cyclosporine are steroid-sparing alternatives.
teachingPoints: Thygeson SPK is a chronic, bilateral, relapsing epithelial keratitis of unknown etiology—the hallmark is coarse raised epithelial opacities in a white, quiet eye.
The condition is self-limited but can recur for years; each episode responds dramatically to mild topical steroids.
The white, quiet eye distinguishes TSPK from viral keratitis (which causes injection) and dry eye (which causes diffuse SPK, not focal raised opacities).
There is no associated systemic disease; it is purely a corneal condition.

clues: A 60-year-old male from West Africa presents with a painful, progressive peripheral corneal ulcer in his right eye that has been advancing circumferentially and centrally over weeks.

A crescent-shaped peripheral corneal ulcer with an undermined, overhanging central edge is present. The ulcer base is vascularized. There is stromal thinning progressing centrally. The conjunctiva adjacent to the ulcer is inflamed.

Corneal cultures are negative for bacteria, fungi, and viruses. Autoimmune and vasculitis workup is negative (no associated systemic disease). The ulcer does not respond to intensive antibiotic therapy.
The patient has hepatitis C. He is from an endemic region for helminthic infections. The ulcer is unilateral and painful (aggressive unilateral type).

Conjunctival resection adjacent to the ulcer (removes the source of destructive enzymes). Topical and systemic immunosuppression (cyclosporine, mycophenolate). Lamellar keratoplasty for severe thinning.
teachingPoints: Mooren ulcer is a painful, idiopathic, progressive peripheral corneal ulceration with NO associated systemic vasculitis (distinguishing it from PUK).
Two clinical types: bilateral (younger patients, West African, less aggressive, associated with helminthic infections) and unilateral (older patients, more aggressive, more painful).
The key distinction from PUK is the absence of associated systemic vasculitis; PUK occurs in the setting of RA, GPA, or other systemic disease.
Conjunctival resection adjacent to the ulcer reduces the local immune attack by removing the conjunctival source of inflammatory cells and matrix metalloproteinases.

clues: A 60-year-old female presents with involuntary, bilateral, forceful, symmetric eyelid closure that has been progressively worsening over the past year, causing functional blindness.

Bilateral involuntary spasms of the orbicularis oculi and adjacent facial muscles occur frequently throughout the day. The spasms are exacerbated by bright light, stress, and fatigue. She cannot keep her eyes open voluntarily during spasms.
Neurological examination is otherwise normal. MRI brain is unremarkable. There is no apraxia of eyelid opening. The spasms are absent during sleep.

The patient initially developed increased blinking, which progressed to forceful involuntary closure. She has difficulty driving and reading. She tries pulling her lids open with her fingers (sensory trick—geste antagoniste).
Botulinum toxin injection (onabotulinumtoxinA) into the orbicularis oculi and procerus muscles every 3-4 months is the first-line treatment.
teachingPoints: Benign essential blepharospasm is a focal dystonia of the orbicularis oculi muscles; the cause is thought to involve basal ganglia dysfunction.
Botulinum toxin injection is highly effective (>90% improvement) and is the standard of care; the effect lasts approximately 3-4 months.
A sensory trick (touching the periorbital skin, wearing glasses, talking) can temporarily suppress the spasms—this is characteristic of focal dystonias.
Myectomy (surgical removal of the protractor muscles) is reserved for patients who do not respond adequately to botulinum toxin.

clues: A 30-year-old male presents with bilateral decreased vision 24 hours after sustaining a severe crush injury to the chest in a motor vehicle accident.

Visual acuity is 20/200 bilaterally. Fundoscopy shows bilateral multiple white retinal patches (Purtscher flecken) in the peripapillary and macular region, along with cotton-wool spots and retinal hemorrhages. There is disc edema.
FA shows arteriolar non-perfusion and retinal ischemia in the posterior pole. OCT shows inner retinal thickening and hyperreflectivity from ischemia.
The patient had a severe thoracic compression injury. He has also developed acute pancreatitis. No direct ocular trauma occurred.
No proven treatment. Observation; many patients recover some vision spontaneously over months. Systemic corticosteroids have been used anecdotally but lack evidence.
teachingPoints: Purtscher retinopathy is an occlusive microvasculopathy caused by complement activation and microemboli (fibrin, fat, air) reaching the retinal vasculature after trauma.
Purtscher-like retinopathy refers to the same fundus picture occurring without trauma—causes include acute pancreatitis, SLE, thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura, and renal failure.
Purtscher flecken are polygonal patches of retinal whitening between retinal arterioles, representing ischemic inner retinal edema with a clear zone around the optic disc.
Visual prognosis is variable; approximately 50% of patients recover to 20/60 or better, but some have permanent severe visual loss.

clues: A 20-year-old female presents with progressively worsening vision in both eyes since her teenage years.

Slit-lamp shows bilateral diffuse corneal stromal haze extending from limbus to limbus (including the peripheral cornea), with focal, dense, gray-white, irregular stromal opacities. The intervening stroma between opacities is also hazy (not clear).

The cornea is thinner than normal (reduced pachymetry). Histopathology of excised tissue would show glycosaminoglycan (GAG) deposits staining with Alcian blue and colloidal iron. Genetic testing shows CHST6 gene mutation.
Both parents are unaffected carriers (autosomal recessive). The patient's vision is 20/80 bilaterally at age 20.
Penetrating keratoplasty or deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty (DALK) when vision is significantly impaired. Recurrence in the graft is possible but less common than in other TGFBI dystrophies.
teachingPoints: Macular corneal dystrophy is the only autosomal recessive corneal dystrophy among the classic stromal dystrophies (granular and lattice are autosomal dominant).
Unlike granular (discrete deposits, clear intervening stroma) and lattice (branching lines), macular dystrophy has diffuse haze WITH focal opacities—the intervening stroma is NOT clear.
The cornea is characteristically thinner than normal (the opposite of Fuchs endothelial dystrophy), because the abnormal GAGs affect stromal hydration.
It presents earlier and progresses faster than granular or lattice dystrophy, often requiring keratoplasty by the 3rd-4th decade.

clues: A 65-year-old male notices a new central scotoma in his right eye 1 day after prolonged cataract surgery complicated by a dropped nucleus requiring vitrectomy.

Visual acuity is 20/80 OD (expected to be better after surgery). Fundoscopy reveals a well-demarcated, oval, yellowish lesion at the fovea corresponding to the shape of the coaxial illumination from the operating microscope.
OCT shows a focal disruption of the outer retinal layers (ellipsoid zone) at the fovea, matching the area of the fundus lesion. FA shows a window defect in the corresponding area.
The surgery lasted over 3 hours with intense coaxial illumination. The lesion shape matches the microscope light projection on the retina.
No treatment available. Observation; partial recovery is possible over weeks to months as some photoreceptor recovery can occur, but permanent foveal damage may remain.
teachingPoints: Photic retinopathy from operating microscope light is a photochemical injury (blue light toxicity) to the foveal photoreceptors and RPE, caused by prolonged exposure during intraocular surgery.
The risk increases with longer surgical duration, higher light intensity, shorter wavelength (blue light), and a clear (aphakic/pseudophakic) optical media.
Preventive measures include using yellow filters on the microscope, minimizing light intensity, intermittent illumination, and using oblique rather than coaxial illumination.
The lesion often has a characteristic oval or rectangular shape corresponding to the microscope light footprint on the retina.

clues: A 2-year-old boy is brought in with a droopy right upper eyelid that has been present since birth. He tilts his chin up to see.

The right upper lid margin covers 3 mm of the superior pupil (moderate ptosis). Levator function is poor (4 mm excursion, normal >15 mm). The lid crease is absent or high. On downgaze, the right lid hangs higher than the left (lid lag—the ptotic lid does not descend).
Bell phenomenon is good. The pupil is normal (no Horner syndrome). Cornea is clear (no amblyopia from deprivation). Cycloplegic refraction shows mild astigmatism OD.
The ptosis is unilateral and has been present since birth. There is no jaw-winking (Marcus Gunn phenomenon). Levator function is poor.
Frontalis sling procedure (with silicone rod, PTFE, or fascia lata) is indicated for severe ptosis with poor levator function. Levator resection for moderate ptosis with fair levator function.
teachingPoints: Congenital ptosis is most commonly caused by dystrophy of the levator palpebrae superioris muscle, in which muscle fibers are replaced by fibrotic and adipose tissue.
The fibrotic muscle cannot contract normally (causing ptosis) OR relax normally (causing lid lag on downgaze)—both features together are pathognomonic.
Amblyopia from visual axis occlusion or astigmatism is the primary concern; early surgery is indicated if the visual axis is compromised.
Frontalis sling is preferred over levator resection when levator function is <4 mm, as the fibrotic muscle cannot be adequately shortened.

clues: A 1-year-old infant is noted to have a droopy left eyelid that dramatically elevates when the baby is suckling or chewing.

There is moderate left upper lid ptosis at rest. When the child opens the mouth widely, moves the jaw to the contralateral side, or suckles, the ptotic left eyelid briskly retracts (elevates) to a normal or even retracted position.
The jaw-winking phenomenon is reproducible and dramatic. Levator function is moderate to good. No other cranial nerve abnormalities.
The phenomenon was first noticed by the mother during breastfeeding. There is no family history of similar findings.

Observation if the ptosis and wink are mild. For significant ptosis with prominent winking: levator muscle disinsertion bilaterally + bilateral frontalis sling to eliminate the synkinetic movement and provide symmetric lift.
teachingPoints: Marcus Gunn jaw-winking is a congenital synkinesis caused by aberrant innervation of the levator palpebrae by a branch of the trigeminal nerve (motor division V3, pterygoid branch).
It accounts for approximately 5% of congenital ptosis cases and is almost always unilateral.
Bilateral levator disinsertion (Beard procedure) + bilateral frontalis sling eliminates the synkinetic wink by disconnecting the levator from its aberrant motor supply.
Amblyopia must be monitored, although the intermittent lid elevation during feeding and oral activity usually keeps the visual axis clear.

clues: A 60-year-old female presents with chronic persistent epiphora (tearing) from both eyes. She has no eye pain or redness.

The lower lacrimal puncta are tiny, pinpoint, or fibrosed shut bilaterally. Fluorescein dye disappearance test shows retained dye at 5 minutes. There is no distension of the lacrimal sac (no dacryocystitis).
Probing of the punctum meets resistance; irrigation (once punctum is dilated) shows patent nasolacrimal duct. The problem is isolated to the punctal opening.
The patient has been on chronic topical anti-glaucoma drops (particularly those with BAK preservative) for years. She also has chronic blepharitis.
Punctal dilation with or without 1-snip punctoplasty (vertical incision to enlarge the opening). Mini-Monoka stent placement through the newly dilated punctum for 2-3 months.
teachingPoints: Punctal stenosis is a common cause of epiphora in older adults, often caused by chronic inflammation from topical medications (especially those with benzalkonium chloride preservative), blepharitis, or cicatrizing conditions.
Docetaxel (chemotherapy) is a well-known systemic cause of canalicular and punctal stenosis.
The diagnosis is made at the slit lamp: the punctum appears narrowed, fibrosed, or absent. Irrigation after dilation confirms the rest of the system is patent.
Punctoplasty is a simple, office-based procedure with a high success rate; recurrence is prevented by addressing the underlying cause (switching to preservative-free drops, treating blepharitis).

clues: A 7-year-old boy presents with bilateral decreased vision found on school screening. He has no other medical problems.
Visual acuity is 20/60 bilaterally. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral foveal schisis with a characteristic 'spoke-wheel' pattern of cystic-like spaces radiating from the fovea.

OCT shows splitting of the retinal layers at the fovea (foveal schisis cavities primarily in the inner nuclear layer). ERG shows a characteristic 'electronegative' pattern (reduced b-wave with preserved a-wave). Genetic testing confirms an RS1 gene mutation.
His maternal uncle has severe visual impairment from the same condition. The mother is an asymptomatic carrier.

No treatment for the foveal schisis. Topical or oral carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (dorzolamide, acetazolamide) can reduce foveal schisis cavities in some patients. Avoid contact sports (risk of vitreous hemorrhage from peripheral schisis).
teachingPoints: X-linked retinoschisis is the most common macular dystrophy in young males, caused by RS1 gene mutations affecting retinoschisin protein (important for retinal cell adhesion).

The spoke-wheel foveal schisis pattern is virtually pathognomonic; peripheral schisis (splitting of the nerve fiber layer from the underlying retina) occurs in approximately 50% and can cause vitreous hemorrhage.
The electronegative ERG (reduced b:a ratio) reflects inner retinal dysfunction and is similar to CSNB, but the clinical presentation and genetics are distinct.
Gene therapy trials for XLRS (intravitreal RS1 gene delivery) are actively ongoing with promising early results.

clues: A 20-year-old female presents for evaluation of a pigmented spot on the white of her right eye that has been present since childhood.

A flat-to-slightly-elevated, well-circumscribed, brown pigmented lesion is present on the bulbar conjunctiva near the limbus. Intralesional clear cysts (inclusion cysts) are visible within the lesion. The lesion moves freely with the conjunctiva.
Anterior segment OCT shows a thin, well-defined, flat-to-slightly-elevated lesion with intrinsic cysts. There is no feeder vessel. Anterior segment photography is obtained for serial comparison.
The lesion has been stable in size and color since childhood. There are no recent changes, no feeder vessels, and no inflammation.
Observation with serial photography. Excisional biopsy if growth, color change, or new vascularity develops.
teachingPoints: Conjunctival nevi are the most common melanocytic lesion of the conjunctiva, typically appearing in the first two decades of life.
Intralesional cysts (clear, round, epithelial inclusion cysts) are a hallmark feature of benign nevi and are NOT seen in melanoma.
Malignant transformation to melanoma is rare (<1%), but any documented growth, color change, or new vascularity warrants biopsy.
Most conjunctival nevi are junctional or compound; they can darken during puberty, pregnancy, or with UV exposure.

clues: A 40-year-old female presents with sudden, severe eye pain upon waking that is accompanied by tearing, photophobia, and blurred vision. She has had multiple identical episodes over the past year.

Slit-lamp shows a focal area of loose, irregular corneal epithelium with a small epithelial defect in the right eye. There is an underlying map-dot-fingerprint pattern (epithelial basement membrane dystrophy) visible in both corneas.
Negative staining (pooling of fluorescein in a depression) marks the area of epithelial irregularity. After the acute epithelial defect heals, the underlying EBMD pattern becomes visible.
She had a fingernail scratch to her cornea 2 years ago, after which the recurrent episodes began. The episodes consistently occur upon first opening her eyes in the morning.

Acute: lubrication ointment, bandage contact lens, cycloplegic. Prophylaxis: bedtime lubricating ointment for months. For refractory cases: anterior stromal micropuncture or PTK (phototherapeutic keratectomy).
teachingPoints: Recurrent erosions occur because the corneal epithelium fails to form proper adhesion complexes (hemidesmosomes) to the underlying basement membrane, allowing it to be sheared off by lid friction.
Morning onset is characteristic: overnight the lids adhere to the poorly attached epithelium, and upon waking, the eyelid movement avulses the epithelium.

Underlying epithelial basement membrane dystrophy (EBMD/map-dot-fingerprint dystrophy) is the most common predisposing condition; prior corneal trauma (especially fingernail, paper cut) is the other major cause.
PTK removes the abnormal epithelium and superficial Bowman layer, promoting formation of new, well-anchored basement membrane with a >90% success rate.

clues: A cluster of 5 patients develop severe anterior segment inflammation within 12-24 hours after cataract surgery performed on the same day at the same surgery center.

All patients show diffuse corneal edema (limbus-to-limbus), marked anterior chamber fibrin and cells, and hypopyon. However, there is minimal pain compared to the severity of inflammation (unlike endophthalmitis). IOP is elevated in some cases.
Gram stain and cultures of anterior chamber tap are negative for organisms. The inflammation began within 24 hours (endophthalmitis typically presents 3-7 days postoperatively).
Investigation reveals that the surgical center's autoclave had a malfunction, leading to residual detergent on the instruments. All affected cases share common instruments and solutions.
Intensive topical corticosteroids (prednisolone acetate every 1-2 hours). IOP management. Most cases respond well if treated promptly, though corneal endothelial damage may be permanent.
teachingPoints: TASS is a sterile, acute postoperative anterior segment inflammation caused by a non-infectious contaminant introduced during surgery (detergent, preservative, endotoxin, denatured OVD, wrong BSS concentration).
TASS differs from endophthalmitis in onset (12-24 hours vs. 3-7 days), pain (less pain in TASS), vitreous involvement (absent in TASS), and culture results (negative in TASS).
Limbus-to-limbus corneal edema (diffuse endothelial damage) is characteristic of TASS; in endophthalmitis, corneal edema is typically more focal.
A cluster of cases on the same surgical day strongly suggests TASS from a contaminated shared substance or instrument.

clues: A 45-year-old male with muscle weakness and difficulty releasing his grip presents for an eye exam due to worsening vision.

Slit-lamp shows bilateral polychromatic ('Christmas tree') cataracts with iridescent, multicolored opacities in the posterior subcapsular and cortical regions. Bilateral ptosis with poor levator function is present.
Pupils are small and react poorly to light (miotic pupils with light-near dissociation). IOP is low (10 mmHg bilaterally). Corneal endothelium is normal.

The patient has myotonia (difficulty releasing a handshake), frontal balding, temporal wasting, and a hatchet-faced appearance. EMG shows myotonic discharges. Genetic testing reveals a CTG trinucleotide repeat expansion in DMPK gene.

Cataract surgery for visually significant cataracts (with caution—increased risk of complications). Ptosis repair if indicated. Cardiac evaluation is essential before any surgery (risk of cardiac arrhythmias).
teachingPoints: Myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) is the most common adult muscular dystrophy; Christmas tree cataracts are nearly pathognomonic and may be the presenting finding.
The iridescent, polychromatic, multicolored cortical opacities are distinct from typical age-related cataracts and should prompt evaluation for myotonic dystrophy.
Cardiac conduction abnormalities (heart block) are common and potentially life-threatening; ECG is mandatory before any surgical procedure.
Patients with myotonic dystrophy have increased sensitivity to anesthetic agents and are at higher risk of respiratory complications during surgery.

clues: A 45-year-old female presents with slowly progressive painless vision loss in her right eye over 2 years, along with mild proptosis.
Visual acuity is 20/100 OD. There is a right RAPD. The right optic disc shows optociliary shunt vessels (retinochoroidal collateral vessels) on the disc surface. Mild proptosis is present.

MRI orbits with gadolinium shows 'tram-track sign'—enhancement of the optic nerve sheath surrounding a dark central optic nerve on axial views. On coronal view, the nerve shows a ring of enhancement around the nerve.
The lesion enhances brightly and uniformly with gadolinium. The optic nerve itself does not enhance (distinguishing from optic nerve glioma which enhances diffusely).

Fractionated stereotactic radiotherapy is the treatment of choice for progressive vision loss, preserving vision in many cases. Observation for stable, mild cases. Surgery is reserved for blind eyes with disfiguring proptosis.
teachingPoints: Optic nerve sheath meningioma arises from the meningothelial cap cells of the arachnoid surrounding the optic nerve; it is most common in middle-aged women.

The classic triad is progressive vision loss, optic atrophy, and optociliary shunt vessels—the shunt vessels develop as the tumor compresses the central retinal vein, creating collateral drainage pathways.
The tram-track sign on MRI (peripheral enhancement around a dark central nerve) is virtually pathognomonic.
Unlike optic nerve glioma (which is more common in children and NF1), meningioma is more common in middle-aged women without NF1 (though NF2 is associated).

clues: A 40-year-old male presents with progressively worsening vision despite updated spectacle prescriptions. He has high against-the-rule astigmatism.

Slit-lamp shows bilateral inferior peripheral corneal thinning in a crescentic band 1-2 mm from the inferior limbus. The area of maximum protrusion is ABOVE the area of maximum thinning (unlike keratoconus where protrusion and thinning coincide). The central cornea is clear.
Corneal topography reveals a characteristic 'crab-claw' or 'butterfly' pattern with steep inferotemporal and inferonasal axes against a flat superior cornea. Pachymetry confirms inferior thinning.
The patient has been told he has 'irregular astigmatism' but contact lens fitting has been unsuccessful. He does not have keratoconus on standard screening.

Scleral or large-diameter RGP contact lenses for visual rehabilitation. Crescentic lamellar keratoplasty for progressive thinning. Corneal cross-linking may be considered, though less studied than in keratoconus.
teachingPoints: PMD is a non-inflammatory corneal thinning disorder affecting the inferior peripheral cornea, causing against-the-rule astigmatism and the classic crab-claw topography pattern.
The key distinction from keratoconus: in PMD, maximum protrusion is above the band of thinning (the cornea buckles above the thin zone), whereas in keratoconus, the cone and thinning coincide.
Fleischer ring and Vogt striae are absent in PMD (they are signs of keratoconus).
Standard keratometry and topography can miss PMD; inferior corneal thinning must be specifically evaluated.

clues: A 6-month-old infant with a facial port-wine stain on the right side involving the upper eyelid is referred for elevated IOP.

There is a right-sided port-wine stain (nevus flammeus) in the V1 distribution involving the forehead and upper eyelid. IOP is 28 mmHg OD. The right eye has a deep anterior chamber, episcleral hemangioma (dilated episcleral vessels), and a diffuse choroidal hemangioma producing a 'tomato-ketchup' red fundus.
Gonioscopy shows a developmental angle anomaly with a high iris insertion. B-scan shows diffuse choroidal thickening. MRI brain reveals a leptomeningeal angioma in the right parieto-occipital region.
The port-wine stain involves V1 (upper eyelid) which carries the highest risk of glaucoma. The child has had seizures.

Glaucoma management: angle surgery (goniotomy/trabeculotomy) for infantile-onset glaucoma; medical therapy or tube shunt for later-onset glaucoma. Monitor for choroidal effusion during filtration surgery.
teachingPoints: Sturge-Weber syndrome is a neurocutaneous disorder with a somatic GNAQ mutation causing a facial port-wine stain, ipsilateral leptomeningeal angioma, and ocular findings (glaucoma, choroidal hemangioma).
Glaucoma occurs in approximately 30-70% of SWS patients when the port-wine stain involves the upper eyelid (V1 dermatome).
Glaucoma results from elevated episcleral venous pressure (from the episcleral hemangioma) and developmental angle anomalies.
Filtration surgery in SWS carries a high risk of intraoperative expulsive choroidal hemorrhage or effusion due to the choroidal hemangioma and elevated venous pressure.

clues: A 3-month-old infant born to an unvaccinated mother who had a febrile rash illness in the first trimester presents with bilateral cataracts and a heart murmur.
Both eyes show dense 'pearl-white' nuclear cataracts. The right eye has microphthalmos. Salt-and-pepper (pigmentary) retinopathy is present in both fundi with a mottled pigment pattern.
Rubella IgM antibody is positive in the infant's serum. Audiologic screening reveals bilateral sensorineural hearing loss. Echocardiography shows patent ductus arteriosus.
The mother traveled to a non-vaccinated region during pregnancy and developed a maculopapular rash at 8 weeks gestation. She was not immune to rubella.
Early cataract extraction to prevent deprivation amblyopia (within first 6 weeks of life). Aggressive amblyopia management. The child may shed virus for up to 1 year (infection control precautions).
teachingPoints: The classic triad of congenital rubella syndrome is cataracts, sensorineural deafness, and cardiac defects (PDA, pulmonary stenosis).
The rubella cataract is characteristically pearly-white and dense, with live rubella virus potentially present within the lens material.
Salt-and-pepper retinopathy of rubella is usually non-progressive and does not cause significant visual impairment by itself.
Congenital rubella is now rare in vaccinated populations but remains a significant cause of congenital blindness in developing countries.

clues: A 55-year-old male presents with gradually worsening vision in his right eye and notices a dark lesion visible through the pupil when dilated.

Dilated exam reveals a dome-shaped, pigmented mass behind the iris in the ciliary body region, visible through a dilated pupil. There is sentinel episcleral vessel (dilated episcleral vessel overlying the tumor). A sector cataract is present in the quadrant of the tumor.

UBM shows a solid, low-to-medium reflectivity mass arising from the ciliary body. Transillumination shows a shadow in the area of the tumor. MRI shows the mass enhancing with gadolinium. B-scan shows characteristic acoustic hollowing.
The tumor has displaced the lens and iris forward, causing a sector shallow anterior chamber. There is secondary angle closure in the involved quadrant with elevated IOP.
Plaque brachytherapy (iodine-125 or ruthenium-106) for smaller tumors. Proton beam radiation for larger tumors. Enucleation for very large tumors with extensive involvement.
teachingPoints: Ciliary body melanomas represent approximately 10% of uveal melanomas and are often diagnosed later because they are hidden behind the iris.
Sentinel (dilated) episcleral vessels overlying the tumor, sector cataract, and lens displacement are classic presenting signs.
Ciliary body melanomas tend to be larger at diagnosis than iris or choroidal melanomas because they grow silently in a hidden location.
Ring melanoma of the ciliary body (circumferential growth involving >180 degrees) carries a particularly poor prognosis.

clues: A 45-year-old HIV-positive male with CD4 count of 15 presents with rapidly progressive bilateral vision loss over a few days.

Fundoscopy reveals bilateral multifocal, deep, white-yellow retinal lesions starting in the posterior pole and rapidly coalescing. There is MINIMAL or NO vitritis. The retinal vasculature appears relatively uninvolved initially.
PCR of aqueous humor is positive for varicella-zoster virus. The lesions are rapidly coalescing and progressing toward the fovea. Retinal detachment is developing.
The patient is severely immunosuppressed (AIDS with CD4 <50). Unlike ARN, there is no significant vitritis (the posterior retinal layers are preferentially affected).
Intravitreal ganciclovir or foscarnet PLUS systemic IV antivirals (ganciclovir or foscarnet). Initiate ART. Prognosis is very poor with retinal detachment rates >70%.
teachingPoints: PORN is a devastating herpesvirus retinitis (usually VZV) occurring in severely immunosuppressed patients, characterized by rapid outer retinal necrosis with minimal inflammation.
