The key distinction from ARN: PORN has NO vitritis (the immune system is too compromised to mount an inflammatory response), starts posteriorly (not peripherally), and progresses much faster.
Retinal detachment occurs in >70% of eyes due to extensive full-thickness retinal necrosis creating multiple retinal breaks.
Visual prognosis is extremely poor; most affected eyes progress to NLP despite aggressive treatment. Bilateral involvement is common.

clues: A 25-year-old asymptomatic male is found to have bilateral unusual pigmentation along the retinal veins during a routine dilated exam.

Fundoscopy reveals bilateral bone-spicule-like pigmentation and chorioretinal atrophy distributed along and adjacent to the retinal veins, extending from the disc to the periphery. The arterioles are relatively spared.

Visual acuity is 20/20 bilaterally. Visual fields show scotomas corresponding to the atrophic areas. ERG is usually normal or mildly abnormal. OCT shows outer retinal loss in the affected perivenous zones.
The condition is bilateral, stable, and was discovered incidentally. The patient has no night blindness or progressive visual symptoms. There is no family history.
No treatment is needed. Observation with periodic monitoring. The condition is typically non-progressive or very slowly progressive.
teachingPoints: PPRCA is a rare, usually non-progressive condition characterized by RPE atrophy and pigment accumulation distributed along the retinal veins.
It must be distinguished from retinitis pigmentosa (which has diffuse bone-spicule pigment not confined to veins, attenuated arterioles, and progressive ERG deterioration).
The cause is unknown; it may represent a post-inflammatory sequela or a developmental anomaly.
Most patients retain good central vision throughout life since the fovea is typically spared.

clues: A 5-year-old boy with known nephropathic cystinosis on cysteamine therapy is referred for eye evaluation due to photophobia and tearing.

Slit-lamp examination reveals innumerable fine, needle-shaped, refractile, polychromatic crystals deposited throughout the full thickness of the corneal stroma bilaterally, most dense in the anterior peripheral cornea. The conjunctiva also has crystal deposits.
The crystals are also visible in the iris, lens capsule, and retina. Fundoscopy shows a patchy depigmented retinopathy. Visual acuity is mildly reduced (20/40).
The child has Fanconi syndrome with rickets, growth retardation, and is on renal replacement therapy. He has the infantile (nephropathic) form of cystinosis (CTNS gene mutation).
Topical cysteamine (Cystaran) eye drops dissolve the corneal crystals and reduce photophobia. Must be used frequently (every waking hour initially). Systemic cysteamine for the systemic disease.
teachingPoints: Cystinosis is a lysosomal storage disease caused by CTNS gene mutations, leading to accumulation of cystine crystals in tissues throughout the body, including the cornea.
Corneal cystine crystals cause photophobia and are present in virtually 100% of patients by age 1-2 years; they are a diagnostic hallmark.
Topical cysteamine 0.44% (Cystaran) or 0.37% (Cystadrops) specifically dissolves the corneal crystals and must be used chronically.
The retinal depigmentation seen in cystinosis can cause progressive visual loss and is not treated by topical cysteamine (requires systemic therapy).

clues: A 40-year-old female presents with slowly progressive, painless proptosis of the left eye noticed over the past year.
There is mild, axial proptosis of the left eye (globe displaced straight forward). Visual acuity is 20/20. Extraocular movements are full. No pulsation, no bruit, no change with Valsalva.

MRI orbits shows a well-circumscribed, ovoid, intraconal mass with progressive ('fill-in') contrast enhancement from periphery to center (pathognomonic). CT shows a smooth, well-encapsulated mass with no bony changes.
The mass does not change in size with position or Valsalva (distinguishing from lymphangioma/venous malformation). It is intraconal and retrobulbar.

Observation if asymptomatic. Surgical excision via lateral orbitotomy if proptosis is significant, vision is affected, or compressive optic neuropathy develops. The lesion is well-encapsulated and shells out cleanly.
teachingPoints: Cavernous hemangioma (now termed cavernous venous malformation) is the most common benign intraconal orbital tumor in adults.
The progressive, centripetal 'fill-in' pattern on dynamic MRI contrast is pathognomonic and reflects the slow blood flow through the cavernous channels.
Unlike capillary hemangiomas (children, proliferative), cavernous hemangiomas are slow-flow, encapsulated lesions in adults that grow slowly and are surgically curable.
The well-defined capsule allows for clean surgical excision with minimal disruption of surrounding orbital tissues.

clues: A 35-year-old female with rheumatoid arthritis presents with sudden-onset vertical diplopia and pain in the superonasal orbit of her right eye.

There is limited elevation of the right eye in adduction with an audible and palpable click over the trochlea region. The superior oblique tendon area is tender to palpation. In primary position there is a mild right hypotropia.
CT orbits shows inflammation and edema around the trochlea. The superior oblique tendon appears thickened. RA serologies are positive.
The onset was sudden with periorbital pain. The patient has active RA with joint inflammation. There is no history of congenital strabismus.
Peritrochlear corticosteroid injection provides rapid relief. Oral NSAIDs or systemic steroids for recurrent episodes. Treatment of the underlying RA.
teachingPoints: Acquired (inflammatory) Brown syndrome results from inflammation of the trochlea and superior oblique tendon sheath, restricting free movement of the tendon through the trochlea.
Unlike congenital Brown syndrome (painless), acquired Brown syndrome is painful, sudden in onset, and associated with peritrochlear inflammation.
Associated conditions include RA, lupus, sinusitis, and trauma to the trochlear region.
The 'click' felt or heard by the patient during eye movements is caused by the inflamed tendon intermittently catching on the trochlea.

clues: A 50-year-old female with facial rosacea presents with chronic bilateral red, burning eyes and recurrent chalazia.

The eyelid margins show telangiectasia, meibomian gland dysfunction with inspissated secretions, and recurrent chalazia. The cornea shows inferior peripheral neovascularization with marginal infiltrates.
Tear break-up time is reduced (3 seconds). There is inferior punctate epithelial erosion. Facial exam shows rhinophyma, facial erythema, and papulopustular lesions.
The patient has had facial rosacea for 10 years. Her eye symptoms were initially attributed to allergies.
Oral doxycycline (40 mg modified-release, anti-inflammatory dose) is the mainstay. Lid hygiene, warm compresses, and artificial tears. Topical azithromycin for lid margin disease.
teachingPoints: Ocular rosacea affects approximately 50% of patients with cutaneous rosacea and can be the presenting manifestation in 20% of cases.
Corneal involvement (rosacea keratitis with peripheral neovascularization and infiltrates) is the most sight-threatening complication.
Low-dose (subantimicrobial) doxycycline works through anti-inflammatory and anti-MMP (matrix metalloproteinase) mechanisms rather than antimicrobial activity.
Ocular rosacea is commonly underdiagnosed because it can occur without significant skin disease; chronic meibomian gland dysfunction and recurrent chalazia should raise suspicion.

clues: A 5-year-old girl is found to have an abnormal optic disc during a routine exam. Her visual acuity is 20/40 OS.
The left optic disc is enlarged with a deep, white, excavated area (coloboma) in the inferior portion of the disc. The inferior neuroretinal rim is absent. The superior rim is relatively preserved.

OCT shows the excavation of the inferior optic disc with peripapillary thinning. Visual fields show a superior visual field defect corresponding to the inferior disc coloboma. MRI brain and orbits rule out basal encephalocele.
The patient also has an inferior iris coloboma (keyhole pupil) and an inferonasal chorioretinal coloboma in the same eye. The right eye is normal.
No treatment for the coloboma itself. Monitor for serous macular detachment (rare complication). Correct refractive error and treat amblyopia if present.
teachingPoints: Optic disc coloboma results from incomplete closure of the embryonic fissure during fetal development (the fissure closes inferonasally, which is why colobomas are in the inferior disc).
Colobomas can involve any structure along the embryonic fissure: iris, ciliary body, zonules, choroid, retina, and optic nerve.
Renal anomalies (CHARGE syndrome, renal coloboma syndrome/PAX2 mutations) should be screened for, especially if bilateral.
Serous retinal detachment can occur from fluid tracking through the coloboma; this is more common with optic disc pit than coloboma but can occur in both.

clues: A 45-year-old male presents with unilateral red, painful left eye with photophobia and blurred vision. He has a history of recurrent cold sores.

Slit-lamp shows unilateral anterior chamber inflammation with fine-to-medium granulomatous KPs, patchy iris atrophy in a sectoral distribution, and elevated IOP (32 mmHg) in the inflamed eye. Corneal sensation is reduced.
The iris atrophy shows a sectoral pattern (not diffuse). The pupil is irregular from posterior synechiae. PCR of aqueous humor is positive for HSV-1 DNA.
The patient has had 3 episodes of unilateral anterior uveitis in the same eye over 2 years, each with elevated IOP. He has a history of oral herpes simplex.
Topical prednisolone acetate for inflammation. Topical and/or oral antiviral therapy (valacyclovir). IOP-lowering drops. Long-term oral antiviral prophylaxis to reduce recurrences.
teachingPoints: HSV anterior uveitis is characterized by unilateral, recurrent, hypertensive (elevated IOP) uveitis with sectoral iris atrophy and reduced corneal sensation.
The triad of unilateral recurrent anterior uveitis, elevated IOP (trabeculitis), and sectoral iris atrophy is highly suggestive of herpesvirus (HSV or VZV) uveitis.
IOP elevation occurs from viral trabeculitis (inflammation of the trabecular meshwork) and is a distinguishing feature from other causes of anterior uveitis.
PCR of aqueous humor is the most sensitive diagnostic test for confirming herpesvirus uveitis when clinical features are suggestive but not definitive.

clues: A 12-year-old overweight girl presents with daily headaches, transient visual obscurations lasting seconds, and pulsatile tinnitus over the past 2 months.
Visual acuity is 20/20 bilaterally. Bilateral papilledema (Frisén grade III) is present with peripapillary hemorrhages. CN6 palsy is present on the right (causing esotropia at distance).
MRI brain is normal (no mass, hydrocephalus, or venous sinus thrombosis). MRV is normal. Lumbar puncture shows opening pressure of 35 cm H2O with normal CSF composition.
The patient has a BMI of 32 (obese). She recently started taking minocycline for acne. No history of growth hormone therapy or vitamin A use.

Discontinue minocycline. Weight loss counseling. Acetazolamide for IOP reduction. Serial visual fields and OCT to monitor for progressive optic nerve damage. CSF shunting or optic nerve sheath fenestration if vision is threatened.
teachingPoints: Pediatric IIH differs from adult IIH: it can occur in prepubertal children of normal weight, and in postpubertal children it follows the adult pattern (obese females).
Medications associated with pediatric IIH include tetracyclines (minocycline, doxycycline), growth hormone, vitamin A, and corticosteroid withdrawal.
CN6 palsy is a false localizing sign of raised intracranial pressure—the abducens nerve is vulnerable due to its long intracranial course.
Visual field monitoring (Humphrey perimetry) is the most important metric for disease progression and treatment decisions.

clues: A 65-year-old female with recently diagnosed ovarian carcinoma presents with rapidly progressive bilateral vision loss over weeks.

Fundoscopy reveals bilateral multiple slightly elevated, round, pigmented and non-pigmented uveal lesions (resembling nevi) scattered throughout the choroid. There are exudative retinal detachments bilaterally. The lens is rapidly developing cataracts.

FA shows a distinctive 'giraffe pattern' with multifocal early hyperfluorescent patches corresponding to the non-pigmented lesions. B-scan shows multiple low-elevation choroidal lesions without acoustic hollowing.
The ocular findings developed AFTER the diagnosis of ovarian cancer. The rapid bilateral lens changes (cataracts) are unusual for her age.
Treatment of the underlying malignancy is essential. Plasmapheresis may temporarily improve vision. Intravitreal anti-VEGF for exudative retinal detachments. Prognosis is very poor.
teachingPoints: BDUMP is a rare paraneoplastic syndrome characterized by bilateral diffuse uveal melanocytic proliferation, exudative retinal detachments, and rapidly progressive cataracts, most commonly associated with ovarian, lung, and pancreatic carcinomas.
The pathogenesis involves a circulating factor (cultured melanocyte elongation and proliferation factor—CMEPF) produced by the tumor that stimulates uveal melanocyte proliferation.
The rapid development of bilateral cataracts in the setting of cancer should raise immediate suspicion for BDUMP.
Visual prognosis is extremely poor, and the diagnosis typically indicates advanced systemic malignancy with poor overall survival.

clues: A 35-year-old male from Connecticut presents with bilateral facial nerve palsy, headache, and blurred vision. He recalls a tick bite and target-like rash on his thigh 6 weeks ago.
Bilateral CN7 palsy with lagophthalmos. Slit-lamp shows bilateral intermediate uveitis with vitritis. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral disc edema and perivascular sheathing (retinal vasculitis).
Lyme IgM and IgG antibodies are positive (two-tiered testing with ELISA confirmed by Western blot). CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis. Lumbar puncture shows elevated protein.
The patient lives in an endemic area for Borrelia burgdorferi. He developed erythema migrans followed by arthritis and then neurologic and ocular symptoms (Stage 2-3 Lyme disease).
IV ceftriaxone for neuroborreliosis (2-4 weeks). Topical steroids for uveitis. Lubricating drops and taping for lagophthalmos from CN7 palsy.
teachingPoints: Lyme disease can affect virtually any ocular structure: conjunctivitis (early), intermediate uveitis, vitritis, retinal vasculitis, optic neuritis, cranial neuropathies (especially CN7, bilateral), and keratitis.
Bilateral facial nerve palsy is highly suggestive of Lyme disease in endemic areas and should prompt immediate testing.
Ocular inflammation typically occurs in the secondary or tertiary stages of Lyme disease (weeks to months after initial infection).
IV ceftriaxone is the treatment of choice for neuroborreliosis; oral doxycycline is appropriate for early Lyme without neurologic involvement.

clues: A 55-year-old French-Canadian male presents with progressive bilateral ptosis and difficulty swallowing that has worsened over the past decade.

Bilateral symmetric ptosis with poor levator function. Progressive external ophthalmoplegia (limited extraocular movements in all directions) without diplopia (because the limitation is symmetric and gradual). Orbicularis weakness is present.
Genetic testing reveals a GCN trinucleotide (GCG) repeat expansion in the PABPN1 gene on chromosome 14 (autosomal dominant). EMG shows myopathic changes. MRI is normal.
The patient has progressive dysphagia requiring dietary modification. He is of French-Canadian ancestry. His father had similar symptoms and died of aspiration pneumonia.
Frontalis sling for ptosis. Cricopharyngeal myotomy for dysphagia. Swallowing therapy and aspiration precautions. No specific disease-modifying treatment.
teachingPoints: OPMD is an autosomal dominant muscular dystrophy caused by a trinucleotide repeat expansion in the PABPN1 gene, with high prevalence in French-Canadian and Bukhara Jewish populations.
The classic presentation is late-onset (5th-6th decade) progressive ptosis and dysphagia.
Aspiration pneumonia from progressive dysphagia is the primary cause of mortality.
OPMD must be distinguished from mitochondrial chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia (CPEO), which has similar ocular features but different genetics and systemic associations.

clues: A 20-year-old male presents with slowly progressive bilateral ptosis and limited eye movements that he has noticed worsening since his early teens. He denies diplopia.

Bilateral symmetric ptosis and progressive external ophthalmoplegia (severely limited eye movements in all directions). The ophthalmoplegia is painless and symmetric, so the patient does not experience diplopia. Orbicularis weakness is present.

Muscle biopsy shows ragged-red fibers (on Gomori trichrome stain) and cytochrome oxidase (COX)-negative fibers. Mitochondrial DNA analysis reveals a large single deletion. ECG shows cardiac conduction defects (heart block).
The patient also has pigmentary retinopathy (bone-spicule-like changes), short stature, and sensorineural hearing loss—consistent with Kearns-Sayre syndrome (KSS).
Cardiac monitoring is essential (risk of complete heart block requiring pacemaker). CoQ10 supplementation (limited evidence). Frontalis sling for ptosis. No proven disease-modifying therapy.
teachingPoints: CPEO is a mitochondrial myopathy; when onset is before age 20 with pigmentary retinopathy and cardiac conduction defects, it is classified as Kearns-Sayre syndrome (KSS).
The absence of diplopia despite severe ophthalmoplegia is characteristic—the symmetric, gradual onset allows the brain to adapt.
Cardiac surveillance is mandatory in KSS because fatal heart block can occur suddenly; prophylactic pacemaker insertion may be indicated.
Ragged-red fibers on muscle biopsy represent accumulations of abnormal mitochondria beneath the sarcolemma and are the histopathologic hallmark of mitochondrial myopathies.

clues: A 2-year-old child is brought in with a painless, firm lump at the outer brow area of the right eye present since birth.
A firm, round, non-tender, subcutaneous mass is palpable at the superotemporal orbital rim. It is non-pulsatile, non-compressible, and does not transilluminate. It is fixed to the periosteum.
CT orbits shows a well-defined, encapsulated cystic lesion at the frontozygomatic suture with smooth bony scalloping. No intracranial extension.
The mass has been present since birth and has grown slowly with the child. It is not inflamed.
Surgical excision with intact capsule removal to prevent recurrence and inflammatory reaction from cyst contents (keratin, sebaceous material, hair).
teachingPoints: Orbital dermoid cysts are the most common orbital tumors in children, representing developmental choristomas containing ectodermal elements (skin appendages) trapped at bony suture lines.
The superotemporal orbital rim (frontozygomatic suture) is the most common location; the superonasal orbit (frontoethmoidal suture) is the second most common.
Deep orbital dermoids can present later in life with proptosis and have a higher risk of rupture, causing a severe lipogranulomatous inflammatory reaction.
Complete excision with intact capsule is essential; rupture of the cyst during surgery causes intense inflammation from the keratin and sebaceous contents.

clues: A 60-year-old female on bimatoprost drops for glaucoma presents concerned about asymmetric changes around her eyes. She uses drops only in the right eye.

Compared to the left (untreated) eye, the right shows deepening of the upper eyelid sulcus (enophthalmos-like appearance), periorbital skin darkening (hyperpigmentation), mild ptosis, longer and darker eyelashes (hypertrichosis), and iris darkening (heterochromia—more brown).
The periorbital fat shows atrophy on comparison. IOP is well-controlled at 14 mmHg OD on bimatoprost.
All changes correlate with the treated side only. She has been on bimatoprost for 5 years. The fellow eye is untreated.

Counsel the patient about the cosmetic side effects. Consider switching to a non-prostaglandin IOP-lowering agent if the changes are unacceptable. Some changes (fat atrophy) may be partially reversible upon discontinuation.
teachingPoints: Prostaglandin analogs (bimatoprost, latanoprost, travoprost) cause periorbital fat atrophy, eyelash growth, skin hyperpigmentation, and iris darkening through their effect on local adipocytes and melanocytes.
These changes are cosmetic and not harmful, but can be distressing to patients, especially with unilateral use.
The deepened sulcus (PAP) can mimic enophthalmos and is caused by atrophy of the orbital fat pads.
Bimatoprost's eyelash growth effect led to its FDA approval as Latisse for cosmetic eyelash enhancement.

clues: A 40-year-old male presents with acute bilateral blurred vision and photophobia. He has a history of a genital ulcer 4 months ago that healed spontaneously.

Bilateral anterior uveitis with posterior synechiae. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral large, circular, yellowish placoid lesions centered on the macula at the level of the RPE—syphilitic posterior placoid chorioretinitis. Disc edema is present.
RPR is reactive at 1:128. FTA-ABS is positive. HIV test is positive (co-infection). Lumbar puncture shows CSF pleocytosis and positive CSF VDRL (neurosyphilis confirmed).
The patient had a painless genital chancre 4 months ago (primary syphilis) and now presents with secondary/tertiary ocular syphilis. He has a maculopapular rash on his palms.
IV penicillin G (24 million units/day for 10-14 days) for neurosyphilis. Topical steroids for anterior uveitis. Systemic steroids WITH penicillin cover for severe posterior inflammation.
teachingPoints: Ocular syphilis is considered neurosyphilis by definition (even without CSF abnormalities) and must be treated with the neurosyphilis regimen (IV penicillin).
Syphilitic posterior placoid chorioretinitis is a characteristic finding—a large, round, yellow placoid lesion at the level of the RPE, usually in the macula.
Syphilis is the 'great imitator' in uveitis; it should be considered in the differential of virtually any uveitic presentation.
All patients diagnosed with ocular syphilis should be tested for HIV and vice versa, as co-infection is common and alters management.

clues: A 20-year-old male presents with acute pain, swelling, and redness in the outer upper portion of his right eyelid for 3 days following an upper respiratory infection.
The lateral upper eyelid is swollen, tender, and erythematous with an S-shaped curve to the lid. The lacrimal gland is enlarged and palpable. There is inferonasal globe displacement.
CT shows an enlarged, enhancing right lacrimal gland without bony changes. WBC is elevated. There is no abscess formation.
The patient had a viral URI 1 week ago. He has bilateral parotid gland swelling (mumps). He was not vaccinated.
Viral dacryoadenitis (mumps): supportive care, cold compresses, NSAIDs. Bacterial dacryoadenitis: oral antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate). Incision and drainage if abscess forms.
teachingPoints: Acute dacryoadenitis is inflammation of the lacrimal gland, most commonly viral (EBV, mumps, adenovirus) in young patients and bacterial (S. aureus, S. pyogenes) in older patients.
The classic S-shaped upper eyelid deformity results from swelling of the lacrimal gland in the superolateral orbit pushing the lateral lid down.
The swelling is above the lateral canthal tendon (unlike chalazion or preseptal cellulitis which are more central or medial).
Chronic or recurrent dacryoadenitis should prompt investigation for sarcoidosis, IgG4-related disease, lymphoma, or Sjögren syndrome.

clues: A 15-year-old male presents for evaluation of a unilateral retinal vascular abnormality found on routine exam. He is asymptomatic.

Fundoscopy reveals markedly dilated, tortuous retinal vessels with direct arteriovenous communications (no intervening capillary bed) in the right eye. The vessels are dramatically enlarged and intertwined.
FA shows rapid arteriovenous transit with no capillary phase—dye passes directly from arteries to veins. There is no leakage, exudation, or hemorrhage.
The condition is unilateral and congenital. MRI brain reveals an ipsilateral AV malformation in the midbrain and thalamus. There is a facial cutaneous AVM in the V1-V2 distribution.
No treatment for the retinal AVM unless complications develop (rare vitreous hemorrhage). Neurosurgical evaluation for the intracranial AVM. No treatment for stable retinal lesions.
teachingPoints: Wyburn-Mason syndrome is a rare, non-hereditary phakomatosis characterized by unilateral retinal, intracranial, and sometimes facial arteriovenous malformations.
The retinal AVMs are direct arteriovenous shunts with NO intervening capillary bed—unlike most vascular conditions, they do not typically leak or bleed.
The intracranial component (usually in the ipsilateral optic pathway, midbrain, or thalamus) can cause seizures, hemorrhage, or neurologic deficits.
Unlike most other phakomatoses, Wyburn-Mason is not inherited and is a sporadic developmental anomaly.

clues: A 60-year-old male presents with sudden onset of floaters, cobwebs, and reduced vision in his right eye since this morning.
Visual acuity is 20/200 OD (reduced by vitreous blood). Dense vitreous hemorrhage obscures the fundus view. Red reflex is diminished.
B-scan ultrasound shows dispersed vitreous echoes (blood) with a posterior vitreous detachment. The retina is attached. No retinal mass or retinal tear is visible through the blood.
The patient is not diabetic. He has no history of trauma. He was straining at the gym when symptoms began.

Close monitoring with serial B-scan until the view clears enough for a complete dilated retinal exam. Position with head elevated. Vitrectomy if the hemorrhage does not clear in 2-3 months or if retinal detachment is suspected.
teachingPoints: The most common cause of spontaneous vitreous hemorrhage in non-diabetic patients is a retinal tear associated with acute PVD.
A retinal tear is present in approximately 15% of acute PVDs with vitreous hemorrhage; if the hemorrhage is too dense to see the retina, serial B-scans are essential.
Other non-diabetic causes include retinal vein occlusion, retinal macroaneurysm, Terson syndrome, and blood dyscrasias.
Once the blood clears enough for visualization, a thorough peripheral retinal exam with scleral depression is mandatory to rule out retinal tears.

clues: A 25-year-old female presents with bilateral itchy, watery, red eyes every spring when the pollen count is high. Symptoms have occurred seasonally for years.
Bilateral conjunctival injection with chemosis and mild papillary reaction on the tarsal conjunctiva. There is watery mucoid discharge. The cornea is clear. No follicles.
Conjunctival scraping would show eosinophils. Serum IgE is elevated. Skin prick testing shows sensitivity to tree and grass pollens.
The patient has a history of allergic rhinitis and eczema (atopic triad). Symptoms are clearly seasonal (spring/summer).

Topical mast cell stabilizer/antihistamine combination drops (olopatadine, ketotifen). Cold compresses. Avoid rubbing eyes. Oral antihistamine for systemic symptoms. Artificial tears to dilute allergens.
teachingPoints: Seasonal allergic conjunctivitis is an IgE-mediated type I hypersensitivity reaction to airborne allergens, the most common form of ocular allergy.
Itching is the hallmark symptom; its absence should prompt consideration of other diagnoses.
Papillae (not follicles) are the characteristic conjunctival finding in allergic conjunctivitis.
Dual-action drops combining mast cell stabilization and antihistamine activity (e.g., olopatadine) are the most effective single agents.

clues: A 35-year-old male with severe lifelong atopic dermatitis presents with chronic bilateral eye irritation, mucoid discharge, and progressively worsening vision over years.

The eyelid skin is lichenified and eczematous. The tarsal conjunctiva shows a papillary response with subepithelial fibrosis and symblepharon formation. The cornea has a shield ulcer superiorly with neovascularization and scarring. An anterior subcapsular cataract is developing.
Cultures show Staphylococcus aureus colonization of the lid margins. Corneal scraping shows eosinophils. Serum IgE is markedly elevated. The patient has keratoconus in the right eye.
The patient has had severe eczema since infancy. He has asthma and food allergies. He chronically rubs his eyes.

Topical cyclosporine or tacrolimus for chronic inflammation (steroid-sparing). Lid hygiene and anti-staphylococcal treatment. Monitor for and manage complications: keratoconus, cataracts, and corneal scarring.
teachingPoints: AKC is a severe, chronic, bilateral ocular allergic disease associated with atopic dermatitis; it is more sight-threatening than seasonal or perennial allergic conjunctivitis.
Complications include corneal scarring, vascularization, shield ulcers, keratoconus (from chronic eye rubbing), anterior subcapsular cataracts, and conjunctival cicatrization.
AKC shares features with both allergic (eosinophils, IgE) and cicatrizing (symblepharon, fibrosis) conjunctival diseases.
Atopic cataracts are characteristically anterior subcapsular ('shield-like') and occur at a younger age than typical age-related cataracts.

clues: A 10-year-old girl from a rural village in sub-Saharan Africa presents with chronic bilateral red eyes, mucopurulent discharge, and eyelash irritation.

The superior tarsal conjunctiva shows follicles (>5 follicles >0.5 mm) and papillary hypertrophy. There is superior corneal pannus (vascularization from above). Early trichiasis is present from tarsal scarring.

Herbert pits (scarred follicles at the superior limbus) are visible. Arlt line (horizontal conjunctival scar on the superior tarsus) is present. Giemsa stain of conjunctival scraping shows basophilic intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies.
Multiple family members and village children have similar symptoms. Hygiene conditions are poor with limited access to clean water.
WHO SAFE strategy: Surgery (for trichiasis), Antibiotics (oral azithromycin single dose community-wide), Facial cleanliness, Environmental improvement (access to clean water and sanitation).
teachingPoints: Trachoma (Chlamydia trachomatis serotypes A-C) is the leading infectious cause of blindness worldwide, affecting an estimated 1.9 million people with visual impairment.
Blindness results from corneal scarring due to repeated cycles of infection, inflammation, conjunctival scarring, trichiasis, and corneal abrasion.
The WHO simplified grading system: TF (follicular), TI (intense inflammation), TS (scarring), TT (trichiasis), CO (corneal opacity).
Mass azithromycin distribution to endemic communities is a key component of trachoma elimination programs.

clues: A 10-year-old boy is referred for evaluation of elevated IOP found during a school screening. He has dental and facial abnormalities.

Slit-lamp shows a prominent, anteriorly displaced Schwalbe line (posterior embryotoxon) visible as a white ridge at the peripheral cornea. Gonioscopy reveals iris strands bridging from the peripheral iris to the displaced Schwalbe line. IOP is 28 mmHg bilaterally. Iris shows corectopia (displaced pupil) and polycoria (iris holes).

Corneal diameter is normal. Anterior segment OCT shows the prominent Schwalbe line. Dental exam reveals microdontia (small teeth) and oligodontia (missing teeth). There is maxillary hypoplasia and redundant periumbilical skin.
Genetic testing reveals a PITX2 gene mutation. Family screening shows the mother has posterior embryotoxon with a normal IOP.
Glaucoma management with topical IOP-lowering agents. Angle surgery (trabeculotomy/goniotomy) or trabeculectomy/tube shunt for refractory cases.
teachingPoints: Axenfeld-Rieger syndrome is an autosomal dominant anterior segment dysgenesis (PITX2 or FOXC1 mutations) with iris, angle, and systemic developmental abnormalities.
Approximately 50% of Axenfeld-Rieger patients develop glaucoma due to angle dysgenesis.
Posterior embryotoxon alone (prominent Schwalbe line) is common (present in ~15% of normal eyes) and does not indicate Axenfeld-Rieger unless iris and angle changes are also present.
Systemic features include dental (microdontia, oligodontia), facial (midface hypoplasia), umbilical (redundant periumbilical skin), and cardiac anomalies.

clues: A 25-year-old female soft contact lens wearer presents with increasing bilateral itching, mucoid discharge, and contact lens intolerance over the past month.

Eversion of the upper eyelids reveals large (>1 mm) cobblestone-like papillae on the superior tarsal conjunctiva bilaterally. There is thick, ropy mucous discharge. The contact lens surface is coated with deposits.
The patient's contact lenses show significant protein buildup. Corneal evaluation shows superior punctate staining from the papillary mechanical effect.
She wears monthly replacement soft contact lenses and admits to over-wearing them and sleeping in them occasionally. She has not replaced her lens case in months.
Discontinue contact lens wear for 2-4 weeks. Switch to daily disposable lenses when resuming. Topical mast cell stabilizer/antihistamine drops. Improve lens hygiene.
teachingPoints: GPC is a non-IgE-mediated inflammatory response to deposits on contact lenses (or other foreign bodies like exposed sutures, ocular prostheses) causing papillary hypertrophy of the upper tarsal conjunctiva.
Switching to daily disposable lenses eliminates the deposit accumulation that triggers the immune response and is the most effective long-term strategy.
GPC can also occur from exposed sutures (especially nylon corneal sutures) and ocular prostheses.
Hydrogen peroxide-based lens care systems provide superior disinfection and reduce protein deposits compared to multipurpose solutions.

clues: A 50-year-old male with chronic diarrhea, weight loss, and arthralgias develops a peculiar rhythmic eye movement combined with jaw movements.

There is a characteristic pendular convergent-divergent nystagmus synchronized with rhythmic contractions of the masticatory muscles (oculomasticatory myorhythmia—OMM). This specific finding occurs about once per second.
Small bowel biopsy shows PAS-positive macrophages. PCR is positive for Tropheryma whipplei. MRI brain shows white matter lesions.
The patient has had a 3-year history of malabsorption, weight loss, and polyarthralgia before the neurologic symptoms developed.
Prolonged antibiotic therapy (ceftriaxone IV for 2 weeks followed by TMP-SMX orally for at least 1 year). OMM may persist even after successful treatment.
teachingPoints: Oculomasticatory myorhythmia is pathognomonic for CNS Whipple disease—no other condition produces this specific finding.
Whipple disease is a rare systemic infection caused by Tropheryma whipplei that can involve the CNS, causing dementia, ophthalmoplegia, and the diagnostic OMM.
Other ocular manifestations include supranuclear gaze palsy, papilledema, and uveitis.
Without treatment, CNS Whipple disease is fatal; early recognition of OMM can lead to life-saving diagnosis.

clues: A 70-year-old fair-skinned male presents with a slowly growing, painless nodule on his right lower eyelid that occasionally bleeds.

A pearly, firm, nodular lesion with rolled (raised) borders, central ulceration, and telangiectatic surface vessels is present on the medial aspect of the right lower eyelid. There is madarosis (lash loss) in the area of the lesion.
The lesion measures 8 mm. There is no regional lymphadenopathy. Incisional biopsy confirms nodular basal cell carcinoma with clear deep margins.
The patient has a history of extensive sun exposure as a farmer. He has had prior BCC removed from his nose.
Surgical excision with histologically confirmed clear margins (Mohs micrographic surgery preferred for periocular BCC). Oculoplastic reconstruction of the eyelid defect.
teachingPoints: BCC is the most common malignant eyelid tumor (90-95% of eyelid malignancies), most commonly occurring on the lower eyelid (50-60%), followed by medial canthus.
The medial canthus location is associated with higher recurrence due to deep invasion potential along periosteum and into the orbit.
Mohs surgery provides the highest cure rate (99%) with maximal tissue preservation, important for eyelid reconstruction.
Neglected BCC can invade the orbit; orbital exenteration may be required for advanced orbital invasion.

clues: A 55-year-old female on amiodarone for atrial fibrillation presents for a routine eye exam. She reports no visual symptoms.
Slit-lamp shows bilateral golden-brown, whorl-shaped (vortex) epithelial deposits in the inferior two-thirds of both corneas, resembling a cat's whisker pattern. Visual acuity is 20/20.
The deposits are superficial, at the level of the corneal epithelium. They do not affect the stroma or endothelium. The retina is normal.
The patient has been on amiodarone 200 mg daily for 3 years. The corneal findings are incidental.
No treatment required. The deposits are visually insignificant and reversible upon drug discontinuation if needed. Continue amiodarone if medically necessary.
teachingPoints: Cornea verticillata (vortex keratopathy) is caused by deposition of drug metabolites in the corneal epithelium, most commonly from amiodarone (nearly 100% of users) but also chloroquine, indomethacin, and tamoxifen.
The whorl pattern results from the normal centripetal migration of corneal epithelial cells (X, Y, Z hypothesis of epithelial renewal).
Amiodarone corneal deposits are almost universal and are NOT an indication to stop the drug—they rarely affect vision.
The identical corneal pattern can occur in Fabry disease (alpha-galactosidase A deficiency) without drug exposure—this is an important genetic mimic.

clues: A 15-year-old boy with episodes of burning pain in his hands and feet presents for an eye exam. He has been diagnosed with Fabry disease.

Slit-lamp shows bilateral cornea verticillata (golden-brown whorl-shaped epithelial deposits) identical in appearance to amiodarone keratopathy, but this patient takes no medications causing this pattern. A spoke-like posterior subcapsular cataract (Fabry cataract) is present. Conjunctival and retinal vessel tortuosity is noted.
Alpha-galactosidase A enzyme activity is markedly reduced. Genetic testing confirms a GLA gene mutation (X-linked). Renal function shows proteinuria.
The patient has acroparesthesias (painful neuropathy), angiokeratomas on the trunk, and early renal insufficiency.
Enzyme replacement therapy (agalsidase alfa or beta) is the systemic treatment. Corneal deposits require no specific ocular treatment.
teachingPoints: Fabry disease is an X-linked lysosomal storage disease caused by GLA gene mutations leading to accumulation of globotriaosylceramide (Gb3) in tissues throughout the body.
Cornea verticillata in Fabry disease is identical in appearance to drug-induced vortex keratopathy; the key difference is the ABSENCE of causative medication.
The spoke-like posterior subcapsular cataract ('Fabry cataract') is characteristic but does not usually cause significant visual impairment.

Finding cornea verticillata in a patient not on causative medications should prompt investigation for Fabry disease, as early diagnosis allows enzyme replacement therapy before irreversible organ damage.

clues: A 45-year-old female presents with sudden onset of right facial weakness, inability to close her right eye, and dry, irritated right eye since yesterday.

Right facial nerve palsy with complete lagophthalmos (inability to close the eyelid). Bell phenomenon is present (eye rolls up on attempted closure). Lower lid ectropion from orbicularis weakness. The cornea shows inferior exposure keratopathy with punctate erosions.
No vesicular rash (ruling out Ramsay Hunt syndrome). MRI is normal. The remainder of the neurologic exam is normal. There is no forehead sparing (lower motor neuron pattern).
The onset was sudden and painless. The patient has no history of diabetes, Lyme disease, or tumor. This is consistent with idiopathic Bell palsy.

Aggressive ocular lubrication: preservative-free artificial tears frequently during the day and lubricating ointment at night. Moisture chamber goggles. Tape eyelid shut at night. Gold weight implant for severe lagophthalmos if no recovery.
teachingPoints: The most important ophthalmologic complication of CN7 palsy is exposure keratopathy from lagophthalmos, which can progress to corneal ulceration and perforation if untreated.
Bell phenomenon (upward eye rolling on attempted lid closure) is protective; if absent, the cornea is at much higher risk.
Most Bell palsy patients recover within 3-6 months; gold or platinum weight implantation in the upper lid is considered if lagophthalmos persists beyond 3 months.
Ramsay Hunt syndrome (herpes zoster oticus) presents with facial palsy, vesicular ear rash, and hearing loss—it requires antiviral treatment and has a worse prognosis than Bell palsy.

clues: A 30-year-old female who had an ICL (implantable collamer lens) placed 2 years ago for high myopia presents with acute eye pain, halos, and blurred vision.

IOP is 48 mmHg OD. Slit-lamp shows an anterior chamber phakic IOL (Visian ICL) that is oversized, causing it to vault excessively. The peripheral iridotomy is blocked by the ICL edge. The anterior chamber is shallow peripherally.
UBM shows the ICL touching the natural crystalline lens posteriorly and obstructing the peripheral iridotomy anteriorly. There is pupillary block.
The patient had the ICL placed for -14.00 D myopia. The lens sizing was based on white-to-white measurement (which can underestimate sulcus-to-sulcus diameter).

Acute management: laser peripheral iridotomy (new PI in a location not blocked by the ICL). Definitive: ICL exchange for a properly sized lens, or ICL explantation with conversion to LASIK or PRK if eligible.
teachingPoints: Phakic IOL complications include pupillary block glaucoma (from PI closure), cataract formation (from lens-ICL contact), pigment dispersion, and endothelial cell loss.
Proper sizing of posterior chamber phakic IOLs is critical; excessive vault risks angle closure and iris chafing, while insufficient vault causes anterior subcapsular cataract.
Peripheral iridotomy (usually two PIs) is mandatory before or during ICL implantation to prevent pupillary block.
Modern ICLs with a central port (EVO ICL) eliminate the need for PI, reducing the risk of pupillary block.

clues: A 5-year-old boy with a history of multiple bone fractures presents for eye exam. His parents note his eyes have a bluish tint.

Both sclerae have a striking blue-gray appearance. Corneal pachymetry shows thin corneas (460 microns bilaterally). IOP by Goldmann applanation is 12 mmHg (which may be artifactually low due to thin corneas).
Keratometry shows normal curvature. Axial length is normal. The blue scleral color is uniform and bilateral. There is no megalocornea or Haab striae.
The child has had 8 fractures from minimal trauma, has dentinogenesis imperfecta (discolored teeth), and hearing loss. Genetic testing confirms a COL1A1 mutation.
No specific ocular treatment needed for the blue sclerae. Correct IOP readings for thin corneas. Monitor for open-angle glaucoma (potentially underdiagnosed due to thin corneas).
teachingPoints: Blue sclerae in OI result from thinning of the scleral collagen, allowing the underlying dark uveal tissue (choroid) to show through.
Thin corneas in OI lead to artifactually low IOP readings on applanation tonometry; this can mask glaucoma.
The differential for blue sclerae includes OI, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, Marfan syndrome, and iron deficiency anemia.
Types I and IV OI most commonly have blue sclerae; Type I is the mildest form with the most prominent blue discoloration.

clues: A 35-year-old male metalworker presents with gradually worsening vision in his right eye over several months. He recalls a 'grinding injury' 6 months ago that he ignored.

Visual acuity is 20/80 OD. Slit-lamp shows heterochromia—the right iris is darker (rust-brown) than the left. There is an iron-stained anterior subcapsular cataract and iris pigmentation. Dilated exam shows vitreous haze and a small metallic foreign body on the retinal surface inferiorly.

CT orbits confirms a metallic density in the posterior segment. ERG shows reduced b-wave amplitude in the right eye (siderotic retinal toxicity). Gonioscopy shows dark pigmentation of the trabecular meshwork.
The patient works with an angle grinder without safety glasses. The initial injury caused transient pain and redness that resolved.
Surgical removal of the IOFB via pars plana vitrectomy with intraocular magnet. Cataract extraction if visually significant. ERG monitoring for siderosis progression.
teachingPoints: Retained iron-containing (ferrous) IOFB causes siderosis bulbi—a toxic iron deposition in intraocular tissues (iris, lens, retina, trabecular meshwork) leading to progressive damage.
Siderosis causes heterochromia (darker iris), cataract, retinal toxicity (ERG deterioration), secondary glaucoma, and eventually phthisis if untreated.
Copper foreign bodies cause chalcosis (more acutely toxic) with a sunflower cataract, while inert materials (glass, plastic, stone) may be tolerated without removal.
ERG is the most sensitive monitor for early siderotic retinal damage; declining b-wave amplitude indicates progressive toxicity and the need for IOFB removal.

clues: A 45-year-old female presents with bilateral red, painful eyes along with a swollen, red, tender ear. She has had recurrent episodes of auricular inflammation.

Bilateral diffuse anterior scleritis with deep scleral injection that does not blanch with phenylephrine. There is a peripheral corneal ulcer with stromal thinning (PUK-like). The right ear is swollen, red, and tender but the earlobe is spared.
CRP and ESR are elevated. Biopsy of auricular cartilage shows loss of basophilia and perichondrial inflammation. Anti-collagen type II antibodies are positive.
The patient has had 3 episodes of bilateral auricular chondritis (sparing the earlobes which lack cartilage), nasal chondritis, and now ocular inflammation.
Systemic immunosuppression (dapsone for mild, methotrexate/cyclophosphamide for severe). Systemic corticosteroids for acute flares. Topical therapy alone is insufficient for scleritis/PUK.
teachingPoints: Relapsing polychondritis is a rare autoimmune condition targeting cartilaginous structures throughout the body; ocular involvement (scleritis, PUK, uveitis, orbital inflammation) occurs in ~50% of patients.
Ear cartilage inflammation that spares the earlobe (which has no cartilage) is the hallmark clinical sign.
Scleritis and PUK in RP can be necrotizing and sight-threatening, requiring aggressive systemic immunosuppression.
Laryngotracheal chondritis (airway collapse) is the most life-threatening manifestation.

clues: A newborn infant presents with bilateral cloudy corneas at birth. The child is otherwise healthy.

Both corneas are diffusely edematous and cloudy (ground-glass appearance) with increased corneal thickness (>1000 microns). There is no tearing, photophobia, or blepharospasm (distinguishing from congenital glaucoma). IOP is normal.

Pachymetry shows markedly thickened corneas bilaterally. Specular microscopy (if obtainable) shows absent or markedly abnormal endothelial cells. Corneal diameters are normal (no megalocornea). Genetic testing reveals SLC4A11 mutation.
IOP under anesthesia is normal. Corneal diameter is normal. There are no Haab striae. Parents are consanguineous (autosomal recessive inheritance).
Penetrating keratoplasty or DSEK/DMEK to replace the dysfunctional endothelium. Amblyopia management is critical given the corneal opacity present from birth.
teachingPoints: CHED (autosomal recessive, SLC4A11 mutations) presents at birth with bilateral corneal edema from absent/dysfunctional corneal endothelium; it must be distinguished from congenital glaucoma.
The key distinction from congenital glaucoma: CHED has normal IOP, normal corneal diameter, no tearing/photophobia, and no Haab striae.
Early corneal transplantation (within the first few months of life) gives the best visual outcome by minimizing deprivation amblyopia.
SLC4A11 mutations also cause Harboyan syndrome (CHED + progressive sensorineural hearing loss).

clues: A 55-year-old female with severe preeclampsia at 36 weeks gestation develops sudden bilateral blurred vision.

Bilateral serous retinal detachments without retinal breaks are present. The subretinal fluid is shifting with positional changes. There are no retinal tears, and the vitreous is clear (no PVD or hemorrhage).
B-scan confirms bilateral retinal detachments without breaks. OCT shows subretinal fluid. Blood pressure is 190/120 mmHg. There is proteinuria and thrombocytopenia.
The patient has severe preeclampsia. Fundoscopy also shows bilateral arteriolar narrowing and focal constriction consistent with hypertensive retinopathy.

Emergent management of preeclampsia (delivery of the baby, magnesium sulfate, antihypertensives). The serous retinal detachments typically resolve spontaneously within weeks after blood pressure is controlled.
teachingPoints: Exudative/serous retinal detachments occur from fluid leakage beneath the retina without a retinal break; causes include preeclampsia/eclampsia, VKH, posterior scleritis, choroidal tumors, and central serous chorioretinopathy.
Preeclampsia causes choroidal ischemia and RPE disruption, leading to subretinal fluid accumulation.
Serous RD in preeclampsia is typically bilateral and resolves after delivery and blood pressure control, with good visual recovery.
No retinal surgery is needed for exudative RD—treating the underlying cause resolves the detachment.

clues: A 45-year-old male who works outdoors presents with a yellowish bump on the white of his eye near the colored part. It is mildly irritated.
A yellowish-white, slightly elevated, well-circumscribed lesion is present on the nasal bulbar conjunctiva, NOT extending onto the cornea. There is mild surrounding injection (pingueculitis).
The lesion is within the interpalpebral fissure zone. There is no corneal involvement. Visual acuity is unaffected.
The patient has significant UV exposure history. He does not wear sunglasses regularly.
Artificial tears for irritation. Short course of mild topical steroid or NSAID for pingueculitis (inflamed pinguecula). Sun protection (sunglasses with UV protection).
teachingPoints: A pinguecula is a benign degenerative lesion of the conjunctival substantia propria containing elastotic degeneration (actinic damage), located in the interpalpebral zone adjacent to the limbus.
Unlike a pterygium, a pinguecula does NOT extend onto the cornea.
Pingueculitis (inflammation of a pinguecula) can cause redness and irritation mimicking other conditions.
Surgical removal is rarely indicated unless cosmetically unacceptable or if it impedes contact lens wear.

clues: A 40-year-old HIV-positive male on MAC (Mycobacterium avium complex) prophylaxis presents with unilateral painful red eye, photophobia, and blurred vision.
Slit-lamp shows a dense fibrinous anterior uveitis with a large hypopyon in the right eye. There is no corneal lesion. IOP is low (6 mmHg). Vitritis is present.

Cultures of aqueous humor are negative for bacteria and fungi. The inflammation began 2 weeks after the dose of rifabutin was increased. His concurrent medications include clarithromycin and fluconazole.
The patient was recently started on higher-dose rifabutin (600 mg/day) and fluconazole (which inhibits rifabutin metabolism via CYP3A4, increasing drug levels).
Discontinue or reduce rifabutin dose. Intensive topical corticosteroids and cycloplegics for the uveitis. Symptoms resolve after dose adjustment.
teachingPoints: Rifabutin-induced uveitis is a dose-dependent drug toxicity that occurs more frequently when rifabutin is co-administered with CYP3A4 inhibitors (fluconazole, clarithromycin) that increase rifabutin serum levels.
The characteristic presentation is a dense fibrinous anterior uveitis with hypopyon; it can be unilateral or bilateral.
It is a sterile inflammation, not an infection; aqueous cultures are negative.
Dose reduction and avoidance of drug interactions typically prevent recurrence; the inflammation resolves completely with treatment.

clues: A 3-year-old child presents with leukocoria and strabismus in the left eye. Initial exam raises concern for Coats disease.

Dilated exam shows massive subretinal exudation with lipid deposits and a total exudative retinal detachment in the left eye. However, careful examination reveals a white retinal mass with calcification underlying the exudation.
B-scan shows calcification within a solid retinal mass (consistent with retinoblastoma). CT confirms intraocular calcification. MRI shows no optic nerve involvement.
The exudative response mimicked Coats disease but the calcified mass confirms retinoblastoma with a Coat's-like exudative reaction.
Treat as retinoblastoma: enucleation for advanced unilateral disease, or systemic/intra-arterial chemotherapy with focal consolidation for salvageable eyes.
teachingPoints: Coat's response occurs in approximately 2-5% of retinoblastoma cases, where the tumor triggers an exudative reaction that can mask the underlying tumor.
The critical distinguishing feature is calcification (present in retinoblastoma, absent in Coats disease) on ultrasound or CT.
Any child with a clinical presentation of Coats disease must have calcification ruled out to exclude retinoblastoma masquerading with a Coat's-like response.
Retinoblastoma with Coat's response tends to be more aggressive and may have a worse prognosis.

clues: A 45-year-old female with thyroid disease presents with chronic bilateral foreign body sensation, burning, and redness localized to the superior portion of her eyes.

Eversion of the upper lid reveals papillary hypertrophy of the superior tarsal conjunctiva. The superior bulbar conjunctiva is thickened and injected with redundant tissue. Rose bengal staining shows intense staining of the superior bulbar conjunctiva and adjacent superior cornea. Filamentary keratitis is present superiorly.
The findings are isolated to the superior limbal region. The inferior conjunctiva and cornea are normal. Thyroid function tests show hyperthyroidism.
The patient has Graves disease (TED is a known association). She has failed conventional dry eye therapy.

Silver nitrate 0.5-1% application to the superior tarsal and bulbar conjunctiva. Alternatives: large-diameter bandage contact lens, conjunctival resection, or thermocautery of the superior bulbar conjunctiva.
teachingPoints: SLK is a chronic inflammatory condition of the superior bulbar conjunctiva and cornea, strongly associated with thyroid dysfunction (especially hyperthyroidism).
Rose bengal (or lissamine green) staining of the superior bulbar conjunctiva in a characteristic pattern is diagnostic.
Silver nitrate cauterization of the superior conjunctiva is remarkably effective, often producing long-term remission after one or two treatments.
Approximately 30-50% of SLK patients have concurrent thyroid disease; thyroid function should be checked in all SLK patients.

clues: A 60-year-old female with a history of breast cancer treated 5 years ago presents with blurred vision and photopsias in her left eye.
Fundoscopy reveals a flat-to-slightly-elevated, creamy-yellow, placoid choroidal lesion in the posterior pole with overlying subretinal fluid. The lesion is less pigmented than a melanoma.

B-scan shows a flat, echogenic choroidal lesion with medium-high internal reflectivity (unlike melanoma's low reflectivity). OCT shows the flat choroidal mass with a 'lumpy-bumpy' surface contour and overlying subretinal fluid.
The patient had ER-positive breast cancer treated with mastectomy and tamoxifen 5 years ago. CT chest and PET-CT show new pulmonary and hepatic metastases.
External beam radiation therapy to the eye (most common treatment) or plaque brachytherapy. Systemic chemotherapy for the metastatic disease. Anti-VEGF for subretinal fluid.
teachingPoints: The choroid is the most common site of intraocular metastasis; breast cancer (in women) and lung cancer (in men) are the most common primary tumors.

Choroidal metastases are typically flat/placoid, creamy-yellow, and have medium-high reflectivity on B-scan—all distinguishing features from choroidal melanoma (dome/mushroom-shaped, pigmented, low reflectivity).
Bilateral involvement occurs in approximately 20-40% of cases, and multifocal lesions are common.
A new choroidal lesion in a patient with a history of cancer is a metastasis until proven otherwise.

clues: A 3-year-old child with Down syndrome is referred for an eye evaluation as part of routine screening.

Slit-lamp shows bilateral Brushfield spots (light-colored, ring-shaped spots on the iris surface arranged in a ring around the pupil). There is bilateral upslanting palpebral fissures and epicanthal folds. Cycloplegic refraction shows high myopia.
Retinoscopy reveals bilateral high hyperopia with significant astigmatism. Cover test shows an accommodative esotropia. There is no cataract at this age.
The child has the classic facial features of Down syndrome (flat nasal bridge, epicanthal folds, upslanting palpebral fissures). He has a prior cardiac repair.
Spectacle correction for refractive error. Patching or atropine for amblyopia if present. Annual eye exams. Monitor for keratoconus as the child grows older.
teachingPoints: Children with Down syndrome have a high prevalence of ocular abnormalities: refractive errors (35-75%), strabismus (20-45%), nasolacrimal duct obstruction (15-35%), Brushfield spots (38-90%), and increased risk of keratoconus.
Brushfield spots are a classic iris finding—they represent normal iris stroma surrounding areas of iris hypoplasia and are more easily visible in lighter-colored eyes.
Keratoconus is significantly more common in Down syndrome (5-15% vs. 0.05% general population), likely related to chronic eye rubbing.
AAP guidelines recommend eye examination by a pediatric ophthalmologist by 6 months of age, then annually, for all children with Down syndrome.

clues: A 2-year-old child is referred for evaluation of an unusual iris appearance and elevated IOP in the right eye.

The right iris shows a smooth, glistening, flat sheet of pigmented tissue on the anterior iris surface extending from the pupillary margin outward (ectropion uveae—posterior pigment epithelium growing onto the anterior surface). Glassy iris membrane is present. IOP is 30 mmHg OD.
Gonioscopy shows a high iris insertion with dysgenetic angle. The iris surface shows a thickened, smooth, featureless surface. Corneal diameter is normal.
The condition is unilateral. There is no history of uveitis, trauma, or neovascularization (which can cause acquired ectropion uveae).
Glaucoma management with angle surgery (goniotomy) or tube shunt. The ectropion uveae itself does not require treatment.
teachingPoints: Congenital ectropion uveae is a rare developmental anomaly in which the iris pigment epithelium proliferates onto the anterior iris surface, associated with ipsilateral glaucoma from angle dysgenesis.
It must be distinguished from acquired ectropion uveae, which is caused by neovascularization (NVG), uveitis, or iris melanoma pulling the pigment epithelium forward.
Congenital ectropion uveae is often associated with neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1)—the patient should be evaluated for café-au-lait spots and other NF1 features.
Glaucoma in congenital ectropion uveae is often refractory and may require multiple surgical interventions.

clues: A 40-year-old female presents with severe bilateral simultaneous vision loss over 3 days with eye pain on movement. She had a transverse myelitis episode 1 year ago.

Visual acuity is counting fingers bilaterally. Bilateral RAPD (by reverse testing). Severe color vision loss. Both optic discs are swollen with surrounding hemorrhages. The visual loss is much more severe than typical MS-related optic neuritis.

MRI shows long-segment (>3 vertebral segments) optic nerve enhancement bilaterally. MRI spine shows a longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis lesion (>3 spinal segments). Serum aquaporin-4 (AQP4-IgG) antibody is positive.
The severity of visual loss (bilateral, severe, disc edema) is atypical for MS optic neuritis. The history of transverse myelitis raises the suspicion for NMOSD.

Acute: high-dose IV methylprednisolone followed by plasma exchange (PLEX) if no improvement. Long-term: eculizumab, inebilizumab, or satralizumab (FDA-approved for NMOSD). Do NOT treat with MS disease-modifying therapies.
teachingPoints: NMOSD-related optic neuritis is typically more severe than MS optic neuritis: bilateral, simultaneous, with profound vision loss and poor recovery.
AQP4-IgG antibody testing is essential; this distinguishes NMOSD from MS, as the treatment and prognosis differ substantially.
Several MS therapies (interferon-beta, natalizumab, fingolimod) can worsen NMOSD—correct diagnosis is critical.
Eculizumab (complement C5 inhibitor), inebilizumab (anti-CD19), and satralizumab (anti-IL-6R) have all been FDA-approved specifically for AQP4-IgG-positive NMOSD.

clues: A 35-year-old male with bilateral granulomatous panuveitis (suspected VKH) has multifocal small yellow-white lesions at the level of the RPE on dilated exam.

Fundoscopy reveals small, round, yellow-white nodules scattered throughout the mid-periphery at the level of the RPE and Bruch membrane. They are beneath the neurosensory retina. There is bilateral exudative retinal detachment.
FA shows the nodules as pinpoint areas of early hyperfluorescence that increase in intensity. Histopathologically, they represent collections of epithelioid cells between Bruch membrane and the RPE.
The patient meets diagnostic criteria for VKH disease. He has bilateral serous retinal detachments, vitritis, and auditory symptoms.
Treat the underlying condition (VKH or sympathetic ophthalmia) with aggressive systemic immunosuppression. The nodules resolve with control of inflammation.
teachingPoints: Dalen-Fuchs nodules are small granulomas located between the RPE and Bruch membrane, classically associated with sympathetic ophthalmia and VKH disease.
They are NOT pathognomonic for any single disease—they can also be seen in sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, and other granulomatous inflammations.
Histologically, they consist of collections of epithelioid macrophages, lymphocytes, and depigmented RPE cells.
Their presence helps support the diagnosis of granulomatous choroidal inflammation when found in the appropriate clinical context.

clues: A 22-year-old female contact lens wearer presents with acute onset of severe pain, redness, photophobia, and decreased vision in her left eye that began 2 days ago.
A central corneal infiltrate (2 mm) with overlying epithelial defect and surrounding stromal edema is present. There is a small hypopyon. The contact lens is still in the eye.
Corneal scrapings are obtained for gram stain and culture on blood agar, chocolate agar, and Sabouraud agar before starting empiric treatment. Gram stain shows gram-positive cocci in clusters.
The patient sleeps in her 2-week replacement contact lenses and has been using them for 6 weeks. She rinses her case with tap water.
Remove the contact lens immediately. Start empiric fortified topical antibiotics (cefazolin + tobramycin) or fluoroquinolone monotherapy every 30-60 minutes. Never patch. Follow up in 24 hours.
teachingPoints: Contact lens wear is the number one risk factor for bacterial keratitis in developed countries; overnight wear increases risk 6-8 fold.
The most important risk factors are sleeping in lenses, poor hygiene, extended wear beyond recommended replacement schedule, and tap water exposure.
Culture before treatment is essential for central, large, or deep ulcers to guide antibiotic adjustment if the empiric regimen fails.
Even after resolution, corneal scarring may require penetrating keratoplasty for visual rehabilitation if central.

diagnosis: Periocular Capillary Malformation (Port-Wine Stain without Sturge-Weber)
clues: A 6-month-old infant presents with a flat, red-purple birthmark on the left upper eyelid and forehead. The parents want to know if it affects the eyes.

A flat, non-blanching, red-purple capillary malformation (port-wine stain) is present in the left V1 dermatome. IOP is 14 mmHg bilaterally. Corneal diameters are equal. There is no episcleral hemangioma or choroidal abnormality.
MRI brain with gadolinium shows no leptomeningeal enhancement (no Sturge-Weber syndrome). The fundus is normal bilaterally. The anterior segment is normal.
The birthmark involves V1 but all ocular and neurological evaluations are normal at this time.
Annual ophthalmologic screening including IOP measurement (glaucoma can develop later in life even without SWS). Laser treatment (pulsed dye laser) for the cosmetic port-wine stain.
teachingPoints: Not all periocular port-wine stains have Sturge-Weber syndrome; isolated capillary malformations involving V1 still carry a lifetime risk of ipsilateral glaucoma (~10%).
The risk of SWS and glaucoma is highest when the port-wine stain involves BOTH the V1 and V2 dermatomes.
Glaucoma can develop at any age—congenital, childhood, or adult-onset—requiring lifelong monitoring.
Pulsed dye laser is effective for lightening port-wine stains and is most effective when started in infancy.

diagnosis: Central Retinal Artery Occlusion with Ophthalmic Artery Occlusion
clues: A 75-year-old male presents with sudden complete vision loss (no light perception) in the left eye 1 hour ago.

Visual acuity is NLP OS. There is a fixed, dilated pupil with no direct light response. Fundoscopy shows diffuse retinal whitening WITHOUT a cherry-red spot. The optic disc is pale. There is no choroidal perfusion.

FA shows NO choroidal filling (delayed or absent choroidal flush) in addition to absent retinal arterial filling. The absent cherry-red spot indicates that even the choroidal blood supply to the fovea is compromised.
The patient has atrial fibrillation and recent TIA symptoms. Carotid Doppler shows complete occlusion of the left internal carotid artery.
Emergent stroke protocol. Thrombolysis consideration if within the treatment window. Carotid and cardiac workup. Prognosis for visual recovery is extremely poor.
teachingPoints: Ophthalmic artery occlusion is more devastating than CRAO alone: both the retinal AND choroidal circulations are compromised, eliminating the foveal blood supply and abolishing the cherry-red spot.
Absence of a cherry-red spot in the setting of diffuse retinal whitening indicates combined retinal and choroidal ischemia—a much worse prognosis than isolated CRAO.
ERG shows extinction of both a-wave (photoreceptor, choroidal supply) and b-wave (inner retina), unlike CRAO where the a-wave may be preserved.
This represents a complete ocular stroke and mandates urgent systemic vascular workup for carotid disease and cardiac embolic sources.

clues: A 45-year-old male with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes presents with gradual progressive vision loss in his right eye over weeks.

Visual acuity is counting fingers OD. Fundoscopy shows a concave, immobile retinal detachment in the posterior pole with fibrovascular membranes pulling the retina forward from the disc and along the vascular arcades. There are no retinal breaks visible.
OCT shows the macula is tented up by a tractional membrane. B-scan shows a non-mobile, taut retinal detachment (not undulating like rhegmatogenous RD). Prior PRP scars are visible in the periphery.
The patient has had proliferative diabetic retinopathy for years with prior PRP treatment. HbA1c is 11%. He has dialysis-dependent nephropathy.
Pars plana vitrectomy with membrane peeling, endolaser, and silicone oil or gas tamponade. Anti-VEGF injection preoperatively (3-7 days before) to reduce intraoperative bleeding.
teachingPoints: Tractional RD in diabetes is caused by contraction of fibrovascular proliferative membranes that grow along the posterior hyaloid face, pulling the retina away from the RPE.
TRD involving or threatening the macula is the primary surgical indication; peripheral TRD not involving the macula can be observed.
Combined tractional-rhegmatogenous RD (when a break forms in the thinned, tractionally detached retina) is more urgent as subretinal fluid spreads rapidly.
Preoperative anti-VEGF injection reduces intraoperative hemorrhage but must be timed carefully (3-7 days before surgery), as it can accelerate membrane contraction if surgery is delayed.

clues: A 55-year-old highly myopic female (-14.00 D) presents with gradually worsening metamorphopsia in both eyes.
Fundoscopy reveals bilateral posterior staphyloma with a dome-shaped macular elevation. Visual acuity is 20/80 OD and 20/60 OS.

OCT shows foveoschisis (splitting of the retinal layers at the fovea), a dome-shaped macula (inward convexity of the RPE within the staphyloma), and an epiretinal membrane with vitreomacular traction. There is NO full-thickness macular hole yet.
Axial length is 30 mm bilaterally. The condition is slowly progressive over years.
Observation for stable cases. Pars plana vitrectomy with ILM peeling for progressive foveoschisis, foveal detachment, or if a macular hole develops. Macular buckle is an alternative technique.
teachingPoints: Myopic traction maculopathy encompasses foveoschisis, foveal detachment, and macular hole in highly myopic eyes with posterior staphyloma.
The dome-shaped macula is a protective configuration where the RPE bulges inward within the staphyloma; paradoxically, it may be associated with serous foveal detachment.
Vitrectomy with ILM peeling is the standard surgical approach, but the long axial length increases the technical difficulty.
Macular buckling (external indentation device placed beneath the staphyloma) is an alternative approach that addresses the biomechanical cause of the maculopathy.

diagnosis: Optic Disc Coloboma with Renal Anomaly
clues: A 6-year-old girl is found to have abnormal optic discs and proteinuria during evaluation for growth failure.
Both optic discs are dysplastic with excavated, enlarged cups resembling colobomas. There is a 'morning glory'-like appearance with central glial tissue. Visual acuity is 20/60 bilaterally.
Renal ultrasound shows small, echogenic kidneys bilaterally. Urinalysis shows proteinuria. Genetic testing reveals a PAX2 gene mutation.
The child has both optic disc anomalies and renal hypoplasia. There is no iris coloboma.
Nephrology referral for monitoring of renal function (may progress to renal failure). Correct refractive error. Amblyopia management. Genetic counseling.
teachingPoints: Papillorenal (renal-coloboma) syndrome is an autosomal dominant condition caused by PAX2 mutations, characterized by optic disc dysplasia and renal anomalies (hypoplasia, vesicoureteral reflux).
The optic disc anomaly is NOT a true coloboma (not from fissure closure failure) but rather optic disc dysplasia from PAX2's role in optic nerve development.
Renal disease can progress to end-stage renal failure; early detection through genetic testing and renal screening is important.
Any child with bilateral optic disc anomalies should have renal imaging to screen for papillorenal syndrome.

clues: A 5-year-old girl is referred for evaluation of a unilateral abnormal optic disc found during routine screening.

The left optic disc is enlarged with a funnel-shaped excavation, a central white glial tuft, and radially arranged retinal vessels that emerge from the disc rim. There is an elevated annular ring of chorioretinal pigment surrounding the disc. Visual acuity is 20/200 OS.
OCT shows the deep funnel-shaped excavation with central glial tissue. MRI brain shows no transsphenoidal encephalocele. MRA is obtained to evaluate the intracranial vasculature.
The condition is unilateral. There is no iris coloboma. The right eye is normal.
No treatment for the disc anomaly itself. Monitor for serous retinal detachment (occurs in 30%). Screen for transsphenoidal encephalocele and moyamoya vasculopathy with MRI/MRA.
teachingPoints: Morning glory disc anomaly is a congenital, typically unilateral, optic disc malformation with a distinctive flower-like appearance resembling a morning glory flower.
Associations include transsphenoidal encephalocele (rare but important), moyamoya vasculopathy, and ipsilateral facial anomalies.
Non-rhegmatogenous serous retinal detachment occurs in approximately 30% of morning glory discs, possibly from CSF communication through the disc anomaly.
MRI and MRA should be obtained to rule out basal encephalocele and evaluate cerebral vasculature, especially in patients with midline facial anomalies.

clues: A 20-year-old male presents with progressive bilateral loss of central vision, photophobia, and color vision impairment since his early teens.
Visual acuity is 20/200 bilaterally. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral bull's-eye maculopathy with RPE atrophy surrounding the fovea. The peripheral retina appears normal.

Photopic ERG is markedly reduced (cone function); scotopic ERG is normal (rod function preserved). OCT shows ellipsoid zone loss at the fovea. Color vision testing shows severe protan and deutan defects.
He has progressive photophobia, poor central vision, and impaired color vision, while night vision is normal—the opposite pattern of retinitis pigmentosa.
No proven treatment. Tinted lenses for photophobia. Low vision aids. Genetic counseling. Gene therapy trials are being investigated for some subtypes.
teachingPoints: Cone dystrophies preferentially affect cone photoreceptors, causing progressive central vision loss, color vision deficiency, and photophobia, while rod function and peripheral vision are preserved.
Bull's-eye maculopathy on fundoscopy is characteristic, though not pathognomonic (also seen in hydroxychloroquine toxicity, Stargardt disease, and benign concentric macular dystrophy).
Photopic ERG (cone-mediated) is reduced while scotopic ERG (rod-mediated) is preserved—the reverse of retinitis pigmentosa (rod-cone dystrophy).
Cone-rod dystrophy (where rod function also eventually declines) is a more severe variant with eventual nyctalopia and peripheral field loss.

clues: A 65-year-old female presents with gradually progressive bilateral ptosis. She feels her eyelids have become heavier over the past few years.
Bilateral mild-to-moderate ptosis (2-3 mm) with a high or absent lid crease. Levator function is good-to-excellent (>12 mm). The lid crease is elevated above normal position (>10 mm from lash line).
The ptosis worsens with fatigue but is not as dramatic as in myasthenia. Ice test and rest test are negative. No orbicularis weakness.
The patient has a history of long-term contact lens wear (30 years of hard contact lenses) and prior ocular surgery. Her mother had similar ptosis.
Levator aponeurosis advancement/reinsertion via a skin crease approach is the standard surgical repair with excellent outcomes.
teachingPoints: Aponeurotic (involutional) ptosis is the most common type of acquired ptosis, caused by dehiscence or disinsertion of the levator aponeurosis from the tarsal plate.
The hallmark is ptosis with GOOD levator function and a HIGH lid crease—the muscle works well, but its connection to the tarsal plate is stretched or detached.
Risk factors include aging, contact lens wear (especially rigid lenses), eye rubbing, and prior ocular surgery.
Levator advancement surgery has a success rate >90% and is performed as an outpatient procedure under local anesthesia.

clues: A 30-year-old female with thalassemia major on chronic transfusion therapy and deferoxamine chelation presents with nyctalopia and decreased color vision.
Visual acuity is 20/40 bilaterally. Fundoscopy shows subtle RPE mottling and depigmentation in the macula. The peripheral retina appears relatively normal.

ERG shows reduced scotopic (rod) and photopic (cone) responses. OCT shows disruption of the outer retinal layers. Fundus autofluorescence shows macular hypo-autofluorescence with a hyperautofluorescent border.
The patient has been on high-dose IV deferoxamine (>50 mg/kg/day) for 10 years. She has sensorineural hearing loss diagnosed recently.
Reduce or discontinue deferoxamine in consultation with hematology. Switch to an oral iron chelator (deferasirox). Some recovery may occur after dose reduction.
teachingPoints: Deferoxamine (Desferal) can cause a dose-dependent retinal toxicity affecting both the RPE and photoreceptors, along with sensorineural hearing loss.
The mechanism involves chelation of essential metals (iron, zinc, copper) needed for RPE and photoreceptor function.
Risk factors include high dose (>50 mg/kg/day), low ferritin levels (chelator exceeds available iron and chelates essential metals), and long duration of therapy.
Annual ophthalmologic screening including ERG is recommended for patients on chronic deferoxamine therapy.

clues: A 12-year-old boy presents with intermittent periorbital swelling and proptosis after upper respiratory infections. The swelling resolves between episodes.
Mild proptosis is present on the right. A firm, deep mass is palpable at the lateral orbital rim. It is not freely mobile. During episodes of URI, the mass becomes inflamed and the proptosis worsens.
CT shows a well-circumscribed cystic lesion extending through the frontozygomatic suture into the temporal fossa (dumbbell configuration). Bony scalloping and thinning are present at the suture line.
The lesion extends intracranially or into the temporal fossa (deep extension), unlike the superficial dermoids seen in younger children.
Surgical excision with intact capsule removal, typically requiring a combined lateral orbitotomy and craniotomy approach for dumbbell-shaped lesions with deep extension.
teachingPoints: Deep orbital dermoids can extend through bony sutures into the temporal fossa, infratemporal fossa, or even intracranially, requiring more complex surgical approaches.
Inflammatory episodes from micro-rupture of the cyst contents (keratin, sebaceous material) can cause acute orbital inflammation mimicking cellulitis.
CT with bone windows is essential to evaluate the extent of bony involvement and rule out intracranial extension before surgery.
Unlike superficial dermoids (simple outpatient excision), deep dermoids require careful imaging-guided surgical planning.

clues: A 60-year-old female presents with foreign body sensation, tearing, and irregular astigmatism that has been gradually worsening.

Slit-lamp shows multiple elevated, bluish-white, nodular deposits on the cornea at the level of the anterior stroma, primarily in the mid-periphery but some approaching the visual axis. The overlying epithelium is smooth and intact.
Corneal topography shows irregular astigmatism from the elevated nodules. Pachymetry shows focal thinning between nodules. The deposits are at the level of Bowman layer.
The patient has a history of chronic blepharitis, meibomian gland dysfunction, and prior episodes of corneal erosions. She has used soft contact lenses for 30 years.
