Observation if mild. Superficial keratectomy (manual peeling of nodules at the slit lamp) or PTK for visually significant nodules. Recurrence can occur.
teachingPoints: Salzmann nodular degeneration consists of subepithelial deposits of hyaline material that replace Bowman layer, often secondary to chronic corneal inflammation or contact lens wear.
Common associations include chronic blepharitis, meibomian gland dysfunction, prior keratitis, phlyctenulosis, and long-term contact lens use.
The nodules can be manually peeled at the slit lamp under topical anesthesia in many cases, providing immediate visual improvement.
Recurrence rate after removal is approximately 15-25%; treating underlying blepharitis and inflammation reduces recurrence.

clues: A 50-year-old female presents for a routine eye exam. She has occasional mild blur in the morning that clears throughout the day.

Slit-lamp reveals bilateral geographic map-like patches of gray opacification, small round microcysts (dots), and fine parallel lines (fingerprints) in the corneal epithelium. The findings are best seen with retroillumination and narrow beam.
The epithelium shows areas of irregular thickening and redundant basement membrane. Visual acuity is 20/25. There is no stromal involvement.
The condition is bilateral, chronic, and incidental in most cases. She has no history of trauma.
Observation if asymptomatic. Lubricating drops. If recurrent erosions develop: hypertonic saline drops, bandage contact lens, or PTK in refractory cases.
teachingPoints: EBMD (map-dot-fingerprint dystrophy) is the most common corneal dystrophy, affecting approximately 2-5% of the adult population. Despite its name, it is more accurately classified as a corneal degeneration.
The maps, dots, and fingerprints are all manifestations of aberrant basement membrane produced by the corneal epithelium that folds into the epithelial layer.
Most patients are asymptomatic; it is primarily significant as a predisposing factor for recurrent corneal erosions.
EBMD can cause fluctuating vision from irregular astigmatism and is an important consideration before LASIK (can cause epithelial complications).

clues: A 55-year-old male presents with chronic irritation, foreign body sensation, and tearing in his left eye.

Several eyelashes on the left lower eyelid are misdirected posteriorly, rubbing against the cornea. There is focal inferior corneal epithelial staining at the point of lash contact. The eyelid position is normal (no entropion).
The lid margin architecture is normal. There is no cicatricial changes, no symblepharon, and no conjunctival scarring.
The patient has chronic anterior blepharitis with prior meibomian gland changes. He has no history of trachoma, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, or chemical injury.
Epilation (plucking) for temporary relief (lashes regrow in 4-6 weeks). Electrolysis, radiofrequency ablation, or cryotherapy to the lash follicle for permanent destruction.
teachingPoints: Trichiasis is misdirection of otherwise normally positioned eyelashes toward the globe; it differs from distichiasis (an extra row of lashes from the meibomian gland orifices).
Chronic blepharitis is the most common cause of isolated trichiasis in developed countries; trachoma is the most common cause worldwide.
Corneal complications from chronic lash abrasion include epithelial erosion, stromal ulceration, and corneal vascularization.
Cryotherapy offers the highest permanent success rate (~90%) but can cause lid depigmentation and notching.

clues: A 20-year-old female presents with chronic bilateral eye irritation and photophobia. She has a family history of 'extra eyelashes.'

An accessory row of fine eyelashes is emerging from the meibomian gland orifices (posterior lamella) of both upper and lower eyelids, in addition to the normal lash row. The abnormal lashes are thinner and softer than normal lashes.
The abnormal posterior lashes are contacting the cornea, causing bilateral inferior punctate epithelial erosions. Lower extremity exam reveals bilateral lower limb lymphedema.
The patient has lymphedema-distichiasis syndrome. Genetic testing reveals a FOXC2 gene mutation (autosomal dominant).
Electrolysis or cryotherapy to destroy the aberrant follicles. Lid splitting with cryotherapy to the posterior lamella for extensive involvement. Treat underlying lymphedema.
teachingPoints: Distichiasis is the presence of an accessory row of eyelashes arising from the meibomian gland orifices, distinct from the normal anterior row.
Lymphedema-distichiasis syndrome (FOXC2 mutation) is the classic genetic association: distichiasis + lower limb lymphedema with onset at puberty.
Unlike trichiasis (normal lashes misdirected), distichiasis involves EXTRA lashes from an abnormal location (posterior lid margin).
Congenital distichiasis should prompt evaluation for lymphedema-distichiasis syndrome, which can also include cardiac defects and varicose veins.

clues: A 45-year-old male presents with poor vision in his left eye dating back to a severe corneal ulcer 5 years ago.
A dense, white corneal scar (leucoma) occupies the central 4 mm of the left cornea. Visual acuity is 20/400 OS with best correction. The surrounding cornea is clear. There is no active inflammation.
The scar is well-healed and stable. There is no thinning, vascularization, or active infiltrate. Anterior segment OCT shows the depth of the scar extending into the mid-stroma.
The original ulcer was a Pseudomonas keratitis treated with fortified antibiotics. The scar has been stable for 3 years.

Corneal transplantation (penetrating keratoplasty or deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty) for visual rehabilitation. Rigid gas-permeable contact lens may provide modest improvement by creating a regular refractive surface over the scar.
teachingPoints: Central corneal scarring from infectious keratitis is one of the leading indications for corneal transplantation worldwide.
DALK (deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty) is preferred over PK when the endothelium is healthy, as it preserves the host endothelium and has a lower rejection risk.
Vascularized scars carry a higher risk of graft rejection after transplantation.
In developing countries where corneal transplantation is not available, corneal scarring from infection remains a leading cause of monocular blindness.

clues: A 25-year-old healthy Indian male presents with sudden decreased vision from a vitreous hemorrhage in his right eye.

After the vitreous blood partially clears, fundoscopy reveals perivascular white sheathing around retinal veins (periphlebitis) predominantly in the peripheral retina. There is peripheral retinal neovascularization and non-perfusion.

FA shows extensive peripheral capillary non-perfusion, perivascular leakage from affected veins, and peripheral neovascularization. Systemic workup including TB, sarcoidosis, and autoimmune markers is negative.
The patient is a young, otherwise healthy Indian male. The inflammation is predominantly peripheral periphlebitis. All systemic tests are negative.

PRP to areas of retinal ischemia. Vitrectomy for non-clearing vitreous hemorrhage. Systemic steroids for active vasculitis. Anti-TB therapy is sometimes given empirically given the geographic association.
teachingPoints: Eales disease is an idiopathic peripheral retinal periphlebitis predominantly affecting young healthy men from the Indian subcontinent, causing ischemia, neovascularization, and vitreous hemorrhage.
While officially 'idiopathic,' there is growing evidence of a hypersensitivity reaction to tubercular proteins, which is why anti-TB therapy is sometimes given empirically.
The disease progresses through three stages: inflammation (periphlebitis), ischemia (non-perfusion), and proliferation (neovascularization with hemorrhage).
Recurrent vitreous hemorrhage is the most common cause of visual morbidity; PRP to ischemic retina is the key treatment to prevent neovascularization.

clues: A 65-year-old male with a prior Ahmed valve implant presents with redness and discomfort over the tube shunt site in his left eye.
The conjunctiva overlying the tube has eroded, exposing the tube (bare tube visible). The tube is patent and positioned in the anterior chamber. IOP is 14 mmHg. There is mild surrounding injection.
No endophthalmitis is present (no vitritis, no hypopyon). The plate is palpable in the superotemporal quadrant under intact conjunctiva.
The patient had an Ahmed valve placed 2 years ago for refractory glaucoma. He has thin conjunctiva from multiple prior surgeries and chronic mitomycin C exposure.
Urgent surgical revision with patch graft material (scleral patch, pericardium, or corneal tissue) to cover the exposed tube and re-cover with conjunctival advancement. Prophylactic antibiotics.
teachingPoints: Tube exposure is one of the most common complications of glaucoma drainage devices, occurring in approximately 5-7% of cases, and creates a direct pathway for endophthalmitis.
Risk factors for tube exposure include thin conjunctiva, prior conjunctival surgery, mitomycin C use, and inadequate patch graft coverage.
Tube exposure is a surgical urgency (not emergency) because of the risk of endophthalmitis through the exposed tube—bacteria can track along the tube into the eye.
Other tube shunt complications include hypotony (tube draining too much), diplopia (from plate position), corneal decompensation (tube-endothelium touch), and tube obstruction.

clues: A 25-year-old male presents with sudden vision loss in his right eye after a BB gun pellet struck the orbit (but did not penetrate the globe).

Visual acuity is 20/400 OD. Fundoscopy reveals a large, full-thickness chorioretinal rupture extending from the optic disc to the ora serrata with exposed bare sclera, surrounding subretinal and vitreous hemorrhage, and retinal tissue disruption. The globe is intact.
B-scan confirms the chorioretinal defect without globe rupture. CT orbits shows orbital fracture and periorbital soft tissue injury but intact globe. No intraocular foreign body.
The injury was from a high-velocity projectile that impacted the orbit without penetrating the eye wall. The shockwave transmitted through the orbital tissues caused the damage.
Observation for most cases. The bare sclera eventually becomes covered by fibrous tissue. Vitrectomy if non-clearing vitreous hemorrhage or retinal detachment develops.
teachingPoints: Sclopetaria is a full-thickness chorioretinal rupture caused by the shockwave from a high-velocity projectile passing adjacent to the globe without perforating it.
Despite the dramatic fundus appearance (bare sclera exposed), the globe wall is intact—this is an indirect injury from the transmitted energy.
The exposed sclera typically becomes covered by fibrovascular tissue over weeks to months.
Visual prognosis depends on the location of the rupture relative to the fovea; foveal involvement carries a poor prognosis.

clues: A 55-year-old male presents with a chronic, non-tender, waxy, yellowish mass on the palpebral conjunctiva of his right eye that has been slowly growing.

A smooth, waxy, yellow-pink, firm subconjunctival mass is present on the inferior fornix. The surface has a glassy, waxy sheen. There are small hemorrhages within the lesion (amyloid infiltrates vessel walls making them fragile).

Biopsy shows amorphous, eosinophilic material that stains with Congo red and shows apple-green birefringence under polarized light (pathognomonic for amyloid). Immunohistochemistry identifies AL (immunoglobulin light chain) amyloid.
Systemic amyloidosis workup including serum and urine protein electrophoresis, free light chains, cardiac evaluation, and renal function is negative (localized disease).
Excisional biopsy for localized conjunctival amyloidosis. Systemic workup to rule out systemic amyloidosis. Long-term monitoring for recurrence and systemic disease development.
teachingPoints: Localized conjunctival amyloidosis is a rare condition where amyloid protein deposits in the conjunctival substantia propria; it is usually localized and not associated with systemic amyloidosis.
Congo red staining with apple-green birefringence under polarized light microscopy is the gold standard for diagnosing amyloid deposits.
Despite being usually localized, systemic amyloidosis workup is mandatory at diagnosis to rule out systemic AL amyloidosis.
Spontaneous hemorrhage within the lesion is characteristic because amyloid infiltration weakens vessel walls.

clues: A 35-year-old male who had LASIK 5 years ago presents with progressively worsening vision in his right eye despite updated glasses.

Slit-lamp shows a thin cornea with inferior steepening and visible flap interface. Corneal topography reveals progressive inferior steepening with increasing against-the-rule astigmatism (ectasia pattern). Corneal thickness is reduced to 380 microns centrally.

The preoperative residual stromal bed was calculated to be <250 microns (below the safe threshold of 300 microns). The patient had borderline forme fruste keratoconus on preoperative topography that was not identified.
The ectasia has progressed steadily. The patient was moderate myope with relatively thin corneas preoperatively. No ectasia risk screening tools (like the Randleman criteria) were applied.
Corneal cross-linking (CXL) to halt progression. Rigid gas-permeable or scleral contact lenses for visual rehabilitation. Intracorneal ring segments. Corneal transplant for advanced cases.
teachingPoints: Post-LASIK ectasia is an iatrogenic progressive corneal thinning and bulging that mimics keratoconus, occurring when too much tissue is ablated or the cornea had pre-existing weakness.
Risk factors include thin corneas, high myopia, residual stromal bed <300 microns, abnormal topography (forme fruste keratoconus), and young age.
CXL is the first-line treatment to stabilize the cornea and prevent further progression.
Modern screening tools (Belin-Ambrosio enhanced ectasia display, corneal biomechanics) have reduced but not eliminated the risk of post-LASIK ectasia.

clues: A 70-year-old male presents with a gelatinous, slightly elevated lesion at the limbus of his right eye that has been slowly growing over months.
A flat-to-slightly-elevated, gelatinous, translucent lesion with intrinsic vascularity is present at the temporal limbus, extending onto the adjacent cornea with a frosted, leukoplakic surface.

Anterior segment OCT shows a thickened, hyperreflective epithelium with an abrupt transition from normal to abnormal tissue and no stromal invasion (confirming intraepithelial/pre-invasive disease). Impression cytology shows dysplastic cells.
The patient has significant UV exposure and is immunocompetent. Biopsy confirms CIN III (carcinoma in situ) without stromal invasion.
Primary topical chemotherapy with interferon alpha-2b (first-line) or 5-FU or mitomycin C. Excisional biopsy with cryotherapy for residual or recurrent lesions.
teachingPoints: OSSN encompasses a spectrum from mild dysplasia (CIN I) through carcinoma in situ (CIN III) to invasive squamous cell carcinoma.
Topical interferon alpha-2b has become the preferred primary treatment, with cure rates >90% and minimal side effects.
Risk factors include UV exposure, HPV infection, HIV, advanced age, and fair skin.
AS-OCT is a valuable non-invasive tool for diagnosis and monitoring treatment response, showing the characteristic thickened hyperreflective epithelium.

clues: A 50-year-old male presents with gradually blurred vision in his right eye. He is otherwise healthy.
Visual acuity is 20/60 OD. Fundoscopy reveals unilateral temporal juxtafoveal telangiectatic vessels with surrounding lipid exudation (hard exudates) and microaneurysms. There is macular edema.
FA shows telangiectatic vessels temporal to the fovea with late leakage and microaneurysm formation. OCT shows intraretinal fluid and hard exudates. The left eye is normal.
The condition is unilateral (distinguishing from MacTel Type 2 which is bilateral). The exudation pattern is similar to diabetic macular edema but the patient is not diabetic.
Focal laser photocoagulation to the telangiectatic vessels. Anti-VEGF for macular edema. The condition may be related to a congenital vascular anomaly.
teachingPoints: Type 1 idiopathic juxtafoveal retinal telangiectasia is a unilateral condition of aneurysmal telangiectatic changes in the temporal juxtafoveal capillaries, causing exudation.
It is distinct from MacTel Type 2 (which is bilateral, has minimal exudation, and involves neurodegenerative changes).
The unilateral exudative presentation can mimic branch retinal vein occlusion, diabetic macular edema, or radiation retinopathy.
Focal laser to the telangiectatic vessels is effective at reducing exudation and improving vision.

clues: A 6-month-old infant is noted to have nystagmus and poor visual behavior. The pediatrician notes a small optic disc.

Both optic discs are small (less than half normal size) with a 'double ring sign'—a peripapillary ring of pigment and a ring of exposed sclera surrounding the small disc. Visual acuity is severely reduced.
MRI brain shows absence of the septum pellucidum and pituitary hypoplasia—consistent with septo-optic dysplasia (de Morsier syndrome). Endocrine evaluation reveals growth hormone deficiency.
The child also has neonatal hypoglycemia from cortisol deficiency. Bilateral optic nerve hypoplasia with midline brain anomalies and pituitary dysfunction.

Endocrine replacement for pituitary hormone deficiencies (critical—can be life-threatening). Visual rehabilitation and low vision services. There is no treatment for the optic nerve hypoplasia itself.
teachingPoints: Optic nerve hypoplasia is a congenital condition of underdeveloped optic nerves; when associated with pituitary dysfunction and midline brain defects, it is called septo-optic dysplasia.
The 'double ring sign' on fundoscopy is characteristic: the inner ring is the small optic disc, and the outer ring is the normal-sized scleral canal.
Endocrine evaluation is mandatory for all children with optic nerve hypoplasia, as pituitary hormone deficiencies (especially cortisol and growth hormone) can be life-threatening.
Risk factors include young maternal age, maternal diabetes, fetal alcohol syndrome, and prenatal drug exposure.

clues: A 65-year-old male smoker presents with new photopsias and blurred vision in his left eye. He has a chronic cough.

Fundoscopy reveals a flat, creamy-yellow choroidal lesion in the posterior pole with overlying subretinal fluid. The lesion has an irregular surface and does not have the dome/mushroom shape of melanoma.
B-scan shows a flat, moderately reflective choroidal lesion (higher reflectivity than melanoma). Chest X-ray reveals a large lung mass. CT staging shows metastatic disease.
The patient has a 50-pack-year smoking history. He was unaware of the lung cancer. The ocular lesion is the presenting finding that led to the systemic cancer diagnosis.
External beam radiation therapy to the eye. Systemic oncology for the lung cancer (chemotherapy, immunotherapy based on staging and histology).
teachingPoints: Choroidal metastasis may be the PRESENTING sign of an unknown systemic malignancy; any new choroidal lesion in a patient with cancer risk factors should prompt systemic workup.
Lung cancer is the most common primary in men with choroidal metastasis; breast cancer is most common in women.
Unlike choroidal melanoma (dome/mushroom-shaped, pigmented, low reflectivity), metastases are flat/placoid, creamy-yellow, and have medium-high internal reflectivity.
Bilateral and multifocal involvement is common in choroidal metastases, especially from breast cancer.

clues: A 22-year-old male who had corneal cross-linking for progressive keratoconus 3 months ago presents with persistent haze and reduced vision.

Visual acuity is 20/60 OD (was 20/40 pre-CXL). Slit-lamp shows a demarcation line at 300 microns depth (expected finding) and moderate anterior stromal haze. There is mild superior corneal thinning that appears more pronounced than pre-CXL.
Corneal topography shows initial steepening (common in early post-CXL period) before expected flattening. Pachymetry shows transient thinning (450 microns, down from pre-CXL 470 microns).
The patient's cornea was 450 microns at the thinnest point pre-CXL (below the recommended 400 micron minimum for standard epi-off protocol). Hypo-osmolar riboflavin was used.

Observation with lubrication—most post-CXL haze resolves within 6-12 months. Low-dose topical steroids for persistent haze. Monitor keratometry for stabilization. If haze persists beyond 12 months, PTK may be considered.
teachingPoints: CXL complications include stromal haze (most common), sterile infiltrates, delayed epithelial healing, persistent corneal thinning, and rarely endothelial damage.
The demarcation line at approximately 300 microns is a NORMAL finding indicating the depth of cross-linking effect.
Minimum corneal thickness of 400 microns (after epithelial removal) is recommended for standard epi-off CXL to protect the endothelium from UV damage.
Post-CXL haze typically peaks at 1-3 months and resolves by 6-12 months in most patients.

clues: An 18-year-old male presents after a severe bicycle accident with complete, immediate, and permanent vision loss in his right eye.

Visual acuity is NLP OD. There is a complete RAPD. Fundoscopy reveals a ring of hemorrhage at the optic disc margin with the optic nerve head partially or completely avulsed from the globe. The retinal vessels are disrupted at the disc.
CT orbits shows irregular optic disc contour and possible retrobulbar hemorrhage. B-scan shows disc disruption. There is no optic nerve enhancement on MRI (the nerve is physically torn).
The mechanism was a sudden extreme rotational or anteroposterior force to the globe. The patient had proptosis at the scene from orbital hemorrhage.
No treatment exists to restore vision after optic nerve avulsion. The injury is irreversible. Supportive care, socket management, and prosthetic fitting if the eye becomes phthisical.
teachingPoints: Optic nerve avulsion is the complete or partial tearing of the optic nerve from its attachment to the globe at the lamina cribrosa.
It is caused by sudden extreme rotational forces, anteroposterior compression, or foreign body insertion that stretches or severs the nerve.
The fundoscopic appearance shows a ring of hemorrhage around the disc with partial or complete absence of the normal disc tissue and disrupted retinal vessels.
Visual prognosis is NLP in complete avulsion and is irreversible because the retinal ganglion cell axons are physically transected at the lamina cribrosa.

clues: A 3-year-old malnourished child from a developing country presents with bilateral night blindness, dry eyes, and a white foamy patch on the conjunctiva.
Both eyes show conjunctival xerosis (dry, keratinized conjunctiva), a triangular white foamy lesion on the temporal bulbar conjunctiva (Bitot spot), and punctate corneal epithelial staining.
The cornea of the right eye shows a stromal ulcer with keratomalacia (corneal melting). Serum retinol level is critically low (<10 μg/dL). The child has measles skin rash.
The child has chronic malnutrition with protein-calorie deficiency. Measles infection has precipitated the acute worsening.
Emergency high-dose oral vitamin A (200,000 IU) immediately, repeated at day 2 and day 14 (WHO protocol). Topical antibiotic for corneal ulcer. Nutritional rehabilitation.
teachingPoints: Vitamin A deficiency (xerophthalmia) remains the leading cause of preventable childhood blindness worldwide, affecting an estimated 250 million preschool children.
Bitot spots are pathognomonic—they represent keratinized epithelial debris colonized by Corynebacterium xerosis on the dry conjunctival surface.
Keratomalacia (corneal melting/necrosis) is the most severe manifestation and can rapidly lead to corneal perforation and blindness if untreated.
Measles exacerbates vitamin A deficiency and is a common precipitant of acute corneal destruction in malnourished children.

clues: A 75-year-old fair-skinned male presents with a non-healing, crusted, ulcerated lesion on his left lower eyelid that has been present for 6 months and is enlarging.

An indurated, ulcerated, nodular lesion with everted (rolled outward) margins and a keratinized surface is present on the lower lid margin. There is madarosis. Regional lymphadenopathy is palpable (preauricular node).
Incisional biopsy confirms moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma with perineural invasion. CT orbits shows no orbital invasion.
The patient has extensive sun damage (actinic keratoses on face), a history of prior skin SCC on the scalp, and is immunosuppressed (renal transplant recipient on tacrolimus).
Wide surgical excision with Mohs micrographic surgery for margin control. Sentinel lymph node biopsy given palpable nodes. Adjuvant radiation if perineural invasion is confirmed.
teachingPoints: Eyelid SCC is the second most common eyelid malignancy (5-10%) after BCC; it is more aggressive with higher metastatic potential (up to 20% with perineural invasion).
Perineural invasion is more common in SCC than BCC and significantly increases recurrence and metastatic risk.
Immunosuppressed patients (transplant recipients, HIV) have a dramatically increased risk of cutaneous SCC—up to 65-250 times higher.
Unlike BCC, SCC can metastasize to regional lymph nodes and distant sites, making sentinel node evaluation important.

clues: A 65-year-old highly myopic female (-18 D) presents with progressive esotropia and hypotropia of her left eye over years. She now has constant diplopia.

The left eye is fixed in an esotropic and hypotropic position. Attempted abduction and elevation are severely limited. Forced duction test confirms restriction. The globe appears enlarged and proptotic.

MRI orbits shows the enlarged myopic globe herniating superolaterally between the superior rectus and lateral rectus muscles. The lateral rectus is displaced inferiorly and the superior rectus is displaced nasally.
The patient has axial myopia with an axial length of 32 mm. The globe is too large for the muscle cone, causing it to prolapse between muscles.
Surgical correction with muscle union (Yokoyama procedure)—suturing the superior rectus and lateral rectus muscles together to create a hammock that repositions the globe within the muscle cone.
teachingPoints: Heavy eye syndrome (myopic strabismus fixus) results from progressive globe enlargement in high myopia causing the eyeball to herniate between the superior and lateral rectus muscles.
The Yokoyama muscle union procedure (superior rectus–lateral rectus band) repositions the globe and restores motility—a paradigm shift in treatment.
Traditional recession/resection surgery is ineffective because the problem is globe malposition, not muscle imbalance.
MRI showing globe herniation between the SRM and LRM with muscle displacement is the diagnostic hallmark.

clues: A 10-year-old boy presents with acute bilateral blurred vision, floaters, and mild eye pain developing over a few days.

Fundoscopy reveals dramatic bilateral translucent white perivascular sheathing surrounding both arteries and veins, resembling 'frosted tree branches' in winter. There is moderate vitritis, disc edema, and macular edema.
FA shows diffuse perivascular leakage. OCT shows macular edema. CBC reveals lymphocytosis. CMV and Toxoplasma serologies are negative. The child is immunocompetent.
The child had a viral upper respiratory infection 1 week before onset. He is otherwise healthy with no autoimmune history.
Systemic corticosteroids produce dramatic improvement within days. Most cases resolve completely with good visual recovery over weeks.
teachingPoints: Frosted branch angiitis is a rare, acute retinal vasculitis with characteristic dramatic perivascular sheathing of both arteries and veins, most commonly affecting children and young adults.
In immunocompetent patients (especially children), it is typically idiopathic and post-viral with an excellent prognosis and rapid response to steroids.
In immunocompromised patients, it can be associated with CMV, herpes viruses, lymphoma, or leukemia—with a much worse prognosis.
The dramatic 'frosted branch' appearance is nearly pathognomonic and is one of the most visually striking fundus findings in ophthalmology.

clues: A 20-year-old male presents with poor vision in both eyes. He has blue sclerae and a history of joint hypermobility.

Both corneas have a globular, protuberant shape with diffuse corneal thinning from limbus to limbus. The corneal diameter is normal but the cornea is uniformly thin (~300 microns) and bulges forward. There is high irregular astigmatism.

Corneal topography shows diffuse steepening (not inferior steepening as in keratoconus). Pachymetry confirms diffuse thinning throughout. The patient has Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (Type VI/kyphoscoliotic).
Unlike keratoconus (focal inferior thinning and ectasia), the thinning is global. The patient has blue sclerae, joint hyperextensibility, and skin fragility.

Scleral contact lenses for visual rehabilitation. Epikeratoplasty (corneal onlay) or large-diameter DALK may be needed. Standard PK carries high risk of wound dehiscence due to limbus-to-limbus thinning. Avoid eye trauma.
teachingPoints: Keratoglobus is a rare non-inflammatory corneal ectasia with diffuse thinning (most thin at the periphery), distinct from keratoconus (focal inferior thinning).
It can be associated with Ehlers-Danlos syndrome type VI, osteogenesis imperfecta, and Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome.
The cornea is extremely fragile and susceptible to rupture from minor trauma; protective eyewear is essential.
Standard PK is risky because sutures cannot hold in the thinned peripheral cornea; tectonic grafting techniques or epikeratoplasty are preferred.

clues: A 30-year-old female presents with acute onset of paracentral scotomas in both eyes following a flu-like illness with dehydration and hypotension.

Visual acuity is 20/25 bilaterally. Fundoscopy appears grossly normal or shows very subtle perifoveal grayish discoloration. Near-infrared reflectance imaging shows hyperreflective band-like lesions in the perifoveal region.
OCT shows hyperreflective bands at the level of the inner nuclear layer (INL) corresponding to ischemia of the deep capillary plexus. En face OCT clearly delineates the affected zones.
The patient had a hypotensive episode from severe dehydration during the illness. She takes oral contraceptive pills. Microperimetry confirms dense scotomas corresponding to the OCT findings.
No proven treatment. Observation; the acute hyperreflective bands evolve into INL thinning over weeks. The scotomas may partially improve but often persist.
teachingPoints: PAMM is ischemia at the level of the deep capillary plexus (intermediate and deep retinal capillary plexuses) affecting the inner nuclear layer.
It is often associated with retinal vascular events (BRVO, CRVO), hypotension, dehydration, vasoconstrictive states, and sickle cell disease.
OCT is essential for diagnosis as the fundus often looks normal; the INL hyperreflective bands are the diagnostic finding.
PAMM was only recently characterized (2013) thanks to advances in OCT technology and is increasingly recognized as a cause of unexplained paracentral scotomas.

clues: A 55-year-old female treated with external beam radiation for a nasopharyngeal carcinoma 18 months ago presents with gradually worsening vision in both eyes.

Fundoscopy reveals bilateral cotton-wool spots, dot-blot hemorrhages, hard exudates, telangiectatic vessels, and macular edema. The changes resemble diabetic retinopathy but the patient is not diabetic.
FA shows capillary non-perfusion, microaneurysm formation, and macular edema. OCT confirms macular edema with cystic changes. The total radiation dose was 60 Gy.
The patient received high-dose radiation to the head/orbit region 18 months ago. She has no diabetes. The findings are bilateral, consistent with the radiation field.
Intravitreal anti-VEGF for macular edema. PRP for neovascularization. Laser for macular edema if anti-VEGF-refractory.
teachingPoints: Radiation retinopathy is an occlusive microangiopathy caused by radiation-induced endothelial cell damage, clinically resembling diabetic retinopathy.
It is dose-dependent: typically occurs >30 Gy, with risk increasing with higher total dose and concurrent chemotherapy or diabetes.
The latent period is typically 6 months to 3 years after radiation, but can occur up to 15 years later.
Anti-VEGF therapy has significantly improved outcomes for radiation maculopathy, similar to its role in diabetic macular edema.

clues: A 35-year-old male gardener presents with a unilateral granulomatous conjunctivitis and ipsilateral lymphadenopathy (preauricular and submandibular) that has not responded to antibiotics.

A nodular, ulcerated granulomatous lesion is present on the palpebral conjunctiva. The ipsilateral preauricular and cervical lymph nodes are enlarged, firm, and tender. There is mild periorbital edema.
Conjunctival biopsy shows granulomatous inflammation with yeast forms. Culture on Sabouraud agar grows a dimorphic fungus identified as Sporothrix schenckii.

The patient is a rose gardener who sustained thorn pricks to his hand 3 weeks ago, developing a chain of nodular lesions ascending up his arm (lymphocutaneous sporotrichosis). The conjunctival involvement is from autoinoculation.
Oral itraconazole for 3-6 months. Surgical debulking of the conjunctival granuloma if needed.
teachingPoints: Sporotrichosis is caused by the dimorphic fungus Sporothrix schenckii, typically acquired through thorn pricks or plant material; ocular involvement presents as a Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome.
The classic presentation is the lymphocutaneous form with ascending nodules along lymphatics from the inoculation site.
Ocular involvement (oculoglandular syndrome) occurs from direct inoculation or autoinoculation from a cutaneous lesion.
Sporotrichosis is in the differential diagnosis of Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome, along with cat-scratch disease, tularemia, and tuberculosis.

clues: A 65-year-old female presents with chronic tearing, foreign body sensation, and intermittent blurred vision that is worse with blinking.

Slit-lamp shows redundant, loose, wrinkled folds of bulbar conjunctiva lying over the inferior eyelid margin and lower tear meniscus, disrupting the normal tear film distribution. Fluorescein pooling is seen in the conjunctival folds.
The conjunctival folds mechanically block the lower punctum, impeding tear drainage. The tear meniscus is irregular. There is no inflammation.
The patient has been treated for dry eye for years without improvement. Standard artificial tears provide only temporary relief.

Conservative: aggressive lubrication. Surgical: conjunctival cauterization (thermal or electrocautery) or conjunctival excision/resection of the redundant tissue with or without amniotic membrane grafting.
teachingPoints: Conjunctivochalasis is a common age-related condition of redundant, loose inferior bulbar conjunctiva that disrupts the tear film and can cause epiphora, irritation, and subconjunctival hemorrhage.
It is frequently misdiagnosed as dry eye disease; the key distinguishing feature is the visible redundant conjunctival folds overlying the lower lid margin.
The redundant folds can mechanically obstruct the lower punctum, causing epiphora even with adequate tear production.
Simple office-based thermal cautery of the redundant conjunctiva is effective and can be performed at the slit lamp.

clues: A 20-year-old asymptomatic Japanese female is found to have bilateral retinal flecks during a routine dilated exam.

Fundoscopy reveals bilateral, discrete, large, yellow-white, irregularly shaped flecks at the level of the RPE, predominantly in the midperiphery and equatorial region. The macula and optic disc are normal.
Visual acuity is 20/20 bilaterally. ERG is normal. Dark adaptation is mildly prolonged. OCT shows the deposits at the RPE level without overlying retinal disruption.
The patient is completely asymptomatic with excellent vision. The findings are bilateral and symmetric. Family screening reveals similar findings in her father.
No treatment is needed. The condition is stationary and benign. Reassurance.
teachingPoints: Kandori fleck retina is a rare, benign, stationary condition of retinal flecks at the RPE level with normal visual function, considered a variant of benign fleck retina.
It must be distinguished from Stargardt disease (progressive, macular involvement, abnormal ERG) and fundus albipunctatus (prominent nyctalopia with delayed dark adaptation).
Visual function is preserved throughout life in true fleck retina of Kandori.
The differential diagnosis of flecked retina includes Stargardt/fundus flavimaculatus, fundus albipunctatus, retinitis punctata albescens, drusen, and crystalline retinopathies.

clues: A 25-year-old male presents with bilateral blurred vision and floaters. He has recently been diagnosed with acute lymphoblastic leukemia.

Fundoscopy reveals bilateral retinal hemorrhages in multiple layers (flame-shaped, dot-blot, and white-centered), cotton-wool spots, venous dilation, and Roth spots. There is a focal white retinal infiltrate (leukemic infiltrate) in the left eye.

CBC shows WBC 85,000 (leukocytosis), hemoglobin 6.5 g/dL (severe anemia), and platelets 15,000 (thrombocytopenia). The retinal findings are from the combined effects of hyperviscosity, anemia, and thrombocytopenia.
The retinal changes are secondary to the systemic hematologic disorder. There is no history of diabetes or hypertension.

Treat the underlying leukemia (chemotherapy). Blood transfusion for severe anemia. Platelet transfusion for thrombocytopenia. Intravitreal anti-VEGF or radiation for leukemic infiltrates threatening the macula.
teachingPoints: Retinal manifestations of leukemia result from direct infiltration, hyperviscosity, anemia, and thrombocytopenia—often all contributing simultaneously.
White-centered (Roth spot) hemorrhages in leukemia result from leukemic cells infiltrating vessel walls or from aggregates of white cells in the center of a hemorrhage.
Leukemic infiltrates appear as creamy-white retinal or subretinal lesions distinct from cotton-wool spots; they represent direct leukemic cell infiltration.
Optic nerve infiltration by leukemic cells can cause optic disc edema and is a CNS involvement marker requiring intrathecal chemotherapy.

clues: A 70-year-old male who had trabeculectomy 1 week ago presents with decreased vision, shallow anterior chamber, and low IOP in the operated eye.

IOP is 2 mmHg. The anterior chamber is very shallow. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral dome-shaped, dark, smooth choroidal elevations ('kissing choroidals' if touching in the center). There is no retinal detachment.
B-scan shows large serous choroidal detachments in all quadrants. The suprachoroidal space is filled with transudative fluid. There is no hemorrhage (no echogenicity within the fluid).
The patient had a mitomycin C-augmented trabeculectomy. Excessive filtration has led to profound hypotony.

Conservative: cycloplegics (atropine) to deepen the anterior chamber, reduce filtration by suturing the bleb or injecting viscoelastic into the AC, and steroids to reduce inflammation. Surgical drainage if persistent or if kissing choroidals threaten the macula.
teachingPoints: Choroidal effusions after glaucoma surgery result from hypotony—low IOP allows transudation of fluid into the suprachoroidal space as the osmotic gradient shifts.
Serous choroidal effusions are smooth, dome-shaped, and mobile; they are distinguished from suprachoroidal hemorrhage (which is dark, solid, and immobile on B-scan).
Kissing choroidal detachments (large enough to meet in the center) can cause lens-cornea touch and progressive anterior chamber shallowing.
Prevention includes releasable sutures in the scleral flap, which can be titrated to control flow and prevent excessive early hypotony.

clues: A 3-day-old newborn develops bilateral purulent conjunctival discharge with marked lid swelling.
Bilateral severe conjunctival injection, chemosis, copious purulent (creamy yellow-green) discharge, and lid edema. The cornea shows early peripheral haze.
Gram stain of the discharge reveals intracellular gram-negative diplococci (consistent with Neisseria gonorrhoeae). Culture on Thayer-Martin medium is positive.
The mother had untreated gonorrhea. The infant did not receive adequate prophylaxis at birth.
Systemic (IV or IM) ceftriaxone plus frequent saline lavage of purulent discharge and topical antibiotic drops. Hospitalization is required. Evaluate and treat the mother and partner.
teachingPoints: Gonococcal ophthalmia neonatorum is an ophthalmologic emergency: N. gonorrhoeae can penetrate intact corneal epithelium, causing corneal ulceration and perforation within 24 hours if untreated.
Timing of onset helps identify the cause: chemical (first 24 hrs from prophylaxis), gonococcal (2-5 days), chlamydial (5-14 days), other bacterial (4-5 days), and HSV (6-14 days).
Erythromycin ointment prophylaxis at birth has reduced but not eliminated gonococcal ophthalmia; prenatal maternal screening is the most effective prevention.
Chlamydial neonatal conjunctivitis is the most common infectious cause overall and requires oral erythromycin (topical treatment alone is insufficient and does not prevent chlamydial pneumonia).

clues: A 2-year-old child with very light skin, white hair, and nystagmus is brought in for evaluation of poor vision.

Visual acuity is approximately 20/200 (age-appropriate testing). Bilateral pendular nystagmus is present. The irides are translucent (full iris transillumination). Fundoscopy shows complete absence of RPE and choroidal pigment with prominent visibility of choroidal vessels. Foveal hypoplasia is confirmed.
VEP shows misrouting of optic nerve fibers (crossed asymmetry). OCT confirms foveal hypoplasia with absent foveal pit. The child has near-white hair, pale skin, and light blue-gray irides.
Both parents have normal pigmentation but are carriers. Genetic testing reveals biallelic TYR gene mutations (OCA type 1A—tyrosinase-negative).
Tinted lenses/sunglasses for photosensitivity and UV protection. Refractive correction. Low-vision aids. Sun protection for skin cancer prevention. No treatment exists for the foveal hypoplasia.
teachingPoints: Oculocutaneous albinism is an autosomal recessive group of disorders affecting melanin biosynthesis in the skin, hair, and eyes. OCA1A (TYR mutations) is the most severe form.
Foveal hypoplasia (failure of normal foveal development) is the primary cause of reduced vision—melanin is required for normal foveal differentiation in utero.
Optic nerve fiber misrouting (excess decussation at the chiasm) is pathognomonic for all forms of albinism and is detectable by VEP.
Skin cancer risk (SCC, BCC, melanoma) is significantly increased; lifelong dermatologic surveillance and sun protection are essential.

clues: A 25-year-old male presents with a white, elevated corneal opacity that has been progressively enlarging since a corneal injury 2 years ago.
A white, vascularized, elevated, nodular opacity extends above the corneal surface with irregular margins. It is firm and non-translucent. The lesion extends well beyond the original wound area.

Anterior segment OCT shows a highly hyperreflective, elevated, dome-shaped lesion with disorganized stromal architecture. It is distinct from a simple corneal scar by its exuberant growth beyond wound margins.
The patient had a corneal laceration repaired surgically 2 years ago. He has a history of keloid formation on his chest (skin keloid). He is of African descent.
Excision with adjunctive mitomycin C application or beta-radiation to prevent recurrence. Lamellar keratoplasty for large lesions. Recurrence rate is high without adjunctive therapy.
teachingPoints: Corneal keloids are rare, exuberant fibrous tissue overgrowths that extend beyond the original wound margins, analogous to dermal keloids.
They occur more commonly in patients with a predisposition to keloid formation (African descent, young age, family history of keloids).
Congenital corneal keloids can occur in Lowe syndrome (oculocerebrorenal syndrome) and Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome.
Recurrence is common after simple excision; adjunctive anti-fibrotic therapy (mitomycin C, 5-FU) reduces but does not eliminate recurrence risk.

clues: A 50-year-old female presents with involuntary unilateral twitching of the left eyelid and face that has gradually progressed to involve the entire left side of the face over 2 years.

Involuntary, synchronous, tonic and clonic contractions of the muscles innervated by the left facial nerve. The spasms start with the orbicularis oculi (eyelid) and progress to involve the lower face (orbicularis oris, platysma). The spasms persist during sleep.

MRI/MRA brain shows a vascular loop (usually the posterior inferior cerebellar artery or anterior inferior cerebellar artery) compressing the left facial nerve at its root exit zone from the brainstem.
The spasms are strictly unilateral and persist during sleep (unlike blepharospasm which is bilateral and absent during sleep). There is no facial weakness.
Botulinum toxin injection into the affected muscles for symptomatic relief. Microvascular decompression (MVD, Jannetta procedure) for definitive cure.
teachingPoints: Hemifacial spasm is caused by vascular compression of the facial nerve at the root exit zone in the posterior fossa, causing ephaptic transmission (nerve short-circuiting).
Key distinguishing features from benign essential blepharospasm: HFS is unilateral, involves the entire hemifac, persists in sleep, and is caused by a structural vascular lesion.
Microvascular decompression surgery has a >85% long-term cure rate by placing a Teflon pad between the offending vessel and the facial nerve.
Rarely, HFS can be caused by a posterior fossa tumor rather than vascular compression—MRI is essential before treatment.

clues: A 15-year-old male on vigabatrin for infantile spasms since infancy presents for routine ophthalmologic screening. He reports no visual symptoms.
Visual acuity is 20/20 bilaterally. The fundus appears grossly normal. Automated perimetry reveals bilateral, concentric, peripheral visual field constriction that is asymptomatic.
OCT shows peripheral retinal nerve fiber layer thinning. ERG shows reduced 30-Hz flicker amplitude (cone dysfunction). The visual field constriction is permanent and irreversible.
The patient has been on vigabatrin for 13 years for seizure control. He was not screened ophthalmologically until now.
Discuss risks/benefits of continuing vigabatrin with the neurologist. The visual field loss is irreversible even after drug cessation. If continued, screen with visual fields and ERG every 6 months.
teachingPoints: Vigabatrin causes irreversible bilateral concentric visual field constriction in approximately 30-40% of patients by damaging retinal ganglion cells and the peripheral RNFL.
The mechanism involves GABA accumulation in the retina (vigabatrin irreversibly inhibits GABA transaminase), which is toxic to retinal neurons at high concentrations.
Visual field screening (Humphrey or Goldmann perimetry) is mandatory every 6 months for all patients on vigabatrin.
Despite the visual field toxicity, vigabatrin remains uniquely effective for infantile spasms (West syndrome) and is sometimes the only effective treatment.

clues: A 65-year-old female presents with chronic unilateral tearing, mucopurulent discharge, and redness localized to the medial canthus of her right eye, unresponsive to topical antibiotics.

The right lower punctum is pouting (dilated and everted). Pressure over the lower canaliculus expresses thick, yellow, cheesy material containing yellowish concretions ('sulfur granules'). There is focal erythema and swelling over the involved canaliculus.
The concretions appear as small yellowish particles (dacryoliths). Gram stain shows filamentous gram-positive branching organisms consistent with Actinomyces israelii.
The patient has had chronic medial canthal irritation for 1 year. She was previously treated for chronic conjunctivitis without improvement. She has a punctal plug in the right lower punctum.
Curettage of the canaliculus to remove all concretions (canaliculotomy if needed). Irrigation with penicillin solution. Removal of any punctal plug. Oral penicillin for 2 weeks.
teachingPoints: Canaliculitis is chronic infection of the lacrimal canaliculus, most commonly caused by Actinomyces israelii; it is frequently misdiagnosed as chronic conjunctivitis or dacryocystitis.
A pouting (dilated, everted) punctum with expressible concretions is the classic clinical finding.
Punctal plugs are a known risk factor, as they provide a nidus for infection.
Definitive treatment requires physical removal of all concretions (medical therapy alone is insufficient because the concretions harbor the organisms).

clues: A 50-year-old male who had a corneal transplant 2 years ago (on chronic low-dose topical steroids) presents with a white, branching crystalline pattern in the corneal graft noticed over 2 weeks.

Slit-lamp shows delicate, white, needle-like crystalline opacities in the anterior corneal stroma of the graft, arranged in a branching pattern. There is minimal surrounding inflammation. No epithelial defect.
The crystalline pattern is located within the graft. Corneal culture grows Streptococcus viridans. The patient has been on topical prednisolone for 2 years without antibiotic cover.
The chronic topical steroid use has suppressed the normal inflammatory response, allowing the organism to grow in a non-inflammatory crystalline pattern.
Intensive fortified topical antibiotics (cefazolin). Taper the topical steroid gradually. Prolonged treatment is needed as the organisms are deep within the stroma.
teachingPoints: Infectious crystalline keratopathy is a unique pattern of microbial keratitis characterized by crystalline branching stromal deposits with minimal inflammation, typically caused by Streptococcus viridans.
It almost exclusively occurs in the setting of chronic topical corticosteroid use (post-keratoplasty), which suppresses the host inflammatory response.
The lack of inflammation means the infection can progress insidiously and be mistaken for a non-infectious stromal dystrophy.
Despite the dramatic crystalline appearance, the organism grows slowly and the infection responds to prolonged antibiotic therapy.

clues: A 70-year-old female presents with mild metamorphopsia and a small scotoma in her left eye noticed over months.
Visual acuity is 20/40 OS. Fundoscopy shows a reddish lesion at the fovea with irregular margins, different from a full-thickness macular hole.

OCT shows an irregular foveal defect involving the inner retinal layers with a residual intact outer retinal floor (outer plexiform layer and outer nuclear layer). There is a flat, irregular ERM at the edges. No vitreomacular traction.
The contour is irregular (not round like a full-thickness hole). The outer retina is intact. Watzke-Allen test is negative (no gap perceived—distinguishing from FTMH).
Observation for stable, mildly symptomatic cases. Vitrectomy with ERM/ILM peeling if progressive symptoms or declining acuity.
teachingPoints: Lamellar macular holes are partial-thickness foveal defects involving only the inner retinal layers, with preserved outer retinal tissue forming the 'floor' of the hole.
Visual acuity is typically better preserved than in full-thickness macular holes because the outer retinal photoreceptors remain intact.
Unlike full-thickness macular holes (which often require surgery), many lamellar holes are stable and can be observed.
The associated 'lamellar hole-associated epiretinal proliferation' (LHEP) is a dense, tractional epiretinal tissue distinct from typical ERMs.

clues: A 35-year-old male is incidentally found to have unilateral iris color difference and fine keratic precipitates during an eye exam. He reports no symptoms.

The right iris is lighter (less pigmented) than the left. Slit-lamp reveals scattered, small, stellate keratic precipitates distributed diffusely over the corneal endothelium. There is mild anterior chamber flare without significant cells. No posterior synechiae.

IOP is 24 mmHg OD (borderline). There is early posterior subcapsular cataract developing in the right eye. Gonioscopy shows fine angle vessels (neovascularization—Amsler sign). Aqueous PCR may show rubella virus.
The patient has never noticed the iris color difference. The KPs are non-granulomatous and stellate. There is no synechiae (unusual for uveitis). The anterior chamber never showed significant flare.
Observation for the mild uveitis (often does not require topical steroids). Cataract surgery when visually significant (excellent prognosis). Monitor IOP for secondary glaucoma.
teachingPoints: Fuchs heterochromic iridocyclitis is a chronic, low-grade, unilateral anterior uveitis characterized by iris heterochromia (lighter on the affected side), diffuse stellate KPs, absence of synechiae, and a benign course.
Unlike other uveitic conditions, FHI does NOT form posterior synechiae even without cycloplegic use—this is a key diagnostic feature.
Cataract and secondary glaucoma are the main complications; cataract surgery has excellent visual outcomes in FHI.
Recent studies have linked FHI to chronic rubella virus infection of the eye; rubella virus RNA has been detected in aqueous humor of many FHI patients.

clues: A 20-year-old male presents with a persistently dilated pupil in his right eye following a blunt injury to the eye from a fist 2 weeks ago.

The right pupil is mid-dilated (6 mm) and reacts sluggishly to light and near stimulation. There are iris sphincter tears visible as irregular notches at the pupillary margin. The left pupil is normal (3 mm).
Gonioscopy shows angle recession in the involved quadrant. There is no hyphema. IOP is normal. Pharmacological testing with pilocarpine 1% shows incomplete constriction.
The injury was a direct blunt blow to the right eye. The pupil has remained dilated since the injury. There is no ptosis (ruling out CN3 palsy).

Observation—many cases partially recover over weeks to months. If persistent and symptomatic (photophobia, glare), a tinted contact lens or an artificial iris device may help. Pilocarpine drops provide partial relief.
teachingPoints: Traumatic mydriasis results from damage to the iris sphincter muscle from blunt ocular trauma; iris sphincter tears are often visible at the pupillary margin.
The degree of recovery depends on the extent of sphincter damage—partial tears may recover well, while circumferential damage may be permanent.
It must be distinguished from pharmacologic mydriasis (pilocarpine constricts a traumatic but not pharmacologic pupil) and CN3 palsy (associated with ptosis and motility deficit).
Long-term monitoring for angle recession glaucoma is recommended since the mechanism of injury also damages the trabecular meshwork.

clues: A 40-year-old male with chronic anterior uveitis presents with acute pain, redness, and decreased vision in his left eye.

IOP is 52 mmHg OS. Slit-lamp shows 360-degree posterior synechiae (complete pupillary seclusion) with iris bombé—the peripheral iris is bowed forward while the central iris is flat behind the synechiae. The anterior chamber is shallow peripherally but deep centrally.
Gonioscopy shows 360-degree appositional angle closure from the bowed-forward iris periphery. There is active anterior uveitis with fibrin in the anterior chamber.
The patient has a history of recurrent bilateral anterior uveitis (HLA-B27 positive). He missed follow-up appointments and ran out of his cycloplegic drops.

Urgent Nd:YAG laser peripheral iridotomy (if cornea is clear enough) to break the pupillary block. If not possible, surgical PI or synechiolysis. Intensive anti-inflammatory therapy (topical and possibly systemic steroids) and IOP-lowering agents.
teachingPoints: Pupillary block in uveitis occurs when 360-degree posterior synechiae seal the pupil, preventing aqueous flow from the posterior to the anterior chamber, causing iris bombé.
The aqueous accumulates behind the iris, pushing the peripheral iris forward (bombé configuration) and closing the drainage angle.
Prevention is key: aggressive cycloplegic use (atropine) during uveitis episodes prevents posterior synechiae formation by keeping the pupil mobile.
If synechiae are already established, laser iridotomy provides an alternative pathway for aqueous flow, immediately relieving the pupillary block.

clues: A 70-year-old male is found to have a glistening, refractile body at a retinal arteriolar bifurcation during a routine dilated exam. He is asymptomatic.

A small, yellow, glistening, refractile cholesterol crystal (Hollenhorst plaque) is lodged at a retinal arteriolar bifurcation in the superotemporal arcade. There is no retinal whitening or edema (no acute occlusion). Visual acuity is 20/20.
The plaque is seen clearly at the bifurcation point. There is no cotton-wool spot or hemorrhage. Carotid Doppler reveals 70% stenosis of the ipsilateral internal carotid artery.
The patient has hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and diabetes. He is a former smoker. He has had no visual symptoms.
Urgent carotid imaging (Doppler, CTA, or MRA). Cardiovascular risk factor modification. Aspirin/statin therapy. Vascular surgery consultation for significant carotid stenosis.
teachingPoints: Hollenhorst plaques are cholesterol crystal emboli from ulcerated atherosclerotic plaques, usually in the ipsilateral carotid artery; they are a marker of systemic atherosclerotic disease.
The presence of retinal cholesterol emboli is associated with increased risk of stroke and cardiovascular mortality, even in asymptomatic patients.
Three types of retinal emboli: cholesterol (yellow, glistening, at bifurcations—Hollenhorst), fibrin-platelet (dull white, elongated—Fisher), and calcific (white, round, from cardiac valves).
Finding an asymptomatic Hollenhorst plaque mandates systemic vascular workup—these patients have a significantly higher 5-year mortality from cardiovascular events.

clues: A 55-year-old male with type 2 diabetes for 10 years presents for his annual diabetic eye exam. He has no visual complaints.

Visual acuity is 20/20 bilaterally. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral microaneurysms, scattered dot-and-blot hemorrhages in all four quadrants (more than mild NPDR), and a few hard exudates. No cotton-wool spots, no venous beading, no IRMA.
OCT macula shows no macular edema. Fluorescein angiography shows microaneurysm leakage without significant capillary non-perfusion. HbA1c is 8.2%.
The patient has had diabetes for 10 years with suboptimal control. He has microalbuminuria and peripheral neuropathy.
Optimize glycemic control (target HbA1c <7%), blood pressure, and lipids. Follow-up dilated exam in 6-12 months. No laser or anti-VEGF treatment needed at this stage.
teachingPoints: Moderate NPDR is defined by the presence of more than just microaneurysms (hemorrhages, hard exudates) but without the severe NPDR features (4-2-1 rule).
The 4-2-1 rule for severe NPDR: hemorrhages in all 4 quadrants, OR venous beading in 2+ quadrants, OR IRMA in 1+ quadrant. Meeting ANY one criterion upgrades to severe NPDR.
Tight glycemic control (DCCT/UKPDS) and blood pressure control (ACCORD Eye) significantly reduce progression of diabetic retinopathy.
Annual dilated eye exams are recommended for all diabetic patients; telemedicine retinal screening with fundus photography is an effective alternative.

clues: A 45-year-old female on chronic oral prednisone for lupus presents with progressive glare and difficulty reading despite good distance vision.

Slit-lamp shows a central, granular, plaque-like opacity on the posterior lens capsule bilaterally (posterior subcapsular cataract). The nucleus and cortex are relatively clear. Visual acuity is 20/40 at distance but J6 at near.
Glare testing shows significant disability. The PSC is central, directly in the visual axis. Pupil constriction for near work worsens the effective opacity.
The patient has been on prednisone 15 mg daily for 5 years. She is 45 years old (younger than typical age-related cataracts).
Cataract surgery (phacoemulsification with IOL) when functional impairment warrants it. Attempt to reduce or discontinue systemic steroids if possible.
teachingPoints: Posterior subcapsular cataracts are associated with chronic corticosteroid use, diabetes, radiation exposure, uveitis, and retinitis pigmentosa—they tend to occur in younger patients than nuclear cataracts.
PSC cataracts disproportionately affect near vision and cause glare because the opacity is located at the nodal point of the eye, directly in the visual axis.
Systemic and topical corticosteroids are the most common iatrogenic cause; the risk is dose and duration dependent.
The mechanism involves glucocorticoid-induced inhibition of the Na-K ATPase pump in lens epithelial cells, leading to posterior migration of swollen lens fibers.

clues: A 65-year-old diabetic male with a history of herpes simplex keratitis presents with a corneal epithelial defect in his left eye that has not healed despite 6 weeks of treatment.

An oval, smooth-edged epithelial defect with rolled, heaped-up edges is present paracentrally. The surrounding epithelium appears loose and unhealthy. Corneal sensation is severely diminished (neurotrophic component). The base is clean without infiltrate.

Corneal cultures are negative. The defect has been present for 6 weeks despite preservative-free artificial tears, antibiotic prophylaxis, and bandage contact lens. Diabetes is poorly controlled (HbA1c 10%).
Contributing factors include neurotrophic cornea (from prior HSV), diabetes (impaired wound healing), and chronic dry eye.
Autologous serum tears (20%). Cenegermin (topical nerve growth factor). Amniotic membrane transplant. Tarsorrhaphy. Optimize diabetes control. Address all contributing factors simultaneously.
teachingPoints: Persistent epithelial defects (non-healing >2 weeks) result from a failure of the corneal epithelial healing cascade, often with multiple contributing factors.
The most common contributing factors are neurotrophic cornea, diabetes, dry eye, topical medication toxicity (especially preserved drops), and prior herpes simplex keratitis.
Autologous serum tears contain growth factors (EGF, NGF, fibronectin) that promote epithelial healing and are effective for refractory PEDs.
Untreated PEDs risk progressive stromal melting, secondary infection, and corneal perforation.

clues: A 30-year-old asymptomatic female is found to have a flat, dark retinal lesion during a routine dilated exam.

A well-demarcated, flat, uniformly dark-pigmented, round lesion is seen in the midperipheral retina of the left eye. It has a characteristic ring of depigmentation (halo) around its border and lacunae (depigmented spots) within it.
The lesion is completely flat without elevation. B-scan shows no thickness. Autofluorescence shows hypo-autofluorescence. There is no subretinal fluid or orange pigment (lipofuscin).
The lesion has been stable for years (confirmed by old photos). It is solitary and unilateral. Visual acuity is 20/20.

No treatment needed. Observation. Solitary CHRPE is benign. However, bilateral, multiple, or grouped CHRPE lesions ('bear tracks') should prompt evaluation for familial adenomatous polyposis (Gardner syndrome).
teachingPoints: CHRPE is a congenital, benign, flat pigmented lesion of the RPE consisting of enlarged, heavily pigmented RPE cells.
Lacunae (depigmented windows within the lesion) and a surrounding halo of depigmentation are characteristic features.
Multiple bilateral CHRPE lesions are associated with familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP/Gardner syndrome)—an autosomal dominant condition with near-100% risk of colon cancer.
Solitary CHRPE has no systemic significance and requires no workup beyond routine observation.

clues: A 55-year-old male presents 2 months after scleral buckle surgery for retinal detachment with persistent vertical diplopia and foreign body sensation.

The retina is attached. However, there is extrusion of the buckle element through the conjunctiva inferiorly. Extraocular motility testing reveals restriction in upgaze (the buckle is causing mechanical restriction of the inferior rectus). There is induced myopic shift of -3.00 D.
The diplopia is worse in upgaze and is not improving. The buckle extrusion is causing chronic mucoid discharge and irritation.
The patient had a segmental scleral buckle placed inferotemporally. The retina has been stable and attached for 2 months.
Buckle removal if the retina is securely attached (typically safe after 3-6 months). Strabismus surgery if diplopia persists after buckle removal.
teachingPoints: Common scleral buckle complications include diplopia/strabismus (restrictive), buckle extrusion/infection, induced myopia, choroidal detachment, and anterior segment ischemia.
The myopic shift occurs because the buckle increases the axial length of the eye; each 1 mm of increased axial length causes approximately 3 diopters of myopia.
Buckle extrusion occurs in 3-5% of cases and requires removal to prevent infection (endophthalmitis).
Diplopia from scleral buckle is usually restrictive and is caused by the buckle element indenting the globe beneath an extraocular muscle, limiting its range of motion.

clues: A 20-year-old male presents with decreased vision in his right eye 3 months after being hit by a baseball.

Slit-lamp reveals a characteristic rosette-shaped opacity in the posterior cortex of the right lens (posterior rosette cataract). There is also a Vossius ring (circular imprint of iris pigment on the anterior lens capsule).
Visual acuity is 20/60 OD. The anterior chamber is deep. Gonioscopy shows angle recession in 180 degrees. IOP is normal. The posterior segment is clear.
The injury occurred 3 months ago from blunt trauma. There was an initial hyphema that resolved. The cataract has been progressively worsening.
Cataract extraction (phacoemulsification with IOL). Caution with potential zonular instability from the trauma. Lifelong monitoring for angle recession glaucoma.
teachingPoints: Traumatic cataracts can present immediately after injury (from capsular rupture) or develop weeks to months later (from concussive damage to lens fibers).
The posterior rosette pattern is characteristic of contusion (blunt) injury; it results from disruption of the posterior cortical lens fibers arranged in a stellate pattern along the lens sutures.
Vossius ring (iris pigment stamped onto the anterior lens capsule by the pupil) is a classic sign of blunt ocular trauma.
Zonular weakness should be anticipated during surgery; capsular tension ring may be needed for zonular dialysis.

clues: A 50-year-old Portuguese male presents with bilateral progressive blurred vision with prominent floaters over several years. He has peripheral neuropathy.

Slit-lamp and dilated exam reveal bilateral glass-wool or 'cotton-ball' vitreous opacities—thick, sheet-like, white-yellow deposits on the posterior vitreous face and extending into the vitreous cavity. Scalloped pupillary margins from amyloid deposition are present.

Vitreous biopsy shows Congo red-positive material with apple-green birefringence (amyloid). Genetic testing reveals a transthyretin (TTR) gene mutation (V30M). Nerve conduction studies confirm polyneuropathy.
The patient has a family history of a 'Portuguese nerve disease.' He has progressive peripheral neuropathy, cardiomyopathy, and bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome.

Pars plana vitrectomy to clear the vitreous opacities and improve vision. Systemic treatment for TTR amyloidosis: tafamidis (TTR stabilizer), patisiran (siRNA), or liver transplant for definitive treatment.
teachingPoints: Vitreous amyloidosis is most commonly associated with familial amyloid polyneuropathy (FAP) caused by transthyretin (TTR) mutations, particularly the V30M mutation common in Portuguese, Swedish, and Japanese populations.
The vitreous opacities are composed of amyloid fibrils produced by the retinal pigment epithelium, which independently produces transthyretin.
Vitreous amyloid may recur after vitrectomy because the RPE continues to produce mutant TTR locally.
Liver transplant eliminates the hepatic source of mutant TTR but does NOT prevent ocular recurrence because the RPE produces its own TTR.

clues: A 45-year-old diabetic male presents with decreased vision 1 week after receiving panretinal photocoagulation for proliferative diabetic retinopathy.

Visual acuity has dropped from 20/40 to 20/200. Fundoscopy reveals an exudative retinal detachment in the inferior fundus with subretinal fluid tracking toward the macula. The PRP burns are visible. No retinal break is identified.
B-scan confirms serous (exudative) retinal detachment without retinal break. OCT shows subretinal fluid at the macula.
The patient received aggressive PRP (>2000 burns) in a single session. There is significant choroidal swelling from the laser treatment.
Observation—exudative detachments post-PRP usually resolve spontaneously within 1-2 weeks. Topical steroids and cycloplegics. Avoid further laser until resolved. Anti-VEGF may help.
teachingPoints: Exudative retinal detachment is a recognized complication of aggressive PRP, caused by choroidal inflammation and increased vascular permeability from the laser treatment.
Splitting PRP into multiple sessions (rather than a single heavy session) reduces the risk of this complication.
It must be distinguished from rhegmatogenous RD (which requires surgical repair); B-scan helps by confirming the absence of retinal breaks.
The condition is self-limiting in most cases as the choroidal inflammation subsides.

clues: A 35-year-old male presents with acute bilateral complete ophthalmoplegia, bilateral ptosis, and ataxia developing over 3 days following a respiratory infection.
Complete bilateral external ophthalmoplegia—the eyes cannot move in any direction. Bilateral ptosis. Pupils are dilated and poorly reactive. There is gait ataxia and absent deep tendon reflexes.
Anti-GQ1b antibodies are positive. CSF shows albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated protein with normal cell count). MRI brain is normal. Nerve conduction studies show sensory neuropathy.
The patient had a Campylobacter jejuni gastroenteritis 2 weeks before the neurologic symptoms began.
IVIG or plasmapheresis. Supportive care. Monitor for respiratory compromise (risk of progression to full Guillain-Barré syndrome). Most patients recover fully over weeks to months.
teachingPoints: Miller Fisher syndrome is a variant of Guillain-Barré syndrome characterized by the classic triad: ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, and areflexia, with anti-GQ1b antibodies.
Anti-GQ1b antibodies target the ganglioside GQ1b, which is highly concentrated in the oculomotor, trochlear, and abducens nerves, explaining the ophthalmoplegia.
Most patients (>90%) make a complete recovery, though recovery may take weeks to months.
Overlap with Guillain-Barré (ascending paralysis, respiratory failure) can occur and must be monitored.

clues: A 30-year-old female is admitted to the burn unit with extensive skin blistering and erosions involving >30% body surface area, 5 days after starting carbamazepine. An ophthalmology consult is requested.

Both eyes show severe membranous conjunctivitis with pseudomembrane formation, lid margin erosions, symblepharon formation, and corneal epithelial defects. There is severe fornix foreshortening developing.
Skin biopsy confirms TEN (full-thickness epidermal necrosis). The patient has mucous membrane involvement of the mouth, eyes, and genitalia.
The patient was started on carbamazepine 10 days ago for seizures. She is HLA-B*1502 positive (Southeast Asian descent—high risk for carbamazepine-induced SJS/TEN).

Acute ocular: daily sweeping of fornices to break early synechiae (symblepharon lysis), preservative-free lubricants hourly, topical steroids, amniotic membrane transplant to fornices, scleral ring to maintain fornices.
teachingPoints: Acute ocular management in SJS/TEN is critical—aggressive intervention in the first 2 weeks can prevent or reduce the devastating chronic cicatricial complications.
Chronic complications include dry eye (lacrimal gland duct scarring), trichiasis, symblepharon, ankyloblepharon, limbal stem cell deficiency, and corneal opacification.
Prokinetic lid sweeping and amniotic membrane transplantation during the acute phase significantly reduce long-term scarring and visual morbidity.
HLA-B*1502 testing is recommended before starting carbamazepine in Southeast Asian populations to identify individuals at high risk for SJS/TEN.

clues: A 6-month-old infant is brought in by parents concerned about constant rhythmic eye movements. The child is otherwise healthy.

Bilateral, conjugate, horizontal, predominantly jerk nystagmus that is present in all gaze positions. The nystagmus has an accelerating slow phase (characteristic waveform). A null zone is identified where the nystagmus dampens (the child turns the head to use this zone). Nystagmus dampens with convergence.

Visual acuity (preferential looking) is approximately 20/80. OCT shows a normal foveal structure (ruling out foveal hypoplasia from albinism). No optic nerve or retinal abnormality. MRI brain is normal.
The nystagmus was noted at 2-3 months of age. It is horizontal in all positions and does NOT change direction with gaze (conjugate). No other neurologic abnormalities.

Correction of refractive error (if present). If the null zone requires a significant head turn, the Anderson-Kestenbaum procedure (eye muscle surgery) moves the null zone to primary position. Contact lenses may dampen nystagmus.
teachingPoints: Infantile nystagmus syndrome is a congenital nystagmus beginning before 6 months of age; it is conjugate, predominantly horizontal, and has an accelerating slow phase waveform.
The accelerating slow phase is pathognomonic for INS and distinguishes it from acquired (pathologic) nystagmus (which has constant-velocity or decelerating slow phases).
INS can be idiopathic (motor) or sensory (secondary to afferent visual pathway defects like albinism, aniridia, optic nerve hypoplasia, achromatopsia).
A thorough search for the underlying sensory cause is essential in all infants with nystagmus—ERG, OCT, MRI, and genetic testing may be needed.

clues: A 70-year-old female with wet AMD presents with pain, redness, and decreased vision 2 days after her monthly intravitreal anti-VEGF injection.
Visual acuity is hand motions (was 20/40 at last visit). Slit-lamp shows conjunctival injection, anterior chamber cells and fibrin, and a small hypopyon. Vitreous haze obscures the fundus view.
B-scan shows vitreous opacities without retinal detachment. The injection site appears normal. Vitreous tap culture ultimately grows coagulase-negative staphylococcus.
The patient receives monthly anti-VEGF injections. The injection 2 days ago was performed with standard aseptic technique.
Vitreous tap and intravitreal antibiotics (vancomycin + ceftazidime) immediately. Vitrectomy is considered for severe cases with light-perception-only vision.
teachingPoints: Post-intravitreal injection endophthalmitis occurs in approximately 0.02-0.05% per injection; the most common organism is coagulase-negative staphylococcus from the conjunctival flora.
The risk is minimized by povidone-iodine antisepsis (the single most important preventive step), avoiding speaking during the procedure, and proper technique.
Post-injection endophthalmitis must be distinguished from sterile (non-infectious) inflammation, which typically presents 1-2 days post-injection with vitritis but responds to observation/steroids.
Despite its rarity per injection, the cumulative risk is significant for patients receiving monthly injections over years.

clues: A 45-year-old female presents with metamorphopsia and mild blurred vision in her right eye.

Fundoscopy reveals parallel horizontal or oblique light and dark alternating streaks (folds) at the level of the RPE and Bruch membrane in the posterior pole. The folds are best seen with the red-free filter.

OCT shows undulation of the RPE/Bruch membrane complex. FA shows alternating hyperfluorescent (crests) and hypofluorescent (troughs) lines. B-scan reveals scleral/choroidal thickening. CT orbits shows a retrobulbar mass.
The patient has mild proptosis. An intraconal mass is discovered on imaging.
Treat the underlying cause. In this case, orbital tumor excision. Choroidal folds may resolve once the underlying etiology is addressed.
teachingPoints: Choroidal folds are undulations of the inner choroid, Bruch membrane, and RPE; the mnemonic THIN RPE lists causes: Tumor, Hypotony, Inflammation/Idiopathic, Neovascular membrane, Retrobulbar mass, Papilledema, Extraocular hardware (scleral buckle).
Idiopathic choroidal folds exist but this is a diagnosis of exclusion after ruling out all secondary causes.
Posterior scleritis is an important cause that can be missed if B-scan and clinical evaluation are not performed.
The alternating pattern on FA (bright crests where the RPE is stretched thin, dark troughs where RPE is compressed) is characteristic.

clues: A 35-year-old otherwise healthy male presents with episodic monocular oscillopsia (shimmering vision) and a feeling of the eye 'jumping' in his right eye.

During an episode, slit-lamp biomicroscopy shows small-amplitude, high-frequency, rotatory-vertical oscillations of the right eye (torsional and vertical microtremor). Between episodes, the exam is completely normal.
The episodes are brief (seconds), frequent (multiple per day), and triggered by downgaze, fatigue, or caffeine. There are no other neurologic findings. MRI brain is normal.
The patient describes the sensation as a rapid flutter or shimmer affecting vision momentarily. He is otherwise healthy.
Carbamazepine or gabapentin (membrane-stabilizing agents) are effective for suppressing the episodes. Beta-blockers may also help. Most cases are benign and self-limited.
teachingPoints: Superior oblique myokymia is a benign condition of episodic, involuntary, monocular, small-amplitude, high-frequency torsional-vertical oscillations of one eye caused by spontaneous firing of the trochlear nerve or its nucleus.
The oscillations are often too small to see on gross examination but are visible on slit-lamp with high magnification.
It is typically unilateral, episodic, and benign—MRI is obtained to rule out structural brainstem pathology but is almost always normal.
Carbamazepine is the most effective treatment; surgical options (superior oblique tenectomy or microvascular decompression) are reserved for refractory cases.

clues: An 8-year-old boy who had bilateral cataract extraction in infancy for congenital cataracts presents with elevated IOP and optic nerve cupping in his right eye.

IOP is 32 mmHg OD. Cup-to-disc ratio is 0.7 OD (was 0.3 at age 4). The patient is aphakic (no IOL was placed due to young age at surgery). Gonioscopy shows an open angle with some peripheral anterior synechiae.
OCT RNFL shows inferior thinning OD. Visual fields (age-appropriate testing) show a developing arcuate scotoma. The cornea is clear.
He had bilateral lensectomies at 6 weeks of age for dense congenital cataracts. He wears aphakic contact lenses. IOP has been gradually increasing since age 5.
