Topical IOP-lowering therapy (timolol, dorzolamide). If medical therapy fails, glaucoma drainage device (preferred over trabeculectomy in pediatric aphakic glaucoma).
teachingPoints: Aphakic/pseudophakic glaucoma is the most common long-term complication of infantile cataract surgery, occurring in up to 50% of eyes operated before age 1 year.
The mechanism is multifactorial: developmental angle anomalies, inflammation-related trabecular damage, and vitreous filling the anterior chamber altering aqueous dynamics.
Earlier age at surgery increases the risk of glaucoma; eyes operated at <4 weeks have the highest incidence.
Lifelong IOP monitoring is mandatory for all children who have had congenital cataract surgery, as glaucoma can develop decades later.

clues: A 6-month-old infant presents with nystagmus, photophobia, and no visual response to colored toys.

Pendular nystagmus is present. The child is severely photophobic—squints and avoids bright light. Visual acuity (assessed by preferential looking) is approximately 20/200. Fundoscopy appears grossly normal.

ERG shows absent or severely reduced photopic (cone) responses with normal scotopic (rod) responses—the inverse of rod-cone dystrophy. OCT shows foveal hypoplasia or subtle outer retinal changes. Genetic testing reveals biallelic CNGB3 mutations.
Both parents have normal color vision (carriers). The infant sees better in dim lighting conditions and is most comfortable in low light.
Dark-red-tinted lenses for photophobia. Low vision aids. Gene therapy clinical trials for CNGA3 and CNGB3 achromatopsia are actively underway with promising results.
teachingPoints: Achromatopsia is an autosomal recessive condition of absent cone function, causing severe photophobia, nystagmus, poor visual acuity (20/200), and complete color blindness from birth.
The most common causative genes are CNGB3 and CNGA3, encoding cone-specific cyclic nucleotide-gated channel subunits.
The fundus typically appears normal, which can delay diagnosis; ERG is essential for confirming cone dysfunction.
Gene therapy trials have shown structural and functional improvements in cone photoreceptors, making this one of the most promising targets for inherited retinal disease therapy.

clues: A 45-year-old African American male is referred for evaluation of large optic disc cups found during a routine exam. He is asymptomatic.

Cup-to-disc ratio is 0.7 bilaterally with symmetric cups. The neuroretinal rim follows the ISNT rule (thickest inferiorly, then superiorly, nasally, temporally). No disc hemorrhages. No RNFL wedge defects.
IOP is 18 mmHg bilaterally. OCT RNFL is within normal limits but in the lower percentiles. Visual fields (Humphrey 24-2) are full bilaterally. CCT is 520 microns (thin).
The patient's father has glaucoma. He is African American with thin corneas (risk factors). But currently, there is no evidence of glaucomatous damage.
Establish a baseline with IOP, OCT, visual fields, and disc photos. Monitor annually or semi-annually. No treatment initiated at this time.
teachingPoints: A glaucoma suspect has risk factors for glaucoma (large cups, elevated IOP, thin CCT, family history, race) but no definitive evidence of glaucomatous damage.
Large physiologic cups are common in large optic discs; the cup-to-disc ratio should be interpreted in the context of disc size—a CDR of 0.7 in a large disc may be normal.
The ISNT rule (inferior rim thickest > superior > nasal > temporal) helps distinguish physiologic cupping from glaucomatous damage.
The Ocular Hypertension Treatment Study (OHTS) identified key risk factors for progression: IOP, CCT, CDR, pattern standard deviation on VF, and age.

clues: A 25-year-old female mentions that she always sneezes when she walks into bright sunlight. She asks if this is abnormal.
The patient demonstrates sneezing (2-3 sneezes) when suddenly exposed to bright light in the exam room. Ocular examination is completely normal. No tearing or photophobia.
This is a reproducible photic sneeze reflex. There are no other neurologic findings. Her father has the same phenomenon.
The condition has been present since childhood. It only occurs with sudden bright light exposure, not in darkness. It is autosomal dominant.
No treatment is needed. Reassurance. For patients who find it bothersome (e.g., pilots, surgeons), wearing sunglasses before light exposure can prevent it.
teachingPoints: The photic sneeze reflex (ACHOO syndrome) is an autosomal dominant trait present in approximately 18-35% of the population.
It is thought to result from cross-talk between the trigeminal (V) and optic (II) neural pathways in the brainstem.
It is generally harmless but can be dangerous in specific situations (pilots, drivers entering bright tunnels, surgeons).
Despite its prevalence, the exact neuroanatomical mechanism remains incompletely understood.

clues: A 55-year-old male with known history of tertiary syphilis presents for neurologic follow-up. Ocular examination is requested.

Both pupils are small (miotic), irregular in shape, and react poorly to light but constrict briskly to near stimulation (accommodation)—classic light-near dissociation. Pupils do not dilate well to mydriatics.
RPR is reactive. FTA-ABS is positive. CSF shows chronic inflammatory changes. MRI brain shows meningovascular syphilis changes.
The patient was treated for neurosyphilis 2 years ago. The pupillary findings have not changed despite treatment.
The Argyll Robertson pupil itself does not require specific ocular treatment. Ensure adequate treatment of neurosyphilis (repeat CSF monitoring).
teachingPoints: The Argyll Robertson pupil is pathognomonic for neurosyphilis: bilateral small irregular pupils with light-near dissociation (accommodate but do not react to light).
The mnemonic: 'AR pupils are like a prostitute—they accommodate but don't react' (light-near dissociation).
The lesion is thought to be in the pretectal area of the midbrain, disrupting the light reflex pathway while sparing the near response pathway.
AR pupils may persist even after successful treatment of neurosyphilis, as the pretectal damage is often permanent.

clues: A 70-year-old hyperopic female develops a uniformly shallow anterior chamber with elevated IOP 1 week after routine cataract surgery, despite a patent peripheral iridotomy.
IOP is 40 mmHg. Anterior chamber is uniformly shallow (axially flat). The IOL is well-positioned but pushed forward. The PI is patent. There is no choroidal effusion on B-scan.
UBM shows the ciliary body rotated anteriorly with aqueous trapped behind the vitreous. The anterior hyaloid face is intact and bowed forward.
The patient is hyperopic (+4.00 D before surgery) with a short axial length (21 mm). She has no history of glaucoma.

Intensive cycloplegia (atropine QID) + aqueous suppressants (timolol + dorzolamide) + hyperosmotic agent (mannitol). If refractory: Nd:YAG laser disruption of the anterior hyaloid face through the PI, or vitrectomy.
teachingPoints: Aqueous misdirection (malignant glaucoma) can occur after any intraocular surgery, especially in hyperopic eyes with shallow anterior chambers.
The key distinction from pupillary block: the anterior chamber is uniformly shallow (central AND peripheral), and PI does not resolve it.
Atropine is counterintuitively effective because it relaxes the ciliary body, pulling it posteriorly and redirecting aqueous anteriorly.
Nd:YAG hyaloidotomy through the PI disrupts the anterior hyaloid barrier and allows trapped aqueous to flow anteriorly.

clues: A 22-year-old tall male with known Marfan syndrome and prior lens subluxation surgery presents with acute flashes and floaters in his left eye.

The patient is aphakic in both eyes (prior lensectomy for subluxated lenses). He is highly myopic with flat corneas. Dilated exam reveals a retinal tear at the superotemporal ora serrata with early subretinal fluid (macula-on retinal detachment).
B-scan confirms the retinal detachment. The axial length is 28 mm. There is lattice degeneration in both eyes.
The patient has Marfan syndrome with prior bilateral lens surgery. He has a tall, thin habitus with arachnodactyly and pectus excavatum.
Urgent retinal detachment repair (vitrectomy preferred in aphakic/pseudophakic eyes). Prophylactic laser to lattice lesions in the fellow eye.
teachingPoints: Beyond ectopia lentis, Marfan syndrome is associated with high myopia, flat corneas (low K readings), retinal detachment (from myopia and lattice degeneration), and glaucoma.
Retinal detachment risk is increased in Marfan due to axial myopia, lattice degeneration, and prior lens surgery.
Glaucoma in Marfan can be from angle anomalies, lens-related mechanisms, or secondary to surgical interventions.
Annual dilated retinal exams are essential for all Marfan patients, especially those who are aphakic or pseudophakic.

clues: A 60-year-old female presents with intermittent clear, blister-like swellings on the surface of her right eye that appear and resolve spontaneously.
Multiple clear, thin-walled, cystic, fluid-filled conjunctival vesicles are present on the temporal bulbar conjunctiva. They are non-tender and transilluminate. There is no injection or discharge.
The cysts contain clear, serous fluid (lymph). They collapse when punctured with a needle. There is no blood within the cysts.
The condition has been intermittent for years. It does not affect vision. There is no history of conjunctival surgery or radiation.
Observation for asymptomatic cases. Thermal cautery or excision for symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome lesions. Recurrence is common.
teachingPoints: Conjunctival lymphangiectasia consists of dilated lymphatic channels presenting as clear, thin-walled, cystic conjunctival lesions filled with lymphatic fluid.
They must be distinguished from conjunctival cysts (epithelial-lined, usually post-surgical) and lymphangiomas (which contain blood components—'chocolate cysts').
The condition is benign and primarily causes cosmetic concern or mild irritation.
Recurrence after treatment is common because the underlying lymphatic abnormality persists.

clues: A 35-year-old male presents to the ER with bilateral vision loss, headache, nausea, and confusion 24 hours after consuming bootleg liquor at a party.
Visual acuity is light perception bilaterally. Both pupils are dilated and poorly reactive. Optic discs show hyperemia and edema. There is a severe metabolic acidosis (pH 7.1) with high anion gap.
Serum methanol level is 45 mg/dL. Osmolar gap is elevated. Formic acid levels are elevated. CT brain shows bilateral putaminal necrosis.
He consumed approximately 200 mL of homemade spirit. Other partygoers are also affected. Serum ethanol level is undetectable.

Emergent fomepizole (alcohol dehydrogenase inhibitor) or IV ethanol if fomepizole unavailable. Hemodialysis to remove methanol and formate. IV sodium bicarbonate for acidosis. Folinic acid to enhance formate metabolism.
teachingPoints: Methanol toxicity causes blindness through its metabolite formic acid, which specifically targets the optic nerve and retinal ganglion cells by inhibiting mitochondrial cytochrome oxidase.
The latent period (12-24 hours) corresponds to the time needed for alcohol dehydrogenase to convert methanol to formaldehyde and then to formic acid.
Treatment must be initiated before the acidosis becomes severe; early fomepizole administration can prevent visual loss entirely.
Visual prognosis depends on the severity and duration of formic acid exposure; patients treated early may recover vision, while delayed treatment leads to optic atrophy and permanent blindness.

diagnosis: Iris Nevus (Cogan-Reese Variant vs. True Nevus)
clues: A 40-year-old female is referred for evaluation of a brown spot on the iris of her left eye noticed during a routine exam.

A flat, well-circumscribed, uniformly brown, small (2 mm) lesion is present on the inferior iris stroma. There is no distortion of the pupil, no ectropion uveae, no surface vascularity, and no sectoral cataract. IOP is normal.
Gonioscopy shows no angle involvement. Anterior segment OCT shows a flat, thin lesion without intrinsic vascularity. High-resolution anterior segment photography is obtained for baseline comparison.
The lesion has been present for years and appears stable. The patient has light blue irides. There are no other iris lesions.
Serial observation with anterior segment photography every 6-12 months. No treatment needed for a stable nevus.
teachingPoints: Iris nevi are common benign melanocytic lesions; features concerning for melanoma include documented growth, size >3 mm, intrinsic vascularity, ectropion uveae, sectoral cataract, and elevated IOP.
Iris nevi are more easily detected in light-colored irides and are present in approximately 4-6% of the population.
Transformation to melanoma occurs in approximately 2-5% of suspicious iris lesions over 10 years.
The ABCDEF rule for suspicious iris lesions: Age (young), Blood (hyphema), Clock hour (inferior), Diffuse, Ectropion uveae, Feathery margins.

aliases: Separation of ciliary body from scleral spur
clues: A 30-year-old male presents with persistent blurred vision and low IOP in his left eye 3 months after blunt trauma.

IOP is 3 mmHg OS (versus 16 mmHg OD). Visual acuity is 20/60 OS due to macular folds from chronic hypotony. There is a shallow anterior chamber. Gonioscopy reveals a visible gap between the ciliary body and scleral spur from 8-11 o'clock (cyclodialysis cleft).
UBM confirms the cyclodialysis cleft with communication between the anterior chamber and suprachoroidal space. Choroidal effusions are present. Disc edema and macular folds are noted.
The patient was struck in the eye 3 months ago. He has associated angle recession and a resolved hyphema. The low IOP has been persistent.

Atropine (cycloplegia) to rotate the ciliary body posteriorly and encourage cleft closure. If medical therapy fails: argon laser to the cleft bed, cryotherapy, direct cyclopexy (surgical closure), or vitrectomy with gas tamponade.
teachingPoints: A cyclodialysis cleft is a separation of the ciliary body from the scleral spur, creating a direct communication between the anterior chamber and suprachoroidal space, causing severe hypotony.
Chronic hypotony (IOP <5 mmHg) leads to choroidal effusion, optic disc edema, macular folds (hypotony maculopathy), and progressive visual loss.
Atropine works by relaxing the ciliary muscle, which rotates the ciliary body posteriorly, apposing it against the scleral spur to close the cleft.
If the cleft fails to close medically, surgical cyclopexy (direct suturing of the ciliary body to the scleral spur) is the definitive treatment.

clues: A newborn is noted to have bilateral corneal clouding at birth that appears to be non-progressive.

Both corneas have a diffuse, white, flaky stromal haze with a ground-glass appearance. The haze is uniform and involves the full thickness of the stroma. The epithelium and endothelium are normal. Corneal thickness is normal.

IOP is normal (ruling out congenital glaucoma). Corneal diameter is normal. There are no Haab striae. The haze has not changed over 6 months of observation. Genetic testing reveals a DCN (decorin) gene mutation.
The clouding is bilateral, symmetric, and non-progressive. The child's father has similar corneal findings. Autosomal dominant inheritance.
Deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty (DALK) can be considered for visual rehabilitation. Amblyopia management is important if the visual axis is significantly obscured.
teachingPoints: CHSD is a rare autosomal dominant corneal stromal dystrophy caused by DCN gene mutations affecting decorin, a small leucine-rich proteoglycan involved in collagen fibril organization.
Unlike CHED (endothelial), CHSD has normal endothelium and normal corneal thickness; the pathology is isolated to the stroma.
It is non-progressive (unlike many other congenital corneal opacities), which helps distinguish it from progressive conditions.
Electron microscopy shows abnormally spaced collagen fibrils with filamentous material between lamellae.

clues: A 30-year-old welder presents to the ER at 2 AM with severe bilateral eye pain, tearing, photophobia, and blepharospasm that began 6 hours after welding without protective goggles.

Bilateral conjunctival injection. Fluorescein staining shows diffuse, fine, punctate epithelial erosions concentrated in the interpalpebral zone of both corneas (UV-exposed area). Foreign body sensation is severe.
There is no infiltrate or anterior chamber reaction. Visual acuity is reduced to 20/60 bilaterally from the epithelial damage and tearing.
The patient was arc welding without a protective face shield for approximately 2 hours. The latent period of 6-12 hours between exposure and symptom onset is characteristic.
Topical antibiotic (erythromycin ointment) for prophylaxis. Cycloplegic (cyclopentolate) for pain. Oral analgesics. Cold compresses. Complete recovery expected within 24-48 hours.
teachingPoints: Photokeratitis is an acute UV-B (280-315 nm) radiation burn of the corneal epithelium, analogous to a sunburn of the cornea.
The characteristic 6-12 hour latent period between UV exposure and symptom onset is important for diagnosis—patients often present at night after daytime exposure.
Common causes include welding arc, snow reflection (snow blindness), tanning beds, and high-altitude UV exposure.
The condition is self-limited; complete epithelial healing occurs within 24-48 hours. No long-term sequelae.

clues: A 50-year-old male is found to have a flat pigmented lesion in the choroid during a routine dilated exam. He is asymptomatic.
A small (<5 mm base), flat, uniformly pigmented, gray-brown choroidal lesion is present in the posterior pole. There is no subretinal fluid, no orange pigment (lipofuscin), and no adjacent drusen.

B-scan shows a flat lesion with thickness <2 mm. OCT shows no subretinal fluid, minimal RPE changes, and no overlying photoreceptor disruption. Autofluorescence shows no hyperautofluorescent orange pigment.
The lesion is small, flat, and has no features suspicious for melanoma (using the 'To Find Small Ocular Melanoma Using Helpful Hints Daily' mnemonic—TFSOM-UHHD).
Serial observation with fundus photography, OCT, and autofluorescence every 6-12 months initially, then annually if stable.
teachingPoints: Choroidal nevi are extremely common (present in ~5-10% of Caucasian adults) and are virtually always benign; the lifetime risk of transformation to melanoma is approximately 1 in 8,845.

The TFSOM-UHHD mnemonic identifies risk factors for growth/malignancy: Thickness >2mm, Fluid (subretinal), Symptoms, Orange pigment, Margin <3mm from disc, Ultrasound Hollowness, Halo absent, Drusen absent.
The more risk factors present, the higher the risk of growth; 0 factors = ~3% growth at 5 years, ≥3 factors = ~50% growth.
Annual monitoring with multimodal imaging is the standard of care for choroidal nevi.

clues: A 75-year-old male on warfarin undergoes trabeculectomy for refractory glaucoma. During the procedure, the surgeon notes sudden shallowing of the anterior chamber and a dark mound pushing the retina forward.

Intraoperatively, the eye suddenly becomes firm (IOP spike), the anterior chamber collapses, and a dark, dome-shaped, hemorrhagic choroidal elevation is visible through the pupil. The incision begins to gape with dark blood.
The hemorrhage is from rupture of the long or short posterior ciliary arteries into the suprachoroidal space. B-scan postoperatively shows massive suprachoroidal hemorrhage with 'kissing choroidals.'
Risk factors: advanced age, high preoperative IOP, anticoagulation (warfarin), atherosclerosis, aphakia, and elevated blood pressure during surgery.

Immediate: close the incision as quickly as possible to restore IOP and tamponade. Postoperative: observation for 10-14 days, then drainage of the liquefied suprachoroidal blood with or without vitrectomy.
teachingPoints: Expulsive suprachoroidal hemorrhage is the most feared complication of intraocular surgery, occurring in approximately 0.04-0.08% of cataract surgeries and higher in glaucoma surgery.
It is caused by rupture of the ciliary arteries when sudden IOP drop during surgery decompresses the eye.
Waiting 10-14 days before drainage allows the blood to liquefy, making it easier to drain through sclerotomies.
Visual prognosis depends on severity; limited hemorrhages may have reasonable outcomes, while massive hemorrhages often result in NLP vision.

clues: A 5-year-old child presents with bilateral mild nystagmus and reduced visual acuity (20/60) without an identifiable cause.
Fundoscopy shows absence of the normal foveal reflex bilaterally. The retinal vasculature passes through the foveal area without the normal avascular zone. There is no foveal depression.

OCT reveals absence of the foveal pit with persistence of inner retinal layers at the fovea (failure of normal foveal development). The outer retinal layers are present but the normal foveal architecture is absent.
There is no albinism (normal iris transillumination, normal skin/hair pigment, normal VEP). No aniridia. No PAX6 mutation. The foveal hypoplasia appears isolated.
Correct refractive error (significant astigmatism or hyperopia may be present). Low vision aids if needed. No treatment can create a normal foveal pit.
teachingPoints: Foveal hypoplasia is characterized by the absence of a normal foveal pit and persistence of inner retinal layers over the foveal center, preventing normal cone photoreceptor packing.
It is most commonly associated with albinism, aniridia (PAX6 mutations), or prematurity, but can be isolated (idiopathic or familial).
OCT has revolutionized the diagnosis—previously, foveal hypoplasia was often missed on clinical exam and patients were labeled as having 'unexplained visual loss.'
The degree of foveal hypoplasia (graded 1-4 on OCT) correlates with visual acuity.

clues: A newborn infant presents with a small left eye and a bluish cystic mass protruding from the inferior orbit.
The left eye is microphthalmic (small globe). A large, blue-gray, translucent, cystic mass protrudes from the inferior orbit. The cyst is continuous with the globe through a colobomatous defect.
MRI shows a cystic structure lined by neuroectodermal tissue continuous with the microphthalmic globe through an inferior coloboma. B-scan shows a small, disorganized globe. The right eye is normal.
The condition is congenital and unilateral. The cyst has been present since birth and transilluminates.

If the cyst is growing and causing cosmetic issues or compressing normal orbital structures, surgical excision of the cyst (preserving the globe if it has any visual potential) or enucleation with prosthetic fitting.
teachingPoints: Microphthalmos with cyst results from failure of closure of the embryonic fissure, allowing neuroectodermal tissue to herniate through the coloboma and form a cyst.
The cyst can be larger than the globe itself and may grow with time.
Preservation of the cyst or globe may be beneficial for orbital growth stimulation, as enucleation in infancy can lead to orbital and facial asymmetry.
MRI is the imaging modality of choice to delineate the cyst's extent and its relationship to the globe and optic nerve.

clues: A 78-year-old immunosuppressed male presents with a rapidly growing, painless, red-violet nodule on his right upper eyelid over 3 months.
A firm, non-tender, dome-shaped, red-violet, shiny nodule is present on the upper eyelid. It has telangiectatic surface vessels. There are palpable preauricular and submandibular lymph nodes.

Incisional biopsy shows small, round, blue cells with neuroendocrine differentiation. Immunohistochemistry is positive for CK20 (perinuclear dot pattern) and synaptophysin. Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV) is detected.
The patient is on immunosuppressive therapy after renal transplant. The lesion was initially thought to be a chalazion but grew rapidly.

Wide local excision with Mohs or margin-controlled surgery. Sentinel lymph node biopsy. Adjuvant radiation therapy. Immunotherapy (checkpoint inhibitors—avelumab, pembrolizumab) for metastatic disease.
teachingPoints: Merkel cell carcinoma is a rare, aggressive neuroendocrine skin cancer with a high mortality rate (~33% at 3 years); periocular involvement represents ~5-10% of head and neck MCC.
The AEIOU features suggest MCC: Asymptomatic, Expanding rapidly, Immunosuppression, Older (>50), UV-exposed/fair skin.
CK20 with a perinuclear dot pattern is the most specific immunohistochemical marker for MCC.
Checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy has transformed the treatment of advanced MCC, with response rates of 50-70% in metastatic disease.

clues: A 2-year-old child is brought in with a white, dome-shaped growth at the edge of the cornea that has been present since birth.

A smooth, well-circumscribed, white-yellow, dome-shaped mass is present at the inferotemporal limbus, straddling the cornea and sclera. Fine hairs (vellus hair) may be growing from the surface. It is approximately 5 mm in diameter.
The lesion causes localized corneal flattening and irregular astigmatism. Visual acuity in the affected eye is reduced. The fellow eye is normal.

The lesion is congenital. There are no other facial anomalies. Genetic testing for Goldenhar syndrome (oculoauricular vertebral spectrum) may be considered if there are associated preauricular tags or vertebral anomalies.
Observation if small and not amblyogenic. Lamellar keratectomy (superficial shave excision) for cosmetic improvement and to reduce astigmatism. Deep excision risks corneal perforation.
teachingPoints: Limbal dermoids are congenital choristomas (normal tissue in an abnormal location) containing skin elements (dermis, fat, hair follicles, sweat glands) at the corneal limbus.
They are the most common congenital corneal tumor and are typically located at the inferotemporal limbus.
Goldenhar syndrome association: limbal dermoids + preauricular skin tags + vertebral anomalies (hemifacial microsomia spectrum).
Lamellar excision improves cosmesis and astigmatism but carries a risk of corneal scar and perforation if the lesion is deep.

clues: A 50-year-old female presents with gradual vision loss in the right eye. Fundoscopy reveals unusual vessels on the optic disc.

There are dilated, tortuous venous channels on the surface of the right optic disc that shunt blood from the retinal venous system to the choroidal circulation, bypassing the normal central retinal vein drainage. The disc appears pale.
MRI orbits shows an optic nerve sheath meningioma. The collateral vessels have developed because the tumor is compressing the central retinal vein within the optic nerve.
The classic triad is present: progressive vision loss + optic atrophy + optociliary shunt vessels. The patient also has a RAPD.
Treatment is directed at the underlying cause—in this case, fractionated stereotactic radiotherapy for the optic nerve sheath meningioma.
teachingPoints: Optociliary shunt vessels are dilated retinochoroidal venous collaterals that develop when the central retinal vein is chronically compressed or obstructed.
The three main causes are optic nerve sheath meningioma (most classic), chronic CRVO (old occlusion), and chronic papilledema/optic disc swelling.
They represent a compensatory drainage pathway: when the CRV is blocked, retinal venous blood is rerouted through preexisting but normally invisible channels to the choroidal venous system.
In the setting of progressive vision loss with disc pallor, optociliary shunts should prompt imaging for optic nerve sheath meningioma.

clues: A 65-year-old male presents with a rapidly growing dome-shaped nodule on his right lower eyelid that appeared 4 weeks ago.
A dome-shaped, flesh-colored nodule with a central keratin-filled crater is present on the lower eyelid. It has grown rapidly to 1 cm in 4 weeks. The margins are smooth and rolled.
Biopsy shows a well-differentiated squamous proliferation with a central keratinous crater. The pathologist notes it may be difficult to distinguish from well-differentiated SCC on a small biopsy.
The rapid growth pattern (weeks) and clinical morphology (dome with central crater) are characteristic. The patient has sun-damaged skin.

Excisional biopsy with margin control is recommended (treats the lesion and provides complete histologic assessment). Observation is possible if the diagnosis is certain, as some KAs spontaneously involute.
teachingPoints: Keratoacanthoma is a rapidly growing, self-limiting (in many cases) squamous proliferation that arises from the hair follicle; it is debated whether it is a distinct entity or a variant of well-differentiated SCC.
The rapid growth (reaches full size in 4-8 weeks) followed by potential spontaneous involution over months distinguishes it from typical SCC.
Because distinguishing KA from SCC histologically can be challenging, most are treated as SCC with excision rather than observation.
Periocular KAs are managed more aggressively due to the potential for tissue destruction during growth and the difficulty of definitive histologic differentiation from SCC on small biopsies.

clues: A 45-year-old male presents with progressive bilateral central vision loss over the past 5 years. His father had similar symptoms.
Fundoscopy reveals a well-demarcated, round area of geographic atrophy of the RPE and choriocapillaris centered on the fovea bilaterally, with visible underlying large choroidal vessels.

OCT shows complete loss of RPE, ellipsoid zone, and outer nuclear layer within the atrophic area. Fundus autofluorescence shows a well-demarcated area of hypo-autofluorescence at the fovea with a subtle hyperautofluorescent rim. ERG shows mild cone-rod dysfunction.
The patient's father and paternal grandmother both developed central vision loss in their 40s-50s. Genetic testing reveals a PRPH2 (peripherin/RDS) gene mutation.
No treatment available. Low vision rehabilitation. Genetic counseling. Research into gene therapy is ongoing.
teachingPoints: Central areolar choroidal dystrophy is an autosomal dominant macular dystrophy (most commonly PRPH2 mutations) causing progressive, well-demarcated central RPE and choriocapillaris atrophy.
The atrophy begins as subtle RPE changes and evolves to a well-defined round area of complete atrophy revealing the underlying choroidal vessels.
Visual acuity may remain surprisingly good until the atrophy involves the foveal center, then declines rapidly.
It must be distinguished from geographic atrophy of AMD (which occurs in older patients, is not hereditary, and has drusen).

clues: A 35-year-old female with newly diagnosed Graves disease presents with a wide-eyed, staring appearance.

Bilateral upper eyelid retraction with visible sclera above the superior limbus (scleral show). Lid lag is present on downgaze (von Graefe sign). The superior tarsal fold is elevated. There is mild bilateral proptosis.
Hertel exophthalmometry shows 22 mm bilaterally. Thyroid function tests show suppressed TSH with elevated free T4 and T3. CT orbits shows enlarged extraocular muscles.
The patient has Graves hyperthyroidism with tremor, tachycardia, and weight loss. The staring appearance is her primary cosmetic concern.

Control thyroid function first. Lubrication for exposure. Once TED is stable (inactive for 6 months): surgical correction with upper lid retraction repair (Müllerectomy or levator recession on adjustable sutures).
teachingPoints: Upper eyelid retraction is the most common ocular sign of thyroid eye disease, present in approximately 90% of TED patients.
It is caused by sympathetic overactivity of Müller muscle (smooth muscle), fibrosis and contracture of the levator-Müller complex, and proptosis pushing the lid up.
Lid retraction causes exposure keratopathy, particularly of the inferior cornea, and is the leading cause of ocular surface symptoms in TED.
Surgical correction must wait until the disease is stable; operating during active inflammation risks under- or over-correction.

clues: A 45-year-old female presents with headaches, irregular menses, and recent minor car accidents where she did not see vehicles approaching from the side.
Visual acuity is 20/20 bilaterally. Formal perimetry reveals a complete bitemporal hemianopia respecting the vertical meridian. Optic discs show bilateral temporal pallor (band atrophy).
MRI brain shows a large pituitary macroadenoma (3 cm) extending superiorly and compressing the optic chiasm. Prolactin level is elevated at 450 ng/mL (prolactinoma).
The patient has galactorrhea and amenorrhea. She has been having difficulty with peripheral awareness while driving.

For prolactinoma: dopamine agonist therapy (cabergoline) is first-line—can shrink the tumor and restore visual fields without surgery. Neurosurgery (transsphenoidal) if medical therapy fails or for non-functioning adenomas.
teachingPoints: Bitemporal hemianopia from pituitary adenoma is the classic chiasmal syndrome; the crossing nasal retinal fibers (representing temporal visual fields) are compressed first.
Band atrophy of the optic disc (temporal and nasal pallor with preserved superior and inferior rim) is the disc appearance resulting from chiasmal compression.
Prolactinomas are unique among pituitary adenomas because they respond to medical therapy (dopamine agonists) with tumor shrinkage—visual fields can recover without surgery.
Post-fixational blindness (difficulty with depth perception at near from loss of overlapping visual fields) is a subtle early symptom of bitemporal hemianopia.

clues: A 12-year-old boy presents with a central scotoma in his right eye after his friend shone a green laser pointer directly into his eye during play yesterday.
Visual acuity is 20/60 OD. Fundoscopy reveals a small, round, yellow-white foveal lesion with surrounding hemorrhage.

OCT shows a focal full-thickness disruption of the outer retinal layers at the fovea with a subfoveal hyporeflective cavity. The inner retinal layers are intact. The lesion corresponds to the area of the laser burn.
The laser was a commercially available green (532 nm) laser pointer of unknown power (likely >5 mW, exceeding consumer safety standards). The exposure lasted approximately 2 seconds.

No proven treatment. Observation; some spontaneous improvement is possible over weeks to months as the acute injury partially heals. Topical NSAIDs or steroids have been used anecdotally without clear evidence.
teachingPoints: High-powered handheld laser pointers can cause thermal and photochemical damage to the fovea, resulting in permanent visual scotomas, especially in children.
Green (532 nm) lasers are most dangerous because the wavelength is maximally absorbed by the RPE and the photopic luminosity function makes the retinal exposure reflex less effective.
Many commercially available laser pointers significantly exceed the safe power output of <5 mW, despite misleading labeling.
This is an increasing public health concern, particularly among children; education about the dangers of laser pointer exposure is important.

clues: A 40-year-old male with a history of a severe alkali chemical burn to his right eye 5 years ago presents with persistent redness, pain, and poor vision despite prior corneal transplant attempts.

The right cornea shows a vascularized, opaque surface with conjunctival epithelium growing onto the cornea (conjunctivalization). There is no clear demarcation between conjunctiva and cornea. Fluorescein staining shows late stippled staining (conjunctival epithelium on cornea). Goblet cells are present on the corneal surface (impression cytology).
Previous penetrating keratoplasty failed within months due to the lack of limbal stem cells to maintain the corneal epithelium. The graft became conjunctivalized.
The chemical burn destroyed the limbal stem cells (located at the palisades of Vogt). Without stem cell replenishment, corneal epithelial homeostasis cannot be maintained.

Limbal stem cell transplantation (CLET—cultivated limbal epithelial transplantation or SLET—simple limbal epithelial transplantation from the fellow eye) followed by corneal transplant once the surface is stable.
teachingPoints: The limbal stem cells reside in the palisades of Vogt at the corneoscleral junction and are essential for continuous corneal epithelial renewal and maintenance of the corneal phenotype.
Without functioning limbal stem cells, the corneal surface becomes conjunctivalized—conjunctival epithelium (with goblet cells and blood vessels) replaces the transparent corneal epithelium.
A corneal transplant will inevitably fail in the setting of LSCD because the graft epithelium cannot be maintained without a functioning limbal stem cell population.
The Boston keratoprosthesis (KPro) is an alternative for bilateral LSCD where limbal stem cell transplant is not possible.

clues: A 30-year-old male presents with chronic unilateral anterior uveitis and multiple small corneal and conjunctival nodules that have not responded to standard anti-inflammatory treatment.

Slit-lamp reveals multiple small, yellowish-white, granulomatous nodules on the conjunctiva and in the corneal stroma. High-magnification examination reveals tiny dark barbed structures (caterpillar setae/hairs) within some of the nodules. There is chronic granulomatous anterior uveitis.
The hairs have migrated through the cornea into the anterior chamber, and some can be seen on the iris surface. There is a chronic granulomatous reaction around each hair.
The patient is an arborist who handles trees regularly. He recalls a 'caterpillar sting' to his right eye several months ago from a processionary caterpillar (Thaumetopoea).
Surgical removal of accessible setae from the cornea, conjunctiva, and anterior chamber (vitrectomy if posterior migration). Topical steroids for the granulomatous reaction.
teachingPoints: Ophthalmia nodosa is a chronic granulomatous inflammatory reaction caused by retained caterpillar, tarantula, or plant setae (barbed hairs) in the ocular tissues.
The barbed microstructure of the setae causes them to migrate progressively deeper into ocular tissues—from conjunctiva through cornea into the anterior chamber and even into the vitreous.
The inflammatory response is both mechanical (barbed hairs irritate tissue) and immunologic (granulomatous reaction to the foreign protein).
Prevention includes wearing protective eyewear when handling caterpillars (especially pine processionary caterpillars) or tarantulas.
